fixed-titan.scenario_text: I took out a personal loan with your bank in March 2024. At no point during the application process or at the point of sale was I given any information about your complaints procedure or told how I could complain if something went wrong. I've since had a problem with an overcharge on my account and when I tried to find out how to complain, there was nothing on my loan agreement, nothing in the paperwork I was given, and I had to spend over an hour on the phone just to find out where to send my complaint. I also notice your website has no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service anywhere. I believe you have failed in your duty to make me aware of my rights and I want this addressed.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
The customer is complaining about two distinct failures: (1) not being given information about the firm's internal complaints procedure at or around the point of sale of a personal loan in March 2024; and (2) the firm's website not mentioning the Financial Ombudsman Service.

Regulatory position based on retrieved provisions:

1. DISP 1.2.1R (chunk: disp1s2) requires that summary details of the firm's internal complaints handling procedures are provided to eligible complainants. This obligation applies at, or immediately after, the point when contact is first made with an eligible complainant. If the customer was not given this information at or around the point of sale of the loan, this is a potential breach of a binding rule.

2. DISP 1.2.3G (chunk: disp1s2) provides guidance that the summary details should cover at least: how the firm handles and seeks to resolve complaints; and (where the complaint falls within FOS jurisdiction) that if unresolved, the complainant may be entitled to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service. This is guidance, not a rule, but it informs what adequate disclosure looks like.

3. DISP 1.2.1R also requires firms to provide information about the Financial Ombudsman Service on their website (where one exists) and in general conditions of contracts with eligible complainants. The customer's allegation that the website contains no mention of the FOS is therefore a potential breach of this binding rule.

Recommended action:
- Investigate whether the firm's loan documentation and onboarding process at the time of the March 2024 sale included the required complaints procedure summary. Check whether the website currently carries FOS information.
- If either failure is confirmed, acknowledge the breach, apologise, and confirm remedial steps taken (e.g. updating documentation and website).
- The customer has also experienced a separate substantive issue (overcharge on account) — this complaint does not address that; a separate investigation may be needed.
- The customer has now successfully made a complaint, so a final response will be required under DISP 1.6.4R once the investigation is complete.

Assumptions/uncertainties to confirm before sending:
- Has the firm's loan documentation and onboarding process been reviewed to confirm whether complaints procedure information was or was not provided at point of sale in March 2024?
- Does the firm's website currently carry FOS information? If it does, the customer's allegation on that point may need to be addressed differently.
- The overcharge allegation is a separate substantive complaint — confirm whether this response is intended to address that too, or only the procedural/disclosure complaint. The draft below addresses only the disclosure complaint.
- Human review is required before sending given the potential regulatory breach and the possibility of redress for the time and inconvenience caused.

Note: DISP 1.1A.11R (chunk: disp1s20) imposes equivalent website disclosure obligations on MiFID investment firms specifically. A personal loan is unlikely to be a MiFID product, so that provision has not been relied upon — the general DISP 1.2.1R obligation is the applicable one here.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch and for taking the time to raise these concerns. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you experienced in finding out how to make a complaint, and I want to assure you that we take this seriously.

You have raised two concerns, which I will address in turn.

First, you have told us that when you took out your personal loan in March 2024, you were not given any information about our complaints procedure or how to complain if something went wrong. Our regulator requires us to provide customers with summary details of our internal complaints handling procedures at or around the point when we first deal with them. We are investigating whether this information was provided to you as it should have been at the time of your loan application. We will write to you again once we have completed that review.

Second, you have told us that our website does not mention the Financial Ombudsman Service. We are required to make information about the Financial Ombudsman Service available on our website. We are looking into this as a matter of urgency and will take steps to address any shortcoming identified.

We recognise that you spent significant time trying to find out how to complain, and we are sorry for that inconvenience. We are treating your complaint as a formal complaint and will provide you with a full response once our investigation is complete. If we are unable to provide a final response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint, we will write to explain why and let you know when we expect to be able to do so.

If at any point you are dissatisfied with how we are handling your complaint, you have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can find out more about the Financial Ombudsman Service at [FOS website address]. I have also enclosed their explanatory leaflet for your reference.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I've been trying to call my bank to make a formal complaint about an incorrect fee they charged me. The only number they provide for complaints is an 0871 number, which is costing me a fortune every time I ring. I've called three times now and been on hold for extended periods, racking up charges. I'm on a basic state pension and these call costs are adding up significantly. I shouldn't have to pay premium rates just to complain. I want the bank to reimburse me for the call charges I've incurred and to provide a proper free or local-rate number for complaints.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises a clear regulatory breach. GEN 7.2.1R requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate. GEN 7.2.5R explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant with this call charges rule, as their cost can exceed the geographic rate and varies by tariff. Using an 0871 number as the sole complaints contact line is therefore a breach of GEN 7.2.1R and GEN 7.2.5R.

The customer is asking for: (1) reimbursement of call charges incurred, and (2) provision of a compliant number going forward.

On reimbursement: the retrieved provisions establish the breach clearly, but they do not themselves specify a remedy or quantum of redress. Any decision to reimburse the customer's call costs is a financial/redress decision that must be made by a human reviewer. You will need to confirm the actual charges incurred (the customer mentions three calls with extended hold times — you should ask them to provide evidence, e.g. phone bill extracts).

On the compliant number: GEN 7.2.4R sets out numbers that would comply (01, 02, 03 prefixes; 0800/0808 freephone; standard 07 mobile numbers). You should escalate internally to ensure the complaints line number is changed to a compliant one.

Vulnerability note: the customer has indicated they are on a basic state pension and that the charges are adding up significantly. This is a potential vulnerability indicator (financial hardship) and should be flagged for appropriate handling under your vulnerability procedures.

Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft: (a) that this is indeed Crest Bank's complaints line number (i.e. the complaint is about your firm); (b) the customer's actual call costs — you may wish to ask for evidence before committing to reimbursement; (c) whether your firm has a compliant alternative number already available that can be offered immediately.

Human review is required before sending given the redress element, the vulnerability indicator, and the internal escalation needed on the non-compliant number.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for contacting us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced trying to reach us, and I want to assure you that we take your concerns very seriously.

You have raised two important points, and I want to address both.

**The telephone number we have been providing**

Our regulator's rules are clear that when a firm provides a telephone line for customers to contact it about their contract, the firm must not require customers to pay more than the basic rate for that call. Numbers beginning with 0871 do not meet this standard, as their cost can exceed the basic geographic rate and varies depending on the caller's telephone tariff. We recognise that this is a concern you have rightly raised, and we are looking into this as a matter of urgency. We will ensure that a compliant number — one that does not cost you more than a standard geographic or freephone rate — is made available to you and to all our customers for complaints and other contact.

**The charges you have already incurred**

We understand that you have called us on three occasions and incurred call charges as a result. We are sorry that this has happened, particularly given the circumstances you have described. We would like to look into reimbursing you for the costs you have incurred. To help us do this, please could you provide us with evidence of the charges — for example, a copy of the relevant section of your phone bill showing the calls made to our number and the costs charged. Once we have this, we will review your request and write to you with our decision.

**Your underlying complaint about the incorrect fee**

We also note that your original reason for calling was to raise a complaint about a fee you believe was incorrectly charged. We want to make sure that complaint is properly recorded and investigated. Please set out the details of the fee in question — including the date it was charged and the amount — and we will ensure it is handled in accordance with our complaints process. We will acknowledge your complaint and keep you informed of progress.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If at any stage you remain dissatisfied with how we are handling your concerns, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further information about this right at the appropriate stage of our complaints process.

Thank you again for bringing this to our attention. We are sorry for the inconvenience caused and will do our best to resolve matters for you promptly.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate — the core obligation breached by use of an 0871 number as the complaints line.
Defines 'basic rate' as the simple cost of connection, which must not provide the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues — relevant to why 0871 numbers are non-compliant.
Sets out the categories of number (01, 02, 03, 0800, 0808, standard 07 mobile) that would comply with the call charges rule — relevant to advising the firm on what compliant alternatives exist.
Explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant with the call charges rule because their cost can exceed the geographic rate and varies by tariff — directly applicable to the number used by the firm.

retrieved_chunks.content: # GEN — GEN 7.2 Call charges ## GEN 7.2.1 (Rules) A firm which operates a telephone line for the purpose of enabling a consumer to contact the firm in relation to a contract that has been entered into with the firm, must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate for the telephone call. ## GEN 7.2.2 (Guidance) The contract entered into with the firm may be in writing or otherwise. ## GEN 7.2.3 (Rules) For the purposes of the call charges rule, the basic rate is the simple cost of connection and must not provide the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues. ## GEN 7.2.4 (Rules) The following numbers, if used by firms, would comply with the call charges rule: (1) geographic numbers or numbers which are always set at the same rate, which usually begin with the prefix 01, 02 or 03; (2) calls which can be free of charge to call, for example 0800 and 0808 numbers; and (3) standard mobile numbers, which usually begin with the prefix 07, provided that the firm ordinarily uses a mobile number to receive telephone calls. ## GEN 7.2.5 (Rules) The following numbers, if used by firms, would not comply with the call charges rule: (1) premium rate numbers that begin with the prefix 09; (2) other revenue sharing numbers in which a portion of the call charge can be used to either provide a service or make a small payment to the firm, such as telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 084 or 0871, 0872 or 0873; and (3) telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 0870 as the cost of making a telephone call on such numbers can be higher than a geographic cost and will vary depending on the consumer’s telephone tariff.

metadata.section_name: GEN 7.2 Call charges

Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1. 5. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1. --- ## 6. retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP 1.10B Payment services and electronic money complaints reporting ## DISP 1.10B.1 (Directions) (1) Once a year a credit institution that provides payment services or issues electronic money must provide the FCA with a complete report concerning complaints received about payment services and electronic money. (2) Once a year an electronic money institution, a payment institution or a registered account information service provider must provide the FCA with a complete report concerning complaints received about payment services and electronic money. (3) The report required by (1) and (2) must be set out in the format in DISP 1 Annex 1AD. ## DISP 1.10B.2 (Guidance) (1) In contrast to the other provisions in DISP 1 which generally apply only to complaints from eligible complainants, the complaints reporting directions apply in addition to complaints from payment service users that are not eligible complainants. (2) Payment service providers are reminded that regulation 101 of the Payment Services Regulations contains requirements relating to complaints resolution procedures applicable to complaints from payment service users that are not eligible complainants. ## DISP 1.10B.3 (Directions) A respondent must not include in the report a complaint that has been forwarded in its entirety to another respondent under the complaints forwarding rules. ## DISP 1.10B.4 (Directions) Where a respondent has forwarded to another respondent only part of a complaint or where two respondents may be jointly responsible for a complaint, then the complaint should be reported by both respondents. ## DISP 1.10B.5 (Directions) Respondents that are part of a group may submit a joint report to the FCA. The joint report must contain the information required from all respondents concerned and clearly indicate the respondents on whose behalf the report is submitted. The obligation to provide a report, and the responsibility for the report, remains with each respondent in the group. ## DISP 1.10B.6 (Guidance) Not all the respondents in the group need to submit the report jointly.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for contacting us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced trying to reach us, and I want to assure you that we take your concerns very seriously. You have raised two important points, and I want to address both.
verdicts.reasoning: The response opens with a clear acknowledgement of the complaint, reflects accurate understanding of the subject matter (difficulties reaching the firm via 0871 number, call charges incurred, and the underlying fee complaint), and signals that both points will be addressed.

verdicts.evidence: We recognise that this is a concern you have rightly raised, and we are looking into this as a matter of urgency. We would like to look into reimbursing you for the costs you have incurred... Once we have this, we will review your request and write to you with our decision.
verdicts.reasoning: The response does not state a clear final decision on the complaint itself (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). Instead, it defers the decision on reimbursement pending receipt of evidence, and commits to a future letter. On the non-compliant number, it acknowledges the breach implicitly but commits only to 'looking into' and ensuring a compliant number is made available, without a definitive decision. The reasons engage with the specific points (0871 breach, call charges, underlying fee) but the decision is deferred rather than stated, making this PARTIAL rather than PRESENT.

verdicts.evidence: If at any stage you remain dissatisfied with how we are handling your concerns, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further information about this right at the appropriate stage of our complaints process.
verdicts.reasoning: The response mentions the FOS and states the right to refer, but does not provide the means to do so (no contact details, website, or leaflet signpost). It defers provision of 'further information' to a later stage, which does not satisfy the requirement to inform the complainant how to refer in this final response. This is PARTIAL because the right is stated but the mechanism is omitted.

The response does not state the six-month deadline running from the date of this final response. The reference to 'at any stage you remain dissatisfied' and 'at the appropriate stage of our complaints process' does not anchor a time limit to the final response date, nor does it specify six months.

It does not minimise or dismiss the concerns and treats the complaint on its merits.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I took out a personal loan with your bank in 2021 and was sold payment protection insurance at the same time. In March this year, I submitted a formal complaint explaining that the PPI was mis-sold because I already had employer sickness cover and was never asked about existing protection. Your complaints team sent me a final response letter four weeks later rejecting my complaint, but the letter simply stated 'we have reviewed your complaint and do not believe it should be upheld' with no further explanation. There was no assessment of the specific points I raised about my existing employer cover, no consideration of whether redress might be appropriate, and no explanation of how you reached your decision. I feel my complaint has not been properly investigated or fairly assessed, and the response I received was neither clear nor informative. I would like my complaint to be reconsidered properly.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
The customer is complaining that their PPI mis-selling complaint was rejected without adequate reasoning — specifically, no assessment of their existing employer sickness cover, no consideration of redress, and no explanation of how the decision was reached. This raises a significant concern under DISP App 3 and the final response requirements in DISP 1.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Adequacy of the final response (DISP 1 chunk):** The retrieved provisions set out what a final response must contain. A rejection must 'give reasons for doing so' (DISP 1.6.2R equivalent wording in the retrieved chunk). A bare statement that the complaint is not upheld, with no reasoning, does not satisfy this requirement. The original final response letter appears deficient on its face.

2. **Assessment of the mis-selling complaint (DISP App 3):** DISP App 3.1.1G makes clear that the firm must handle PPI mis-selling complaints by assessing the specific circumstances of the sale. DISP App 3.3.11G and 3.3.12G require the firm to consider whether information was communicated fairly, clearly and not misleadingly, having regard to the complainant's individual circumstances — including, per 3.3.12G(1), any evidence of the complainant's objectives and intentions at the time of the sale. The customer's point about existing employer sickness cover is directly relevant to whether the sale was appropriate and whether the firm took reasonable steps to understand their needs. The original response does not appear to have engaged with this at all.

3. **PPI deadline note:** The sale took place in 2021, which is after the 29 August 2017 cut-off in DISP 2.8.9R. The PPI deadline rule (DISP 2.8.9R) therefore does not bar this complaint. The complaint was also referred in March this year, well within any applicable time limits. No waiver issue arises.

4. **Redress:** If, on proper reconsideration, the complaint is upheld, DISP App 3.7 evidential provisions set out how redress should be calculated (including deducting any rebate received and, for single premium policies added to a loan, restructuring or compensating for the loan balance impact). You will need to establish whether this was a single or regular premium policy before any redress calculation.

**Recommended action:**

- Reopen and properly reinvestigate the complaint. The original final response is likely non-compliant as it gives no reasons and does not engage with the specific grounds raised.
- The reinvestigation must assess: (a) whether the customer was asked about existing protection; (b) whether the sale was appropriate given their employer sickness cover; (c) whether information was communicated fairly and clearly per DISP App 3.3.11G/3.3.12G.
- Issue a new final response that either upholds (with appropriate redress) or rejects (with full reasons addressing the specific points raised), and which includes the FOS leaflet, FOS website address, referral rights, and the time-limit waiver indication per the DISP 1 chunk.
- **Human review is required** before sending: this complaint involves potential redress, the original response appears procedurally deficient, and the reinvestigation outcome is not yet known. The draft below acknowledges the complaint and commits to proper reconsideration — it does not pre-judge the outcome or admit liability. Confirm before sending that: (i) the bank is genuinely reopening the complaint; (ii) the FOS leaflet will be enclosed; (iii) the correct waiver wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R will be inserted where indicated.

**Assumption flagged:** The customer draft below treats this letter as an interim acknowledgement of the reopening, not as a new final response. If the bank is in a position to issue a substantive final response now (i.e., the reinvestigation is already complete), a different letter will be needed.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry to hear that you are dissatisfied with the response you received to your complaint about the payment protection insurance sold alongside your personal loan in 2021.

Having reviewed your letter, we recognise that you raised specific concerns — in particular, that you already held employer sickness cover at the time of the sale and that this was not taken into account. We also note your concern that our previous response did not explain how we reached our decision or address the points you raised.

We take these concerns seriously. We are reopening your complaint and will carry out a proper assessment of the circumstances of the sale, including the specific points you have raised about your existing cover and whether the policy was appropriate for your needs at the time.

We will write to you again with our findings. If we are not in a position to provide a full response within eight weeks of your original complaint, we will write to explain why and let you know when we expect to be able to do so.

In the meantime, we want to make sure you are aware of your rights. You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, and their website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk. [INSERT TIME LIMIT WAIVER WORDING FROM DISP 1 ANNEX 3R AS APPLICABLE]

We are sorry for the frustration this has caused and we will be in touch as soon as our review is complete.

Yours sincerely,

[Name]
[Job title]
[Bank name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Sets out what a final response must contain when rejecting a complaint, including giving reasons for the rejection — the original response's bare rejection without reasoning appears non-compliant with this requirement.
Establishes that the firm must handle PPI mis-selling complaints by assessing the specific circumstances of the sale, including whether the sale was appropriate for the complainant.

Requires the firm to consider whether information was communicated to the complainant in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading and with due regard to the complainant's information needs — directly relevant to whether the customer was properly informed about the policy given their existing cover.

Specifies the evidence the firm should have regard to when assessing the information communicated, including the complainant's individual circumstances, objectives and intentions at the time of the sale — the customer's existing employer sickness cover falls squarely within this.
Sets the 29 August 2019 PPI complaint deadline; the sale in 2021 post-dates the 29 August 2017 cut-off in DISP 2.8.9R(1)(a), so this rule does not bar the complaint and no deadline issue arises.

Sets out how redress should be calculated if the complaint is upheld on reinvestigation, including deduction of any rebate received — relevant to the handler's need to establish premium type before any redress assessment.

retrieved_chunks.content: The firm should consider the available evidence and assess whether or not it gave advice or made a recommendation (explicitly or implicitly) to the complainant. ## DISP App 3.3.11 (Guidance) The firm should consider in all situations whether it communicated information to the complainant in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading and with due regard to the complainant's information needs. ## DISP App 3.3.12 (Guidance) In considering the information communicated to the complainant and the complainant's information needs, the evidence to which a firm should have regard includes: (1) the complainant's individual circumstances at the time of the sale (for example, the firm should take into account any evidence of limited financial capability or understanding on the part of the complainant); (2) the complainant's objectives and intentions at the time of the sale; (3) whether, from a reasonable customer's perspective, the documentation provided to the complainant was sufficiently clear, concise and presented fairly (for example, was the documentation in plain and intelligible language?); (4) in a sale that was primarily conducted orally, whether sufficient information was communicated during the sale discussion for the customer to make an informed decision (for example, did the firm give an oral explanation of the main characteristics of the policy or specifically draw the complainant's attention to that information on a computer screen or in a document and give the complainant time to read and consider it?); (5) any evidence about the tone and pace of oral communication (for example, was documentation read out too quickly for the complainant to have understood it?); and (6) any extra explanation or information given by the firm in response to questions raised (or information disclosed) by the complainant. ## DISP App 3.3.13 (Guidance) The firm should not reject a complaint solely because the complainant had held a payment protection contract previously.

retrieved_chunks.content: If the complainant has received any rebate, for example if the customer cancelled a single premium payment protection contract before it ran full term and received a refund, the firm may deduct the value of this rebate from the amount otherwise payable to the complainant. ## DISP App 3.7.4 (Evidential Provisions) Additionally, where a single premium was added to a loan: (1) for live policies: (a) subject to DISP App 3.7.5 E, where there remains an outstanding loan balance, the firm should, where possible, arrange for the loan to be restructured (without charge to the complainant but using any applicable cancellation value) with the effect of: (i) removing amounts relating to the payment protection contract (including any interest and charges); and (ii) ensuring the number and amounts of any future repayments (including any interest and charges) are the same as would have applied if the complainant had taken the loan without the payment protection contract; or (b) where the firm is not able to arrange for the loan to be restructured (e.g. because the loan is provided by a separate firm), it should pay the complainant an amount equal to the difference between the actual loan balance and what the loan balance would have been if the payment protection contract (including any interest and charges) had not been added, deducting the current cancellation value. The firm should offer to pay any charges incurred if the complainant uses this amount to reduce his loan balance; and (2) for cancelled policies, the firm should pay the complainant the difference between the actual loan balance at the point of cancellation and what the loan balance would have been if no premium had been added (plus simple interest) minus any applicable cancellation value. ## DISP App 3.7.5 (Evidential Provisions) Where a claim was previously paid on the policy, the firm may deduct this from redress paid in accordance with DISP App 3.7.3 E.

retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP App 3.1 Introduction ## DISP App 3.1.1 (Guidance) (1) This appendix sets out how: (a) a firm should handle complaints relating to the sale of a payment protection contract by the firm which express dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling); and (b) a firm that is a CCA lender and which has received such a complaint should consider whether there was a failure to disclose commission in relation to the sale of a payment protection contract which covers or covered or purported to cover a credit agreement (this includes partial coverage). (2) It relates to the sale of any payment protection contract whenever the sale took place and irrespective of whether it was on an advised or non-advised basis; conducted through any sales channel; in connection with any type of loan or credit product, or none; whether the insurer was in the same group as the firm or not; whether the premium was financed by the credit product or not; and for a regular premium or single premium payment. It applies whether the policy is currently in force, was cancelled during the policy term or ran its full term. (3) It does not set out how a firm which has received a complaint referred to in (1)(a) should assess: (a) whether the firm’s conduct of the sale was in breach of a fiduciary duty where there has been a failure to disclose either the existence of, or the level of, any commission and/or profit share paid; (b) whether any omission (other than the omission referred to in DISP App 3.3A.2E) to disclose either the existence of, or level of, commission and/or profit share made the relationship unfair under section 140A of the CCA; (c) any other issue not dealt with in step 1 or step 2 set out in this appendix.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us... we are sorry to hear that you are dissatisfied with the response you received to your complaint about the payment protection insurance sold alongside your personal loan in 2021. ... we recognise that you raised specific concerns — in particular, that you already held employer sickness cover at the time of the sale
verdicts.reasoning: The opening sentences acknowledge the complaint and accurately restate the specific issue (PPI sold despite existing employer cover).
The draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) and provides no reasons for a decision; it only says the complaint is being reopened.

verdicts.evidence: You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, and their website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing text clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS and provides contact details.
No mention is made of the six‑month period within which the complainant may refer the matter to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We take these concerns seriously. We are reopening your complaint and will carry out a proper assessment of the circumstances of the sale... We are sorry for the frustration this has caused and we will be in touch as soon as our review is complete.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is respectful, acknowledges the specific concerns, and promises a proper reassessment, demonstrating fair and non‑dismissive treatment.
The draft does not address whether any redress will be offered or explain why none might be due; it only states the complaint will be reopened.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry to hear that you are dissatisfied with the response you received to your complaint about the payment protection insurance sold alongside your personal loan in 2021. Having reviewed your letter, we recognise that you raised specific concerns — in particular, that you already held employer sickness cover at the time of the sale and that this was not taken into account.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with a clear acknowledgement of the complaint and accurately reflects its subject matter: PPI mis-selling, the specific concern about existing employer sickness cover not being considered, and dissatisfaction with the previous response's lack of reasoning.

The handler answer explicitly flags that this letter is 'an interim acknowledgement of the reopening, not as a new final response.' The customer draft does not state a decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) and does not give reasons for a decision. It commits to a future review but does not resolve the complaint. Since the response stage is marked 'final', a final response must contain a decision and reasons; this draft does not.

verdicts.evidence: You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, and their website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the right to refer to the FOS and provides multiple means to do so: reference to the enclosed leaflet and the FOS website address (www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk).

The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline running from the date of the final response. The handler answer notes that 'the correct waiver wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R will be inserted where indicated' but the draft contains only a placeholder '[INSERT TIME LIMIT WAIVER WORDING FROM DISP 1 ANNEX 3R AS APPLICABLE]' with no actual six-month deadline stated to the customer.

verdicts.evidence: We take these concerns seriously. We are reopening your complaint and will carry out a proper assessment of the circumstances of the sale, including the specific points you have raised about your existing cover and whether the policy was appropriate for your needs at the time. We are sorry for the frustration this has caused.
verdicts.reasoning: The response addresses the substance of the complaint (the specific concern about existing employer cover and the inadequacy of the previous response), uses clear and even-handed language, acknowledges the customer's frustration, and commits to proper reconsideration. It does not dismiss or minimise the concerns raised.

The customer draft does not address redress at all. It commits to a future review but does not communicate any position on whether redress or remedial action is appropriate. The handler answer discusses redress considerations and notes that 'if, on proper reconsideration, the complaint is upheld' redress should be calculated, but this is internal reasoning only and does not appear in the customer-facing text. Since the complaint plainly raises a financial loss (mis-sold PPI), redress must be addressed in the final response to the customer.

claims.claim: A rejection must give reasons for doing so.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (disp1s6) states that a final response which rejects the complaint must give reasons for doing so.

claims.claim: The firm must handle PPI mis‑selling complaints by assessing the specific circumstances of the sale.
claims.reasoning: DISP App 3.1.1G sets out the scope of handling PPI complaints, and DISP App 3.3.11/3.3.12 (covered in provision 5) require consideration of individual circumstances, but the provision does not explicitly phrase a mandatory ‘assessment of specific circumstances’ as stated.

claims.claim: DISP App 3.3.11G and 3.3.12G require the firm to consider whether information was communicated fairly, clearly and not misleading, having regard to the complainant's individual circumstances, including evidence of the complainant's objectives and intentions at the time of the sale.
claims.reasoning: Provision 5 (dispapp3s5) contains the guidance for DISP App 3.3.11 and 3.3.12 that the firm must consider fairness, clarity, and the complainant's individual circumstances and objectives.

claims.claim: The sale took place in 2021, which is after the 29 August 2017 cut‑off in DISP 2.8.9R, so the PPI deadline rule does not bar this complaint.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.9R states that complaints relating to PPI sales on or before 29 August 2017 are excluded; a sale in 2021 falls outside that exclusion.

claims.claim: The complaint was also referred in March this year, well within any applicable time limits.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision confirms that a March referral (2026) is within the applicable time limits; DISP 2.8.9R sets a referral deadline of 29 August 2019, which would be exceeded.

claims.claim: If, on proper reconsideration, the complaint is upheld, DISP App 3.7 evidential provisions set out how redress should be calculated, including deducting any rebate received and, for single‑premium policies added to a loan, restructuring or compensating for the loan balance impact.
claims.reasoning: Provision 6 (dispapp3s7) details DISP App 3.7.4 and 3.7.5 which cover deduction of rebates and restructuring of loan balances for single‑premium policies.

claims.claim: If we are not in a position to provide a full response within eight weeks of your original complaint, we will write to explain why and let you know when we expect to be able to do so.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (disp1s6) requires a written response to explain why a final response cannot be provided within the eight‑week period and to indicate when it will be provided.

claims.claim: You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (disp1s6) states that the complainant must be informed that they may refer the complaint to the FOS free of charge.

claims.claim: We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service explanatory leaflet and provided their website address.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (disp1s6) requires the final response to enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet and to provide the FOS website address.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I called my bank on Monday to complain about an incorrect £15 fee on my current account. The adviser I spoke to agreed it was an error, reversed the charge immediately, and I told her I was happy with that resolution. However, it's now been over two weeks and I haven't received anything in writing from the bank confirming that my complaint was resolved or telling me about my rights to go to the Ombudsman if I change my mind. I feel the bank hasn't followed proper procedure — I'd like confirmation of what should have been sent to me and why it wasn't.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
Note: The message appears to have been submitted from the customer's perspective rather than a handler's perspective. I am treating this as a handler presenting a customer's complaint for regulatory guidance and drafting purposes.

Regulatory position: The customer complained verbally, the bank agreed the fee was an error, reversed it immediately, and the customer indicated satisfaction at the time. This looks like a 'summary resolution communication' scenario under DISP 1.6.4R (referenced in the retrieved provisions at DISP 1.10B.13D and DISP 2.8.2R). However, the exact text of DISP 1.6.4R is not fully reproduced in the retrieved chunks — only referenced — so I cannot confirm its precise requirements from the retrieved context alone. The retrieved provisions do confirm that a 'final response' under DISP 1.6.2R must: (a) accept, partially accept, or reject the complaint with reasons; (b) enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (c) provide the FOS website address; (d) inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (e) include wording on time limit waiver per DISP 1 Annex 3R. The Crest Bank internal policy (section 7.5) also requires the final response to state that the customer has six months from the date of the final response to refer to the FOS, and to include FOS contact details. Whether a summary resolution communication (rather than a full final response) was the appropriate vehicle here depends on facts I cannot confirm — specifically whether your internal procedures permit summary resolution for this type of complaint and whether the correct form was used. Regardless, the customer has not received any written confirmation, which is a procedural gap. Recommended action: Issue a written response now — either a summary resolution communication or a full final response — that meets the DISP 1.6.2R requirements, including FOS referral rights and the FOS leaflet. Because this involves a financial remedy (the £15 reversal) and a potential procedural failing, human review is required before sending. Assumption to confirm: Was the £15 reversal processed correctly and is it reflected on the customer's account? The draft below assumes it has been applied. Please verify before sending.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch. I am writing to confirm the outcome of the complaint you raised with us on [date of call] regarding an incorrect £15 fee applied to your current account.

Having reviewed your account, we agreed that the fee was applied in error. We have reversed the charge in full, and your account has been restored accordingly. We uphold your complaint and apologise for the inconvenience caused.

Although you kindly indicated at the time of your call that you were satisfied with this resolution, we recognise that we should have followed up in writing sooner, and we are sorry for the delay in doing so.

If, having received this letter, you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You have six months from the date of this letter to do so. I have enclosed the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, which sets out how to contact them and what they can do for you. You can also reach them at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by calling 0800 023 4567.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Sets out the requirements a final response must meet, including accepting or rejecting the complaint with reasons, enclosing the FOS explanatory leaflet, providing the FOS website address, and informing the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied.
Establishes that the complainant has six months from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, which must be communicated in the response.

Defines a closed complaint as one where the respondent has sent a final response or the complainant has accepted an earlier response in writing, relevant to determining whether this complaint has been properly closed.

(2) the statement will require the complainant to notify the Ombudsman , before the date specified in the statement, whether he accepts or rejects the determination; (3) if the complainant notifies the Ombudsman that he accepts the determination within that time limit, it is final and binding on both parties; (4) subject to paragraph (4A), if the complainant does not notify the Ombudsman that he accepts the determination within that time limit, the complainant will be treated as having rejected the determination, and neither party will be bound by it; (4A) the complainant is not to be treated as having rejected the determination under paragraph (4) if all the following conditions are met: (a) the complainant notifies the Ombudsman after the specified date of the complainant's acceptance of the determination; (b) the complainant has not previously notified the Ombudsman of the complainant's rejection of the determination; (c) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit for acceptance was as a result of exceptional circumstances; (5) the Ombudsman will notify the respondent of the outcome and, if the complainant is treated as having rejected the determination under paragraph (4), the effect of paragraph (4A). ## DISP 3.6.7 (Rules) (1) An Ombudsman may correct any clerical mistake in the written statement of an Ombudsman's determination, whether or not the determination has already been accepted or rejected. retrieved_chunks.content: The final response must: 1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received. 2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons. 3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated. 4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5). 5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer. All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. Crest Bank will cooperate fully with the FOS in respect of any referred complaints, providing all requested documentation within the timeframes set by the FOS. ### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows: 1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure. 2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes. 3.

or (2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or (2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint: (a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or (b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or (3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme: (a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or (b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme; unless: (4) the respondent consents and: (a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and (b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint. ## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules) Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint. ## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules) The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service: (1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; fixed-titan.scenario_text: I made a formal complaint to my bank on 14 March about an incorrect fee applied to my current account. I received a brief email the same day saying 'thanks, we've got your complaint' but it didn't tell me anything about when I could expect a response or what the process would be. It's now been three weeks and I've heard absolutely nothing since that initial email — no updates, no indication of progress, nothing. I feel like my complaint has just been forgotten about. I want to know what's happening and when I'll get a proper answer.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have been submitted by a customer rather than a complaints handler, but I will treat it as a complaint scenario for you to action.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Acknowledgement obligation:** Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in Crest Bank's internal procedure at section 7.2), a written acknowledgement must be sent within five business days of receipt, confirming the complaint reference number, the handler's name and contact details, and the expected resolution timescale. The initial 'thanks, we've got your complaint' email sent on 14 March does not appear to meet this standard — it did not confirm the expected resolution timescale or handler contact details. You should check whether a compliant acknowledgement was ever sent.

2. **Eight-week deadline:** Under DISP 2.8.1R, the Financial Ombudsman Service can consider a complaint once eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received it (for a standard complaint that is not an EMD or PSD complaint). The complaint was received on 14 March; eight weeks will expire on approximately 9 May. You are not yet at that threshold, but it is approaching.

3. **Final response or holding response:** Under DISP 1.6.2R (chunk: disp1s6), if the bank is not in a position to issue a final response within eight weeks, it must send a written response explaining why it cannot yet respond and indicating when it expects to be able to do so. That holding response must also inform the complainant that they may now refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, and indicate whether the bank consents to waive the relevant DISP 2.8.2R time limits.

**Recommended action:**
- Immediately check the CMS to confirm the complaint was logged on 14 March and that a compliant acknowledgement (with reference number, handler details, and expected timescale) was sent. If not, this is a procedural failing you should escalate.
- Given three weeks have passed with no substantive update, you should either: (a) issue a substantive update/progress letter now, or (b) if a final response cannot be issued before the eight-week deadline, prepare a holding response that meets the DISP 1.6.2R requirements.
- The customer draft below is a holding/progress response. **Assumption to confirm:** this draft assumes the complaint has been properly logged and that a final response cannot yet be issued. If a final response is ready, a different letter (accepting, rejecting, or offering redress) is required instead, and that would need human review given potential redress implications.
- Flag for human review: the complaint concerns an incorrect fee, which may involve financial redress. A senior reviewer should confirm the investigation findings before any final response is issued.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for your patience, and I am sorry that you have not received a proper update since your complaint was acknowledged on 14 March.

I want to confirm that your complaint about the incorrect fee applied to your current account has been received and is being investigated. Your complaint reference number is [REFERENCE NUMBER].

We are working to resolve your complaint as quickly as possible. If you have any questions in the meantime, please contact [Handler Name] at [contact details].

We aim to send you our full response as soon as our investigation is complete. If we are not able to provide you with a final response within eight weeks of the date we received your complaint, we will write to you again to explain why and to let you know when we expect to be able to do so.

If at any point you are not satisfied with the progress of your complaint, you have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. I have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, which explains how to do this and sets out the relevant time limits. You can also find information about the Financial Ombudsman Service at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.

Once again, I apologise for the lack of communication so far. We take your complaint seriously and will be in touch with our findings as soon as possible.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the bank to send a written acknowledgement within five business days confirming the complaint reference, handler details, and expected timescale, and sets out the requirements for a holding response where a final response cannot yet be issued, including informing the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS.

Establishes that the FOS can consider a complaint once eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received it, setting the outer time limit by which a final response must be issued or a holding response sent.

Sets the time limits within which a complainant must refer a complaint to the FOS after receiving a final response, which the holding response must address by indicating whether the bank consents to waive those limits.

retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time? ## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules) The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if: (1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or (2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or (2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint: (a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or (b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or (3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme: (a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or (b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme; unless: (4) the respondent consents and: (a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and (b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint. ## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules) Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint. ## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules) The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service: (1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; retrieved_chunks.content: --- ## 7. Procedures ### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination. All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted. ### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must: 1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected. 2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer. 3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale. ### 7.3 Investigation Process The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include: 1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems. 2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made. 3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure, and referring to the DPO, MLRO, or CTO respectively where applicable. 4. Documenting all investigative steps and evidence reviewed in the CMS. Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target. ### 7.4 Final Response Letters Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must: 1.

put the complainant back into the position the complainant should have been in had the act or omission not occurred. (4) If a respondent reports on the amount of redress paid under DISP 1.10B.7D(2)(b) the redress should not include the amount of a non-executed, defective or unauthorised payment transaction but should include any redress paid as a result of losses incurred by the complainant as a result of the non-executed, defective or unauthorised payment transaction. ## DISP 1.10B.9 (Directions) The relevant reporting period is the year immediately following: (1) where the respondent has an accounting reference date, its accounting reference date; and (2) where the respondent does not have an accounting reference date, 31 December each year. ## DISP 1.10B.10 (Directions) Reports are to be submitted to the FCA within 30 business days of the end of the relevant reporting periods through, and in the electronic format specified in, the FCA complaints reporting system or the appropriate section of the FCA website. ## DISP 1.10B.11 (Directions) If a respondent is unable to submit a report in electronic format because of a systems failure of any kind, the respondent must notify the FCA, in writing and without delay, of that systems failure. ## DISP 1.10B.12 (Rules) (1) If a respondent does not submit a complete report by the date on which it is due, in accordance with DISP 1.10B.10D, the respondent must pay the administrative fee specified in SUP 16.3.14R.(2) The administrative fee in (1) does not apply if the respondent has notified the FCA of a systems failure in accordance with DISP 1.10B.11R. ## DISP 1.10B.13 (Directions) A closed complaint is a complaint where: (1) the respondent has sent a final response; or (2) the complainant has indicated in writing acceptance of the respondent’s earlier response under DISP 1.6.4R (where applicable). ## DISP 1.10B.14 (Guidance) (1) To improve consumer awareness and to help respondents compare their performance against their peers, the FCA may publish: (a) complaints data about the payment services and electronic money sector as a whole; and (b) respondent level complaints data and information giving context to the complaints

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I made a complaint to my bank, Westfield Banking PLC, six weeks ago about a payment protection insurance policy that was sold to me through their branch but was actually underwritten by a separate insurer. Three weeks after I complained, the bank wrote to me saying they were forwarding my complaint to the insurer because the insurer was solely responsible. However, the bank didn't tell me the insurer's contact details in that letter — just said 'the relevant insurer' without a name, address, or phone number. I also feel that three weeks was not 'prompt' — I was left waiting with no update for all that time. I've now heard nothing from the insurer either, and I don't even know who to chase. I want to know why the bank took so long to forward my complaint and why they failed to give me the insurer's details so I could follow up myself.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
Important note: this message appears to have been submitted by the customer directly, not by a complaints handler. Please confirm whether this is a handler query or a misdirected customer message before proceeding. I have treated it as a handler seeking advice on how to respond to a customer complaint, and drafted accordingly — but please verify.

Regulatory position (based solely on retrieved provisions):

1. The retrieved provisions do not contain the specific DISP rules governing a respondent's obligations when forwarding or redirecting a complaint to another firm (e.g. DISP 1.7 on referral to another respondent, or rules requiring disclosure of the third party's contact details). The chunks retrieved relate primarily to: final response content requirements (DISP 1.6.2R), PPI time limits (DISP 2.8.8G, DISP 2.8.9R), PPI mis-selling evidential provisions (DISP App 3.6.3E), and PPI commission disclosure obligations. None of these directly address the obligation to provide the insurer's identity and contact details when redirecting a complaint, or what constitutes a 'prompt' referral.

2. What the retrieved provisions do support: Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in the Crest Bank policy chunk, which references it), a final response must inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS and enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet. The bank has not yet issued a final response — six weeks have elapsed and the eight-week deadline (per the internal policy chunk referencing DISP 1.6.2R) has not yet passed, but it is approaching. If no final response is issued by week eight, the bank must send a written response explaining why it cannot yet respond and informing the customer of their FOS referral right (DISP 1.6.2R).

Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us about your payment protection insurance complaint and for raising your concerns about how we have handled it so far.

We are sorry that you have not received a clearer update. We understand how frustrating it must be to have been waiting for three weeks without hearing from us, and then to receive a letter that did not give you the information you needed to follow up your complaint directly.

We are now looking into the concerns you have raised, including why it took us three weeks to write to you and why our letter did not include the name and contact details of the insurer involved. We will write to you again as soon as we are in a position to give you a full response.

We want to make sure you are not left without a point of contact in the meantime. [HANDLER TO INSERT: name, address, and telephone number of the relevant insurer once confirmed internally.]

Please be aware that we are required to provide you with a final response to your complaint within eight weeks of the date you first raised it with us. If we are not able to do so, we will write to you to explain why and to confirm your options at that stage.

If at any point you are dissatisfied with how your complaint is being handled, you have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet for your reference.

Thank you again for your patience. We will be in touch as soon as possible.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
Westfield Banking PLC

retrieved_chunks.content: The final response must: 1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received. 2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons. 3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated. 4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5). 5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer. All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. Crest Bank will cooperate fully with the FOS in respect of any referred complaints, providing all requested documentation within the timeframes set by the FOS. ### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows: 1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure. 2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes. 3.

The engagement is empathetic but superficial; the response defers all substantive analysis to a future communication. This is partly fair but incomplete.

The customer-facing draft is silent on this.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I took out a payment protection insurance policy with your bank in 2002 and cancelled it in 2005. I recently heard from a friend that I might have been mis-sold PPI and decided to complain. Your bank has sent me a letter saying my complaint is 'time-barred' and that you won't look into whether I was mis-sold the policy. However, the letter simply states my complaint is rejected due to time limits — it doesn't explain my right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service or give me the six-month deadline for doing so. I feel this is unfair as I believe I should at least have been told about my options, even if you consider the complaint too old.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
The customer's complaint raises a legitimate procedural concern. Under DISP 1.8.1R, a respondent may reject a time-barred complaint without considering its merits, but must do so by issuing a final response that complies with DISP 1.6.2R. DISP 1.6.2R requires that a final response (even one rejecting a complaint) must: enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; provide the FOS website address; inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and include the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R indicating whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R. Additionally, given this is a PPI complaint, DISP 2.8.9R and the note to DISP 1.6.2R(1)(f) require that the respondent also provide equivalent messaging about the 29 August 2019 PPI-specific time limit under DISP 2.8.9R.

On the facts as described, the bank's rejection letter appears to have failed to include the FOS referral rights, the explanatory leaflet, the FOS website address, and the required time-limit wording — all of which are mandatory under DISP 1.6.2R even where a complaint is rejected as time-barred. This is a procedural compliance failure.

Recommended action: The bank should issue a remedying letter to the customer that corrects these omissions. The letter should explain the time-bar position, provide the FOS referral right and the six-month referral window from the date of the final response, enclose the FOS leaflet, provide the FOS website address, and include the appropriate DISP 1 Annex 3R wording on time limits.

Assumptions to confirm before sending:
1. You will need to determine which DISP 1 Annex 3R wording applies — specifically, whether the bank consents or does not consent to waive the DISP 2.8.2R time limits. The draft below uses the scenario where the bank does not consent to waive either time limit, but you must confirm this is the bank's position.
2. You should confirm whether the original rejection letter was formally a 'final response' for DISP purposes, as this affects whether the six-month FOS referral clock has already started running. If it has, there is a risk the customer's referral window may already be running or may have been affected — flag for senior review.
3. Given the PPI-specific deadline of 29 August 2019 under DISP 2.8.9R, the Ombudsman is very unlikely to be able to consider the substance of this complaint (the sale took place in 2002 and the complaint was not referred by 29 August 2019). The customer should be informed of this honestly, but the right to refer must still be communicated.

Human review is required before sending — see flags above.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch and for raising your concerns about the letter you received from us regarding your payment protection insurance (PPI) complaint.

We are sorry that our previous letter did not set out your options clearly. Even where we are unable to consider a complaint on its merits because it falls outside the applicable time limits, we are required to explain your rights to you, and we recognise that our earlier letter fell short of that standard.

Our position on your complaint

Your PPI policy was sold in 2002 and cancelled in 2005. Your complaint about the sale of that policy was received by us after 29 August 2019. Under the rules that apply to PPI complaints, the Financial Ombudsman Service is generally unable to consider complaints about PPI sales where the complaint was not referred to the respondent or to the Ombudsman on or before 29 August 2019. For this reason, we are not in a position to consider the merits of your complaint, and we maintain our position that it falls outside the relevant time limits.

Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service

Even though we are unable to uphold your complaint, you have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You must do so within six months of the date of this letter.

Please be aware that the Ombudsman may also be unable to consider your complaint because it relates to a PPI sale that took place before 29 August 2017 and was not referred by 29 August 2019. Whether the Ombudsman can consider your complaint in these circumstances is a matter for the Ombudsman to decide — for example, the Ombudsman may consider it if they believe exceptional circumstances apply. We do not consent to the Ombudsman considering your complaint outside the applicable time limits.

You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at: [FOS website address]. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, which sets out further information about the process and the time limits for referring a complaint.

If you have any questions about this letter, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Name]
[Job title]
[Bank name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Permits the respondent to reject a time-barred complaint without considering its merits, but requires it to do so by issuing a final response that complies with DISP 1.6.2R.

Requires a final response (including one rejecting a complaint as time-barred) to enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and include the appropriate DISP 1 Annex 3R wording on time limits.

Sets the PPI-specific time bar: the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint about a PPI sale on or before 29 August 2017 unless the complaint was referred on or before 29 August 2019 or another specified condition is met.
Prescribes the wording that must be included in a final response to indicate whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant FOS time limits.
