metadata.section_name: DISP 1 Annex 3 Appropriate wording for inclusion in a final response or written acceptance

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch and for raising your concerns about the letter you received from us regarding your payment protection insurance (PPI) complaint.
verdicts.reasoning: The opening sentence acknowledges the complainant’s contact and correctly identifies the subject as a PPI complaint.

verdicts.evidence: For this reason, we are not in a position to consider the merits of your complaint, and we maintain our position that it falls outside the relevant time limits.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft clearly rejects the complaint and provides reasons linked to the PPI‑specific time limit and the date of receipt.

verdicts.evidence: Even though we are unable to uphold your complaint, you have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge... You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at: [FOS website address]. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet...
verdicts.reasoning: The response informs the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS, gives contact details and mentions the enclosed leaflet.

verdicts.evidence: You must do so within six months of the date of this letter.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft specifies a six‑month deadline that runs from the date of the final response.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry that our previous letter did not set out your options clearly... We do not consent to the Ombudsman considering your complaint outside the applicable time limits.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is apologetic, explains the firm’s position, engages with the complainant’s concerns, and does not dismiss or minimise them, demonstrating fair treatment.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch and for raising your concerns about the letter you received from us regarding your payment protection insurance (PPI) complaint. We are sorry that our previous letter did not set out your options clearly.
verdicts.reasoning: The opening clearly acknowledges receipt of the complaint and accurately reflects its subject matter: the customer's concern that the bank's rejection letter failed to explain FOS referral rights. The response demonstrates understanding of what is being complained about (lack of clarity on options), not just the underlying PPI sale.

verdicts.evidence: Our position on your complaint: Your PPI policy was sold in 2002 and cancelled in 2005. Your complaint about the sale of that policy was received by us after 29 August 2019. Under the rules that apply to PPI complaints, the Financial Ombudsman Service is generally unable to consider complaints about PPI sales where the complaint was not referred to the respondent or to the Ombudsman on or before 29 August 2019. For this reason, we are not in a position to consider the merits of your complaint, and we maintain our position that it falls outside the relevant time limits.
verdicts.reasoning: The decision is clearly stated (rejection on time-bar grounds) and the reasons engage specifically with the complainant's circumstances: the 2002 sale date, the 2005 cancellation, the post-29 August 2019 complaint receipt, and the PPI-specific deadline. The response explains why the time limit applies to this specific complaint, not merely a generic conclusion.

verdicts.evidence: Even though we are unable to uphold your complaint, you have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at: [FOS website address]. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, which sets out further information about the process and the time limits for referring a complaint.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer-facing text explicitly states the right to refer this complaint to the FOS and provides multiple means to do so: the FOS website address, the enclosed explanatory leaflet, and clear signposting. The language makes clear the complainant can refer 'this complaint' to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: You must do so within six months of the date of this letter.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the six-month deadline and anchors it to 'the date of this letter' (the final response). This satisfies the requirement to inform the complainant of the six-month period running from the firm's final response.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry that our previous letter did not set out your options clearly. Even where we are unable to consider a complaint on its merits because it falls outside the applicable time limits, we are required to explain your rights to you, and we recognise that our earlier letter fell short of that standard. Please be aware that the Ombudsman may also be unable to consider your complaint because it relates to a PPI sale that took place before 29 August 2017 and was not referred by 29 August 2019. Whether the Ombudsman can consider your complaint in these circumstances is a matter for the Ombudsman to decide — for example, the Ombudsman may consider it if they believe exceptional circumstances apply.
verdicts.reasoning: The response treats the complaint fairly by: (1) acknowledging the procedural failing in the previous letter and apologising; (2) explaining the regulatory requirement to communicate rights even when rejecting on time-bar grounds; (3) being transparent about the FOS's likely inability to consider the complaint while preserving the complainant's right to refer; (4) noting that exceptional circumstances may apply, leaving the door open rather than being dismissive. The language is clear, non-dismissive, and engages substantively with the complainant's concern about being denied information about their options.

claims.claim: Under DISP 1.8.1R, a respondent may reject a time-barred complaint without considering its merits, but must do so by issuing a final response that complies with DISP 1.6.2R.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (DISP 1.8.1R) explicitly states that a respondent may reject a complaint outside the time limits but must explain this in a final response in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R.

claims.claim: DISP 1.6.2R requires that a final response (even one rejecting a complaint) must: enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; provide the FOS website address; inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and include the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R indicating whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (DISP 1.6) lists exactly these mandatory elements (leaflet, website, right to refer, consent wording) for a final response.

claims.claim: Given this is a PPI complaint, DISP 2.8.9R and the note to DISP 1.6.2R(1)(f) require that the respondent also provide equivalent messaging about the 29 August 2019 PPI-specific time limit under DISP 2.8.9R.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 includes a note reminding respondents of the obligation to provide equivalent messaging for the time limit in DISP 2.8.9R (PPI complaints).

claims.claim: The bank's rejection letter appears to have failed to include the FOS referral rights, the explanatory leaflet, the FOS website address, and the required time-limit wording — all of which are mandatory under DISP 1.6.2R even where a complaint is rejected as time-barred.
claims.reasoning: The requirement that those items are mandatory is supported by DISP 1.6.2R, but the factual assertion that the bank's letter omitted them is not verified by any provision.

claims.claim: The letter should explain the time-bar position, provide the FOS referral right and the six-month referral window from the date of the final response, enclose the FOS leaflet, provide the FOS website address, and include the appropriate DISP 1 Annex 3R wording on time limits.
claims.reasoning: All elements listed are required by DISP 1.6.2R for a final response.

claims.claim: The PPI-specific deadline of 29 August 2019 under DISP 2.8.9R means the Ombudsman is very unlikely to be able to consider the substance of this complaint.
claims.reasoning: Provision 2 (DISP 2.8.9) sets the 29 August 2019 deadline and states the Ombudsman cannot consider a PPI complaint unless certain conditions are met, making the statement accurate.

claims.claim: Even where we are unable to consider a complaint on its merits because it falls outside the applicable time limits, we are required to explain your rights to you.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.8.1R requires a final response to explain the reason for rejection when a complaint is time‑barred.

claims.claim: Your PPI policy was sold in 2002 and cancelled in 2005. Your complaint about the sale of that policy was received by us after 29 August 2019.
claims.reasoning: No provision provides or verifies these specific dates; they are factual statements not supported by the retrieved rules.

claims.claim: Under the rules that apply to PPI complaints, the Financial Ombudsman Service is generally unable to consider complaints about PPI sales where the complaint was not referred to the respondent or to the Ombudsman on or before 29 August 2019.
claims.reasoning: Provision 2 (DISP 2.8.9) sets that the Ombudsman cannot consider a PPI complaint unless it was referred by 29 August 2019.

claims.claim: We are not in a position to consider the merits of your complaint, and we maintain our position that it falls outside the relevant time limits.
claims.reasoning: The rule permits a firm to reject a complaint outside the time limits, but the factual assertion that this particular complaint falls outside those limits is not established by the provisions.

claims.claim: You have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You must do so within six months of the date of this letter.
claims.reasoning: Provision 5 (DISP 1.18) sets out the six‑month referral window and the right to refer free of charge.

claims.claim: The Ombudsman may also be unable to consider your complaint because it relates to a PPI sale that took place before 29 August 2017 and was not referred by 29 August 2019.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.9 explicitly states that complaints about PPI sales on or before 29 August 2017 are excluded unless referred by 29 August 2019.

claims.claim: Whether the Ombudsman can consider your complaint in these circumstances is a matter for the Ombudsman to decide — for example, the Ombudsman may consider it if they believe exceptional circumstances apply.
claims.reasoning: Provision 2 lists exceptional circumstances as a condition under which the Ombudsman may still consider a complaint.

claims.claim: We do not consent to the Ombudsman considering your complaint outside the applicable time limits.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R requires the respondent to indicate whether it consents to waive the relevant time limits; stating non‑consent is permissible.

claims.claim: We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, which sets out further information about the process and the time limits for referring a complaint.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R mandates that a final response must enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet, so the requirement is grounded; the factual claim that the leaflet has actually been enclosed is not verified by the provisions.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I opened a current account with your bank online in January 2024. During the application process, I noticed that a 'Card Protection Plan' at £7.99/month was already pre-selected as part of the account setup — there was a tick already in the box. I didn't untick it because I assumed it was a required part of the account, and the page layout made it look like it was bundled in. I've now been charged £7.99 per month for the past 14 months — that's over £110 total — for something I never actively chose. When I look back at the confirmation email and all the paperwork I received when I opened the account, I also notice that at no point were you clear about how to make a complaint or that I could go to the Financial Ombudsman Service if I was unhappy. I only found out about the Ombudsman from a friend. I want a full refund of all charges for this Card Protection Plan and I want to know why I was never told about my rights to complain.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct issues: (1) a pre-ticked opt-in for a Card Protection Plan (a payment protection product) during an online account opening, and (2) failure to disclose complaint-handling rights and FOS referral information at the point of sale.

**Issue 1 – Pre-ticked box / active consent for the Card Protection Plan**
CONC 2.5.8R (referenced in the retrieved context at CONC 2.5.10) addresses credit broking conduct including payment protection products. CONC 2.5.9G(6) indicates that where an introduction to a payment protection product is made, the firm should ensure the customer's consent is preceded by a full explanation of key features and key risks. The customer's account of a pre-ticked box strongly suggests that genuine, informed consent was not obtained — the customer reasonably believed the product was mandatory. This is a significant concern under the FCA's conduct standards. However, I must flag that the retrieved provisions do not contain an explicit rule directly prohibiting pre-ticked boxes for payment protection products (e.g. a specific CONC or ICOBS rule to that effect). The closest applicable provisions are CONC 2.5.9G(6) (guidance on consent and explanation) and ICOBS 5.1.2R (rule requiring reasonable steps to ensure the customer only buys a policy under which they are eligible to claim benefits, and informing them if parts of the cover do not apply). The complaint also engages ICOBS 5.1.3B R, which requires a record of the eligibility assessment to be retained for three years — you should check whether such a record exists for this customer.

Given the customer's account of the pre-ticked box and the apparent lack of active consent, there is a strong basis to investigate whether the sale was compliant. The customer is seeking a full refund of £111.86 (14 × £7.99). Any decision on redress must be made by a human reviewer — do not commit to a refund in the customer letter at this stage.

**Issue 2 – Failure to disclose complaint-handling and FOS referral rights**
ICOBS 6.4.4A R is directly on point: in relation to a payment protection contract, a firm must provide a consumer with information about (1) how the consumer can complain to the insurance undertaking and that complaints may subsequently be referred to the FOS, and (2) the consumer's entitlement to compensation from the compensation scheme — in good time before conclusion of the policy. The customer states this information was absent from the confirmation email and all paperwork. If accurate, this is a breach of a binding rule. You should check the onboarding documentation to confirm whether ICOBS 6.4.4A R disclosures were made.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending:**
- Is the Card Protection Plan a 'payment protection contract' within the meaning of ICOBS? (The name suggests card loss/fraud protection rather than income protection — if it is not a payment protection contract, ICOBS 6.4.4A R and ICOBS 5.1.2R may not apply directly. This is a material uncertainty and the primary reason for human review.)
- Was the product arranged as part of a packaged bank account? If so, ICOBS 5.1.3A R and 5.1.3B R (packaged bank account eligibility rules) would apply instead of ICOBS 5.1.2R.
- Does the eligibility assessment record required by ICOBS 5.1.3B R exist?
- What did the onboarding screens and confirmation email actually say? Screenshots or records of the online journey should be retrieved.
- The complaint involves a potential refund of over £110 — redress decisions require human sign-off.

The customer draft below acknowledges the complaint, confirms it is under investigation, and advises of FOS rights — but does not commit to any refund or admit liability. Human review is required before sending.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about your experience when opening your account and about the concerns you have raised regarding the Card Protection Plan charges and the information you received at the time.

We take complaints of this kind seriously and I want to assure you that we are looking into both of the issues you have raised.

**The Card Protection Plan charges**

You have told us that the Card Protection Plan appeared as a pre-selected option during your online account application in January 2024, and that the page layout led you to believe it was a required part of the account rather than an optional product. You have also told us that you did not actively choose to take it out. We are reviewing the records of your account opening, including the online journey you went through, to understand what happened. We will write to you again once we have completed that review.

**Information about your right to complain**

You have also raised a concern that you were not told, at the time you took out the plan, how to make a complaint or that you could refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. We are required to provide that information to customers in good time before a payment protection policy is concluded, and we are checking our records to establish whether that was done in your case. We take this concern seriously.

**What happens next**

We aim to provide you with a full response as quickly as possible. In the meantime, if you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details below.

If you are not satisfied with our final response, or if we have not resolved your complaint within eight weeks of receiving it, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at:

Financial Ombudsman Service
Exchange Tower
London E14 9SR
Telephone: 0800 023 4567
Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a firm to provide a consumer with information about how to complain and the right to refer to the FOS, in good time before conclusion of a payment protection contract — directly engaged by the customer's allegation that no such information was provided.

Requires a firm arranging a payment protection contract to take reasonable steps to ensure the customer only buys a policy under which they are eligible to claim benefits, and to inform the customer if parts of the cover do not apply — relevant to whether the sale process was compliant.

Requires a firm to make and retain a record of the eligibility assessment for at least three years — the handler should check whether such a record exists for this customer's January 2024 account opening.

Indicates that where a firm makes an introduction to a payment protection product, it should ensure the customer's consent is preceded by a full explanation of the key features and key risks — relevant to the pre-ticked box allegation and whether genuine informed consent was obtained.

CONC 4.4.2 R requires a credit broker to disclose any such fee agreed with the customer in writing or in another durable medium.[Note: paragraphs 2.2, 7th bullet, 3.7l and 4.9 of CBG] (6) Where a firm makes an introduction of the type referred to in CONC 2.5.8R (19) the firm should ensure that the customer's consent is preceded by a full explanation of the key features and key risks of the product to which the introduction applies. [Note: paragraph 4.17f of CBG] (7) A customer's personal data must be processed fairly and lawfully and only for specified purposes. While it may be possible to pass special categories of personal data in specified and limited circumstances to certain third parties without the customer's consent where a condition of data protection legislation applies , a firm (other than where it is under a statutory obligation to pass personal data to a third party) should generally seek the customer's consent before passing such personal data to a third party.[Note: paragraph 3.9t (box) of CBG] (8) An example of where it is likely to be unfair for a credit broker in receipt of a customer's personal data to pass it to a third party, is where the personal data is passed on in return for a fee to a claims management firm, without the customer's consent. (9) Firms should note the effect of the call charges rule in GEN 7. ## CONC 2.5.10 (Rules) In CONC 2.5.8R (14): (1) payment protection product means a product or feature of a product designed to offer customers short-term protection against potential loss of income, by providing the means for them to meet (or temporarily suspend) their financial obligations including repayments under a credit agreement.

For example, if the monthly repayment amount in relation to the loan only is to be £200, the price of the alternative regular premium payment protection contract will be £18. ## DISP App 3.7.14 (Evidential Provisions) Where the firm presumes that the complainant would have purchased a regular premium payment protection contract and if the complainant expressly wishes it, the existing cover should continue until the end of the existing policy term. The complainant should pay the price of the alternative regular premium payment protection contract (at DISP App 3.7.13 E) and should be able to cancel at any time.

retrieved_chunks.content: The type of account fee this guidance is intended to address is a periodic charge a customer is required to pay in order to obtain and maintain access to a personal current account that has an overdraft facility. (ii) CONC App 1.2.3R (Total charge for credit) provides that the costs of maintaining an account recording both payment transactions and drawdowns are included in the total cost of credit to the borrower. There is an exception to this rule (see CONC App 1.2.3R(3)) where: “(a) the opening of the account is optional and the costs of the account have been clearly and separately shown in the regulated credit agreement or in any other agreement with the borrower; (b) in the case of an overdraft facility the costs do not relate to that facility.” (iii) Whether an account fee is required to be included in the calculation of an APR depends on whether the credit under the associated authorised non-business overdraft agreement can be obtained on the same terms without incurring the account fee. If an authorised non-business overdraft agreement is not available on the same equally favourable terms without the imposition of the fee, that fee is likely to be considered to “relate” to the overdraft facility. (b) The following are examples of situations where it is likely that an account fee should be included in the calculation of the total charge for credit and the APR for an authorised non-business overdraft agreement. (i) A personal current account that is subject to an account fee, one of the features of which is an arranged overdraft facility with more favourable terms (for example, a lower interest rate) than those offered on accounts that do not require the payment of an account fee. (ii) A firm that offers personal current accounts with associated arranged overdraft facilities in respect of all of which there is an account fee. (c) A firm may offer a “packaged bank account” that is a composite product with a number of constituent elements, one of which is an overdraft facility, but others of which are different services.

(2) This chapter does not apply in relation to: (a) an account held by a banking customer in respect of whom the main correspondence address held by the firm is outside the United Kingdom; (b) an account held by a banking customer aged under 18 at the start of the relevant current account measurement period (see BCOBS 7.7.1R(1)); or (c) an account which may be used for a currency other than a currency of the United Kingdom. (3) This chapter does not apply to a credit union. (4) This chapter does not apply to a private bank. (5) But this rule is subject to BCOBS 7.1.4.R. ## BCOBS 7.1.3 (Guidance) (1) This chapter applies to a firm separately in relation to personal current accounts and business current accounts, for each of its trading names as notified to the FCA in accordance with SUP 16 Annex 16AR.

retrieved_chunks.content: Another type of significant limitation might be that the contract only operates through certain means of communication, e.g. telephone or internet. ## ICOBS 6.4.4 (Rules) A firm must provide a consumer with a policy summary in good time before the conclusion of a pure protection contract . ## ICOBS 6.4.4A (Rules) In relation to a payment protection contract, a firm must provide a consumer with information about: (1) how the consumer can complain to the insurance undertaking and that complaints may subsequently be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service (or other applicable named complaints scheme); and (2) the consumer’s entitlement to compensation from the compensation scheme (or other applicable compensation scheme), or that there is no compensation scheme, in the event where the insurance undertaking is unable to meet its liabilities; in good time before the conclusion of the policy. ## ICOBS 6.4.5 (Rules) (1) A firm must draw a consumer's attention to the importance of reading payment protection contract documentation before the end of the cancellation period to check that the policy is suitable for the consumer. (2) This must be done orally if a firm provides information orally on any main characteristic of a policy. ## ICOBS 6.4.6 (Rules) A firm must provide price information in a way calculated to enable the customer to relate it to a regular budget. ## ICOBS 6.4.7 (Guidance) Price information is likely also to include at least the total premium (or the basis for calculating it so that the customer can verify it) and, where relevant: (1) for policies of over one year with reviewable premiums, the period for which the quoted premium is valid, and the timing of reviews; (2) other fees, administrative charges and taxes payable by the customer through the firm; and (3) a statement identifying separately the possibility of any taxes not payable through the firm. ## ICOBS 6.4.8 (Guidance) Price information should be given in writing or another durable medium in good time before conclusion of the contract. This is in addition to any requirement or decision to provide the information orally.

FEES TP 17R R In these transitional provisions: From commencement 8 December 2017 (1) References to the Payment Services Regulations 2017 are to the Payment Services Regulations 2017 (SI 2017/752); and (2) references to the Electronic Money Regulations 2011 are to the Electronic Money Regulations 2011 (SI 2011/99) as amended by the Payment Services Regulations 2017. (i) where the applicant is applying to provide the payment services in paragraph(s) (f) (money remittance) and/or (g) (payment initiation services) and/or (h) (account information services) of Part 1 of Schedule 1 to the Payment Services Regulations 2017 the fee is £1,500. retrieved_chunks.content: # ICOBS — ICOBS 5.1 General ## ICOBS 5.1.1 (Guidance) (1) In line with Principle 6, a firm should take reasonable steps to ensure that a customer only buys a policy under which he is eligible to claim benefits. (2) If, at any time while arranging a policy, a firm finds that parts of the cover apply, but others do not, it should inform the customer so he can take an informed decision on whether to buy the policy. (3) This guidance does not apply to policies arranged as part of a packaged bank account. ## ICOBS 5.1.2 (Rules) (1) A firm arranging a payment protection contract must: (a) take reasonable steps to ensure that the customer only buys a policy under which he is eligible to claim benefits; and (b) if, at any time while arranging the policy, it finds that parts of the cover do not apply, inform the customer so he can take an informed decision on whether to buy the policy. (2) This rule does not apply to payment protection contract arranged as part of a packaged bank account. ## ICOBS 5.1.3 (Guidance) (1) For a typical payment protection contract the reasonable steps required in the first part of the eligibility rule are likely to include checking that the customer meets any qualifying requirements for different parts of the policy. (2) This guidance does not apply to payment protection contracts arranged as part of a packaged bank account. ## ICOBS 5.1.3A (Rules) A firm arranging policies as part of a packaged bank account must: (1) take reasonable steps to establish whether the customer is eligible to claim each of the benefits under each policy included in the packaged bank account which must include checking that the customer meets any qualifying requirements to claim each of the benefits under each policy; and (2) inform the customer whether or not he would be eligible to claim each of the benefits under each policy included in the packaged bank account so that the customer can take an informed decision about the arrangements proposed. ## ICOBS 5.1.3B (Rules) A firm must make a record of the eligibility assessment and, if the customer proceeds with the arrangements proposed, retain it for a minimum period of three years from the date on which the assessment was undertaken. ## ICOBS 5.1.3C (Rules) (1) Throughout the term of a policy included in a packaged bank account, a firm

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about your experience ... concerns you have raised regarding the Card Protection Plan charges
verdicts.reasoning: The opening paragraph acknowledges the complaint and accurately restates the issues raised by the customer.
The draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor provide reasons for a decision; it only says the matter is under review.

verdicts.evidence: you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at: Financial Ombudsman Service ...
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing text clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer the complaint to the FOS and provides contact details.
The response mentions an eight‑week resolution period but does not state the six‑month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We take complaints of this kind seriously and I want to assure you that we are looking into both of the issues you have raised.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft engages with the specific points raised, uses courteous language and does not dismiss or minimise the complainant's concerns.
The draft does not address whether any redress (e.g., refund) will be provided or explain why none would be offered.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about your experience when opening your account and about the concerns you have raised regarding the Card Protection Plan charges and the information you received at the time.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with a clear acknowledgement of receipt and accurately reflects both substantive issues: the pre-selected Card Protection Plan charges and the failure to disclose complaint-handling/FOS rights. The acknowledgement is specific to the complainant's circumstances, not generic.

verdicts.evidence: We are reviewing the records of your account opening... We will write to you again once we have completed that review.
verdicts.reasoning: This is a holding response, not a final response. The customer draft states the firm is 'looking into' and 'reviewing' both issues but does not state a decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). The response stage is marked 'final', but the draft contains no decision on either the refund claim or the complaint-handling disclosure breach. A final response must reach and communicate a decision with reasons; deferring to a future letter does not satisfy this element.

verdicts.evidence: If you are not satisfied with our final response, or if we have not resolved your complaint within eight weeks of receiving it, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at: Financial Ombudsman Service Exchange Tower London E14 9SR Telephone: 0800 023 4567 Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the right to refer the complaint to the FOS and provides full contact details (address, telephone, website). The signposting is clear and unambiguous.

The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline running from the date of the final response. It mentions the eight-week response clock ('within eight weeks of receiving it') but does not inform the complainant that they have six months from this final response to refer to the FOS. The six-month referral limit is a distinct and separate requirement from the firm's eight-week response timeline.

verdicts.evidence: We take complaints of this kind seriously and I want to assure you that we are looking into both of the issues you have raised... We take this concern seriously.
verdicts.reasoning: The response is courteous and acknowledges both issues substantively. However, it is evasive on the core question: whether the firm will provide redress. The complainant has suffered a quantifiable loss (£111.86) and raised a potential breach of ICOBS 6.4.4A R (failure to disclose FOS rights). The response does not engage with the merits or explain why a decision is being deferred; it merely states a review is underway. For a final response, this reads as superficial and does not fairly address the substance of the complaint or the complainant's entitlement to a decision.

The complaint raises a clear claim for financial redress (refund of £111.86 in charges for an allegedly non-consensual product) and a potential breach of disclosure obligations. The customer draft does not address redress at all — it neither offers redress nor explains why none is warranted. The handler answer notes that 'any decision on redress must be made by a human reviewer' and advises not to commit to a refund, but this internal caution does not appear in the customer draft. A final response must communicate a position on redress to the customer; deferring the decision to a future letter, without any interim position or explanation, does not satisfy this element.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I took out a personal loan of £8,000 over the phone with your bank on 15 March. It was all done remotely — I never visited a branch. On 22 March, within the 14-day window, I called your cancellation line to exercise my right to cancel the distance contract. The adviser I spoke to said I couldn't cancel because the funds had already been disbursed and I'd started making use of the credit. She refused to process my cancellation and told me I'd need to repay the full loan with all interest over the term. I asked to speak to a manager but was told nobody was available and that I'd get a callback, which never came. I'm now being charged interest on a loan I tried to cancel within the legal timeframe. I'm 74 years old and on a fixed pension income — this additional debt is causing me significant financial distress. I also want to note that when I tried calling your complaints line to raise this issue, the number listed was an 0844 number which cost me over £3 in call charges before I got through. I want the cancellation honoured, all interest charges refunded, and compensation for the distress caused.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious regulatory concerns that require careful handling and human review before any response is sent.

**1. Right to cancel — CONC 11.1.1R**
The customer took out a personal loan (credit agreement) entirely by phone on 15 March, making it a distance contract. Under CONC 11.1.1R, a consumer has a right to cancel a distance credit agreement without penalty and without giving any reason within 14 calendar days. The customer called on 22 March — 7 calendar days later — which is within that window. The adviser's refusal on the basis that funds had been disbursed and the customer had 'started making use of the credit' does not appear to be a valid ground for refusing cancellation under CONC 11.1.1R. The exemptions in COBS 15 Annex 1 (e.g. where performance has been fully completed by both parties at the consumer's express request) are set out in COBS 15 Annex 1.10R, but disbursement of loan funds alone does not constitute full completion by both parties — the customer still has repayment obligations outstanding. You should confirm with your legal/compliance team whether any specific exemption applies here, but on the face of the retrieved provisions, the refusal appears to have been incorrect.

**2. Consequences of cancellation — interest and charges**
The customer is asking for the cancellation to be honoured and all interest refunded. The retrieved provisions do not set out the precise financial consequences of exercising the right to cancel a credit agreement (e.g. whether the customer must repay the principal promptly, and whether interest accrued to the date of cancellation is recoverable). You will need to check the relevant provisions in CONC 11 (beyond what has been retrieved) and/or the Financial Services (Distance Marketing) Regulations 2004 before committing to any specific financial remedy. Do not promise a full interest refund in the customer letter without confirming the legal position.

**3. Premium rate complaints line — CONC 2.6 / GEN 7**
The retrieved provisions in CONC 2.6 (specifically the guidance at CONC 2.6) note that firms should ensure calls are not made on premium rate numbers where a long call is required, and reference the effect of the call charges rule in GEN 7. The customer reports being charged over £3 on an 0844 number to reach your complaints line. This is a potential breach of the call charges rule. You should check GEN 7 and your complaints line number arrangements urgently. The customer's call charges should be reimbursed if the number was non-compliant.

**4. Vulnerability**
The customer is 74 years old, on a fixed pension income, and reports significant financial distress. This is a clear indicator of potential vulnerability. Your vulnerability policies and the FCA's expectations around fair treatment of vulnerable customers must be applied. This complaint must be handled with heightened care.

**5. Compensation for distress**
The customer is requesting compensation for distress. Whether and how much compensation is appropriate is a financial decision that cannot be made on the basis of the retrieved provisions alone. This must go to a senior handler or complaints manager.

**6. Missed callback**
The failure to provide the promised callback is a service failure that should be acknowledged.

**Recommended action:**
- Route immediately to a senior complaints handler or complaints manager given vulnerability indicators and potential regulatory breach.
- Do not send the draft below without senior sign-off and confirmation of the legal position on cancellation consequences.
- Confirm whether any CONC 11.1.1R exemption applies (it does not appear to on the facts as stated).
- Check GEN 7 regarding the 0844 complaints line and arrange reimbursement of call charges if non-compliant.
- Consider whether a Vulnerable Customer flag should be applied to this account.
- The draft below acknowledges the complaint and confirms the complaint is being investigated; it does not commit to specific redress, as the financial consequences of cancellation require further legal/compliance input.

**Assumption flagged:** The draft treats the cancellation attempt as having been made within the 14-day window (15 March to 22 March = 7 days). Please confirm the exact dates from your call records before sending.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for contacting us. I am writing to acknowledge your complaint and to let you know that we take the concerns you have raised very seriously.

You have told us that you took out a personal loan with us entirely over the phone on 15 March, and that on 22 March — within 14 calendar days of the contract being concluded — you called our cancellation line to exercise your right to cancel. You have told us that the adviser you spoke to refused to process your cancellation, that a promised callback from a manager never took place, and that you have since been charged interest on a loan you attempted to cancel within the permitted timeframe. You have also told us that you incurred call charges when contacting our complaints line.

We understand that this situation is causing you significant financial distress, and we are sorry that your experience has fallen short of the standard you should expect from us.

We want to be clear: as a customer who entered into a credit agreement with us entirely at a distance — without visiting a branch — you have a right under the applicable rules to cancel that agreement without penalty and without giving any reason, within 14 calendar days of the contract being concluded. We are investigating whether your cancellation request on 22 March was handled correctly, and whether the reason given for refusing it was valid.

We are also looking into the concerns you have raised about the cost of calling our complaints line, and whether the number you were given meets the standards required of us.

We will write to you again with our findings and our final response. We aim to do this as quickly as possible, and we will keep you informed of our progress.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us. We have noted that you would prefer not to be kept on hold on premium rate numbers, and we will ensure any contact from us is made in a way that does not impose unreasonable costs on you.

Yours sincerely,
[Complaints Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Bank Name]

cited_provisions.relevance: The customer entered into a personal loan (credit agreement) entirely by phone, making it a distance contract; this rule gives the consumer a right to cancel without penalty and without reason within 14 calendar days, which the customer purported to exercise on day 7.

Sets out exemptions to the right to cancel a distance contract, including where performance has been fully completed by both parties at the consumer's express request; relevant to assessing whether the adviser's stated reason for refusal was valid.
Indicates that firms should ensure calls are not made on premium rate numbers where a long call is required, relevant to the customer's complaint about the 0844 complaints line number.

This is a final response stage, yet the customer draft contains no decision. It states 'We are investigating whether your cancellation request on 22 March was handled correctly' and 'We will write to you again with our findings and our final response.' This is a holding response, not a final response. A final response must state whether the complaint is upheld, partially upheld, or rejected, with reasons engaging the specific points. The draft defers the decision entirely, which does not meet the PRESENT or PARTIAL bands for a final response.

The customer draft contains no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service or the complainant's right to refer the complaint to the FOS. This is a critical omission in a final response. The absence of any FOS reference means the MISSING band is met.

The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline for referring to the FOS. Since there is no FOS referral rights statement at all, there cannot be a six-month limit statement. This element is MISSING.

verdicts.evidence: We understand that this situation is causing you significant financial distress, and we are sorry that your experience has fallen short of the standard you should expect from us. We want to be clear: as a customer who entered into a credit agreement with us entirely at a distance — without visiting a branch — you have a right under the applicable rules to cancel that agreement without penalty and without giving any reason, within 14 calendar days of the contract being concluded.
verdicts.reasoning: The response is sympathetic and acknowledges the customer's distress. It affirms the customer's cancellation right clearly. However, it is fundamentally incomplete as a final response: it defers the decision and does not engage with the substantive merits by reaching a conclusion. The response reads as fair in tone but fails to treat the complaint fairly by resolution — it leaves the customer in limbo. This is PARTIAL: the tone and initial engagement are fair, but the failure to decide undermines fair treatment.

The customer draft does not address redress at all. The complaint raises clear financial detriment (interest charges, call charges, and distress). The customer explicitly requests cancellation, interest refund, and compensation. The draft defers investigation and decision but does not communicate any position on redress — neither an offer nor an explanation of why none is due. The handler answer notes that redress decisions require senior sign-off and legal confirmation, but this reasoning is not reflected in the customer-facing text, and no position is communicated to the customer. This is MISSING.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: 'The customer is 74 years old, on a fixed pension income, and reports significant financial distress. This is a clear indicator of potential vulnerability.' Customer draft: 'We have noted that you would prefer not to be kept on hold on premium rate numbers, and we will ensure any contact from us is made in a way that does not impose unreasonable costs on you.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer clearly identifies vulnerability (age, fixed income, financial distress) and recommends heightened care and a Vulnerable Customer flag. However, the customer draft does not explicitly acknowledge the vulnerability or adapt the communication substantively in response to it. The single adaptation — avoiding premium rate numbers — is a practical measure but does not demonstrate recognition of the underlying vulnerability or signpost support. The vulnerability is identified in the handler reasoning but not acted upon in the customer-facing communication in a way that demonstrates appropriate adaptation. This is PARTIAL.

claims.claim: The customer took out a personal loan (credit agreement) entirely by phone on 15 March, making it a distance contract.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision states that a loan taken by phone constitutes a distance contract; this is a factual scenario not supported by the provided provisions.

claims.claim: Under CONC 11.1.1R, a consumer has a right to cancel a distance credit agreement without penalty and without giving any reason within 14 calendar days.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (conc11s1) explicitly sets out this right to cancel for credit agreements within 14 calendar days.

claims.claim: The adviser's refusal to cancel does not appear to be a valid ground for refusing cancellation under CONC 11.1.1R.
claims.reasoning: The provision gives the right to cancel but does not list specific permissible refusals; the claim that the refusal is invalid is an interpretation beyond the text.

claims.claim: The exemptions in COBS 15 Annex 1 (e.g., where performance has been fully completed by both parties at the consumer's express request) are set out in COBS 15 Annex 1.10R, but disbursement of loan funds alone does not constitute full completion by both parties.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 defines the exemption for full completion, confirming the existence of the exemption, but it does not state that mere disbursement is not full completion; that is an interpretive addition.

claims.claim: The retrieved provisions do not set out the precise financial consequences of exercising the right to cancel a credit agreement (e.g., whether the customer must repay the principal promptly, and whether interest accrued to the date of cancellation is recoverable).
claims.reasoning: No provision in the retrieved set addresses the detailed financial consequences of cancellation, so the claim about their absence is not supported.

claims.claim: The retrieved provisions in CONC 2.6 note that firms should ensure calls are not made on premium rate numbers where a long call is required, and reference the effect of the call charges rule in GEN 7.
claims.reasoning: Provision 7 (conc2s6) contains the guidance that firms should not use premium‑rate numbers for long calls and should note the call charges rule in GEN 7.

claims.claim: The customer's call charges should be reimbursed if the number was non‑compliant.
claims.reasoning: The provision requires firms to avoid premium‑rate numbers but does not expressly mandate reimbursement; the reimbursement conclusion extends beyond the text.

claims.claim: Vulnerability indicators require the firm's vulnerability policies and FCA expectations around fair treatment of vulnerable customers to be applied.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision addresses vulnerability policies or FCA expectations on vulnerable customers.

claims.claim: Compensation for distress must be escalated to a senior handler or complaints manager.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provisions do not contain any rule about internal escalation of compensation claims.

claims.claim: The failure to provide the promised callback is a service failure that should be acknowledged.
claims.reasoning: No provision in the retrieved set addresses callbacks or service failure acknowledgment.

claims.claim: You have a right under the applicable rules to cancel that agreement without penalty and without giving any reason, within 14 calendar days of the contract being concluded.
claims.reasoning: Matches the wording of CONC 11.1.1R as set out in Provision 1.

claims.claim: We are also looking into the concerns you have raised about the cost of calling our complaints line, and whether the number you were given meets the standards required of us.
claims.reasoning: Provision 7 (conc2s6) sets the standard that firms should not use premium‑rate numbers for long calls, which is the standard being referenced.

claims.claim: We will ensure any contact from us is made in a way that does not impose unreasonable costs on you.
claims.reasoning: The provision advises firms not to use premium‑rate numbers for long calls, implying avoidance of unreasonable costs, but does not explicitly require a blanket guarantee of no unreasonable costs for all contact.

claims.claim: The customer took out a personal loan with us entirely over the phone on 15 March, and on 22 March — within 14 calendar days of the contract being concluded — called our cancellation line to exercise the right to cancel.
claims.reasoning: These are factual statements about dates and actions; no provision confirms these specific facts.

claims.claim: The adviser you spoke to refused to process your cancellation, a promised callback never took place, and you have been charged interest on a loan you attempted to cancel within the permitted timeframe.
claims.reasoning: These are factual allegations about the firm's conduct; the retrieved provisions do not address these specific events.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am a 72-year-old retired schoolteacher with no prior investment experience beyond a basic savings account. In January 2024, I visited my bank branch to discuss options for my £45,000 in savings. The adviser suggested I purchase a structured product linked to equity indices, describing it as 'like a savings account but with better returns.' I was not asked any questions about my investment knowledge, experience with financial instruments, or understanding of risks. The adviser simply told me to sign the application form. I have since discovered the product carries significant capital-at-risk and I do not understand how it works. I have already lost approximately £3,200 in value. I believe the bank failed to check whether this product was appropriate for someone like me, and I want my money back. I am also finding this situation very stressful as I rely on my savings for day-to-day living in retirement and am struggling financially as a result of this loss.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises serious concerns about a potential suitability/appropriateness failure in the sale of a structured product to a 72-year-old retired customer with no investment experience. Key regulatory issues to consider:

1. **Appropriateness/Suitability assessment failure**: The customer states she was not asked any questions about her investment knowledge, experience, or risk understanding before being sold a capital-at-risk structured product. The retrieved provisions do not include the specific COBS suitability rules (COBS 9) or appropriateness rules (COBS 10/10A) in their full binding form for this type of sale. COBS 10A.4 (chunk cobs10as4) touches on non-complex vs complex instruments and the need for clients to understand risk, but the full appropriateness assessment rules are not fully retrieved here. This means `insufficient_context` is set to true — you should not rely solely on these provisions to determine the regulatory position with confidence.

2. **Vulnerability indicators**: The customer is 72, retired, has no investment experience, relies on savings for day-to-day living, and is experiencing financial stress. These are clear vulnerability indicators. FCA Consumer Duty and vulnerability guidance (not retrieved here) would normally apply, but even on the face of the complaint, this must be escalated for human review.

3. **Financial loss and redress**: The customer has suffered an approximate £3,200 loss and is seeking full restitution. Any decision on redress must be made by a human reviewer with access to the full file, the product documentation, the adviser's records, and the firm's suitability/appropriateness assessment (if any exists).

4. **Recommended action**: 
 - Do NOT send a final response without human review and a full file investigation.
 - Obtain the original application file, any fact-find or KYC documentation, the product key information document, and any records of what the adviser said.
 - Check whether an appropriateness or suitability assessment was conducted and documented.
 - Given the vulnerability indicators, consider whether the customer needs additional support during the complaints process.
 - The customer has a right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service if dissatisfied with the final response.

5. **Assumptions**: The draft below is an acknowledgement letter only. It does not make any admission of liability or promise of redress. It assumes the complaint was received recently and that no final response has yet been issued. Please confirm the date of receipt of the complaint so that the relevant response deadline can be calculated (the retrieved provisions do not supply the specific time limits, so these must be verified against DISP 1 rules not retrieved here).

**IMPORTANT**: The retrieved provisions do not include the core DISP 1 complaints handling time limit rules, the full COBS 9 suitability rules, or the COBS 10/10A appropriateness rules as they apply to this specific sale. Do not treat this draft as a final regulatory assessment. Human review is essential before any substantive response or redress decision is made.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for contacting us about your concerns regarding the structured product you purchased in January 2024. We are sorry to hear that you are finding this situation stressful, and we want to assure you that we take your complaint very seriously.

We understand that you are concerned that the product was not explained to you properly, that you were not asked about your investment knowledge or experience before it was recommended to you, and that you have since experienced a loss in value. We also understand that you rely on your savings for your day-to-day living, and we recognise how worrying this must be.

We are now investigating your complaint carefully. As part of this, we will be reviewing the records from your branch visit in January 2024, including any documentation completed at the time, to understand what happened and whether the right steps were followed.

We will write to you again with our findings. If we are unable to provide a full response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint, we will write to explain why and let you know when we expect to be able to respond.

If at any point you would like to discuss your complaint or need any support during this process, please do not hesitate to contact us at [contact details].

Yours sincerely,
[Name]
[Job Title]
[Firm Name]

cited_provisions.relevance: This provision addresses the distinction between complex and non-complex financial instruments and the need for clients to understand the risks involved, which is directly relevant to whether the structured product sold to this customer required an appropriateness assessment.

retrieved_chunks.content: If a retail client is not willing to accept any risk of the capital value of an investment being reduced then CIS stakeholder products, linked life stakeholder products and stakeholder CTFs should not usually be recommended. However, a firm may, if appropriate, explain the effect of inflation on long-term savings especially in relation to pensions and invite the retail client to consider his attitude to risk in the light of that explanation. 15. If a retail client is willing to accept the risk of capital reduction in some circumstances but not others then, before any recommendation to acquire a CIS stakeholder product or linked life stakeholder product is made, the retail client should be reminded of the other circumstances in which he or she is unwilling to accept risk to capital. Stakeholder pensions 16. A stakeholder pension scheme should not be recommended, and the retail client should be advised to seek alternative or further advice, if it appears that the retail client: (a) has or will have access to an occupational pension scheme; or (b) is likely to view income in retirement from state benefits as sufficient; or (c) already has a pension to which he or she could make further contributions; or (d) wishes to retire within five years. 17. It may also be appropriate to advise the retail client that other courses of action may be more beneficial than buying a stakeholder pension scheme (for example joining an occupational pension scheme). 18. A firm designing a sales process for use in the workplace may take account of the benefits offered by the employer. If a firm recommends a stakeholder pension scheme on the basis of benefits provided by an employer, then it should explain the basis of the recommendation to the retail client and suggest that the retail client seek advice if he or she has any concerns. 19. A firm should design its processes with a view to addressing the risk that retail clients will fail to appreciate the significance of questions about their pension provision and should accordingly incorporate a range of questions and information designed to foster the retail client's understanding of the issues and to elicit appropriate information. 20. Retail client should be told that a stakeholder pension scheme is life-styled and what this means. 21.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I remortgaged my property last month and was charged an early repayment charge (ERC) of £8,400 by my previous lender. When I queried this, I was told it was calculated using a method they couldn't clearly explain to me — they just said it was 'standard practice' and couldn't give me a breakdown showing it as a specific cash value or explain what costs it was meant to cover. I also never recall seeing this charge disclosed in my original mortgage illustration or offer document when I took the product out three years ago. I called to complain on Monday morning and spoke to someone who said they'd 'look into it and get back to me' but I've heard nothing since. It's now been over three weeks. I'm 74 years old and on a fixed pension income, and this unexpected charge has put me into significant financial difficulty — I had to use my savings set aside for care costs to cover it. I want the charge refunded or at minimum a clear justification showing it meets the regulatory requirements.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious regulatory concerns that require careful handling and human review before any response is sent. Here is the regulatory position based on the retrieved provisions:

**1. Validity of the ERC under MCOB 12.3.1R**
MCOB 12.3.1R (binding rule) requires that any ERC on a regulated mortgage contract must (a) be expressible as a cash value, and (b) be a reasonable pre-estimate of the costs arising from early repayment. The customer reports that the lender could not explain the calculation method or demonstrate it as a specific cash value. If accurate, this is a potential breach of MCOB 12.3.1R. You must investigate whether the £8,400 charge meets both limbs of this rule. MCOB 12.3.2G notes that firms may choose their calculation method, but the rule still requires the result to be a reasonable pre-estimate expressed as a cash value.

**2. Disclosure at illustration/offer stage**
MCOB 5.6.84R (binding rule) requires that the mortgage illustration must include, under the heading 'Early repayment charges': an explanation of whether and when ERCs are payable, the basis of calculation, example cash amounts, and the maximum charge the customer could be charged. If the ERC was not disclosed in the original illustration or offer document as required, this is a potential further breach. You should obtain and review the original illustration and offer document to check whether the ERC was disclosed in the required format.

**3. Complaint handling delay**
The customer called to complain over three weeks ago and has received no substantive response. You should check your firm's complaint handling obligations under DISP — note that the retrieved provisions do not include DISP rules, so I cannot cite specific time limits from the retrieved context. However, the absence of any response for three weeks is a concern you should review against your DISP obligations separately.

**4. Customer vulnerability**
The customer is 74 years old, on a fixed pension income, and has suffered significant financial difficulty — having had to use savings set aside for care costs. This is a strong indicator of vulnerability. This must be factored into how the complaint is handled and the urgency with which it is progressed.

**5. Redress**
The customer is seeking a refund of £8,400 or a clear justification. Any decision on redress or refund is a financial decision that must be made by an authorised person — this cannot be committed to in the customer draft. The draft below acknowledges the complaint, confirms investigation, and sets out next steps without admitting liability or promising a specific outcome.

**Assumptions and actions required before sending:**
- Confirm whether the original mortgage was a regulated mortgage contract (MCOB 12.3 applies to regulated mortgage contracts).
- Obtain and review the original mortgage illustration and offer document to check ERC disclosure.
- Obtain the lender's ERC calculation methodology and assess whether it meets MCOB 12.3.1R.
- Check DISP obligations on complaint acknowledgement and response timelines — these are not covered by the retrieved provisions and must be checked separately.
- Given the customer's age, pension income, and use of care savings, apply your firm's vulnerability policy.
- A senior handler or compliance officer should review before this letter is sent, given the potential regulatory breaches, financial impact, and vulnerability indicators.

**Human review is required** before sending any response, given: potential regulatory breaches (MCOB 12.3.1R and MCOB 5.6.84R), a financial redress decision, clear vulnerability indicators, and a possible complaint handling delay.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us about the early repayment charge of £8,400 applied when you remortgaged last month. I am sorry that you have not heard back from us since you first raised this — I understand how frustrating and worrying that must be, particularly given the financial impact this charge has had on you.

We take your concerns seriously and are treating this as a formal complaint. We are now investigating the following issues you have raised:

1. Whether the early repayment charge was calculated correctly and in line with the regulatory requirements that apply to mortgage early repayment charges — specifically, whether it can be shown as a clear cash amount and represents a reasonable estimate of the costs arising from your early repayment.

2. Whether the charge, and the basis on which it was calculated, was properly disclosed to you in the illustration and offer documents you received when you originally took out the mortgage.

We will write to you again with our findings and our response to your complaint. If we uphold your complaint, we will set out what we propose to do to put things right. If we do not uphold it, we will explain our reasons clearly.

We are aware that this situation has caused you significant financial difficulty, and we will treat your complaint with the urgency it deserves.

If you have any documents from when you originally took out the mortgage — such as the mortgage illustration or offer letter — it would be helpful if you could send us copies, though we will also be making our own enquiries.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us at [contact details].

Yours sincerely,
[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Firm Name]

Requires the mortgage illustration to include disclosure of whether ERCs are payable, when they apply, the basis of calculation, example cash amounts, and the maximum charge — relevant to the customer's claim that the ERC was not disclosed in the original illustration.

(2) In cases where the amount of compensation would depend on different factors, such as the amount repaid or the prevailing interest rate at the moment of the early repayment, the firm must indicate how the compensation will be calculated and provide the maximum amount that the charge might be, or where this is not possible, an illustrative example in order to demonstrate to the consumer the level of compensation under different possible scenarios. fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to complain about my home insurance policy. I am a retired 78-year-old and I took out a home contents and buildings policy through your branch last year. When I purchased the policy, I specifically told your adviser that I also rent out a small annexe attached to my property on a buy-to-let basis, and asked whether this would be covered. I was assured it would be fine under my standard home policy. However, when I recently made a claim for storm damage to the annexe roof, you rejected it saying the annexe is a commercial let property and isn't covered under my consumer home insurance policy. I feel I was mis-sold this policy — I was never told I needed a separate commercial policy for the annexe, and your adviser should have categorised my needs properly. I'm now facing a £4,200 repair bill I cannot afford on my pension. To make matters worse, I first raised this complaint by telephone over five weeks ago and have received no acknowledgement whatsoever — no letter, no email, nothing. I had to call again today and was told there was no record of my earlier call. This is completely unacceptable treatment of a loyal customer.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious issues that require careful handling and mandatory human review before any response is sent.

**1. Potential mis-sale / misrepresentation**
The customer states that before taking out the policy, she disclosed to your adviser that she rents out an annexe on a buy-to-let basis and was assured it would be covered under a standard home policy. If that account is accurate, this is a potential mis-sale: the adviser failed to identify her actual needs and placed her in an unsuitable product. The retrieved provisions do not include the specific suitability or mis-sale rules that would govern this (e.g. ICOBS suitability rules), so `insufficient_context` is flagged. However, COBS 17.1.3R (binding rule) provides that an insurer must not unreasonably reject a claim, and COBS 17.1.5G (guidance) indicates that rejection may be unreasonable in certain circumstances. If the customer disclosed the annexe use at point of sale and was assured of cover, the rejection of the claim may be challengeable on that basis — but this requires investigation of the sale records and call logs before any position is taken.

**2. Claim rejection**
The insurer has rejected the claim on the basis that the annexe is a commercial let and not covered. Before confirming this position, you must review: (a) the policy wording; (b) any record of what the customer disclosed at point of sale; (c) what the adviser said in response. If the customer's account is correct, the rejection may be unreasonable under COBS 17.1.3R. COBS 17.1.2R also requires that, as soon as reasonably practicable after receipt of a claim, the insurer must tell the policyholder why the claim has been rejected and whether any future rights to claim exist — check whether this was done properly.

**3. Complaints handling failure**
The customer says she raised this complaint by telephone over five weeks ago and has received no acknowledgement, no letter, and no email. This is a significant procedural concern. The retrieved provisions (DISP 1.6 chunk) set out what a final response must contain, including enclosing the FOS explanatory leaflet, providing the FOS website address, and informing the customer of her right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service. The absence of any acknowledgement or response for over five weeks is a serious compliance concern — you must check your CMS urgently for any record of the original call and establish the actual complaint receipt date, as regulatory time limits for responding to complaints will have been running from that date.

**4. Vulnerability**
The customer is 78 years old, retired, and states she is facing a £4,200 repair bill she cannot afford on her pension. This is a clear indicator of potential financial vulnerability. This must be factored into how the complaint is handled and the urgency with which it is progressed.

**5. Recommended immediate actions**
- Treat the complaint as received from the date of the original telephone call (over five weeks ago) and check whether any regulatory response deadline has already been missed.
- Search CMS and telephony records for the original call.
- Pull the sale file, including any record of what the customer disclosed and what the adviser said.
- Flag the customer as potentially vulnerable.
- Do not confirm or deny the claim rejection position in the customer letter at this stage — the draft below acknowledges the complaint and commits to investigation only.
- This complaint must be reviewed by a senior complaints handler and compliance before any response is sent.

**Assumption flagged:** The draft below does not include the FOS time-limit waiver wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R because it is not clear whether the complaint is being treated as a final response or an interim response. If this letter is intended as a final response, it must include the FOS leaflet, FOS website address, and the Annex 3R wording on time limits. If it is an interim response (because investigation is ongoing), it must explain why a final response cannot yet be given and indicate when one is expected, and still include the FOS leaflet and website address. The draft below is framed as an interim acknowledgement/update — please confirm the intended status before sending.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for contacting us, and I am sorry that you have had to chase us for a response. I understand that you first raised this complaint by telephone approximately five weeks ago and have received no acknowledgement. I want to assure you that we take this very seriously, and I apologise unreservedly for the delay and the distress this has caused you.

I am writing to confirm that your complaint has now been formally recorded and is being investigated as a priority. Your complaint concerns two matters:

1. The rejection of your claim for storm damage to the annexe roof of your property; and
2. The way in which the policy was sold to you, including the advice you received at the time of purchase about whether the annexe would be covered.

We understand that you disclosed to our adviser, before taking out the policy, that you rent out the annexe on a buy-to-let basis, and that you were given assurances that this would be covered under your standard home insurance policy. We take this account seriously and will be reviewing the records of your sale, including any notes or recordings of the advice given, as part of our investigation.

We also recognise that you are facing a significant repair bill and that this situation is causing you real financial concern. We will treat your complaint with the urgency it deserves.

We will write to you again with our full findings and our response to your complaint. In the meantime, if you have any questions or wish to provide any further information, please do not hesitate to contact us at [contact details].

If at any point you are dissatisfied with the progress of your complaint, you have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, which is a free and independent service for resolving disputes between customers and financial businesses. You can contact them at [FOS website address] or by calling [FOS telephone number]. I have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet for your information.

Once again, I am sorry for the difficulties you have experienced.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires that an insurer must not unreasonably reject a claim; relevant to assessing whether the rejection of the annexe storm damage claim is defensible given the customer's account of what was disclosed at point of sale.

Requires the insurer to tell the policyholder, as soon as reasonably practicable after receipt of a claim, why the claim has been rejected and whether any future rights to claim exist; relevant to checking whether the rejection was communicated properly.
Indicates the FCA's view of circumstances in which rejection of a consumer's claim would be unreasonable; relevant to assessing the strength of the customer's challenge to the claim rejection.

Sets out the requirements for a final response, including enclosing the FOS explanatory leaflet, providing the FOS website address, and informing the complainant of the right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service; relevant to the obligations triggered by this complaint and the failure to respond for over five weeks.

retrieved_chunks.content: Unsuitable sale identified on the grounds of affordability and complaint raised on 12th policy anniversary. It has always been the intention of the complainant to retire at State retirement age 65. Term from date of sale to retirement is 20 years and the maturity date of the mortgage is 5 years after retirement. Established facts Established premium paid by investor on policy of original term (25 years): £81.20 Premium that would have been payable on policy with term from sale to retirement (20 years): £111.20 Actual policy value at time complaint assessed: £12,500 Value of an equivalent 20-year policy at time complaint assessed: £21,300 Difference in policy values at time complaint assessed: £8,800 Difference in outgoings (20 year policy - 25 year policy): £4,320 Basis of compensation The policy is reconstructed as if it had been set up originally on a term to mature at retirement age, in this example, a term of 20 years. The difference in the current value of the policy actually sold to the complainant and the current value of the reconstructed policy, as if the premium on the reconstructed policy had been paid from outset, is calculated. The complainant has gained from lower outgoings (lower premiums) of the actual endowment policy to date. In calculating the redress, the gain may be offset against the loss unless the complainant's particular circumstances are such that it would be unreasonable to take account of the gain. Redress generally if it is not unreasonable to take account of the whole of the gain from lower outgoings Loss from current value of reconstructed policy less current value of actual policy: (£8,800) Gain from total lower outgoings under actual policy: £4,320 Net loss: (£4,480) Therefore total redress is: £4,480 Redress if it is unreasonable to take account of gain from lower outgoings Loss from current value of reconstructed policy less current value of actual policy: (£8,800) Gain from total lower outgoings under actual policy: Ignored Therefore total redress is: £8,800 Additional Information If the policy is capable of reconstruction, the complainant must now fund the higher premiums himself for the remainder of the term of the shortened policy until maturity. In this example the higher premium could be £111.20.

For example: (a) section 10 provides situations in which an insurer has no liability under a policy due to a breach of warranty; (b) section 11 places restrictions on an insurer’s ability to reject a claim for breach of a term where compliance is aimed at reducing certain types of risk; and (c) sections 12 and 13 provide for the extent to which a firm is entitled to reject fraudulent claims. ## ICOBS 8.1.2B (Rules) For contracts entered into or variations agreed on or after 1 August 2017, a rejection of a consumer policyholder's claim for breach of a condition or warranty (that is not subject to and within section 10 or 11 of the Insurance Act 2015) is unreasonable unless the circumstances of the claim are connected to the breach. ## ICOBS 8.1.3 (Rules) In this section, a “qualifying misrepresentation” is one made by a consumer before a consumer insurance contract was entered into or varied if: (1) the consumer made the misrepresentation in breach of the duty set out in section 2(2) of the Consumer Insurance (Disclosure and Representations) Act 2012 to take reasonable care not to make a misrepresentation to the insurer; and (2) the insurer shows that without the misrepresentation, that insurer would not have entered into the contract (or agreed to the variation) at all, or would have done so only on different terms. and (2) (for each part of the claim it rejects), why the claim has been rejected and whether any future rights to claim exist. ## COBS 17.1.3 (Rules) An insurer and a managing agent must not unreasonably reject a claim. ## COBS 17.1.4 (Rules) For contracts entered into or variations agreed before 1 August 2017, except where there is evidence of fraud, an insurer and a managing agent must not reject a claim for: (1) (in relation to contracts entered into or variations agreed on or before 5 April 2013) non-disclosure of a fact material to the risk which the policyholder could not reasonably have been expected to disclose;
