fixed-titan.scenario_text: I opened an execution-only share dealing account with your firm last year to buy and sell shares listed on the London Stock Exchange. When I signed up, I was never told anything about your complaints procedure — no leaflet, no mention of it at all. I wasn't even told about the Financial Ombudsman Service. More recently, I purchased units in a structured product through the same account. The product turned out to be far more complex than I expected — it embeds a derivative and I don't understand the risks at all. Nobody at your firm asked me any questions about my knowledge or experience before letting me buy it, and I wasn't warned that you weren't assessing whether it was appropriate for me. I've now lost a significant amount of money. I believe your firm failed in its obligations both by not assessing appropriateness for a complex product and by never informing me of my rights to complain or access the ombudsman.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues. I can partially address them from the retrieved provisions, but there are significant gaps that require human review before any response is sent.

**Issue 1: Failure to provide complaints procedure information at point of sale**
DISP 1.2.1R (retrieved in chunk disp1s2.md) requires respondents to provide eligible complainants with information about their internal complaints handling procedures, including a reference to the Financial Ombudsman Service, at or immediately after the point of first contact. DISP 1.2.3G further indicates that summary details should cover how the firm handles complaints and that the complainant may be entitled to refer unresolved complaints to the FOS. If the customer was never given this information at account opening, there is a prima facie breach of DISP 1.2.1R. You should check your records to confirm whether any complaints information was provided at or around account opening — for example, in contractual documentation or a leaflet referenced therein (DISP 1.2.4G notes this is permissible).

**Issue 2: Appropriateness assessment for the structured product**
The customer alleges that no appropriateness assessment was conducted before they purchased a complex structured product (embedding a derivative) through an execution-only account, and that they were not warned that no such assessment was being made. The retrieved provisions do not include the specific COBS rules on appropriateness (e.g. COBS 10 or COBS 10A) that would govern this obligation. The MCOB execution-only provisions retrieved (MCOB 4.8A) relate to regulated mortgage contracts, not share dealing or structured products, and are not applicable here. The COBS 4 chunk retrieved relates to risk warnings for specific investment types but does not set out the appropriateness assessment framework. I therefore cannot ground a regulatory position on the appropriateness failure from the retrieved provisions alone.

**Recommendation:**
- Flag `insufficient_context` as true for the appropriateness issue.
- Do not make any admission of liability or quantify redress in the customer letter.
- The complaint involves a significant financial loss, which independently requires human review before any response is sent.
- You must confirm: (a) what complaints information, if any, was provided at account opening; (b) whether an appropriateness assessment was conducted or a warning given before the structured product purchase; (c) whether the customer may be vulnerable (significant financial loss is mentioned).
- The customer draft below acknowledges the complaint, confirms it is being investigated, and informs the customer of their FOS rights — but does not make any concessions on either issue pending investigation and human review.

**Assumption flagged:** The customer draft treats this as an acknowledgement/holding response, not a final response. If this is intended to serve as a final response, it will need to be substantially revised once the facts are confirmed and the appropriateness rules are retrieved and applied.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. We are sorry to hear about your experience and we take your concerns seriously.

You have raised two issues: first, that you were not provided with information about our complaints procedure or your right to refer complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service when you opened your account; and second, that you were not assessed for the suitability of the structured product you purchased, nor warned that no such assessment was being carried out, before you made that purchase.

We are now investigating both of these concerns carefully. We will review our records and assess what information was provided to you at the time of account opening and at the time of your structured product purchase. We will write to you again with our findings.

In the meantime, we want to make sure you are aware of your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. If you are not satisfied with our response once we have completed our investigation, you may be entitled to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. Further information about the Financial Ombudsman Service and how to contact them is available at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.

We are sorry for any distress this situation has caused you and we will aim to respond to you as promptly as possible.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Firm name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the respondent to provide eligible complainants with information about internal complaints handling procedures, including reference to the FOS, at or immediately after the point of first contact — directly relevant to the customer's allegation that no such information was given at account opening.

Indicates that summary details of complaints handling procedures should cover how the firm handles complaints and that the complainant may be entitled to refer unresolved complaints to the FOS — relevant to the scope of what should have been communicated.

Indicates that summary complaints details may be set out in a leaflet and their availability referred to in contractual documentation — relevant to assessing whether the firm may have met its DISP 1.2.1R obligation through documentation provided at account opening.

retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP 1.1A Complaints handling requirements for MiFID complaints ## DISP 1.1A.1 (Rules) This section: (1) applies to the MiFID complaints of a MiFID investment firm and does not apply to complaints that are not MiFID complaints; (2) also applies to the MiFID complaints of a third country investment firm received from a retail client or an elective professional client but does not apply to complaints that are not MiFID complaints; and (3) applies certain other provisions in DISP 1 to such complaints. ## DISP 1.1A.3 (Guidance) A MiFID complaint is, amongst other things, a complaint about:(1) the provision of investment services or ancillary services to a client by an investment firm;(2) the provision of one or more investment services to a client by a CRD credit institution;(3) selling structured deposits to clients, or advising clients on them, where the sale or advice is provided by an investment firm or a CRD credit institution;(4) the activities permitted by the UK provisions which implemented article 6(3) of the UCITS Directive when carried on by a collective portfolio management investment firm; and(5) the activities permitted by the UK provisions which implemented article 6(4) of the AIFMD when carried on by a collective portfolio management investment firm.[Note: see article 1(1), 1(3) and 1(4) of MiFID] ## DISP 1.1A.4 (Guidance) A MiFID complaint is also a complaint about the equivalent business of a third country investment firm. [Note: see articles 39 and 41 of MiFID] ## DISP 1.1A.5 (Guidance) In contrast to the other provisions in DISP 1 which generally apply to complaints from eligible complainants, subject to DISP 1.1A.6R: (1) the obligations in this section that apply to the MiFID complaints of MiFID investment firms, apply to complaints from “clients” as defined in the UK provisions which implemented MiFID (which includes retail clients, professional clients and (in relation to eligible counterparty business) eligible counterparties; and (2) the obligations in this section that apply to the MiFID complaints of third country investment firms, apply to complaints from retail clients and elective professional clients.

retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP App 1.1 Introduction ## DISP App 1.1.1 (Guidance) This appendix sets out the approach and standards which firms should use when investigating complaints relating to the sale of endowment policies for the purposes of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage. It is not intended to be comprehensive. It is primarily concerned with the assessment of whether the complainant may have suffered financial loss, and if so, how much that loss is, and therefore what amount a firm should consider offering by way of fair and appropriate compensation in circumstances where the firm's investigation of a complaint reveals: (1) the complainant has received negligent advice on investments; and (2) if this advice had not been negligent, either: (a) the complainant would be unlikely to have acquired the endowment policy but instead would have taken out the same amount of loan on a repayment basis; or (b) the complainant would have acquired an endowment mortgage for a shorter term. ## DISP App 1.1.2 (Guidance) There will also be cases where a firm will conclude after investigation that, notwithstanding its own failure to give compliant and proper advice, the complainant would nevertheless have proceeded with the endowment policy as sold, in which case no compensation will be due. ## DISP App 1.1.3 (Guidance) This appendix only addresses how firms should approach the assessment of loss and compensation where negligence on the part of the firm is established. ## DISP App 1.1.4 (Guidance) This appendix is relevant both to the obligations arising under the complaints handling rules contained in DISP 1 and to the FCA's approach to the supervision of firms. ## DISP App 1.1.5 (Guidance) This appendix is also relevant to complaints which the Ombudsman may investigate under the Compulsory Jurisdiction or Voluntary Jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service established under Part XVI of the Act (The Ombudsman Scheme). ## DISP App 1.1.6 (Guidance) Before proceeding to assess the extent of a complainant's financial loss, a firm will usually have completed the following stages: (1) gathering all relevant facts and information; (2) making a fair and objective assessment whether it has failed to comply with a relevant duty owed to the complainant; and (3) assessing whether any failure of duty by it was in the circumstances a material failure in the sense that if it had not occurred the complainant would

or (2) the subject matter of the complaint has been dealt with, or is being dealt with, by a comparable ADR entity; or (3) the subject matter of the complaint has been the subject of court proceedings where there has been a decision on the merits; or (5) dealing with such a type of complaint would otherwise seriously impair the effective operation of the Financial Ombudsman Service. ## DISP 3.3.4B (Guidance) Examples of a type of complaint that would otherwise seriously impair the effective operation of the Financial Ombudsman Service may include: (1) where it would be more suitable for the complaint to be dealt with by a court or a comparable ADR entity; or (2) where the subject matter of the complaint has already been dealt with by a comparable dispute resolution scheme; or (3) where the subject matter of the complaint has previously been considered or excluded under the Financial Ombudsman Service (unless material new evidence which the Ombudsman considers likely to affect the outcome has subsequently become available to the complainant); or (4) it is a complaint which: (a) involves (or might involve) more than one eligible complainant; and (b) has been referred without the consent of the other eligible complainant or complainants, and the Ombudsman considers that it would be inappropriate to deal with the complaint without that consent.

verdicts.reasoning: The language is courteous, acknowledges the complainant's distress, and promises an impartial investigation without dismissing the issues.
The draft does not address whether any redress or remedial action will be offered, nor does it explain why none is due.

verdicts.evidence: You have raised two issues: first, that you were not provided with information about our complaints procedure or your right to refer complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service when you opened your account; and second, that you were not assessed for the suitability of the structured product you purchased, nor warned that no such assessment was being carried out, before you made that purchase.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with 'Thank you for getting in touch with us' and then accurately reflects both substantive issues raised in the complaint: (1) failure to provide complaints procedure information at account opening, and (2) failure to assess appropriateness for the structured product and lack of warning. The acknowledgement is specific and demonstrates understanding of what is being complained about.

The response states 'We are now investigating both of these concerns carefully' but does not reach or communicate any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). The handler answer explicitly notes this is treated as an acknowledgement/holding response, not a final response, and flags that 'If this is intended to serve as a final response, it will need to be substantially revised.' Since the response stage is marked as 'final', the absence of a decision and substantive reasons on either issue is a critical gap. The response merely promises future investigation without resolving the complaint.

verdicts.evidence: If you are not satisfied with our response once we have completed our investigation, you may be entitled to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. Further information about the Financial Ombudsman Service and how to contact them is available at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the right to refer to the FOS and provides the means to do so (website URL). It identifies the FOS by name and makes clear that the complainant may refer 'your complaint' to them.

The response mentions the right to refer to the FOS but does not state the six-month deadline running from the date of this final response. The phrase 'If you are not satisfied with our response once we have completed our investigation' does not anchor a time limit to the final response date, nor does it specify six months.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry to hear about your experience and we take your concerns seriously... We are now investigating both of these concerns carefully... We are sorry for any distress this situation has caused you and we will aim to respond to you as promptly as possible.
verdicts.reasoning: The response uses empathetic language ('sorry to hear', 'take your concerns seriously', 'sorry for any distress') and does not dismiss the complaint. However, it relies heavily on templated language and does not substantively engage with the merits of either issue. The substance of the complaint—the alleged regulatory breaches and financial loss—is acknowledged but not addressed on its merits. For a final response, this superficial engagement falls short of fair treatment, though the tone is not dismissive.

The complaint plainly raises a significant financial loss ('I've now lost a significant amount of money'). The response does not address whether redress or remedial action is appropriate. The handler answer explicitly states 'Do not make any admission of liability or quantify redress in the customer letter' and flags the need for human review, but this means redress is not addressed in the customer-facing text at all. For a final response, the complete absence of any position on redress—whether an offer, a reasoned explanation of why none is due, or a commitment to consider it—is a material omission.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I have a regulated mortgage with your bank and I'm writing to complain about two issues. First, when I fell into arrears last year, you applied a £250 'arrears management fee' each month for three consecutive months, plus compound interest on those fees. I've checked with other lenders and nobody charges anything close to that — most charge £25-50 at most. I believe these charges are grossly excessive and the compound interest on a second charge mortgage is unlawful. Second, when I tried to call your complaints line to raise this issue, the only number provided was an 0871 number. I was on hold for 45 minutes and the call cost me over £18. I shouldn't have to pay a premium rate number just to make a complaint. I'm currently on long-term sick leave following a cancer diagnosis and am struggling financially as a result of reduced income. I'd like a full refund of the excessive charges, the interest applied to them, and reimbursement of my phone costs.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises three distinct regulatory issues, all of which have clear grounding in the retrieved provisions. There is also a strong vulnerability indicator (cancer diagnosis, financial hardship) that requires careful handling throughout.

**Issue 1: Arrears management charges (£250/month)**
MCOB 12.4.1 (not directly retrieved but MCOB 12.4 is referenced in the retrieved provisions) governs payment shortfall charges. The retrieved provisions in MCOB 12.4 make clear that payment shortfall charges must reflect the actual additional administration costs incurred — and MCOB 12.4.4R explicitly prohibits including certain cost categories (funding/capital, general bank charges not caused by the shortfall, unrecovered fees, advertising costs, regulatory fines, and certain executive staff costs) in that calculation. MCOB 12.5.2R requires that charges in connection with administering a regulated mortgage contract must not be excessive. MCOB 12.5.3G sets out factors for assessing excessiveness: comparison with market rates, abuse of trust, and disclosure. The customer's comparison with market rates (£25–50 vs £250) is directly relevant to MCOB 12.5.3G(1). You will need to review the firm's cost-basis calculation for these charges to assess whether they comply with MCOB 12.4.4R. If the charges cannot be justified by actual additional administration costs (excluding the prohibited categories), they are likely non-compliant.

**Issue 2: Compound interest on arrears charges (second charge mortgage)**
MCOB 12.5.5R is directly on point: a second charge lender may only charge simple interest on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract. If this is a second charge regulated mortgage contract, compound interest on the arrears management fees is unlawful under MCOB 12.5.5R. You must confirm whether this is a first or second charge mortgage. If it is a second charge, the compound interest element must be reversed. If it is a first charge, MCOB 12.5.5R does not apply, but the excessive charges rules under MCOB 12.5.2R may still be relevant.

**Issue 3: 0871 complaints line**
GEN 7.2.1R requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate. GEN 7.2.5R explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant. This is a clear breach. The customer is entitled to reimbursement of the excess call cost (i.e., the amount above basic rate). You should calculate the reimbursable amount based on the difference between the 0871 rate and a basic geographic rate for the duration of the call.

**Vulnerability**
The customer has disclosed a cancer diagnosis and financial hardship. This is a clear vulnerability indicator. The response must reflect appropriate sensitivity and the firm's obligations to treat vulnerable customers fairly (Principle 6, referenced in MCOB 12.2.1G). This complaint must be escalated for human review before any response is sent.

**Recommended actions before sending:**
1. Confirm whether this is a first or second charge regulated mortgage contract (determines applicability of MCOB 12.5.5R on compound interest).
2. Obtain the firm's cost-basis calculation for the £250 arrears management fee and assess against MCOB 12.4.4R prohibited cost categories.
3. Calculate the excess call cost for reimbursement (0871 premium over basic rate for ~45 minutes).
4. Flag the customer's vulnerability to the relevant team and ensure the response and any ongoing contact reflects appropriate adjustments.
5. Do not admit liability or quantify redress in the customer letter — the draft below acknowledges the complaint and confirms investigation, but redress decisions must be made by a human reviewer after the above steps.

**Assumption flagged:** The customer draft below does not confirm whether the mortgage is first or second charge, and does not promise reversal of compound interest — it acknowledges the concern and confirms investigation. If confirmed as second charge, the final response letter will need to confirm the reversal of compound interest as a matter of regulatory obligation under MCOB 12.5.5R.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear about your health and the financial pressures you are facing, and I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint seriously.

You have raised three concerns, which I will address in turn.

**Arrears management charges**

You have told us that you were charged £250 per month for three consecutive months in arrears management fees, and that comparable charges at other lenders are significantly lower. We are required to ensure that charges applied in connection with administering your mortgage are not excessive. In assessing whether a charge is excessive, we are expected to consider, among other things, how our charges compare with those for similar products or services in the market. We take this concern seriously and are reviewing the basis on which these charges were calculated and applied.

**Interest applied to arrears charges**

You have also raised a concern about the interest charged on top of those fees. We are reviewing the type of interest applied and whether it was applied correctly in accordance with the rules that govern your mortgage. We will address this as part of our investigation.

**Cost of calling our complaints line**

We are required to ensure that when you contact us in relation to your mortgage, you are not charged more than the basic rate for that call. The number you were directed to use should not have resulted in you paying a premium rate. We apologise for this. We will investigate the call charge you incurred and, where we find that you were charged above the basic rate, we will reimburse you for the excess amount.

**Next steps**

We are now investigating all three aspects of your complaint. We will write to you again with our findings and our response. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us — and we will ensure that any number we provide for you to use does not cost you more than the basic rate.

If, once you have received our final response, you remain dissatisfied, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further details about this right when we send our final response.

Thank you again for bringing these matters to our attention.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Firm name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires that charges to a customer in connection with administering a regulated mortgage contract must not be excessive — directly applicable to the £250 monthly arrears management fees.

Sets out factors for assessing whether a charge is excessive, including comparison with market rates and the degree to which charges abuse the customer's trust — relevant to the customer's comparison with competitor charges of £25–50.
Requires that a regulated mortgage contract must not impose, and cannot be used to impose, excessive charges upon a customer.

Provides that a second charge lender may only charge simple interest on charges applied for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract — directly applicable to the compound interest complaint if this is a second charge mortgage.

Prohibits certain cost categories (including funding/capital costs, general bank charges, unrecovered fees, advertising costs, and regulatory fines) from being included in the calculation of additional administration costs for payment shortfall charges — relevant to assessing whether the £250 fee is properly calculated.

Confirms that payment shortfall charge and excessive charge requirements continue to apply after a regulated mortgage contract ends, and that references to 'customer' include former customers — relevant to the ongoing applicability of MCOB 12 protections.
Explains that MCOB 12 reinforces Principle 6 (treating customers fairly) and the obligation to make charges transparent — relevant to the overall fairness assessment and the customer's vulnerability.

Requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate — directly applicable to the complaints line charge complaint.
Explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant with the call charges rule — confirms the complaints line number used was in breach.
Defines the basic rate as the simple cost of connection, which must not provide the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues — relevant to calculating the reimbursable excess call cost.

retrieved_chunks.content: # MCOB — MCOB 12.5 Excessive charges: regulated mortgage contracts, home reversion plans and regulated sale and rent back agreements ## MCOB 12.5.1 (Rules) A firm must ensure that any regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement that it enters into does not impose, and cannot be used to impose, excessive charges upon a customer. ## MCOB 12.5.2 (Rules) A firm must ensure that its charges to a customer in connection with the firm entering into, making a further advance or further release on, administering, arranging or advising on a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement , or arranging or advising on a variation to the terms of a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement are not excessive. ## MCOB 12.5.3 (Guidance) When determining whether a charge is excessive, a firm should consider: (1) the amount of its charges for the services or products in question compared with charges for similar products or services on the market; (2) the degree to which the charges are an abuse of the trust that the customer has placed in the firm; and (3) the nature and extent of the disclosure of the charges to the customer. ## MCOB 12.5.4 (Guidance) Mortgage lenders are also subject to requirements relating to responsible lending (see MCOB 11). ## MCOB 12.5.5 (Rules) A second charge lender may only charge interest on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract if the interest is simple interest.

retrieved_chunks.content: # MCOB — MCOB 12.2 Purpose ## MCOB 12.2.1 (Guidance) (1) Principle 6 requires a firm to pay due regard to the interests of its customers and treat them fairly. A firm is also under an obligation, as a consequence of this sourcebook's disclosure requirements, to make charges transparent to customers. This chapter reinforces these requirements by preventing a firm from imposing unfair and excessive charges. (2) The level of charges under a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement is not typically a matter for regulation. However, in certain limited circumstances, the FCA believes that customers should be protected from unfair and excessive charging practices. This chapter considers four specific circumstances, where: (a) the charges imposed upon a customer seeking to terminate a regulated mortgage contract before the end of the term of the contract do not reflect the cost of termination to the firm; (b) the charges imposed on a customer in payment difficulties are not based upon the costs incurred by the firm; (c) the charges (including rates of interest) imposed on a customer under a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement are excessive and contrary to the customer's interests; and (d) the charges made to a customer in connection with a firm entering into, making a further advance on, administering, arranging or advising on a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement , or arranging or advising on a variation to the terms of a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement are excessive.

(d) The definitions of an arranged overdraft charge and an unarranged overdraft charge are not limited to charges that are described as financial consideration for the provision of credit. They could include, for example, a charge that is expressed as being referable to the execution of the payment transaction, if the charge is payable only where the transaction results in the account being in an overdrawn position or remaining in such a position. A charge for a payment transaction that is payable irrespective of whether or not the current account has a credit balance or a debit balance is not, however, caught by these definitions. (e) The definitions also do not include charges for operating or maintaining a personal current account (as distinct from charges for granting or continuing to make available an arranged overdraft facility in connection with the account), provided that the incidence and amount of the charges are not affected by whether or how much the customer uses an overdraft. A monthly account charge could be an example of such a charge. (9) CONC 5C.2 requires firms to use only a rate of interest expressed as a percentage applied on an annual basis to the relevant balance of arranged overdraft or unarranged overdraft. If interest is compounded, firms are free to choose the intervals at which they add arranged overdraft charges and unarranged overdraft charges to the principal balance, provided that the same compounding frequency is used in relation to the customer’s arranged overdraft and unarranged overdraft in respect of the same personal current account. (10) Firms are reminded of the obligation in CONC 3.5.3R(1) to include a representative example (including the representative APR) in a financial promotion that indicates a rate of interest or an amount relating to the cost of credit. Firms are also reminded of the obligation in CONC 3.5.7R(1) to include in a financial promotion a representative APR if the financial promotion states or includes certain matters.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I took out a home insurance policy through my bank's branch last year. I've since discovered that the bank received a significantly higher commission from the insurer they recommended compared to two other insurers whose products were arguably better suited to my needs and cheaper. When I asked the branch advisor at the time whether there were other options, they simply said 'this is the best one for you' without explaining why or mentioning any alternatives. I feel I was steered into a more expensive, less suitable policy because the bank was incentivised by the commission arrangement. I raised this with the bank's complaints team three weeks ago and received an acknowledgement, but I'm now writing to request a full resolution. I'm a retired pensioner on a fixed income and the additional premium cost has been a real stretch for my household budget.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises serious concerns that require careful handling. The customer alleges they were steered into a more expensive, less suitable home insurance policy because the bank received higher commission from the recommended insurer — and that the advisor failed to disclose alternatives or explain the recommendation. There are also clear vulnerability indicators: the customer is a retired pensioner on a fixed income who has experienced financial strain as a result.

**Regulatory position based on retrieved provisions:**

1. **ICOBS 6A (customer's best interests rule):** The retrieved chunk from icobs6as5.md references the 'customer's best interests rule' and states that where a firm receives greater remuneration (e.g. a higher commission rate) compared to other arrangements available to it, it must demonstrate how that selection was consistent with the customer's best interests rule. It also states that where remuneration conflicts with the duty to comply with the customer's best interests rule, the firm must take appropriate action. This is directly relevant to the allegation that the bank recommended a more expensive, less suitable policy because of higher commission.

2. **CONC 4.5.6R (discretionary commission arrangements):** This rule prohibits lenders and credit brokers from entering into or relying on discretionary commission arrangements. While this provision is primarily directed at motor finance credit broking, you should confirm whether any credit element (e.g. premium finance) was involved in this home insurance sale, as that could bring CONC 4.5 into scope.

**Important caveats and assumptions you must confirm before sending any response:**
- The retrieved provisions do not include the full ICOBS 6A sourcebook or the specific rule reference for the customer's best interests rule (e.g. ICOBS 6A.2.1R or similar). The chunk references the rule by name but does not give a standalone rule reference I can cite with confidence. I have therefore flagged `insufficient_context` as true and this must go to human review.
- The complaint involves a potential financial remedy (reimbursement of excess premium, or compensation for unsuitable advice), which requires human review before any commitment is made.
- The customer shows clear vulnerability indicators (retired, fixed income, financial hardship). This independently requires human review under your firm's vulnerability policy.
- The complaint was acknowledged three weeks ago. You should check whether any regulatory deadline for a final response is approaching or has been missed — the retrieved provisions do not include DISP timescales, so I cannot confirm the deadline from context alone.
- The customer's letter is written in the first person ('I took out…'), suggesting this message may be the customer's own complaint letter rather than a handler's internal query. If so, the customer draft below is addressed to the customer directly.

**Recommended action:** Do not issue a final response without human review. A senior complaints handler or compliance officer should assess: (a) whether the sale met the customer's best interests rule; (b) whether commission disclosure obligations were met; (c) whether redress is appropriate; and (d) whether the DISP final response deadline has been or is about to be reached. In the meantime, a holding response acknowledging the substantive complaint and confirming it is under investigation is appropriate.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us about your home insurance policy and for setting out your concerns so clearly. I am sorry to hear that this situation has caused financial pressure for you, and I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint seriously.

You have raised concerns that when you purchased your home insurance policy through our branch, you were not given a full explanation of the options available to you, and that the policy recommended to you may have been influenced by the level of commission our bank received from the insurer, rather than being the most suitable and cost-effective option for your needs.

Our regulator requires that where we receive a higher level of remuneration — such as a higher commission — from one provider compared to others available to us, we must be able to demonstrate that our recommendation was still made in your best interests. Where commission arrangements conflict with that duty, we are required to take appropriate action. Your complaint raises a genuine question about whether that standard was met in your case, and we take that question seriously.

We are currently investigating the full circumstances of your purchase, including the advice given to you at the branch and the commission arrangements in place at the time. We will write to you again with our findings and our final response as soon as our investigation is complete.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details below.

Yours sincerely,
[Complaints Handler Name]
[Bank Name] Complaints Team

[Note: paragraph 3.7i (box) of CBG] ## CONC 4.5.5 (Guidance) The purpose of CONC 4.5.6R to CONC 4.5.8G is to prohibit credit brokers and lenders to whom they introduce customers wishing to enter into regulated credit agreements to finance the acquisition of motor vehicles from making or relying on arrangements under which credit brokers are given authority to decide or negotiate the prices of those regulated credit agreements on behalf of lenders and the amount of commission the credit brokers earn is affected by those prices. ## CONC 4.5.6 (Rules) A lender or credit broker must not: (1) enter into or have rights or obligations under a discretionary commission arrangement; or (2) seek to exercise, enforce or rely on rights or obligations under a discretionary commission arrangement, including any rights or obligations to receive or tender payment of commission, fee or other financial consideration. ## CONC 4.5.7 (Guidance) The following are examples of discretionary commission arrangements: (1) An agreement under which the lender sets a minimum rate of interest and the commission payable by the lender to the credit broker in respect of a regulated credit agreement entered into by the lender is calculated by reference to the difference between the rate of interest negotiated by the credit broker and payable by the customer under the regulated credit agreement and the minimum rate of interest. These types of arrangements are often referred to as “increasing difference in charges” or “interest rate upward adjustment” arrangements. (2) An agreement under which the lender sets a maximum rate of interest and the commission payable by the lender to the credit broker in respect of a regulated credit agreement entered into by the lender is calculated by reference to the difference between the rate of interest negotiated by the credit broker and payable by the customer under the regulated credit agreement and the maximum rate of interest. These types of arrangements are often referred to as “decreasing difference in charges” or “interest rate downward adjustment” arrangements. (3) An arrangement or agreement under which the commission payable by the lender to the credit broker in respect of a regulated credit agreement entered into by the lender varies (within set parameters) according to the rate of interest negotiated by the credit broker and payable by the customer under the regulated credit agreement.

metadata.section_name: CONC 4.5 Commissions

or (b) if the additional product has no close matched product, apply ICOBS 6B.2.39R; (2) where the additional product is not a policy, the firm must apply ICOBS 6B.2.39R. ## ICOBS 6B.2.39 (Rules) A firm must ensure that it does not systematically discriminate against customers based on their tenure, when determining: (1) an equivalent new business price; fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am 78 years old and have limited mobility due to a hip replacement. In February, my bank switched my longstanding savings account to a new 'Digital Flex Saver' product. This account requires customers to manage everything through a smartphone app and offers no branch or telephone access for transactions. I complained to the bank by phone on Monday morning, and by Wednesday afternoon the adviser called me back saying they'd move me back to my old account type and waive the fees I'd been charged for the failed app transactions. I said that sounded fine and thanked them. However, I never received anything in writing afterwards — no letter, no email, nothing confirming what happened or telling me about the Ombudsman. I'm now worried because I've since realised the new product was completely unsuitable for someone like me who cannot use a smartphone, and I want to understand my rights. I feel the bank shouldn't have moved me onto this product in the first place given my age and circumstances, and I also want to know why I didn't get the written confirmation I believe I'm entitled to after my complaint was resolved.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have come in directly from the customer rather than from a handler — please confirm whether you are the complaints handler or the customer before sending any response. I will treat this as a handler query for now and flag accordingly.

Regulatory position:

1. WRITTEN CONFIRMATION AFTER RESOLUTION: The complaint appears to have been resolved verbally by close of business on the next business day (complaint received Monday morning, resolved Wednesday afternoon — this is outside the 'next business day' window under DISP 1.5 referenced in the internal policy). This means it cannot be treated as an informal next-business-day resolution. It must be treated as a formal complaint that required a written final response. Under the retrieved provisions (DISP 1 as reflected in chunk disp1s6.md), a final response must: (a) accept or reject the complaint with reasons, or offer redress/remedial action; (b) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (c) provide the FOS website address; (d) inform the complainant they may refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (e) indicate whether the bank consents to waive the relevant FOS time limits. None of this was done. The bank is in breach of its final response obligations.

2. SIX-MONTH FOS REFERRAL CLOCK: Under DISP 2.8.2R, the six-month window for the customer to refer to the FOS is only triggered by a valid final response (DISP 2.8.3G confirms this). Because no compliant written final response was issued, the six-month clock has not started running. The customer's right to refer to the FOS is therefore not yet time-barred on that basis, though the six-year/three-year backstop limits in DISP 2.8.2R(2) remain relevant.

3. SUITABILITY / PRODUCT SWITCH CONCERN: The customer raises a substantive concern that the Digital Flex Saver was unsuitable given their age, limited mobility, and inability to use a smartphone. The retrieved provisions do not include the specific conduct-of-business or suitability rules that would govern a savings account product switch of this kind (e.g. BCOBS appropriateness obligations beyond what is retrieved). The BCOBS 4.1 provisions retrieved relate to notice requirements for account changes and fixed-term savings, and provide some support for the obligation to give clear information about changes and options — but they do not fully cover the suitability/vulnerability angle. This gap means `insufficient_context` is set to true for that aspect.

4. VULNERABILITY: The customer is 78, has limited mobility following a hip replacement, and cannot use a smartphone. These are clear indicators of potential vulnerability. This complaint must be reviewed by a senior handler or the Head of Customer Operations before any response is sent.

Recommended actions:
- Issue a compliant written final response immediately (draft below), confirming the remedial action already agreed verbally, enclosing the FOS leaflet, providing the FOS website, and including the appropriate DISP 1 Annex 3R wording on time limits.
- Separately investigate the substantive suitability concern about the product switch — this will require input from the Product team and a review of what information was given to the customer before the switch, including whether adequate notice and options were communicated under BCOBS 4.1.
- Flag this customer as potentially vulnerable in the CMS and ensure all future communications are accessible (paper/telephone, not app-only).
- Do NOT treat the verbal resolution as a summary resolution communication unless you can confirm it meets all the requirements — on the facts as described, it does not.
- Human review is required before sending this letter, given vulnerability indicators, potential redress (fee waiver already agreed but not confirmed in writing), and the unresolved suitability question.

Assumptions to confirm before sending:
- Confirm the exact dates of complaint receipt and verbal resolution to verify whether the next-business-day rule could apply (it appears it cannot, but verify).
- Confirm the fee waiver and account reinstatement have actually been actioned, or are being actioned, so the letter accurately reflects what is happening.
- Confirm whether the bank consents to waive the FOS time limits (required for the Annex 3R wording).
- The suitability/product switch aspect requires further investigation — the customer draft below acknowledges this is being reviewed but does not commit to an outcome on that point.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. We are sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced following the change to your savings account, and we want to make sure we address your concerns properly.

We are writing to confirm the outcome of your complaint, which you raised with us on [date of complaint].

What you told us

You told us that in February your savings account was moved to our new Digital Flex Saver product, which requires customers to manage their account through a smartphone app, with no branch or telephone access for transactions. You explained that this was not suitable for you given your circumstances, and that you were charged fees for failed app transactions as a result. You also told us that when you spoke to one of our advisers on [date of call], they agreed to move you back to your previous account type and waive the fees you had been charged, but that you did not receive anything in writing to confirm this.

What we have done

We have reviewed your complaint and we can confirm that we are arranging for your account to be returned to your previous account type and that the fees charged for the failed app transactions will be waived. We apologise that you did not receive written confirmation of this sooner — you were entitled to receive this, and we are sorry for the inconvenience and uncertainty this has caused.

We are also looking into your concern that the Digital Flex Saver product should not have been applied to your account given your personal circumstances. We take this seriously and want to make sure we have considered it fully. We will write to you separately once we have completed that part of our review.

Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service

If you are dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at [FOS website address]. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, which explains how to do this and the time limits that apply.

[Insert DISP 1 Annex 3R prescribed wording on time limits here — to be confirmed by handler depending on whether the bank consents to waive relevant time limits.]

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us. Our details are set out below.

Yours sincerely,

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the bank to issue a written final response that accepts or rejects the complaint, encloses the FOS explanatory leaflet, provides the FOS website address, informs the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and includes the appropriate Annex 3R wording on time limits — none of which was done following the verbal resolution.

Sets the time limits within which a complainant must refer a complaint to the FOS, including the six-month limit running from the date of a valid final response — relevant because no valid final response has been issued, so the six-month clock has not started.

Confirms that the six-month FOS referral time limit is only triggered by a response that constitutes a valid final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication — supporting the position that the verbal resolution did not start the clock.

The internal policy references the next-business-day rule under DISP 1.5, which permits informal resolution without a full final response only where the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day — the verbal resolution here occurred on Wednesday afternoon for a Monday complaint, placing it outside this window.

Requires firms to provide clear information to banking customers about changes to their accounts and the options available to them, relevant to the customer's concern that they were not adequately informed before being moved to the Digital Flex Saver product.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.1 Purpose, interpretation and application

retrieved_chunks.content: --- ## 7. Procedures ### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination. All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted. ### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must: 1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected. 2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer. 3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale. ### 7.3 Investigation Process The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include: 1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems. 2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made. 3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure, and referring to the DPO, MLRO, or CTO respectively where applicable. 4. Documenting all investigative steps and evidence reviewed in the CMS. Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target. ### 7.4 Final Response Letters Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must: 1.

: (1) shall apply at, or immediately after, the point when contact is first made with an eligible complainant; and (2) where the respondent is a not-for-profit debt advice body: (a) may be met at, or immediately after, the point when contact is first made with an eligible complainant, by making an oral reference to the availability of the information if the respondent does not communicate with the eligible complainant in writing then; and (b) must be met in writing on the first occasion on which the respondent communicates with the eligible complainant in writing. ## DISP 1.2.2A (Rules) If an MCD credit intermediary has, before or at the point of sale, provided an eligible complainant with appropriate information in a durable medium about their internal procedures for the reasonable and prompt handling of complaints pursuant to another rule, the MCD credit intermediary need not refer to the availability of that information again under DISP 1.2.1R(2)(b). ## DISP 1.2.3 (Guidance) The summary details concerning internal complaints handling procedures should cover at least: (1) how the respondent fulfils its obligation to handle and seek to resolve relevant complaints; and (2) (where the complaint falls within the jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service) that, if the complaint is not resolved, the complainant may be entitled to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service. ## DISP 1.2.4 (Guidance) Those summary details may be set out in a leaflet, and their availability may be referred to in contractual documentation. ## DISP 1.2.5 (Guidance) Respondents may also display or reproduce the Financial Ombudsman Service logo (under licence) in: (1) branches and sales offices to which eligible complainants have access; or (2) marketing literature or correspondence directed at eligible complainants; provided it is done in a way which is not misleading. ## DISP 1.2.6 (Guidance) Where respondents are required to provide information in relation to the Financial Ombudsman Service, they may also, where relevant, do so in relation to the Pensions Ombudsman on the same basis as set out in DISP 1.2.1(4)R and DISP 1.2.3G. ## DISP 1.2.7 (Guidance) Where respondents are permitted to display or reproduce the Financial Ombudsman Service

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us. We are sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced following the change to your savings account... What you told us... you explained that this was not suitable for you given your circumstances
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately restates the key facts and concerns raised by the complainant.

verdicts.evidence: We have reviewed your complaint and we can confirm that we are arranging for your account to be returned to your previous account type and that the fees charged for the failed app transactions will be waived.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft indicates remedial action, which implies a decision to uphold the complaint, but it does not explicitly state a decision (e.g., ‘we uphold your complaint’) nor provide detailed reasons linking the decision to each point raised.

verdicts.evidence: If you are dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at [FOS website address]. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet...
verdicts.reasoning: The customer-facing text clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS and provides contact details and a leaflet.
The draft contains a placeholder for DISP 1 Annex 3R wording but does not state the six‑month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We apologise that you did not receive written confirmation of this sooner — you were entitled to receive this, and we are sorry for the inconvenience and uncertainty this has caused. We are also looking into your concern that the Digital Flex Saver product should not have been applied to your account given your personal circumstances.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the complainant’s issues, apologises, offers remedial action and promises further review, using respectful and non‑dismissive language.

verdicts.evidence: We can confirm that we are arranging for your account to be returned to your previous account type and that the fees charged for the failed app transactions will be waived.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft explicitly addresses redress by confirming the remedial actions (account reinstatement and fee waiver) that will be taken.

verdicts.evidence: Flag this customer as potentially vulnerable in the CMS and ensure all future communications are accessible (paper/telephone, not app-only).
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer recognises the customer's age and mobility issues as vulnerability indicators and sets out specific actions (flagging, adapting communication) to address them.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us. We are sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced following the change to your savings account, and we want to make sure we address your concerns properly... You told us that in February your savings account was moved to our new Digital Flex Saver product, which requires customers to manage their account through a smartphone app, with no branch or telephone access for transactions. You explained that this was not suitable for you given your circumstances, and that you were charged fees for failed app transactions as a result. You also told us that when you spoke to one of our advisers on [date of call], they agreed to move you back to your previous account type and waive the fees you had been charged, but that you did not receive anything in writing to confirm this.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with a clear acknowledgement of the complaint and accurately reflects all three substantive elements: the product switch, the unsuitability for the customer's circumstances, the fee charges, the verbal resolution, and the absence of written confirmation. This is specific and tailored, not generic.

verdicts.evidence: We have reviewed your complaint and we can confirm that we are arranging for your account to be returned to your previous account type and that the fees charged for the failed app transactions will be waived. We apologise that you did not receive written confirmation of this sooner — you were entitled to receive this, and we are sorry for the inconvenience and uncertainty this has caused. We are also looking into your concern that the Digital Flex Saver product should not have been applied to your account given your personal circumstances.
verdicts.reasoning: The response states a clear decision on the remedial action (account reinstatement and fee waiver) and gives a reason for the apology (failure to provide written confirmation). However, on the substantive suitability complaint about the product switch itself, the response defers ('We are also looking into...') rather than reaching a decision. The customer is left without a clear determination on whether the bank upholds or rejects the suitability complaint. This is a material gap in the decision-making on a core complaint point.

verdicts.evidence: If you are dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at [FOS website address]. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, which explains how to do this and the time limits that apply.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the right to refer to the FOS, identifies it by name, provides the website address, and references the enclosed explanatory leaflet. The means to contact the FOS are signposted.

The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline running from the date of the final response. The reference to 'time limits that apply' in the FOS paragraph is vague and does not specify the six-month period. The placeholder '[Insert DISP 1 Annex 3R prescribed wording on time limits here — to be confirmed by handler depending on whether the bank consents to waive relevant time limits.]' indicates the required wording has not been drafted into the customer-facing text. The six-month referral limit is not communicated to the customer.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced... We apologise that you did not receive written confirmation of this sooner — you were entitled to receive this, and we are sorry for the inconvenience and uncertainty this has caused. We take this seriously and want to make sure we have considered it fully.
verdicts.reasoning: The response is clear, even-handed, and non-dismissive. It acknowledges the customer's concerns, apologises for the failure to provide written confirmation, validates the customer's entitlement, and commits to taking the suitability concern seriously. The language is respectful and appropriate to the complainant's circumstances. There is no evasion or minimisation.

verdicts.evidence: We have reviewed your complaint and we can confirm that we are arranging for your account to be returned to your previous account type and that the fees charged for the failed app transactions will be waived.
verdicts.reasoning: The response reaches a clear position on redress: it offers two forms of remedial action (account reinstatement and fee waiver) and communicates this to the customer. The basis is implicit (the verbal agreement already made and now being confirmed in writing). The redress is addressed and communicated.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: 'The customer is 78, has limited mobility following a hip replacement, and cannot use a smartphone. These are clear indicators of potential vulnerability. This complaint must be reviewed by a senior handler or the Head of Customer Operations before any response is sent... Flag this customer as potentially vulnerable in the CMS and ensure all future communications are accessible (paper/telephone, not app-only).' Customer draft: 'You explained that this was not suitable for you given your circumstances' and 'We take this seriously and want to make sure we have considered it fully.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer explicitly identifies vulnerability indicators (age 78, limited mobility, inability to use smartphone) and recommends appropriate action: senior review, flagging in CMS, and ensuring accessible communications (paper/telephone). The customer draft acknowledges the customer's circumstances without patronising language and commits to serious consideration. The vulnerability is recognised and acted upon in the recommended handling.

claims.claim: The verbal resolution is outside the 'next business day' window under DISP 1.5, so it cannot be treated as an informal next‑business‑day resolution.
claims.reasoning: The policy document explicitly defines the ‘next business day rule’ under DISP 1.5 and states that only complaints resolved by close of business on the next business day can be treated informally. The facts described place the resolution later than that, matching the provision.

claims.claim: Because it is not an informal next‑business‑day resolution, the complaint must be treated as a formal complaint that requires a written final response.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1 (as captured in disp1s6) sets out that a formal complaint must receive a written final response containing specific elements.

claims.claim: A final response must (a) accept or reject the complaint with reasons or offer redress, (b) enclose a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service’s standard explanatory leaflet, (c) provide the FOS website address, (d) inform the complainant they may refer to the FOS if dissatisfied, and (e) indicate whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant FOS time limits.
claims.reasoning: The listed elements correspond exactly to the requirements set out in disp1s6 (a)–(f).

claims.claim: The bank has not complied with those final‑response requirements and is therefore in breach of its final‑response obligations.
claims.reasoning: If the firm fails to include the mandatory elements described in disp1s6, it is in breach of its final‑response obligations under DISP 1.

claims.claim: The six‑month window for a complainant to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service only starts when a valid final response (or redress determination or summary resolution communication) is sent.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.3 (guidance) states that the six‑month time limit is triggered only by a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication.

claims.claim: Because no compliant written final response was issued, the six‑month clock has not started running.
claims.reasoning: Given the rule in DISP 2.8.3, the absence of a valid final response means the six‑month referral period does not commence.

claims.claim: The six‑year and three‑year back‑stop limits in DISP 2.8.2R(2) still apply.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2R(2) sets out the six‑year limit from the event and the three‑year limit from when the complainant became aware, which remain applicable irrespective of the six‑month clock.

claims.claim: BCOBS 4.1 provisions relate to notice requirements for account changes and fixed‑term savings and provide some support for the obligation to give clear information, but they do not cover the suitability or vulnerability aspects of this product switch.
claims.reasoning: The BCOBS 4.1 text indeed governs notice of changes and information provision, but it does not mention suitability or vulnerability, so the statement that it does not cover those aspects goes beyond the provision.

claims.claim: The customer’s age, limited mobility and inability to use a smartphone are indicators of vulnerability, so the complaint must be reviewed by a senior handler or the Head of Customer Operations before any response is sent.
claims.reasoning: None of the retrieved provisions set a specific requirement that a complaint flagged as vulnerable must be escalated to a senior handler before a response; the claim is not supported by the provided excerpts.

claims.claim: You were entitled to receive written confirmation of the outcome sooner.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1 requires a written final response, giving the customer a right to receive it, but the provision does not prescribe a specific timeframe for ‘sooner’; therefore the entitlement to written confirmation is grounded, the timing element is not.

claims.claim: If you are dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: Disp1s6 (e) explicitly states that the final response must inform the complainant that they may refer the complaint to the FOS if dissatisfied.

claims.claim: We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service’s explanatory leaflet, which explains how to do this and the time limits that apply.
claims.reasoning: Disp1s6 (d) requires the respondent to enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet in the final response.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I opened a cash deposit ISA with your bank in March after seeing your online promotion advertising a 4.5% fixed rate for 12 months. The promotion clearly stated '4.5% AER fixed for 12 months' with no mention of any conditions or restrictions. When I transferred £20,000 from my previous ISA provider, I was placed on a 3.8% variable rate instead. I queried this immediately and was told the 4.5% rate was only available for new money, not ISA transfers — but this was never stated in the promotion I responded to. I wrote a formal complaint letter six weeks ago and received an acknowledgement email, but it didn't tell me anything about timescales for a response or what would happen if I remained unhappy. I've heard nothing since. I'm 78 years old and this ISA represents a significant portion of my retirement savings. The difference in interest rate is costing me approximately £140 over the year, and I'm worried about my finances as I rely on interest income to supplement my state pension.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious concerns that require careful handling and mandatory human review before any response is sent.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Misleading financial promotion (COBS 4.7 / BCOBS 2.4.2):** The customer opened a cash deposit ISA in response to an online promotion. BCOBS 2.4.2G indicates that COBS 4.7.1R applies to financial promotions relating to cash deposit ISAs. The customer's account of the promotion — '4.5% AER fixed for 12 months' with no mention of the restriction to new money only — raises a real concern that the promotion may not have been fair, clear and not misleading. This is a substantive regulatory issue that needs investigation: you should retrieve and review the actual promotion as it appeared at the time.

2. **Acknowledgement deficiency (DISP 1.6.2R / internal policy):** The acknowledgement email the customer received six weeks ago did not inform them of timescales or their right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remained dissatisfied. Under DISP 1.6.2R and Crest Bank's own complaints procedure (section 7.5), FOS referral rights must be communicated. The acknowledgement as described appears non-compliant.

3. **Eight-week deadline:** The complaint was received six weeks ago. Under Crest Bank's internal policy (aligned to DISP 1.6.2R), a final response must be issued within eight weeks of receipt. You have approximately two weeks remaining. If a full investigation cannot be completed in time, a written holding response must be issued immediately — explaining why a final response is not yet possible, indicating when one is expected, and informing the customer of their right to refer to the FOS now, enclosing the FOS standard explanatory leaflet and providing the FOS website address.

4. **Customer vulnerability:** The customer is 78 years old, relies on interest income to supplement their state pension, and the ISA represents a significant portion of their retirement savings. This is a clear vulnerability indicator. The complaint must be handled with appropriate care and prioritisation.

5. **Financial redress:** The customer estimates a loss of approximately £140 in interest over the year. Under Crest Bank's internal policy (section 7.5 / 7.3), any final response involving financial redress of £500 or more requires countersignature by the Head of Customer Operations. The amount here is below £500, but the complaint has potential regulatory significance (misleading promotion, vulnerable customer) and should be assessed accordingly.

**Recommended immediate action:**
- Issue the holding response drafted below without delay, given the acknowledgement gap and the approaching eight-week deadline.
- Retrieve and review the original online promotion to assess whether the 'new money only' restriction was disclosed.
- Escalate to the Head of Customer Operations given the vulnerability indicators and potential regulatory significance.
- Flag for root cause analysis — if the promotion was genuinely silent on the restriction, this may be a systemic issue affecting other customers.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft:**
- Confirm the exact date the complaint was received (to verify the eight-week deadline).
- Confirm whether a final response can realistically be issued within the remaining time. If yes, a final response (not a holding letter) should be issued instead.
- The draft below is a holding response. If you are in a position to issue a final response, do not send this — a final response has different required content under DISP 1.6.2R.
- The FOS six-month referral time limit stated in the customer draft is drawn from Crest Bank's internal policy (section 7.5); confirm this is accurate before sending.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for your formal complaint letter, which we received on [date]. I am sorry that our initial acknowledgement did not give you the information you needed about what would happen next, and I apologise for the lack of communication since then.

I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint seriously. You have raised important concerns about the interest rate applied to your cash deposit ISA and whether the online promotion you responded to accurately described the conditions attached to the advertised rate. We are investigating this carefully.

We aim to send you a full written response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint. We are working to complete our investigation as quickly as possible and will write to you again with our findings and conclusions.

If we are not able to send you a full response within eight weeks of the date we received your complaint, we will write to you again to explain why and to tell you when you can expect to hear from us.

You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service at any time. The Financial Ombudsman Service is a free, independent service for resolving disputes between customers and financial firms. Once you have received our final response, you will have six months from the date of that letter to refer your complaint to them if you remain dissatisfied.

You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at:
Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
Telephone: 0800 023 4567

I have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet for your information.

We understand that your savings are important to you and we will do our best to resolve this matter promptly. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Contact details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the respondent's written response to inform the complainant of their right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service, enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, and provide the FOS website address — obligations that the original acknowledgement appears to have failed to meet and which this holding response now addresses.

Indicates that COBS 4.7.1R applies to financial promotions relating to cash deposit ISAs, making the content and accuracy of the online promotion directly relevant to the regulatory assessment of this complaint.
Sets out the scope of the direct offer financial promotions rules, which apply to cash deposit ISAs and are therefore engaged by the customer's complaint about the online promotion.

cited_provisions.provision: Crest Bank Customer Complaints Handling Procedure — section 7.5
cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the final response to be issued within eight weeks of receipt, to notify the customer of FOS referral rights including the six-month time limit, and to include FOS contact details; also requires a written holding response at eight weeks if a final response cannot be issued.
