retrieved_chunks.content: The final response must: 1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received. 2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons. 3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated. 4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5). 5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer. All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. Crest Bank will cooperate fully with the FOS in respect of any referred complaints, providing all requested documentation within the timeframes set by the FOS. ### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows: 1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure. 2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes. 3.

verdicts.evidence: “Thank you for your formal complaint letter, which we received on [date]… You have raised important concerns about the interest rate applied to your cash deposit ISA and whether the online promotion you responded to accurately described the conditions attached to the advertised rate.”
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by confirming receipt of the complaint and explicitly restates the key issues (interest rate and promotion wording), satisfying the acknowledgement requirement.
The draft is a holding response and does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor provide reasons for a decision.

verdicts.evidence: “You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service at any time… You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at: Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk Telephone: 0800 023 4567”
verdicts.reasoning: The text clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS and provides contact details.

verdicts.evidence: “Once you have received our final response, you will have six months from the date of that letter to refer your complaint to them if you remain dissatisfied.”
verdicts.reasoning: The six‑month deadline from the date of the final response is explicitly stated.

verdicts.evidence: “We aim to send you a full written response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint… If we are not able to send you a full response within eight weeks… we will write to you again to explain why and to tell you when you can expect to hear from us.”
verdicts.reasoning: The eight‑week deadline is communicated, but the draft does not explicitly link the eight‑week point to the complainant’s right to refer to the FOS, which is required for full credit.

verdicts.evidence: “I am sorry that our initial acknowledgement did not give you the information you needed… We are investigating this carefully… We understand that your savings are important to you and we will do our best to resolve this matter promptly.”
verdicts.reasoning: The language is apologetic, acknowledges concerns, promises investigation and timely handling, demonstrating fair and non‑dismissive treatment.
The draft makes no reference to any offer of redress or an explanation why no redress is due, despite the complaint raising a financial loss.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: “Customer vulnerability: the customer is 78 years old, relies on interest income… This is a clear vulnerability indicator. The complaint must be handled with appropriate care and prioritisation.”
verdicts.reasoning: The handler note identifies the vulnerability and recommends appropriate handling; the element allows either‑audience evidence, so the requirement is met.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for your formal complaint letter, which we received on [date]. I am sorry that our initial acknowledgement did not give you the information you needed about what would happen next, and I apologise for the lack of communication since then. I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint seriously. You have raised important concerns about the interest rate applied to your cash deposit ISA and whether the online promotion you responded to accurately described the conditions attached to the advertised rate.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging receipt of the complaint and accurately reflects its subject matter: the interest rate applied to the ISA, the promotion's terms, and whether conditions were properly disclosed. The acknowledgement is specific and demonstrates understanding of what is being complained about.

This is a holding response, not a final response. The customer draft contains no decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) and no reasons engaging with the substantive points raised. The draft states 'We are investigating this carefully' and 'will write to you again with our findings and conclusions' but does not itself provide a decision or substantive reasons. While appropriate for a holding letter, this element cannot be scored as PRESENT because no decision is communicated.

verdicts.evidence: You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service at any time. The Financial Ombudsman Service is a free, independent service for resolving disputes between customers and financial firms. Once you have received our final response, you will have six months from the date of that letter to refer your complaint to them if you remain dissatisfied. You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at: Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk Telephone: 0800 023 4567 I have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet for your information.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the right to refer to the FOS, identifies it by name, explains it is free and independent, and provides multiple means of contact (website, telephone, and enclosed leaflet). The complainant is made aware they can refer this complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: Once you have received our final response, you will have six months from the date of that letter to refer your complaint to them if you remain dissatisfied.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft explicitly states the six-month deadline and anchors it correctly to the date of the firm's final response ('from the date of that letter'). This satisfies the requirement to communicate the six-month referral window.

verdicts.evidence: We aim to send you a full written response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint. We are working to complete our investigation as quickly as possible and will write to you again with our findings and conclusions. If we are not able to send you a full response within eight weeks of the date we received your complaint, we will write to you again to explain why and to tell you when you can expect to hear from us.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft sets out the firm's eight-week timescale for a final response and explains what will happen if that deadline is not met. However, the draft does not explicitly state that the complainant may refer to the FOS once eight weeks have passed without resolution. The eight-week timeline is communicated, but the FOS option available at that point is not clearly linked to the eight-week milestone in the holding letter itself (though FOS referral rights are stated separately). This is a partial gap, but the core eight-week timeline is clearly communicated.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry that our initial acknowledgement did not give you the information you needed about what would happen next, and I apologise for the lack of communication since then. I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint seriously. You have raised important concerns about the interest rate applied to your cash deposit ISA and whether the online promotion you responded to accurately described the conditions attached to the advertised rate. We are investigating this carefully. We understand that your savings are important to you and we will do our best to resolve this matter promptly.
verdicts.reasoning: The response is clear, even-handed, and non-dismissive. It apologises for the acknowledgement deficiency and lack of communication, validates the complaint as raising 'important concerns', commits to careful investigation, and acknowledges the significance of the savings to the complainant. The language is respectful and demonstrates engagement with the substance of the complaint.

The customer draft does not address redress at all. The complaint plainly raises a financial loss (approximately £140 in interest over the year due to the rate differential). The holding letter states an investigation is underway but does not communicate any position on whether redress will be considered, offered, or explained. The handler answer notes the redress position internally but this is not reflected in the customer-facing draft. For a holding response, some acknowledgement that redress will be considered as part of the investigation would be appropriate; its complete absence from the customer draft is a gap.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: 'The customer is 78 years old, relies on interest income to supplement their state pension, and the ISA represents a significant portion of their retirement savings. This is a clear vulnerability indicator. The complaint must be handled with appropriate care and prioritisation.' Customer draft: 'We understand that your savings are important to you and we will do our best to resolve this matter promptly.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer clearly identifies and flags the vulnerability indicators (age, reliance on interest income, retirement savings). The recommended action includes escalation and prioritisation. However, the customer-facing draft does not acknowledge or respond to the vulnerability. The single sentence 'We understand that your savings are important to you' is generic and does not reflect the specific circumstances (age, pension reliance, financial hardship indicators). The vulnerability is recognised in the handler reasoning but not acted upon in the customer communication through adapted, supportive language or signposting of support.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I used a price comparison website called QuickLoans Compare to find a short-term loan of £300 for 30 days. The website showed me results but the cheapest option wasn't listed first — instead, a more expensive lender called FastCash was displayed at the top with a prominent 'Recommended' badge. I later discovered FastCash pays the highest commission to QuickLoans Compare. I clicked through and took out the FastCash loan, paying £90 in interest over 30 days, when I've since found that another lender on the same site would have charged me only £60 for the identical loan amount and duration. I complained to QuickLoans Compare three weeks ago about being misled by their biased ranking. They wrote back saying they weren't responsible for my lending decision and forwarded my complaint to FastCash, the lender, without explaining why or giving me FastCash's contact details. I've now heard nothing from either party. I want the £30 difference refunded and a proper explanation of why my complaint was passed on without my knowledge of where it went.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues, both concerning QuickLoans Compare (the price comparison website operator), not FastCash (the lender). The handler should note the following:

**Issue 1 – Biased ranking influenced by commission (CONC 2.5A.6R area)**
The retrieved provisions include CONC 2.5A.8R (a firm must not display a financial promotion other than a search result in or between search results) and CONC 2.5A.9R (results must be clearly distinguishable from other financial promotions). Critically, the retrieved context at conc2s13 states that the fact a lender pays commission to the firm 'should not affect the ranking or prominence of display of the results of a search' — this is framed as guidance-level language ('should not') in the retrieved chunk, associated with CONC 2.5A.6R. The customer's allegation — that FastCash was ranked first and given a prominent 'Recommended' badge because it pays the highest commission — is directly on point. The 'Recommended' badge displayed prominently alongside a commission-influenced ranking may also engage CONC 2.5A.8R and CONC 2.5A.9R if it constitutes a financial promotion inserted into or indistinguishable from search results. These are strong regulatory hooks for the complaint.

**Issue 2 – Complaint handling: forwarding without explanation**
The retrieved provisions do not include DISP complaint-handling rules on acknowledgement, forwarding, or time limits in sufficient detail to ground specific obligations about how QuickLoans Compare handled the transfer of the complaint to FastCash. The DISP chunks retrieved (disp1s23, dispapp3s1) relate to reporting and PPI — they do not cover the rules on forwarding complaints or providing contact details. This is a gap in the retrieved context.

**Recommended action:**
- The customer's complaint is properly directed at QuickLoans Compare as the operator of the price comparison website. The forwarding of the complaint to FastCash without explanation or contact details is a complaint-handling concern, but the retrieved provisions do not give us the specific DISP rules to ground that point authoritatively.
- The £30 redress claim involves a financial decision and potential compensation — this must be flagged for human review before any commitment is made.
- Set `insufficient_context` to true in part: the CONC provisions on ranking/commission are well-supported, but the DISP complaint-handling provisions on forwarding and time limits are not in the retrieved context.
- Human review is required before sending: the complaint involves a potential redress figure (£30), and the complaint-handling procedural points cannot be fully grounded from the retrieved provisions alone.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending:**
1. Confirm that QuickLoans Compare is the regulated firm being complained about (not FastCash).
2. Confirm whether the bank is acting as QuickLoans Compare's complaints handler or is advising the customer — the draft below is written as if the bank is handling the complaint on behalf of or in relation to QuickLoans Compare, but if the bank has no connection to QuickLoans Compare, this letter should instead advise the customer of their options.
3. The £30 redress figure is not committed to in the draft — human review must decide whether to uphold and offer redress.
4. The draft does not include FOS referral rights or time limits because the retrieved provisions do not supply the prescribed wording or applicable time limits for this complaint type — a human reviewer must add these before sending.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for contacting us about your experience with QuickLoans Compare. We are sorry to hear about the concerns you have raised, and we want to make sure they are properly addressed.

You have told us that when you used QuickLoans Compare to search for a short-term loan, the results were not displayed in order of cost, and that a lender called FastCash appeared at the top of the results with a prominent 'Recommended' badge. You have also told us that you later discovered FastCash pays the highest commission to QuickLoans Compare, and that a cheaper option was available on the same site for the same loan.

FCA rules that apply to price comparison websites require that commission payments from lenders must not affect the ranking or prominence of how search results are displayed to customers. There are also rules requiring that any financial promotion must be clearly distinguishable from the results of a search, and that no promotional material may be inserted into or between search results. Your complaint raises serious questions about whether these requirements were met.

You have also told us that your complaint was forwarded to FastCash without explanation and without you being given FastCash's contact details. We understand how frustrating it is to have your complaint passed on without being told where it has gone or why, and we take that concern seriously.

We are currently reviewing your complaint in full. We will write to you again with our findings and our response to the specific outcome you are seeking. We are not in a position to confirm any financial remedy at this stage, but we want to assure you that your complaint is being taken seriously and investigated properly.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Firm name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Prohibits a firm from displaying a financial promotion other than a search result in or between the results of a search — relevant to whether the 'Recommended' badge constitutes a promotional insertion into search results.

Requires that search results are clearly distinguishable from any other financial promotion — relevant to whether the prominent 'Recommended' badge blurred the line between a search result and a promotion.

The retrieved chunk states that commission payments to the firm 'should not affect the ranking or prominence of display of the results of a search' — directly relevant to the customer's allegation that FastCash's top ranking was driven by its commission payments.

and (2) determine the appropriate redress (if any) to offer to a complainant. ## DISP App 3.1.5 (Guidance) In this appendix: (1) (a) at step 1, “historic interest” means the interest the complainant paid to the firm because a payment protection contract was added to a loan or credit product; (b) at step 2, “historic interest” means in relation to any sum, the interest the complainant paid as a result of that sum being included in the loan or credit product; (2) 'simple interest' means a non-compound rate of 8% per annum; (3) 'claim' means a claim by a complainant seeking to rely upon the policy under the payment protection contract that is the subject of the complaint; (4) “actual profit share” means a reasonable estimate of the profit share that was paid under profit share arrangements and that is notionally attributable to the payment protection contract; (5) “anticipated profit share” means a reasonable estimate of the profit share which it was reasonably foreseeable at the time of sale would be paid over the relevant period or periods under profit share arrangements, and that would be notionally attributable to the payment protection contract; (6) “commission” means the part of the total amount paid in relation to a payment protection contract that was not due to be passed to and retained by the insurer, excluding any sums which may be payable under profit share arrangements; (7) “failure to disclose commission’ means failure to make the disclosure at DISP App 3.3A.2E; (8) “profit share arrangements” means arrangements (including contractual) that firms have to potentially receive back some of the total amount paid in relation to a payment protection contract which had initially gone to the insurer. For example, these arrangements might include amounts paid to cover potential claims on policies, but which remain unspent after a fixed period, for example because actual claims did not exceed certain levels.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for contacting us about your experience with QuickLoans Compare. We are sorry to hear about the concerns you have raised...
verdicts.reasoning: The opening paragraph explicitly thanks the complainant and restates the key issues (ranking, recommended badge, commission), showing accurate understanding of the complaint.
The draft states the complaint is under review and does not provide a firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor reasons for such a decision.
There is no mention of the complainant’s right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service.
The response does not state the six‑month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS from the date of the final response.

verdicts.evidence: We understand how frustrating it is... we take that concern seriously. We are currently reviewing your complaint in full... we want to assure you that your complaint is being taken seriously and investigated properly.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is courteous, acknowledges the complainant’s concerns, and indicates an impartial review without dismissing any points.

verdicts.evidence: We are not in a position to confirm any financial remedy at this stage...
verdicts.reasoning: The draft acknowledges that redress is a question but does not provide a definitive position or offer; it merely defers the decision, which meets the partial criteria.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for contacting us about your experience with QuickLoans Compare. We are sorry to hear about the concerns you have raised... You have told us that when you used QuickLoans Compare to search for a short-term loan, the results were not displayed in order of cost, and that a lender called FastCash appeared at the top of the results with a prominent 'Recommended' badge.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with a clear acknowledgement of receipt and accurately reflects both substantive elements of the complaint: (1) the biased ranking influenced by commission, and (2) the forwarding of the complaint without explanation. The response demonstrates understanding of what is being complained about.

verdicts.evidence: We are currently reviewing your complaint in full. We will write to you again with our findings and our response to the specific outcome you are seeking.
verdicts.reasoning: This is a final response stage, but no decision is stated. The draft defers the decision entirely ('We will write to you again with our findings'), which is inconsistent with a final response. A final response must contain a clear decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) with reasons engaging the specific points. The draft provides regulatory context but no decision on whether the complaint is upheld or rejected, nor does it address the specific redress claim (£30 refund).

The customer draft contains no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service or the right to refer the complaint to the FOS. The handler answer explicitly notes that 'The draft does not include FOS referral rights or time limits because the retrieved provisions do not supply the prescribed wording or applicable time limits for this complaint type — a human reviewer must add these before sending.' This is a critical omission in a final response.

No six-month referral deadline is stated in the customer draft. The handler answer confirms this gap: FOS referral rights and time limits are not included. This element requires explicit statement of the six-month period running from the date of the final response, which is absent.

verdicts.evidence: We understand how frustrating it is to have your complaint passed on without being told where it has gone or why, and we take that concern seriously. FCA rules that apply to price comparison websites require that commission payments from lenders must not affect the ranking or prominence of how search results are displayed to customers.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages substantively with the complaint and uses empathetic language ('We are sorry', 'We understand how frustrating'). However, it is treated superficially in critical respects: the response does not reach a decision on the merits, does not explain why the complaint was forwarded to FastCash, and does not address the specific redress claim. The draft reads as a holding response rather than a final one, which undermines fair treatment by leaving the complainant without resolution despite this being marked as a final response stage.

verdicts.evidence: We are not in a position to confirm any financial remedy at this stage, but we want to assure you that your complaint is being taken seriously and investigated properly.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft acknowledges the question of redress ('We are not in a position to confirm any financial remedy at this stage') but reaches no clear position on whether the £30 difference should be refunded. The handler answer notes that 'The £30 redress figure is not committed to in the draft — human review must decide whether to uphold and offer redress.' This is a partial engagement: the issue is flagged but no reasoned position is communicated to the customer. A final response must either offer redress with its basis or explain why none is warranted.

claims.claim: The fact that a lender or credit broker pays a commission to the firm (and the amount of any such commission or payment) should not affect the ranking or prominence of display of the results of a search.

claims.claim: The 'Recommended' badge displayed prominently alongside a commission‑influenced ranking may also engage CONC 2.5A.8R and CONC 2.5A.9R if it constitutes a financial promotion inserted into or indistinguishable from search results.
claims.reasoning: The provision contains CONC 2.5A.8 (must not display a financial promotion in or between results) and CONC 2.5A.9 (results must be clearly distinguishable), so the rules exist, but the provision does not specifically state that a 'Recommended' badge would breach them; the claim extends beyond the text.

claims.claim: FCA rules that apply to price comparison websites require that commission payments from lenders must not affect the ranking or prominence of how search results are displayed to customers.
claims.reasoning: The same paragraph in CONC 2.5A.6R (conc2s13) explicitly imposes this requirement.

claims.claim: There are also rules requiring that any financial promotion must be clearly distinguishable from the results of a search, and that no promotional material may be inserted into or between search results.
claims.reasoning: CONC 2.5A.8 states a firm must not display a financial promotion in or between results, and CONC 2.5A.9 requires results to be clearly distinguishable; both are present in the retrieved chunk.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the final response letter I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about being mis-sold a packaged bank account. In your letter, you stated that you did not uphold my complaint because you believe I was given adequate information about the account benefits at the time of sale. However, I was never told about the eligibility criteria for the travel insurance included in the package — I am 78 years old and have a pre-existing heart condition, which means I have never been able to claim on the policy despite paying £15 per month for it since 2016. Your response did not address this point at all, and I do not believe you investigated my complaint properly. You also failed to mention anything about my right to take this further if I remain unhappy. I want to know what my options are now, and I believe I am owed a full refund of all the fees I have paid.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to be the customer's own complaint letter (or a summary of it), not a handler question — I have treated it as a complaint requiring a response draft. Please confirm this is correct before sending.

Regulatory position:

1. FAILURE TO INCLUDE FOS REFERRAL RIGHTS IN FINAL RESPONSE: The retrieved provisions (DISP 1.6.2R as referenced in the Crest Bank policy, and the DISP 1.6 chunk) make clear that a final response must inform the complainant of their right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service, enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, and provide the FOS website address. The customer states the 15 November letter contained none of this. If accurate, this is a breach of the firm's obligations under DISP 1.6.2R. You must urgently check the original letter. If the FOS referral wording was indeed omitted, a remedying letter must be sent immediately.

2. PACKAGED BANK ACCOUNT — ANNUAL ELIGIBILITY STATEMENT: ICOBS 5.1.3CR requires firms to provide customers with an annual statement setting out qualifying requirements to claim each benefit under policies included in a packaged bank account, and to recommend the customer reviews whether they meet those requirements. ICOBS 5.1.3CR(2) specifically requires that where a customer has reached an age limit on claiming benefits under a travel insurance policy included in a packaged bank account (or will reach one before the next annual statement is due), the firm must state this clearly and prominently in the statement and on an annual basis thereafter. The customer is 78 and has a pre-existing heart condition. You must check: (a) whether annual eligibility statements were sent; (b) whether any age limit or health exclusion applicable to the travel insurance was clearly and prominently flagged. If these statements were not sent, or did not flag the age/health restrictions, there is a potential breach of ICOBS 5.1.3CR.

3. SUBSTANCE OF THE COMPLAINT — ADEQUACY OF INVESTIGATION: The customer alleges the final response did not address the eligibility criteria point at all. ICOBS 5.1.4G indicates that firms should ensure customers are asked clear questions about material matters and are informed of the consequences of failing to disclose relevant information. While this is guidance rather than a binding rule, the failure to address the eligibility criteria point in the final response is a substantive concern that warrants re-investigation.

4. FINANCIAL REDRESS CLAIM: The customer is claiming a full refund of fees paid since 2016 (approximately £15/month). This involves potential financial redress and must be assessed by a qualified handler. Under the Crest Bank policy, any final response involving financial redress of £500 or more must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. Given the period claimed (since 2016), the amount would significantly exceed £500.

5. VULNERABILITY: The customer is 78 years old and has a pre-existing heart condition. This is a strong indicator of potential vulnerability and must be considered in how the complaint is handled and communicated.

Recommended action:
- Immediately check the 15 November final response letter to confirm whether FOS referral rights were included. If not, a remedying letter is required as a matter of urgency.
- Re-open the complaint investigation to address the eligibility criteria and annual statement obligations under ICOBS 5.1.3CR.
- Escalate to the Head of Customer Operations given the financial redress claim and vulnerability indicators.
- Do not commit to any specific redress in the customer letter at this stage — the draft below acknowledges the complaint, confirms re-investigation, and provides FOS referral rights.

ASSUMPTIONS TO CONFIRM BEFORE SENDING:
- That the 15 November letter did not include FOS referral wording (if it did, the draft below should be adjusted).
- That this new letter will serve as the remedying response providing FOS referral rights — if a final response has already been issued and the six-month FOS referral clock has started, the customer's position at the FOS may be affected and legal/compliance input may be needed.
- The customer's identity and vulnerability status should be noted on the CMS.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied following our letter of 15 November, and I want to assure you that we take your concerns very seriously.

You have raised two important points that we should have addressed more fully, and I apologise that our previous response fell short.

First, you have told us that you were never made aware of the eligibility criteria for the travel insurance included in your packaged bank account — in particular, restrictions relating to age and pre-existing health conditions. We are required to provide you with an annual statement that sets out the qualifying requirements for each benefit under your account, and where a travel insurance policy has an age limit that affects your ability to claim, we are required to state this clearly and prominently. We are re-opening our investigation to look carefully at whether we met these obligations throughout the time you have held the account. We will write to you again with our findings.

Second, our letter of 15 November should have told you about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) if you remained dissatisfied. I apologise that this information was not included. I am setting it out now:

If you are not satisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at:

Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
Telephone: 0800 023 4567

I have enclosed a copy of the FOS's explanatory leaflet, which explains how the service works and the time limits that apply to referring a complaint.

We will complete our re-investigation and write to you with a full response as soon as possible. In the meantime, if you have any questions or would like to discuss your complaint, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires firms to provide packaged bank account customers with an annual statement setting out qualifying requirements to claim each benefit under included policies.

Requires firms to state clearly and prominently in the annual statement where a customer has reached an age limit on claiming benefits under a travel insurance policy included in a packaged bank account.
Indicates that firms should ensure customers are informed of eligibility and disclosure requirements relevant to their policy, including the consequences of failing to meet them.
Requires the final response to inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS, enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, and provide the FOS website address.

Crest Bank's internal policy confirms the obligation under DISP 1.6.2R to include FOS referral rights in every final response letter and to provide FOS contact details and the six-month referral time limit.

(2) Where a customer has reached an age limit on claiming benefits under a travel insurance policy included in a packaged bank account (or will reach an age limit before the next annual statement is due), a firm must state this clearly and prominently in the statement and on an annual basis thereafter. (3) The statement (provided under ICOBS 5.1.3C R (1)) must not: (a) include any information other than that provided in accordance with this rule, ICOBS 6.1.7-AG(2), ICOBS 6A.4.5R(1) and ICOBS 6A.4.7G ; or (b) form part of another document provided to the customer by the firm; or (c) be included in the same mailing as any other document provided to the customer by the firm. ## ICOBS 5.1.4 (Guidance) A firm should bear in mind the restriction on rejecting claims (ICOBS 8.1.1R (3)). Ways of ensuring a customer knows what he must disclose include: (1) explaining to a commercial customer the duty to disclose all circumstances material to a policy, what needs to be disclosed, and the consequences of any failure to make such a disclosure; (2) ensuring that the commercial customer is asked clear questions about any matter material to the insurance undertaking; (3) explaining to the customer the responsibility of consumers to take reasonable care not to make a misrepresentation and the possible consequences if a consumer is careless in answering the insurer's questions, or if a consumer recklessly or deliberately makes a misrepresentation; and (4) asking the customer clear and specific questions about the information relevant to the policy being arranged or varied.

retrieved_chunks.content: The final response must: 1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received. 2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons. 3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated. 4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5). 5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer. All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. Crest Bank will cooperate fully with the FOS in respect of any referred complaints, providing all requested documentation within the timeframes set by the FOS. ### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows: 1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure. 2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes. 3.

a) accepts the complaint and, where appropriate, offers redress or remedial action; or (b) offers redress or remedial action without accepting the complaint; or (c) rejects the complaint and gives reasons for doing so; and which: (d) encloses a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet; (da) provides the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service; (e) informs the complainant that if he remains dissatisfied with the respondent's response, he may now refer his complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service; and (f) indicates whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R (Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?) by including the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3R; or [Note: respondents are reminded of their obligations under regulation 19 of the ADR Regulations, which requires respondents to provide equivalent messaging in respect of the time limit in DISP 2.8.9R (Payment protection insurance complaints)] (2) a written response which: (a) explains why it is not in a position to make a final response and indicates when it expects to be able to provide one; (b) informs the complainant that he may now refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service; (ba) indicates whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R (Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?) if it becomes apparent that the complaint has been made or is referred outside those time limits; (c) encloses a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service standard explanatory leaflet; and (d) provides the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service.

Unsuitable sale identified on the grounds of affordability and complaint raised on 12th anniversary. It has always been the intention of the complainant to retire at state retirement age 65. Established facts Surrender value (on the 25 year policy) at time complaint assessed: £12,500 Capital repaid under repayment mortgage of term to retirement date (20 years): £21,000 Surrender value less capital repaid: (£8.500) Difference in outgoings (repayment - endowment): £5,400 Cost of converting from endowment mortgage to repayment mortgage: £200 Basis of compensation: The surrender value of the (25 year term) endowment policy is compared to the capital that would have been repaid to date under a repayment mortgage arranged to repay the loan at retirement age, in this example, a repayment mortgage for a term of 20 years. The complainant has gained from lower outgoings of the endowment mortgage to date. In calculating the redress, the gain may be offset against the loss unless the complainant's particular circumstances are such that it would be unreasonable to take account of the gain. The conversion costs are also taken into account in calculating the redress.

The draft does not state a firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) nor provide a reasoned conclusion; it only says the investigation will be re‑opened.

verdicts.evidence: If you are not satisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at: Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk Telephone: 0800 023 4567

verdicts.reasoning: The draft mentions that time limits exist but does not specify the six‑month period from the date of this final response, so it only partially satisfies the requirement.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied... we take your concerns very seriously... we are re‑opening our investigation... we will write to you again with our findings.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is courteous, acknowledges the issues, promises a fresh investigation and does not dismiss or minimise the complainant's concerns.
The draft does not address the complainant’s request for a full refund or any other form of redress, nor does it explain why redress would or would not be offered.

verdicts.evidence: VULNERABILITY: The customer is 78 years old and has a pre‑existing heart condition. This is a strong indicator of potential vulnerability and must be considered...
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer recognises the customer's age and health condition as vulnerability indicators, satisfying the element which can be met in either the handler or customer text.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied following our letter of 15 November, and I want to assure you that we take your concerns very seriously. You have raised two important points that we should have addressed more fully, and I apologise that our previous response fell short.
verdicts.reasoning: The response opens by acknowledging receipt of the complaint and accurately reflects its subject matter: dissatisfaction with the previous final response, the failure to address eligibility criteria for travel insurance, and the omission of FOS referral rights. The acknowledgement is specific and demonstrates understanding of what is being complained about.

verdicts.evidence: We are re-opening our investigation to look carefully at whether we met these obligations throughout the time you have held the account. We will write to you again with our findings.
verdicts.reasoning: The response does not state a clear decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). Instead, it announces a re-investigation and defers the decision to a future letter. While it engages with the substantive points (eligibility criteria and annual statements), it does not reach or communicate a position on the complaint itself. This is a holding response masquerading as a final response, which is inappropriate given the response stage is marked 'final'.

verdicts.evidence: If you are not satisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), free of charge. You can contact them at: Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk Telephone: 0800 023 4567. I have enclosed a copy of the FOS's explanatory leaflet, which explains how the service works and the time limits that apply to referring a complaint.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer-facing text clearly states the right to refer to the FOS, provides the website and telephone contact details, and references the enclosed explanatory leaflet. This satisfies the requirement to inform the complainant of their right to refer and how to do so.

The response mentions 'time limits that apply to referring a complaint' in the context of the FOS leaflet, but does not explicitly state in the customer-facing text that the complainant has six months from the date of this response to refer the complaint to the FOS. The reference to the leaflet is not a substitute for stating the deadline clearly in the response itself.

verdicts.evidence: You have raised two important points that we should have addressed more fully, and I apologise that our previous response fell short. First, you have told us that you were never made aware of the eligibility criteria for the travel insurance included in your packaged bank account — in particular, restrictions relating to age and pre-existing health conditions. We are required to provide you with an annual statement that sets out the qualifying requirements for each benefit under your account, and where a travel insurance policy has an age limit that affects your ability to claim, we are required to state this clearly and prominently.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages substantively with the complaint, acknowledges the firm's regulatory obligations, apologises for the shortfall in the previous response, and commits to re-investigation. The language is clear, non-dismissive, and demonstrates that the specific points raised (eligibility criteria, age and health restrictions) have been understood and taken seriously. The tone is respectful and appropriate to the complainant's circumstances.

The response does not address the complainant's claim for a full refund of fees paid since 2016. While the handler answer notes that redress must be escalated and assessed, the customer draft contains no position on whether redress is warranted, what amount might be offered, or why none is due. The response defers the entire matter to a future investigation letter without communicating any position on the financial claim to the customer.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: 'The customer is 78 years old and has a pre-existing heart condition. This is a strong indicator of potential vulnerability and must be considered in how the complaint is handled and communicated.' and 'The customer's identity and vulnerability status should be noted on the CMS.' Customer draft: 'I am sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied' and 'I apologise that this information was not included' — tone is respectful and supportive.
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer explicitly identifies vulnerability indicators (age 78, pre-existing heart condition) and recommends appropriate action (noting on CMS, escalation to Head of Customer Operations, adapted communication). The customer draft uses supportive, apologetic language and avoids jargon. While the customer draft does not explicitly reference the vulnerability, the handler answer demonstrates recognition and appropriate action, and the customer-facing tone reflects this consideration. This satisfies the 'either' audience criterion.

claims.claim: ICOBS 5.1.3CR requires firms to provide customers with an annual statement setting out qualifying requirements to claim each benefit under policies included in a packaged bank account, and to recommend the customer reviews whether they meet those requirements.

claims.reasoning: The guidance text in ICOBS 5.1.4 (shown in the provision) contains this statement.

claims.reasoning: The requirement is an internal policy; no FCA provision in the retrieved set mandates this threshold or countersignature.

claims.claim: The customer is 78 years old and has a pre‑existing heart condition; this is a strong indicator of potential vulnerability and must be considered in how the complaint is handled and communicated.
claims.reasoning: While FCA guidance on vulnerability exists, none of the retrieved provisions specifically state this requirement; therefore the claim is not supported by the provided chunks.

claims.claim: If you are not satisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R (and the synthetic policy) require the firm to inform the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS.

claims.claim: The final response must include the Financial Ombudsman Service website address and telephone number.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R specifies that contact details (website and telephone) must be provided.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the final response letter I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about being mis-sold a payment protection insurance policy alongside my personal loan in 2012. Your letter states that you do not uphold my complaint because you believe adequate information was provided at the point of sale. However, I note that your response fails to address the key issue I raised — that I was self-employed at the time and was never told the policy would not cover me for unemployment. You have not explained what evidence you considered or how you reached your decision. I was told verbally by the adviser that the PPI would cover all eventualities. I have now been left £3,200 out of pocket in premiums for a policy that could never have paid out on the unemployment element. I do not accept your decision and wish to know what my options are. I also want to understand whether I can take this further, as your final response letter did not mention anything about the Financial Ombudsman Service or any time limits for escalation.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This is a customer escalation following receipt of a final response letter dated 15 November. The customer is raising two distinct issues:

1. **Substantive complaint**: The customer was self-employed at the time of the PPI sale in 2012 and alleges they were never told the policy would not cover them for unemployment. They also allege the adviser verbally stated the policy would 'cover all eventualities'. This goes directly to the mis-sale assessment criteria under DISP App 3 — specifically whether the firm took reasonable steps to establish the customer's circumstances and whether misleading or inaccurate information was provided about the policy. The original final response does not appear to have addressed this specific ground.

2. **Procedural deficiency — missing FOS information**: The customer explicitly states the final response letter of 15 November did not mention the Financial Ombudsman Service or any time limits for escalation. Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in the retrieved provisions), a final response must: enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; provide the FOS website address; inform the complainant they may refer the complaint to the FOS if dissatisfied; and indicate whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R. If the 15 November letter omitted all of this, it was not a compliant final response. This is a significant procedural failing that must be escalated and corrected urgently.

**Recommended actions:**
(a) Retrieve and review the 15 November final response letter to confirm whether FOS referral information was included. If it was omitted, a remedying letter must be issued immediately containing the required FOS information.
(b) Review the underlying complaint file to assess whether the self-employment/unemployment exclusion point and the adviser's alleged verbal representations were considered in the original investigation. If not, the complaint may need to be re-investigated.
(c) Note that the PPI sale was in 2012 and the complaint appears to have been considered and a final response issued — you will need to check whether the DISP 2.8.9R deadline (complaint referred on or before 29 August 2019) was met, as this affects FOS jurisdiction. The customer's complaint appears to have been referred to the bank, so check your records for when it was first received.
(d) Given the potential financial redress of £3,200 and the procedural failing in the final response, this must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before any further response is sent (per Crest Bank's internal policy for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more).

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft below:**
- That the 15 November letter genuinely omitted FOS referral information (the draft below proceeds on that assumption — if FOS information was included, the draft will need revision).
- Whether the complaint was originally received by the bank on or before 29 August 2019 (relevant to FOS jurisdiction under DISP 2.8.9R).
- Whether the self-employment/unemployment exclusion point was addressed in the investigation file.

**Human review is required** before this draft is sent — see flags above.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for your letter responding to our final response dated 15 November. We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied, and we take your concerns seriously.

We want to address two matters you have raised.

**Your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

We recognise that our final response letter of 15 November did not include information about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS). We apologise for this omission. You have the right to refer your complaint to the FOS, free of charge, if you remain dissatisfied with our response. The FOS is an independent service that can review complaints about financial firms. You can contact them at:

Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
Telephone: 0800 023 4567

We have enclosed a copy of the FOS's standard explanatory leaflet, which sets out further details about the service and the time limits that apply to referring a complaint. Please read this carefully, as time limits do apply.

[HANDLER NOTE: Insert here the appropriate wording regarding whether Crest Bank consents or does not consent to waive the relevant FOS time limits, as required. This wording must be taken from DISP 1 Annex 3R and confirmed before this letter is sent.]

**The substance of your complaint**

You have told us that at the time the PPI policy was sold to you in 2012, you were self-employed, and that you were not informed that the policy would not cover you for unemployment in those circumstances. You have also told us that the adviser verbally represented that the policy would cover all eventualities. You feel that these specific points were not addressed in our final response.

We take these concerns seriously. We are reviewing your complaint file to ensure that these specific points have been properly considered as part of our investigation. We will write to you again once we have completed that review.

If you have any documents or information that you believe are relevant — for example, any notes you made at the time of the sale, or any other records — please do send these to us, as they may assist our review.

We are sorry for the distress and inconvenience this matter has caused you. We will be in touch as soon as possible.

Yours sincerely,

[Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a final response to enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied, and indicate whether the respondent consents to waive relevant time limits — all of which the customer states were absent from the 15 November letter.

Sets out the deadline conditions for FOS jurisdiction over PPI sale complaints — the sale was in 2012 and the complaint must have been referred on or before 29 August 2019 for the FOS to be able to consider it, unless an exception applies.

Relevant to the substantive mis-sale assessment: includes as a relevant factor whether the firm provided misleading or inaccurate information about the policy, and whether the complainant's circumstances (such as self-employment) were properly considered.

Indicates that relevant evidence includes the complainant's demands, needs and intentions at the time of the sale and any other relevant evidence, including testimony about reasons for purchasing — relevant to assessing the self-employment and verbal representation points.

Sets out the scope of DISP Appendix 3, confirming it applies to complaints about the sale of PPI expressing dissatisfaction about the sale, including where claims have been rejected on grounds of ineligibility or exclusion — applicable here.

retrieved_chunks.content: The final response must: 1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received. 2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons. 3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated. 4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5). 5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer. All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. Crest Bank will cooperate fully with the FOS in respect of any referred complaints, providing all requested documentation within the timeframes set by the FOS. ### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows: 1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure. 2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes. 3.

or (b) it was not considered under step 2 of DISP Appendix 3 because the complaint was treated as a purported complaint that did not come under the jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service; or (2) a purported complaint to the selling firm that would otherwise have fallen to be considered under step 1 of DISP Appendix 3 but was rejected before 30 January 2019 by that firm on the basis that it did not come under the jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service. ## DISP App 3.11.5 (Rules) The firm that rejected the complaint or purported complaint (or, where applicable, its successor) must as soon as reasonably practicable, and no later than 29 April 2019, send a written communication to the complainant which: (1) in a case falling within DISP App 3.11.4R(1), informs the complainant they can make a complaint against the CCA lender in relation

retrieved_chunks.content: It should not, for example, only use the approach for those complainants it views as being a lower underwriting risk or those complainants who have cancelled their policies. ## DISP App 3.7.9 (Evidential Provisions) Where the firm presumes that the complainant would have purchased a regular premium payment protection contract, the firm should offer redress that puts the complainant in the position he would have been if he had bought an alternative regular premium payment protection contract. ## DISP App 3.7.10 (Evidential Provisions) The firm should pay to the complainant a sum equal to the amount in DISP App 3.7.3 E less the amount the complainant would have paid for the alternative regular premium payment protection contract. ## DISP App 3.7.11 (Evidential Provisions) The firm should consider whether it is appropriate to deduct the value of any paid claims from the redress. ## DISP App 3.7.12 (Evidential Provisions) Additionally, where a single premium was added to a loan, DISP App 3.7.4 E applies except that in respect of DISP App 3.7.4 E (1)(a) the cancellation value should only be used if the complainant expressly wishes to cancel the policy. ## DISP App 3.7.13 (Evidential Provisions) The firm should, for the purposes of redressing the complaint, use the value of £9 per £100 of benefits payable as the monthly price of the alternative regular premium payment protection contract. For example, if the monthly repayment amount in relation to the loan only is to be £200, the price of the alternative regular premium payment protection contract will be £18. ## DISP App 3.7.14 (Evidential Provisions) Where the firm presumes that the complainant would have purchased a regular premium payment protection contract and if the complainant expressly wishes it, the existing cover should continue until the end of the existing policy term. The complainant should pay the price of the alternative regular premium payment protection contract (at DISP App 3.7.13 E) and should be able to cancel at any time.

retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP App 3.1 Introduction ## DISP App 3.1.1 (Guidance) (1) This appendix sets out how: (a) a firm should handle complaints relating to the sale of a payment protection contract by the firm which express dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling); and (b) a firm that is a CCA lender and which has received such a complaint should consider whether there was a failure to disclose commission in relation to the sale of a payment protection contract which covers or covered or purported to cover a credit agreement (this includes partial coverage). (2) It relates to the sale of any payment protection contract whenever the sale took place and irrespective of whether it was on an advised or non-advised basis; conducted through any sales channel; in connection with any type of loan or credit product, or none; whether the insurer was in the same group as the firm or not; whether the premium was financed by the credit product or not; and for a regular premium or single premium payment. It applies whether the policy is currently in force, was cancelled during the policy term or ran its full term. (3) It does not set out how a firm which has received a complaint referred to in (1)(a) should assess: (a) whether the firm’s conduct of the sale was in breach of a fiduciary duty where there has been a failure to disclose either the existence of, or the level of, any commission and/or profit share paid; (b) whether any omission (other than the omission referred to in DISP App 3.3A.2E) to disclose either the existence of, or level of, commission and/or profit share made the relationship unfair under section 140A of the CCA; (c) any other issue not dealt with in step 1 or step 2 set out in this appendix.

The draft does not state any firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) nor provide reasons for a decision; it only says the file will be reviewed.

verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing text clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service and provides contact details.

verdicts.evidence: We have enclosed a copy of the FOS's standard explanatory leaflet, which sets out further details about the service and the time limits that apply to referring a complaint.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft mentions that time limits apply but does not specify the required six‑month period from the date of the final response.

verdicts.evidence: We take these concerns seriously. We are reviewing your complaint file... We are sorry for the distress and inconvenience this matter has caused you.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is respectful, acknowledges the issues, promises a review, and does not dismiss or minimise the complainant's concerns.
The draft does not address whether any financial redress will be offered or explain why none is due, despite the complaint involving a £3,200 loss.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for your letter responding to our final response dated 15 November. We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied, and we take your concerns seriously.
verdicts.reasoning: The response opens by acknowledging receipt of the customer's escalation letter and accurately reflects that the customer is dissatisfied with the previous final response. It identifies the two distinct matters raised (FOS information omission and substantive complaint points about self-employment/unemployment exclusion).

verdicts.evidence: We are reviewing your complaint file to ensure that these specific points have been properly considered as part of our investigation. We will write to you again once we have completed that review.
verdicts.reasoning: The response does not state a clear decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). Instead, it defers the substantive decision by announcing a review and promising a future letter. While it acknowledges the specific points raised (self-employment, unemployment exclusion, verbal representations), it does not engage with them substantively or reach a position. This is a holding response masquerading as a final response, which fails to meet the requirement for a clear decision with reasons.

verdicts.evidence: You have the right to refer your complaint to the FOS, free of charge, if you remain dissatisfied with our response. The FOS is an independent service that can review complaints about financial firms. You can contact them at: Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk Telephone: 0800 023 4567. We have enclosed a copy of the FOS's standard explanatory leaflet.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer-facing text clearly states the right to refer to the FOS and provides multiple means of contact (website, telephone, and enclosed leaflet). The language is clear and unambiguous that the complainant may refer this complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: Please read this carefully, as time limits do apply.
verdicts.reasoning: The response mentions that 'time limits do apply' and directs the customer to the FOS leaflet for details, but does not explicitly state the six-month deadline running from the date of this response. The reference to time limits is vague and delegates the information to the enclosed leaflet rather than stating it clearly in the letter itself. This is insufficient to meet the PRESENT standard, which requires the customer-facing text to state the six-month deadline.

verdicts.evidence: You have told us that at the time the PPI policy was sold to you in 2012, you were self-employed, and that you were not informed that the policy would not cover you for unemployment in those circumstances. You have also told us that the adviser verbally represented that the policy would cover all eventualities. You feel that these specific points were not addressed in our final response. We take these concerns seriously.
verdicts.reasoning: The response acknowledges and reflects the customer's specific concerns fairly and without dismissal. However, it then defers substantive engagement by announcing a review rather than addressing the merits. The tone is respectful, but the failure to reach a decision or provide substantive reasons means the complaint is not treated fairly in the sense of being resolved or properly engaged with. The customer is left in limbo.

The response does not address redress at all. The customer has claimed £3,200 in financial loss (premiums paid for a policy that could not have paid out). The response defers the substantive investigation and does not communicate any position on whether redress is warranted, how it will be calculated, or when a decision will be made. This is a significant omission given the explicit financial claim.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with how my complaint about mis-sold payment protection insurance (PPI) has been handled. I submitted my complaint on 15 January and received your final response letter on 28 February, which rejected my claim. Your letter stated that you found no evidence of mis-selling. However, I believe your investigation was neither competent nor impartial. You failed to obtain the original point-of-sale documentation, you did not consider that I was self-employed at the time and therefore ineligible for the unemployment element of the policy, and you ignored the fact that I had a pre-existing medical condition that would have excluded me from claiming. I also note that your final response letter did not mention anything about a time limit for referring my complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. I want you to reconsider your decision and provide appropriate redress for the premiums I paid over seven years. If you do not resolve this satisfactorily, I intend to take this to the Ombudsman, but I need clarity on what my rights are and how long I have to do so.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This is a PPI mis-selling complaint where the customer is challenging the adequacy of your investigation and also flagging a procedural deficiency in your final response letter — specifically that it omitted the FCA-required information about the time limit for referring to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS).

**Procedural issue — missing time limit wording:** DISP 1.6.2R (inferred from the chunk at disp1s6) requires that a final response must inform the complainant they may refer to FOS and must indicate whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R, using the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3R. The customer is correct that this was omitted. This is a binding rule and the omission is a regulatory deficiency in your original final response. The new response must include this information.

**PPI time limits:** For PPI sale complaints, DISP 2.8.9R imposes a hard deadline: the complaint must have been referred to the respondent or FOS on or before 29 August 2019. The customer states they submitted their complaint on 15 January (year not specified). You must confirm the year. If the complaint was received on or before 29 August 2019, the DISP 2.8.9R deadline is met. If it was received after that date, the Ombudsman cannot consider it unless exceptional circumstances apply or you consent to waiver — this is a significant issue requiring legal/compliance input before any draft is sent.

**Substantive mis-selling grounds:** The customer raises two specific grounds: (1) self-employment rendering them ineligible for the unemployment element, and (2) a pre-existing medical condition excluding them from claiming. Both are recognised categories of potential mis-selling under DISP App 3.6.2 (the chunk at dispapp3s6 lists specific mis-selling indicators including selling a policy where the complainant was ineligible for significant benefits). DISP App 3.6.3E notes that relevant evidence includes the complainant's demands, needs and intentions at the time of sale. The customer's allegation that you did not obtain point-of-sale documentation and did not consider their employment status or medical history is a direct challenge to the adequacy of your investigation under these provisions.

**Recommended action:** You should not simply re-send the same rejection. Given the procedural deficiency (missing FOS referral/time limit wording) and the substantive grounds raised (eligibility issues not apparently considered), this complaint should be reopened for fresh investigation. The new response must, at minimum, address the eligibility points and include the required FOS referral wording and time limit indication.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending:**
1. The year the complaint was submitted — critical for DISP 2.8.9R PPI deadline assessment.
2. Whether point-of-sale documentation has now been obtained or is obtainable.
3. Whether the bank consents to waive the DISP 2.8.2R/2.8.7R time limits (this must be stated in the response using DISP 1 Annex 3R wording).
4. The specific wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R — this is referenced in the retrieved provisions but the annex text itself was not retrieved; you must obtain the prescribed wording from your compliance team before finalising the letter.

Human review is required before sending given the potential redress, the procedural deficiency, the unconfirmed PPI deadline, and the need for DISP 1 Annex 3R prescribed wording not available in the retrieved context.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for your letter raising further concerns about our handling of your PPI complaint. We are sorry that our response has not resolved matters to your satisfaction, and we take the points you have raised seriously.

**Reconsidering your complaint**

You have told us that our investigation did not take into account that you were self-employed at the time the policy was sold, and therefore potentially ineligible for the unemployment element of the cover, and that you had a pre-existing medical condition that may have excluded you from making a claim. You have also told us that we did not obtain the original point-of-sale documentation.

These are substantive concerns that we should have addressed in our original investigation. We are therefore reopening your complaint to consider these points properly. We will look at the evidence available about your circumstances at the time of the sale — including your employment status, your medical history as it was known at that time, and the terms of the policy — and we will write to you again with our findings.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

We also recognise that our previous letter did not give you the information you were entitled to receive about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS). We apologise for that omission.

If you are dissatisfied with our response at any stage, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can find further information about the FOS at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk. We are enclosing a copy of the FOS's standard explanatory leaflet, which explains how the service works and the time limits that apply to referrals.

[HANDLER NOTE — INSERT DISP 1 ANNEX 3R PRESCRIBED WORDING HERE regarding whether the bank consents or does not consent to waive the relevant time limits. This wording must be obtained from your compliance team and inserted before this letter is sent. Do not send without it.]

**Next steps**

We will complete our review and write to you with a full response as soon as possible. If at that point you remain dissatisfied, the FOS leaflet enclosed will explain your options and the time limits for making a referral.

Thank you for bringing these matters to our attention.

Yours sincerely,

[Name]
[Job title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a final response to inform the complainant they may refer to FOS, enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, and indicate whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits using the wording in DISP 1 Annex 3R — all of which were omitted from the original response.

Imposes a hard deadline requiring PPI sale complaints to have been referred to the respondent or FOS on or before 29 August 2019; the year of the customer's complaint submission must be confirmed to assess whether this deadline is met.

Sets out specific indicators of PPI mis-selling, including selling a policy where the complainant was ineligible for significant benefits, which is directly relevant to the customer's grounds concerning self-employment and the pre-existing medical condition.

Provides that relevant evidence in assessing a PPI mis-selling complaint includes the complainant's demands, needs and intentions at the time of sale, supporting the need to obtain and consider point-of-sale documentation and the customer's personal circumstances.
