While this is procedurally appropriate given that the complaint is being reopened, the customer-facing text does not reach or communicate a position on redress in this response. The handler answer notes that redress may be warranted but the customer draft does not address whether redress will be offered or explain the basis for any decision. For a final response, this is incomplete; for a holding response, it is acceptable but the rubric requires a final response to address redress.

claims.claim: DISP 1.6.2R requires that a final response must inform the complainant they may refer to FOS and must indicate whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R, using the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3R.
claims.reasoning: The disp1s6 provision explicitly states that a final response must inform the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS and must indicate consent to waive the time limits using the wording in DISP 1 Annex 3R.

claims.claim: The omission of the required FOS referral and time‑limit wording in the original final response is a regulatory deficiency.
claims.reasoning: Disp1s6 makes the inclusion of that wording mandatory; therefore its absence constitutes a breach of the rule.

claims.claim: DISP 2.8.9R imposes a hard deadline: the complaint must have been referred to the respondent or the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019.
claims.reasoning: The DISP 2.8.9R rule in the disp2s8 provision sets the 29 August 2019 deadline.

claims.claim: If the complaint was received on or before 29 August 2019, the DISP 2.8.9R deadline is met.
claims.reasoning: The provision defines the deadline; meeting it satisfies the rule.

claims.claim: If the complaint was received after that date, the Ombudsman cannot consider it unless exceptional circumstances apply or the respondent consents to waive the time limits.
claims.reasoning: Disp2s8 lists the three conditions under which a complaint outside the deadline may still be considered, including exceptional circumstances and respondent consent.

claims.claim: Both self‑employment ineligibility and pre‑existing medical condition are recognised categories of potential mis‑selling under DISP App 3.6.2.
claims.reasoning: Dispapp3s6 enumerates mis‑selling indicators, including selling where the complainant is ineligible for benefits, which covers the stated categories.

claims.claim: DISP App 3.6.3E notes that relevant evidence includes the complainant's demands, needs and intentions at the time of sale.
claims.reasoning: The provision explicitly states that such evidence is relevant.

claims.claim: The new response must, at minimum, address the eligibility points and include the required FOS referral wording and time‑limit indication.
claims.reasoning: The rule obliges inclusion of the FOS wording and time‑limit indication (grounded), but it does not expressly require the firm to address eligibility points; that requirement is inferred from good practice rather than the provision.

claims.claim: The bank must state whether it consents to waive the DISP 2.8.2R/2.8.7R time limits using the wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3R.
claims.reasoning: Disp1s6 requires the final response to indicate consent (or not) to waive the time limits using the Annex 3R wording.

claims.claim: We are reopening your complaint to consider these points properly.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved FCA provision mandates or authorises a firm to reopen a complaint; the statement is a factual description of intended action, not supported by the provisions.

claims.claim: We will look at the evidence available about your circumstances at the time of the sale … and we will write to you again with our findings.
claims.reasoning: The provisions do not prescribe this specific investigative step or timeline; therefore the claim is not grounded.

claims.claim: If you are dissatisfied with our response at any stage, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: Disp1s6 obliges the firm to inform the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS (grounded). The addition that the service is "free of charge" is not mentioned in the provision, so the claim is only partially grounded.

claims.claim: We are enclosing a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, which explains how the service works and the time limits that apply to referrals.
claims.reasoning: Disp1s6 requires the final response to enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet and to provide information on the time limits.

claims.claim: The FOS leaflet enclosed will explain your options and the time limits for making a referral.
claims.reasoning: The same provision mandates that the leaflet contain information on the time limits and options for the complainant.

claims.claim: We will complete our review and write to you with a full response as soon as possible.
claims.reasoning: No specific FCA rule sets a requirement to complete the review "as soon as possible"; the claim is not supported by the retrieved provisions.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I received a text message from my bank three days ago saying my complaint about a £35 overdraft fee had been resolved after I agreed on the phone to accept a refund. However, the written summary I then received by post only said 'your complaint is now closed' — it didn't mention anything about the Financial Ombudsman Service, didn't give me their website address, and didn't tell me I could refer the matter to them if I changed my mind. Well, I have changed my mind. The £35 refund doesn't address the fact that the fee was applied incorrectly in the first place due to a system error, and I believe I'm owed compensation for the stress and time spent chasing this. I want to escalate this complaint. I also want to know why the bank failed to give me proper information about my rights when it sent me that summary letter.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have been submitted by the customer directly, not by a complaints handler. Please confirm whether you are the handler or the customer before sending any response. I will treat this as a handler query for now and provide the regulatory position.

**Regulatory position on the deficient summary resolution communication:**
The written summary sent to the customer ('your complaint is now closed') appears to have been intended as a summary resolution communication. Under DISP 1.5.4R (referenced in the retrieved provisions via DISP 1.5.5G context) and the requirements reflected in DISP 1.6.2R, a final response or summary resolution communication must: (a) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (b) provide the FOS website address; and (c) inform the complainant that if they remain dissatisfied they may refer the complaint to the FOS. The written summary as described failed all three of these requirements. This is a compliance gap that should be escalated internally.

**Regulatory position on the customer's right to escalate:**
Under DISP 2.8.2R, the six-month time limit for referring a complaint to the FOS runs from the date the respondent sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication. However, because the summary resolution communication was deficient (it did not include the required FOS referral information), there is a question as to whether it constitutes a valid final response or summary resolution communication at all. This is a material uncertainty that requires legal/compliance review before any position is communicated to the customer.

**Regulatory position on the substance of the complaint:**
The customer is now asserting that the £35 refund does not fully address the underlying complaint (system error causing incorrect fee) and is claiming additional compensation for stress and time. This involves potential redress beyond the original offer and must be reviewed on its merits. Any financial redress decision must be reviewed and, per Crest Bank's internal policy, countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations if the total redress is £500 or more.

**Recommended action:**
1. Do not treat the deficient written summary as a valid final response. Issue a remedying final response that complies fully with DISP 1.6.2R requirements, including FOS leaflet, website address, and referral rights.
2. Escalate the substantive complaint (system error, compensation claim) for fresh investigation before issuing that remedying final response.
3. Flag the deficient summary resolution communication to your compliance team as a potential systemic issue.
4. Human review is required before any customer-facing communication is sent, given the compliance failure, the unresolved redress question, and the uncertainty about whether the six-month FOS referral clock has validly started.

**Assumption to confirm:** I am assuming the written summary was intended to close the complaint and was not merely an acknowledgement. Please confirm. Also confirm the exact date the written summary was sent, as this affects the FOS referral time limit position.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch. I am sorry to hear that you are dissatisfied with the way your complaint has been handled and that you wish to take matters further.

We have reviewed the written summary you received and we recognise that it did not contain all of the information it should have. In particular, it did not tell you about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if you remained dissatisfied, and it did not include the Financial Ombudsman Service's contact details. We apologise for this.

We are treating your complaint as remaining open and will investigate the concerns you have raised — including the underlying system error that led to the fee being applied, and your request for further redress — and write to you again with our findings.

If, once you have received our response, you remain dissatisfied, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. Their website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk. We will include a copy of their explanatory leaflet with our next letter to you.

We will be in touch as soon as possible. Thank you for your patience.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Crest Bank]

cited_provisions.relevance: Sets out the requirements a final response must meet, including enclosing the FOS explanatory leaflet, providing the FOS website address, and informing the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS — all of which were absent from the written summary sent to the customer.

The six-month time limit for referring a complaint to the FOS runs from the date of the final response or summary resolution communication; the validity of that clock starting is in question given the deficient written summary.

The FOS can only consider a complaint once a final response or summary resolution communication has been sent, or eight weeks have elapsed — relevant to whether the customer can currently refer to the FOS.

The FCA indicates that FOS referral information in a summary resolution communication should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently — the written summary as described did not meet this standard.

Where this is the case, a firm that is required under the rules in this section to inform a banking customer that they will or may be charged should take care that the alert is fair, clear, and not misleading. fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with how my complaint was handled. Three weeks ago, I contacted your bank about an incorrect fee of £45 charged to my current account. Your staff member called me back the same day and offered to refund the fee, which I accepted over the phone. I then received a short letter saying the complaint was resolved, but the letter did not mention the Financial Ombudsman Service at all — no website address, no information about my right to refer the matter if I changed my mind, nothing. I have since looked into this further and I believe the fee was actually part of a wider pattern of overcharging on my account going back several months. I am no longer satisfied with the resolution and wish to escalate this matter. I want the bank to investigate the full pattern of charges and I want to know why I was not properly informed of my rights when the complaint was first closed.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct issues that must be addressed separately.

**Issue 1: Deficient summary resolution communication / final response**
The customer received a short letter closing the complaint without any mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service, its website address, or the customer's right to refer. Under DISP 1.6.2R (Provision 1), a final response must: enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; provide the FOS website address; and inform the complainant that if they remain dissatisfied they may refer the complaint to the FOS. The letter sent to the customer did not meet these requirements. Crest Bank's own internal policy (Provision 3) also requires FOS contact details and a statement that the customer has six months from the date of the final response to refer to the FOS. You should acknowledge this failure plainly in the response to the customer.

Note on the six-month referral clock: Under DISP 2.8.2R (Provision 6), the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint referred more than six months after the respondent sent its final response. However, where the final response did not include the required FOS information, the position on whether the clock has started running may be affected — this is a legal/regulatory point that requires human review before you commit to any statement about the customer's current referral rights or time limits.

**Issue 2: New / wider complaint about a pattern of overcharging**
The customer is now raising a materially different and broader complaint — a pattern of alleged overcharging going back several months — which was not the subject of the original complaint. Under DISP 1.4.1R (Provision 8), once a complaint is received you must investigate it competently, diligently and impartially, assess it fairly, consistently and promptly, and offer redress or remedial action where appropriate. This new complaint must be treated as a fresh complaint, logged, and investigated properly. The eight-week clock for issuing a final response (Provision 3, internal policy referencing DISP 1.6.2R) will run from the date this new complaint was received.

**Assumptions requiring confirmation before sending:**
1. Confirm the exact date the original complaint was received and the date the closing letter was sent — this is needed to assess whether any regulatory deadline has already been missed on the original complaint.
2. Confirm whether the closing letter was intended as a 'summary resolution communication' or a 'final response' — the requirements in DISP 1.6.2R apply to both, but the framing of the customer letter may differ.
3. The new complaint about a pattern of overcharging involves potential financial redress beyond the original £45 — this must be reviewed by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch per Crest Bank's internal policy (Provision 3, section 7.5 / complaints involving financial redress).
4. Do not state a specific six-month referral deadline in the customer letter until the legal position on whether the clock has started running (given the deficient original letter) has been confirmed.

Human review is required before this letter is sent.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry for the concerns you have raised, and I want to address them fully.

**Our original response — what went wrong**

You are right to raise the issue with the letter you received when your complaint about the £45 fee was closed. That letter should have told you about your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service if you remained dissatisfied, and it should have included the Financial Ombudsman Service's website address and a copy of their explanatory leaflet. It did not do so, and I apologise for that. We are required to provide this information whenever we close a complaint, and we fell short of that standard.

The Financial Ombudsman Service can be contacted at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567. Their service is free of charge to customers. We will arrange for their explanatory leaflet to be sent to you separately.

**Your new concerns about a wider pattern of charges**

You have now told us that you believe the £45 fee may be part of a wider pattern of overcharging on your account going back several months. We take this seriously. We are treating this as a new complaint and will investigate it fully. Under our obligations, we are required to investigate your complaint competently, diligently and impartially, to assess it fairly and promptly, and to offer redress or remedial action if we find that is appropriate.

We will write to you with our findings. If we are not in a position to provide a full response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint, we will write to explain why and to confirm your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service at that stage.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you are dissatisfied with how we handle your new complaint, or if you wish to pursue the original complaint about the £45 fee, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. Please contact us or the Financial Ombudsman Service directly if you have any questions about this.

Thank you again for bringing these matters to our attention.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a final response to enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, and inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS — none of which were included in the original closing letter.

Sets the six-month time limit from the date of the final response within which a complainant must refer a complaint to the FOS — relevant to assessing whether the customer's referral rights on the original complaint are still live, given the deficient closing letter.
Requires the bank to investigate the new wider complaint about a pattern of overcharging competently, diligently and impartially, and to offer redress or remedial action where appropriate.
Indicates that the bank should aim to resolve complaints at the earliest possible opportunity, minimising referrals to the FOS — relevant to the handling of the new complaint.

or (5) dealing with such a type of complaint would otherwise seriously impair the effective operation of the Financial Ombudsman Service. ## DISP 3.3.4B (Guidance) Examples of a type of complaint that would otherwise seriously impair the effective operation of the Financial Ombudsman Service may include: (1) where it would be more suitable for the complaint to be dealt with by a court or a comparable ADR entity; or (2) where the subject matter of the complaint has already been dealt with by a comparable dispute resolution scheme; or (3) where the subject matter of the complaint has previously been considered or excluded under the Financial Ombudsman Service (unless material new evidence which the Ombudsman considers likely to affect the outcome has subsequently become available to the complainant); or (4) it is a complaint which: (a) involves (or might involve) more than one eligible complainant; and (b) has been referred without the consent of the other eligible complainant or complainants, and the Ombudsman considers that it would be inappropriate to deal with the complaint without that consent.

retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP 1.4 Complaints resolution rules ## DISP 1.4.1 (Rules) Once a complaint has been received by a respondent, it must: (1) investigate the complaint competently, diligently and impartially, obtaining additional information as necessary; (2) assess fairly, consistently and promptly: (a) the subject matter of the complaint; (b) whether the complaint should be upheld; (c) what remedial action or redress (or both) may be appropriate; (d) if appropriate, whether it has reasonable grounds to be satisfied that another respondent may be solely or jointly responsible for the matter alleged in the complaint; taking into account all relevant factors; (3) offer redress or remedial action when it decides this is appropriate; (4) explain to the complainant promptly and, in a way that is fair, clear and not misleading, its assessment of the complaint, its decision on it, and any offer of remedial action or redress; and (5) comply promptly with any offer of remedial action or redress accepted by the complainant. ## DISP 1.4.2 (Guidance) Factors that may be relevant in the assessment of a complaint under DISP 1.4.1R (2) include the following: (1) all the evidence available and the particular circumstances of the complaint; (2) similarities with other complaints received by the respondent; (3) relevant guidance published by the FCA , other relevant regulators, the Financial Ombudsman Service or former schemes; and (4) appropriate analysis of decisions by the Financial Ombudsman Service concerning similar complaints received by the respondent (procedures for which are described in DISP 1.3.2A G). ## DISP 1.4.3 (Guidance) The respondent should aim to resolve complaints at the earliest possible opportunity, minimising the number of unresolved complaints which need to be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service. ## DISP 1.4.4 (Rules) Where a complaint against a respondent is referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service, the respondent must cooperate fully with the Financial Ombudsman Service and comply promptly with any settlements or awards made by it. ## DISP 1.4.4A (Guidance) Full co-operation with the Financial Ombudsman Service includes, as appropriate, complying with directions on evidence or requests for information from the Financial Ombudsman Service required to assess a complaint. ##

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the final response letter I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about unauthorised overdraft charges totalling £340 applied to my current account between June and September this year. Your letter rejected my complaint, stating the charges were applied correctly under the account terms and conditions. However, the letter did not include any information about the Financial Ombudsman Service, no leaflet was enclosed, no website address was provided, and there was no mention of any time limits for me to refer my complaint elsewhere. I feel I have been left completely in the dark about my options. I believe the charges were unfair as I was never notified that my account had gone overdrawn before the charges were applied, and I want a full refund. I also want to know what my rights are now, since your so-called 'final response' told me nothing about where I can escalate this matter.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have been submitted by the customer directly, but I will treat it as a complaint handler query requiring a customer-facing draft response.

Regulatory position:

1. DEFECTIVE FINAL RESPONSE: The 15 November final response letter was non-compliant. Under DISP 1.6.2R, a final response must: (a) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (b) provide the FOS website address; (c) inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (d) include wording on whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R. None of these were included. This is a material breach of DISP 1.6.2R.

2. SIX-MONTH TIME LIMIT: Under DISP 2.8.2R, the six-month window for the customer to refer to the FOS is only triggered by a valid final response that tells the complainant about the time limit (confirmed by DISP 2.8.3G). Because the 15 November letter did not include this information, the six-month clock has arguably not yet started running. You should confirm this with your compliance team before sending the remedying letter, as the new letter will likely serve as the effective final response for time-limit purposes.

3. UNARRANGED OVERDRAFT CHARGES: The customer alleges they were not notified before charges were applied. CONC 5C.3.1R/5C.3.2R are relevant if the unarranged overdraft charges exceeded what would have been payable under an arranged overdraft facility — if so, the obligation to pay is unenforceable and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid. The substance of the charges complaint (£340, June–September) requires separate investigation. Do not admit or deny liability on the charges in the remedying letter.

4. INTERNAL POLICY: Crest Bank's own procedure (section 7.5) requires FOS contact details (www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; 0800 023 4567) and the six-month referral window to be included in all final response letters. This was not done.

5. REDRESS THRESHOLD: The complaint involves £340, which is below the £500 threshold requiring Head of Customer Operations countersignature under internal policy — but given the regulatory breach in the original letter and the potential CONC 5C issue, human review is strongly recommended before despatch.

Recommended action:
- Issue a new compliant final response letter (draft below) that remedies the DISP 1.6.2R deficiencies.
- Treat this new letter as the operative final response for time-limit purposes — flag this assumption to compliance.
- Separately investigate the substance of the charges complaint (notification failure and CONC 5C.3.1R cap) before confirming the rejection or upholding. The draft below maintains the rejection position pending that review — if the investigation changes the outcome, the letter must be updated before sending.
- Do NOT send the draft below without human review given the regulatory breach, the unresolved CONC 5C question, and the assumption about the new letter serving as the effective final response.

Assumptions to confirm:
- That the rejection of the charges complaint on the merits is still the correct outcome after reviewing the CONC 5C.3.1R cap and the notification failure allegation.
- That compliance agrees the new letter resets/starts the six-month FOS referral clock.
- Whether the car finance extended time limit provisions (DISP App 5.2) apply — they do not appear to on these facts (overdraft, not motor finance), but confirm.
- Whether the FOS standard explanatory leaflet is available to enclose.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch. I am writing to you about your complaint regarding unarranged overdraft charges of £340 applied to your current account between June and September this year, and also to address a serious shortcoming in our letter to you dated 15 November.

We owe you an apology. Our letter of 15 November did not include important information about your right to take your complaint further. It should have told you about the Financial Ombudsman Service, enclosed their explanatory leaflet, provided their contact details, and explained the time limit you have to refer your complaint to them. We are sorry that this information was missing, and we want to put that right now.

Our response to your complaint

Having reviewed your complaint, we are not upholding it at this stage. We have considered your account records and concluded that the charges applied between June and September were applied in accordance with your account terms and conditions. We recognise this is not the outcome you were hoping for, and we have set out our reasons below.

[Handler to insert the specific reasons for rejection here, including any findings on the notification issue and the basis on which the charges were assessed as correctly applied.]

Your right to go to the Financial Ombudsman Service

If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. The Financial Ombudsman Service is an independent service that resolves disputes between financial businesses and their customers.

You can contact them at:
Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
Telephone: 0800 023 4567

You have six months from the date of this letter to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. We have enclosed a copy of their standard explanatory leaflet, which explains how the service works and the time limits that apply.

[Handler to insert wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R regarding whether Crest Bank consents or does not consent to waive the relevant time limits, as required.]

If you have any questions about this letter or your complaint, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a final response to enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and include wording on time-limit waiver — none of which were included in the 15 November letter.
Sets the six-month time limit from the date of the final response within which the complainant may refer their complaint to the FOS, which is the referral window the customer must now be informed of.

Confirms that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that is a valid final response and that tells the complainant about the time limit — supporting the position that the defective 15 November letter did not start the clock.

Prohibits firms from imposing unarranged overdraft charges that exceed what would have been payable under an arranged overdraft facility, which is directly relevant to the customer's complaint about the £340 in charges.

Provides that if an unarranged overdraft charge is imposed in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R, the obligation to pay is unenforceable and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid — relevant to the merits investigation before confirming rejection.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. (4) Certain accounts provide for arranged or unarranged borrowing to be free of charge in limited circumstances, or for certain amounts. This is a situation where the customer ‘may be charged’ for their overdraft use. Where this is the case, a firm that is required under the rules in this section to inform a banking customer that they will or may be charged should take care that the alert is fair, clear, and not misleading. In particular where the borrowing does not give rise to a charge the firm may: (a) inform the banking customer that they may be charged; (b) inform the banking customer that they will not be charged in the circumstances that have triggered the alert and set out the circumstances in which the banking customer may be charged in the future; or (c) where the firm has put in place arrangements to provide a further alert to the banking customer if a charge arises in the future: (i) make no reference to charges in the alert; or (ii) inform the banking customer that they will not be charged based on the usage that has triggered the alert. (5) Firms should have regard to available evidence about the content and presentation that is most effective at engaging banking customers when designing the content and presentation of alerts in compliance with these rules. (6) The requirement to send alerts as soon as practicable in BCOBS 8.4.18R(1) does not preclude a firm from grouping alerts due to be sent to banking customers in ‘batches’, provided that alerts are sent at reasonable intervals.

(2) In CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b), CONC 5C.3.1R(3)(d)(ii) and CONC 5C.3.2R, “impose” an unarranged overdraft charge includes creating the contractual right to receive it, and relying on, or enforcing, the contractual right or purporting to do so (“imposes” and “imposed” should be read accordingly). (3) CONC 5C.3.1R does not affect an unarranged overdraft charge, liability for which accrued before the date on which CONC 5C.3.1R came into force.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with the final response I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about unauthorised transactions on my current account. Your letter rejected my complaint, stating that I had failed to keep my PIN secure. However, I believe this conclusion is unfair as I have never shared my PIN with anyone and I reported the transactions within hours of them occurring. Your final response letter did not include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, nor did it provide their website address. It also failed to tell me whether you consent to waive any time limits if I refer my complaint to the Ombudsman. I feel I have been treated unfairly and I want to know what my options are now. I am a pensioner on a fixed income and the loss of £1,200 from these transactions has left me unable to pay my heating bills this winter.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to be from the customer themselves, not a handler query — please confirm whether you are forwarding this as a complaint to action, or whether this is a handler question. I am treating it as a complaint requiring a response.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Defective final response (DISP 1.6.2R):** The retrieved provisions confirm that a final response must enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied, and indicate whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R. The 15 November final response omitted all three of these required elements. This is a breach of DISP 1.6.2R.

2. **FOS referral rights and time limits (DISP 2.8.2R / DISP 2.8.3G):** The six-month time limit for the customer to refer to the FOS is only triggered by a valid final response that tells the complainant about that time limit. Because the 15 November letter did not include the required FOS information, it is arguable that it did not constitute a compliant final response and the six-month clock may not have started running. You should seek legal/compliance input on this point before confirming the position to the customer.

3. **Unauthorised transactions / liability (BCOBS 5.1.12R):** The customer disputes liability for £1,200 in unauthorised transactions, denying they shared their PIN and noting they reported promptly. BCOBS 5.1.12R limits customer liability for unauthorised payments and provides that a firm must not seek to make a customer liable for losses where the unauthorised payment arises after the customer has notified the firm. The original rejection was based on alleged failure to keep the PIN secure (which could engage gross negligence under BCOBS 5.1.12(2)(b)), but the customer's account — prompt reporting, no sharing of PIN — may not meet that threshold. The substance of the rejection should be reviewed.

4. **Vulnerability:** The customer has identified themselves as a pensioner on a fixed income who cannot pay heating bills as a result of this loss. This is a clear indicator of financial vulnerability and potential harm. This must be factored into how the complaint is handled and prioritised.

**Recommended actions:**
- Treat this as a new complaint or a continuation of the existing complaint requiring a remedied final response.
- Issue a corrected final response that includes all required DISP 1.6.2R elements (FOS leaflet, FOS website address, FOS referral right, and the waiver/time-limit wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R).
- Refer the substantive rejection back to the investigating handler for review in light of BCOBS 5.1.12R and the customer's account of events.
- Given the financial redress involved (£1,200 — above the £500 threshold in Crest Bank's own policy), this letter must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.
- Flag vulnerability and prioritise accordingly.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft below:**
- That the bank has not already issued a compliant remedying letter since 15 November.
- That the substantive rejection is being reviewed (the draft below does not uphold or reject — it acknowledges the procedural failure and confirms next steps only).
- The correct DISP 1 Annex 3R wording on time-limit waiver must be inserted by the handler — I cannot reproduce prescribed wording without confirming which version applies.
- Confirm whether the six-month FOS referral clock is considered to have started; compliance input is recommended given the defective original response.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced, and I want to address both the concerns you have raised about our letter of 15 November and the underlying complaint about the unauthorised transactions on your account.

**Our letter of 15 November**

Having reviewed our final response letter of 15 November, I can confirm that it did not include all of the information it should have. Specifically, it did not enclose a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, did not provide the Financial Ombudsman Service's website address, and did not tell you whether we consent to waive any relevant time limits should you wish to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. We apologise for these omissions.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. Their website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk and their telephone number is 0800 023 4567. I have enclosed a copy of their standard explanatory leaflet, which explains how the service works and the time limits that apply to referring a complaint.

[INSERT DISP 1 ANNEX 3R PRESCRIBED WORDING ON TIME-LIMIT WAIVER — to be completed by handler before despatch]

**The substance of your complaint**

We take your account of events seriously. You have told us that you did not share your PIN with anyone and that you reported the transactions to us within hours of them occurring. We are reviewing the decision set out in our letter of 15 November in light of what you have told us, and we will write to you again with our findings as soon as possible.

We understand that the loss of £1,200 has caused you real financial difficulty, and we are treating your complaint as a priority.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a final response to enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and indicate whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits — all of which were omitted from the 15 November letter.
Sets the time limits within which a complainant may refer a complaint to the FOS, and requires the final response to address whether the respondent consents to waive those limits.

Indicates that the six-month time limit for FOS referral is only triggered by a response that is a valid final response and that tells the complainant about the six-month limit — relevant to whether the defective 15 November letter started the clock.

Sets out the limits on customer liability for unauthorised payments, including that a firm must not seek to make a customer liable for losses arising after the customer has notified the firm, and that gross negligence is required to impose full liability — directly relevant to the substantive rejection.

(1A) Paragraph (1) does not apply where: (a) the loss, theft or misappropriation of the payment instrument was not detectable by the banking customer prior to the payment; or (b) the loss was caused by acts or omissions of an employee or branch of the firm or of an entity which carried out activities on behalf of the firm. (3) Except where a banking customer has acted fraudulently, a firm must not, in an agreement for a retail banking service, seek to make a banking customer liable for any losses in respect of unauthorised payments where: (a) the unauthorised payment arises after the banking customer has notified the firm of the loss, theft, misappropriation or unauthorised use of the payment instrument; (b) the firm has failed to ensure that appropriate means are available at all times to enable the banking customer to notify it of the loss, theft, misappropriation or unauthorised use of a payment instrument; or (c) the payment instrument has been used in connection with (i) a distance contract; or (ii) a distance selling contract other than an excepted contract. fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with your bank's handling of my complaint about unauthorised overdraft charges totalling £487 applied to my current account in March 2024. I originally submitted my complaint on 15 April 2024. I received your final response letter dated 28 May 2024 which rejected my complaint, stating the charges were correctly applied under the account terms and conditions. However, I believe your response was inadequate and unfair. You failed to properly consider that I was in hospital at the time the charges accrued and had no ability to manage my account. Furthermore, I note that your final response letter did not include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, nor did it provide their website address. It simply said I could 'contact the ombudsman if unhappy' without any further detail about the time limits for doing so. I want to know what my rights are and whether I can still escalate this matter, as it has now been approximately five months since I received your response.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have been submitted by the customer directly, not by a handler. I am treating it as a complaint escalation for the handler to review and act upon.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Defective final response:** The customer's allegation that the final response letter dated 28 May 2024 did not include a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, did not provide the FOS website address, and did not properly inform them of the time limits for referral is a serious concern. DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in the retrieved provisions) requires a final response to: (a) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (b) provide the FOS website address; (c) inform the complainant they may refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (d) indicate whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R. If the original final response failed to meet these requirements, it may not constitute a compliant final response under DISP 1.6.2R.

2. **FOS referral time limit:** Under DISP 2.8.2R, the FOS cannot ordinarily consider a complaint referred more than six months after the date of the final response. The final response was dated 28 May 2024 and approximately five months have elapsed. The six-month window is therefore still open but will close around 28 November 2024. This is urgent — the handler must act promptly.

3. **Defective final response and time limit implications:** Where a final response is defective (e.g. fails to include the required FOS information), there is a question as to whether the six-month clock under DISP 2.8.2R has validly started running. The handler should seek legal/compliance input on this point, as the retrieved provisions do not fully resolve it.

4. **Substance of the complaint — unarranged overdraft charges:** The customer disputes £487 in unarranged overdraft charges applied in March 2024, citing hospitalisation and inability to manage the account. CONC 5C.3.1R and CONC 5C.3.2R are relevant: if unarranged overdraft charges exceeded what would have been payable under an arranged overdraft facility, the obligation to pay may be unenforceable and the customer may be entitled to recover sums paid. The handler must check whether the charges applied comply with CONC 5C.3.1R. Additionally, BCOBS 8.4.13R requires the bank to have sent an alert when the account entered unarranged overdraft, informing the customer of the reason, potential charges, and the opportunity to take action. The handler should verify whether compliant alerts were sent in March 2024.

5. **Vulnerability:** The customer has disclosed hospitalisation at the time the charges accrued. This is a potential vulnerability indicator and must be considered in the bank's assessment of the complaint and any remedy.

6. **Redress threshold:** The complaint involves £487. Under Crest Bank's internal policy, financial redress of £500 or more requires countersignature by the Head of Customer Operations. This complaint is just below that threshold, but given the regulatory significance of the defective final response and vulnerability indicators, the handler should consider whether it meets the 'regulatory significance' threshold requiring the same review.

**Recommended action:**
1. Urgently review the original final response to confirm whether it was defective as alleged.
2. Seek compliance/legal input on whether the six-month FOS referral clock has validly started and whether a remedying letter is needed.
3. Review whether the unarranged overdraft charges comply with CONC 5C.3.1R.
4. Check whether BCOBS 8.4.13R-compliant alerts were sent in March 2024.
5. Consider the customer's vulnerability (hospitalisation) in any reassessment.
6. Issue a new response to the customer promptly, given the approaching FOS deadline.

**Assumptions to confirm:** (a) That the original final response was indeed defective as described — the handler must retrieve and check the original letter. (b) That the charges were unarranged overdraft charges (not arranged). (c) Whether alerts were sent. (d) Whether the complaint meets the 'regulatory significance' threshold for Head of Customer Operations countersignature.

Human review is required before any customer-facing response is sent, given the defective final response, potential vulnerability, financial redress implications, and the approaching FOS deadline.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied with the way your complaint has been handled, and we take your concerns very seriously.

We have reviewed the points you have raised and wish to address them as follows.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

We are concerned by your account of the information provided in our final response letter of 28 May 2024. A final response is required to include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, provide their website address, and clearly inform you of your right to refer your complaint to the FOS and the time limit for doing so. We apologise if our letter fell short of these requirements.

We are enclosing a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet with this letter. You can also find information about the FOS at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by calling 0800 023 4567. If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. Please be aware that there is a time limit for doing so — ordinarily six months from the date of a final response letter — and we would encourage you to act promptly if you wish to pursue this route. [NOTE TO HANDLER: Do not send this paragraph in its current form until compliance has confirmed whether the six-month clock has validly started, given the potentially defective original final response. The time limit wording must reflect the confirmed legal position.]

**The substance of your complaint**

You have told us that unarranged overdraft charges of £487 were applied to your account in March 2024 at a time when you were in hospital and unable to manage your account. We are sorry to hear of the difficult circumstances you were facing, and we want to assure you that we take this context seriously.

We are reviewing your complaint again in light of the points you have raised, including whether the charges applied were consistent with our regulatory obligations and whether you received appropriate notifications about your account at the relevant time. We will write to you again with our findings.

**Next steps**

We aim to provide you with a full response as quickly as possible. In the meantime, if you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

[Enc: Financial Ombudsman Service explanatory leaflet]

Sets the six-month time limit from the date of the final response within which a complainant must refer their complaint to the FOS — relevant because approximately five months have elapsed since the final response dated 28 May 2024.

Confirms the conditions under which the FOS can consider a complaint, including that a final response has been sent or eight weeks have elapsed — relevant to assessing whether the FOS referral route is currently open.

Prohibits firms from imposing unarranged overdraft charges that exceed what would have been payable under an arranged overdraft facility — directly relevant to the customer's dispute about the £487 charges.

Provides that if an unarranged overdraft charge is imposed in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R, the obligation to pay is unenforceable and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid — relevant to the potential remedy if the charges were non-compliant.

Requires the firm to send an alert when a personal current account enters unarranged overdraft, including information about charges and the opportunity to take action — relevant to whether the bank met its obligations in March 2024 when the charges accrued.

retrieved_chunks.content: The final response must: 1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received. 2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons. 3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated. 4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5). 5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer. All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. Crest Bank will cooperate fully with the FOS in respect of any referred complaints, providing all requested documentation within the timeframes set by the FOS. ### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows: 1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure. 2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes. 3.

(2) The alert must communicate to the banking customer in plain simple language: (a) the reason why the alert has been sent; (b) that the banking customer will or may incur charges if they enter or remain in unarranged overdraft, if this is the case; and (c) that the banking customer has a period of time during which they have an opportunity to take action to avoid or reduce charges, and specify: (i) the actions which may be taken; and (ii) the time by which the customer must take such action to reduce or avoid the charge or charges. ## BCOBS 8.4.14 (Guidance) (1) The period communicated under BCOBS 8.2.13R(2)(c) should give the banking customer as good an opportunity to take action to avoid or reduce charges as possible, having regard to: (a) the time when the alert is required to be sent; (b) the terms and conditions applicable to the personal current account; and (c) the firm’s obligations under: (i) the Payment Services Regulations; and (ii) BCOBS 5.1.1R. (2) Firms should keep in mind regulation 89 of the Payment Services Regulations (Value date and availability of funds).

(2) In CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b), CONC 5C.3.1R(3)(d)(ii) and CONC 5C.3.2R, “impose” an unarranged overdraft charge includes creating the contractual right to receive it, and relying on, or enforcing, the contractual right or purporting to do so (“imposes” and “imposed” should be read accordingly). (3) CONC 5C.3.1R does not affect an unarranged overdraft charge, liability for which accrued before the date on which CONC 5C.3.1R came into force.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I took out a personal loan with your bank in March 2024. At no point during the application process or at the point of sale was I given any information about your complaints procedure or told how I could complain if something went wrong. I've since had a problem with an overcharge on my account and when I tried to find out how to complain, there was nothing on my loan agreement, nothing in the paperwork I was given, and I had to spend over an hour on the phone just to find out where to send my complaint. I also notice your website has no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service anywhere. I believe you have failed in your duty to make me aware of my rights and I want this addressed.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
The customer's complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues under DISP 1.2.1R (Consumer awareness rules):

1. **Failure to provide complaints procedure information at point of sale**: DISP 1.2.1R(2)(b) requires respondents to refer eligible complainants to the availability of complaints procedure information in writing at, or immediately after, the point of sale (for non-payment service products). A personal loan taken out in March 2024 would fall under this obligation. The customer states they received nothing in their loan agreement or accompanying paperwork — if accurate, this is a breach of DISP 1.2.1R(2)(b).

2. **Failure to publish FOS information on the website**: DISP 1.2.1R(4)(a) requires respondents to provide information about the Financial Ombudsman Service (including its website address) on the respondent's website, where one exists. The customer states there is no mention of the FOS on your website — if accurate, this is a further breach of DISP 1.2.1R(4)(a).

3. **Failure to provide complaints procedure information on request / when acknowledging the complaint**: DISP 1.2.1R(3) requires this information to be provided in writing and free of charge on request and when acknowledging a complaint. You should ensure this is done in your response.

**Recommended action**: You should acknowledge both failures, confirm whether the facts as stated are accurate (i.e. check whether point-of-sale documentation did include the required information and whether the website does carry FOS information), and address the underlying overcharge complaint separately. The customer is an eligible complainant and is entitled to the complaints procedure information and FOS details now if not already provided.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the draft**:
- Was the personal loan a payment service? If so, slightly different point-of-sale disclosure obligations apply under DISP 1.2.1R(2)(a). A standard personal loan is unlikely to be a payment service, but please confirm.
- Has the overcharge complaint itself been acknowledged and logged? That is a separate complaint that will need to be handled within the applicable time limits.
- Has the customer already received the complaints procedure information and FOS details as part of the complaint acknowledgement? If so, the draft can confirm this.
- Human review is required given the potential regulatory breach and the separate unresolved overcharge complaint.

Note: The retrieved provisions do not include the full text of DISP 1.6 (final response requirements) or the specific time limits for resolving the complaint. The draft below is limited to the consumer awareness/disclosure failure and does not address the overcharge substantively. The FOS referral right mentioned in the draft is grounded in DISP 1.2.1R(4) and DISP 1.6 (chunk disp1s6-002) which references the FOS leaflet and website address requirements.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you experienced in finding out how to make a complaint, and I want to address the concerns you have raised.

You have told us that when you took out your personal loan in March 2024, you were not given any information about our complaints procedure, either during the application process or at the point of sale, and that nothing was included in your loan agreement or accompanying paperwork. You have also told us that our website does not carry any information about the Financial Ombudsman Service. We take these concerns seriously.

We are required to make information about our internal complaints procedure available to customers, and to refer customers to that information in writing at or around the point of sale. We are also required to publish information about the Financial Ombudsman Service — including its website address — on our website. I am sorry that your experience suggests we may have fallen short of these obligations, and I want to assure you that we are looking into this.

So that you have this information now, I am enclosing details of our complaints procedure. We are also enclosing a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet and can confirm that their website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk. This service is free of charge to customers.

We have also noted your separate concern about an overcharge on your account. We are treating this as a complaint and will investigate it fully. We will write to you again with our findings.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Bank name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires respondents to publish complaints procedure information, refer eligible complainants to it in writing at or after the point of sale, provide it on request and when acknowledging a complaint, and publish FOS information (including website address) on their website — all of which the customer alleges were not done.
Requires the final response to enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet and provide the FOS website address, supporting the inclusion of these in the customer response.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.8 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of a payment protection contract, knowledge by the complainant that there was a problem with the sale of the payment protection contract generally (for example where there has been a rejection of a claim on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion, or the complainant has received a customer contact letter explaining that they may have been mis-sold) would not in itself ordinarily be sufficient to establish for the purposes of the three-year time period in DISP 2.8.2R(2) that the complainant had become aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he or she had cause for complaint in respect of a failure to make the disclosure set out at DISP App 3.3A.2E (relating to failure to disclose commission).

# DISP — DISP 1.2 Consumer awareness rules

## DISP 1.2.1 (Rules)

To aid consumer awareness of the protections offered by the provisions in this chapter, respondents must:

(1) publish appropriate information regarding their internal procedures for the reasonable and prompt handling of complaints;

(2) refer eligible complainants to the availability of this information:

(a) in relation to a payment service, in the information on out-of-court complaint and redress procedures required to be provided or made available under regulations 43(2)(e) (Information required prior to the conclusion of a single payment service contract) or 48 (Prior general information for framework contracts) of the Payment Services Regulations; or

(aa) in relation to CBTL arrangers, in the information on registering complaints internally and out-of-court complaint and redress procedures provided under article 7(1)(h) of Schedule 2 to the MCD Order; or

(b) otherwise, in writing at, or immediately after, the point of sale ; and

(c) in relation to a payment service, at the branch where the service is provided;

(3) provide such information in writing and free of charge to eligible complainants:

(a) on request; and

(b) when acknowledging a complaint; and

(4) provide information to eligible complainants, in a clear, comprehensible and easily accessible way, about the Financial Ombudsman Service including the Financial Ombudsman Service's website address:

(a) on the respondent's website, where one exists; and

(b) if applicable, in the general conditions of the respondent's contract with the eligible complainant.

[Note: article 15 of the UCITS Directive, article 13(2) of the ADR Directive, article 14(1) of the ODR Regulation, regulation 19 of the ADR Regulations and article 101 of the Payment Services Directive ]

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.2 Consumer awareness rules

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# DISP — DISP INTRO 1 Introduction

## DISP INTRO 1.4

Chapters 2 - 4: The Financial Ombudsman ServiceChapters 2, 3 and 4 set out how the Financial Ombudsman Service (operated by FOS Ltd) considers unresolved complaints.Chapter 2 sets out the scope of the Financial Ombudsman Service's two jurisdictions:• the Compulsory Jurisdiction; and• the Voluntary Jurisdiction.The scope of the two jurisdictions is defined by: the type of activity to which the complaint relates; the place where the activity took place; the eligibility of the complainant; and the time limits for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.Chapter 3 sets out the procedures of the Financial Ombudsman Service, including consideration and determination of complaints and how the Financial Ombudsman Service deals with information received.Chapter 4 sets out the terms under which VJ participants participate in the Voluntary Jurisdiction.

## DISP INTRO 1.5

Appendix 1: FCA’s guidance on handling mortgage-endowment complaintsThis appendix contains the FCA's guidance to firms on handling complaints relating to mortgage endowments.

## DISP INTRO 1.6

Appendix 3: FCA’s rules and guidance on handling payment protection insurance complaintsThis appendix sets out the approach which firms should use when handling complaints relating to the sale of payment protection contracts.

## DISP INTRO 1.7

Financial Ombudsman Service feesThe rules on fees charged in respect of the Financial Ombudsman Service are in Chapter 5 of the Fees manual.
