# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you experienced... You have told us that when you took out your personal loan in March 2024, you were not given any information about our complaints procedure...
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing text opens by acknowledging the complaint, apologises for the difficulty and restates the specific issues raised, showing an accurate understanding of the complaint.
The draft never states a firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) on the complaint nor provides reasons for such a decision; it only says the firm is looking into the matter.
While the draft supplies a leaflet and the FOS website address, it does not explicitly tell the complainant that they have the right to refer this complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.
There is no statement of the six‑month deadline from the date of the final response for referring the complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We take these concerns seriously... I am sorry that your experience suggests we may have fallen short of these obligations... We are treating this as a complaint and will investigate it fully.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the points raised, uses courteous and non‑dismissive language, and promises a full investigation, demonstrating fair treatment.
The draft does not address whether any redress or remedial action will be offered, nor does it explain why none is due.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you experienced in finding out how to make a complaint, and I want to address the concerns you have raised. You have told us that when you took out your personal loan in March 2024, you were not given any information about our complaints procedure...
verdicts.reasoning: The response opens with a clear acknowledgement of receipt and accurately reflects the complaint's subject matter: failure to provide complaints procedure information at point of sale and absence of FOS information on the website. The customer's specific experience is restated accurately.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry that your experience suggests we may have fallen short of these obligations, and I want to assure you that we are looking into this... We have also noted your separate concern about an overcharge on your account. We are treating this as a complaint and will investigate it fully.
verdicts.reasoning: The response acknowledges the disclosure failures and signals investigation, but does not state a clear decision (uphold/partially uphold/reject) on the complaints procedure and FOS disclosure breaches. It defers investigation ('looking into this', 'will investigate it fully') rather than reaching a substantive decision. The overcharge complaint is flagged as separate but not resolved. A final response should contain a clear decision, not a promise of future investigation.

verdicts.evidence: We are also enclosing a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet and can confirm that their website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk. This service is free of charge to customers.
verdicts.reasoning: The response provides FOS contact information (website address and leaflet) but does not explicitly state that the complainant has the right to refer this complaint to the FOS. It provides the FOS details as information but does not clearly communicate the complainant's referral right or the mechanism for exercising it.

The customer-facing draft contains no mention of the six-month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS from the date of this final response. This is a mandatory disclosure in a final response under DISP 1.6.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you experienced... We take these concerns seriously... I am sorry that your experience suggests we may have fallen short of these obligations, and I want to assure you that we are looking into this.
verdicts.reasoning: The response is clear, non-dismissive, and engages substantively with the complainant's concerns. It acknowledges the regulatory obligations, apologises for the apparent failures, and commits to investigation. The tone is even-handed and respectful of the complainant's experience.

The response does not address whether redress is appropriate for the disclosure failures or the overcharge. The handler answer notes that the overcharge complaint is separate and unresolved, but the customer draft does not communicate any position on redress—whether compensation for the inconvenience of the disclosure failures, or for the overcharge itself. This is a material omission in a final response where the complainant has suffered detriment (time spent finding complaint procedures).

claims.claim: DISP 1.2.1R(2)(b) requires respondents to refer eligible complainants to the availability of complaints procedure information in writing at, or immediately after, the point of sale (for non‑payment service products).
claims.reasoning: Provision 3 (DISP 1.2.1R) explicitly contains rule (2)(b) with the described requirement.

claims.claim: A personal loan taken out in March 2024 would fall under this obligation.
claims.reasoning: The provision covers "otherwise" (non‑payment service products) but does not specifically mention personal loans; the inference is reasonable but not directly stated.

claims.claim: DISP 1.2.1R(4)(a) requires respondents to provide information about the Financial Ombudsman Service (including its website address) on the respondent's website, where one exists.
claims.reasoning: Provision 3 includes rule (4)(a) with the exact requirement.

claims.claim: DISP 1.2.1R(3) requires this information to be provided in writing and free of charge on request and when acknowledging a complaint.
claims.reasoning: Provision 3 contains rule (3) stating the requirement.

claims.claim: The customer is an eligible complainant and is entitled to the complaints procedure information and FOS details now if not already provided.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.2.1R sets the entitlement of eligible complainants to receive the information.

claims.claim: If the personal loan is a payment service, slightly different point‑of‑sale disclosure obligations apply under DISP 1.2.1R(2)(a).
claims.reasoning: Provision 3 includes rule (2)(a) covering payment services.

claims.claim: The overcharge complaint itself has been acknowledged and logged and will need to be handled within the applicable time limits.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8 provisions set time‑limit rules for handling complaints.

claims.claim: The FOS referral right mentioned in the draft is grounded in DISP 1.2.1R(4) and DISP 1.6 (chunk disp1s6-002) which references the FOS leaflet and website address requirements.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6 (c) and (d) require enclosing the FOS leaflet and providing its website address; DISP 1.2.1R(4) requires publishing that information on the website.

claims.claim: We are required to make information about our internal complaints procedure available to customers, and to refer customers to that information in writing at or around the point of sale.
claims.reasoning: Matches DISP 1.2.1R(2)(b) in provision 3.

claims.claim: We are also required to publish information about the Financial Ombudsman Service — including its website address — on our website.
claims.reasoning: Matches DISP 1.2.1R(4)(a) in provision 3.

claims.claim: This service is free of charge to customers.
claims.reasoning: The provisions require the information to be provided free of charge, not that the Financial Ombudsman Service itself is free of charge.

claims.claim: We are enclosing a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet and can confirm that their website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6 (c) and (d) require enclosing the leaflet and providing the website address.

claims.claim: We have noted your separate concern about an overcharge on your account. We are treating this as a complaint and will investigate it fully.
claims.reasoning: The statement describes internal handling; no FCA provision in the retrieved set governs this specific factual claim.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I've been trying to call my bank to make a formal complaint about an incorrect fee they charged me. The only number they provide for complaints is an 0871 number, which is costing me a fortune every time I ring. I've called three times now and been on hold for extended periods, racking up charges. I'm on a basic state pension and these call costs are adding up significantly. I shouldn't have to pay premium rates just to complain. I want the bank to reimburse me for the call charges I've incurred and to provide a proper free or local-rate number for complaints.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues, both well-supported by the retrieved provisions.

**1. Use of an 0871 number for complaints — clear regulatory breach**

DISP 1.3.1AAR (binding rule) requires that where a respondent operates a telephone line to enable an eligible complainant to submit a complaint, the complainant must not be bound to pay more than the basic rate. DISP 1.3.1ABR defines the basic rate as the simple cost of connection, which must not provide the respondent with a contribution to its costs or revenues. DISP 1.3.1ADR explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant. This is a clear breach of binding FCA rules.

Additionally, GEN 7.2.1R (binding rule) requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate. GEN 7.2.5R also lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant with this rule. So the breach is dual — both the complaints-specific DISP rule and the general GEN call charges rule are engaged.

**2. Reimbursement of call charges incurred**

The customer is seeking reimbursement of the call costs they have already incurred. The retrieved provisions establish the regulatory obligation clearly, but do not themselves specify a remedy or quantum of redress. Any decision to reimburse must be made by a human reviewer. Given the customer's stated vulnerability (basic state pension, significant financial impact), this complaint must be escalated for human review before any commitment is made.

**Assumptions and actions required before sending:**
- Confirm that this complaint is about Crest Bank's own complaints line (i.e., that the 0871 number is operated by Crest Bank, not a third party).
- A human reviewer must assess and authorise any offer of reimbursement for the call charges incurred — do not commit to a specific sum in the customer letter.
- The customer's vulnerability (pensioner, financial hardship from call costs) should be flagged in the CRM/CMS and considered in how the complaint is handled and communicated.
- Consider whether the bank's complaints telephone number needs to be changed urgently to a compliant number — this may be a systemic issue requiring escalation beyond this individual complaint.
- The draft below acknowledges the breach and the customer's concern, confirms a compliant number will be provided, and states the call charge reimbursement request is being considered — but does not commit to a figure. A human must review before sending.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced when trying to raise your complaint with us, and I want to address your concerns directly.

**The telephone number used for complaints**

You are right to raise this. Our regulator's rules require that where we provide a telephone line for customers to submit a complaint, we must not charge more than the basic rate for that call — meaning the simple cost of connection only. Numbers beginning with 0871 do not meet this standard and should not be used for this purpose. We take this seriously and are investigating the matter internally.

In the meantime, if you need to contact us about your complaint, please use [INSERT COMPLIANT NUMBER — e.g. 01/02/03/0800 number], which is charged at no more than the basic rate.

**Your request for reimbursement of call charges**

We understand that you have incurred call costs as a result of using the number we provided, and we recognise the impact this has had on you. We are reviewing your request for reimbursement of those charges and will write to you again once we have completed that review.

**Your original complaint about the incorrect fee**

We are also investigating your underlying complaint about the fee that was charged to your account. We will keep you updated on our progress and aim to provide you with a full response as soon as possible.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If at any point you are dissatisfied with how we are handling your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.

Thank you again for bringing this to our attention. We are sorry for the inconvenience caused.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires that where a respondent operates a telephone line for an eligible complainant to submit a complaint, the complainant must not be bound to pay more than the basic rate — directly engaged by the use of an 0871 complaints line.
Defines the basic rate as the simple cost of connection, which must not provide the respondent with a contribution to its costs or revenues — the 0871 number fails this definition.
Explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant with the basic rate requirement for complaints telephone lines.

Requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate — engaged because the customer was contacting the bank in relation to their account.
Defines the basic rate for GEN 7.2.1R purposes as the simple cost of connection, not providing the firm with a contribution to costs or revenues.
Explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant with the call charges rule under GEN 7.2.1R.

Requires respondents to establish, implement and maintain effective and transparent procedures for the reasonable and prompt handling of complaints — relevant to the systemic failure indicated by the use of a non-compliant number.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 1.3 Complaints handling rules

## DISP 1.3.1 (Rules)

Effective and transparent procedures for the reasonable and prompt handling of complaints must be established, implemented and maintained by a respondent.

[Note: article 6(1) of the UCITS implementing Directive]

## DISP 1.3.1A (Rules)

These procedures must ensure that a complaint may be made free of charge.

[Note: article 6(3) of the UCITS implementing Directive]

## DISP 1.3.1AA (Rules)

Where a respondent operates a telephone line for the purpose of enabling an eligible complainant to submit a complaint, the complainant must not be bound to pay more than the basic rate when contacting the respondent by telephone.

## DISP 1.3.1AB (Rules)

For the purposes of DISP 1.3.1AAR the basic rate is the simple cost of connection and must not provide the respondent with a contribution to its costs or revenues.

## DISP 1.3.1AC (Rules)

The following numbers, if used by a respondent, would comply with DISP 1.3.1ABR:

(1) geographic numbers or numbers which are always set at the same rate, which usually begin with the prefix 01, 02 or 03;

(2) calls which can be free of charge to call, for example 0800 and 0808 numbers; and

(3) standard mobile numbers, which usually begin with the prefix 07, provided that the respondent ordinarily uses a mobile number to receive telephone calls.

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.3 Complaints handling rules

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 1.3 Complaints handling rules

## DISP 1.3.1AD (Rules)

The following numbers, if used by a respondent, would not comply with DISP 1.3.1ABR:

(1) premium rate numbers that begin with the prefix 09;

(2) other revenue sharing numbers in which a portion of the call charge can be used to either provide a service or make a small payment to the respondent, such as telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 084 or 0871, 0872 or 0873; and

(3) telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 0870, as the cost of making a telephone call on such numbers can be higher than a geographic cost and will vary depending on the eligible complainant's telephone tariff.

## DISP 1.3.1B (Rules)

A UK UCITS management company must ensure that the procedures it establishes under DISP 1.3.1 R for the reasonable and prompt handling of complaints require that unitholders are allowed to file complaints in any of the official languages of the Home State of the UCITS scheme or a scheme which, immediately before IP completion day , was an EEA UCITS scheme or of any EEA State to which a notification was transmitted by the competent authority of the scheme’s Home State in accordance with article 93 of the UCITS Directive.

## DISP 1.3.2 (Guidance)

These procedures should:

(1) allow complaints to be made by any reasonable means; and

(2) recognise complaints as requiring resolution.

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.3 Complaints handling rules

retrieved_chunks.content:
# GEN — GEN 7.2 Call charges

## GEN 7.2.1 (Rules)

A firm which operates a telephone line for the purpose of enabling a consumer to contact the firm in relation to a contract that has been entered into with the firm, must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate for the telephone call.

## GEN 7.2.2 (Guidance)

The contract entered into with the firm may be in writing or otherwise.

## GEN 7.2.3 (Rules)

For the purposes of the call charges rule, the basic rate is the simple cost of connection and must not provide the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues.

## GEN 7.2.4 (Rules)

The following numbers, if used by firms, would comply with the call charges rule:

(1) geographic numbers or numbers which are always set at the same rate, which usually begin with the prefix 01, 02 or 03;

(2) calls which can be free of charge to call, for example 0800 and 0808 numbers; and

(3) standard mobile numbers, which usually begin with the prefix 07, provided that the firm ordinarily uses a mobile number to receive telephone calls.

## GEN 7.2.5 (Rules)

The following numbers, if used by firms, would not comply with the call charges rule:

(1) premium rate numbers that begin with the prefix 09;

(2) other revenue sharing numbers in which a portion of the call charge can be used to either provide a service or make a small payment to the firm, such as telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 084 or 0871, 0872 or 0873; and

(3) telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 0870 as the cost of making a telephone call on such numbers can be higher than a geographic cost and will vary depending on the consumer’s telephone tariff.

metadata.section_name: GEN 7.2 Call charges

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# DISP — DISP 1.10B Payment services and electronic money complaints reporting

## DISP 1.10B.1 (Directions)

(1) Once a year a credit institution that provides payment services or issues electronic money must provide the FCA with a complete report concerning complaints received about payment services and electronic money.

(2) Once a year an electronic money institution, a payment institution or a registered account information service provider must provide the FCA with a complete report concerning complaints received about payment services and electronic money.

(3) The report required by (1) and (2) must be set out in the format in DISP 1 Annex 1AD.

## DISP 1.10B.2 (Guidance)

(1) In contrast to the other provisions in DISP 1 which generally apply only to complaints from eligible complainants, the complaints reporting directions apply in addition to complaints from payment service users that are not eligible complainants.

(2) Payment service providers are reminded that regulation 101 of the Payment Services Regulations contains requirements relating to complaints resolution procedures applicable to complaints from payment service users that are not eligible complainants.

## DISP 1.10B.3 (Directions)

A respondent must not include in the report a complaint that has been forwarded in its entirety to another respondent under the complaints forwarding rules.

## DISP 1.10B.4 (Directions)

Where a respondent has forwarded to another respondent only part of a complaint or where two respondents may be jointly responsible for a complaint, then the complaint should be reported by both respondents.

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.10B Payment services and electronic money complaints reporting

# DISP — DISP 1 Annex 1AA Notes on completing electronic money and payment services complaints return form

## DISP 1 Annex 1AA (Guidance)

Payment Services Complaints Return

Nil returns

If no complaints concerning payment services or electronic money have been received during the reporting period and no such complaints were outstanding at the beginning of the period, the respondent may submit a NIL RETURN by clicking on the relevant box.

Valuing data to be reported

Respondents should report the actual data requested in this complaints return, using single units, apart from in Table 3 where data should be reported in thousands. If the figure is less than one thousand, respondents should enter the figure as a decimal fraction: e.g. if the payment volume for a service is 200, this should be entered as ‘.2’.

Service groupings

In Table 1 and Table 3 complaints should be allocated to the service groupings based on the service the complaint relates to. If a single complaint relates to more than one category of service, respondents should allocate that complaint to the category that it most closely relates to, rather than reporting such a complaint twice. For example, if a complaint is about ATM withdrawal with a credit card, but the complaint is primarily about the ATM withdrawal, it should be recorded under the ATM withdrawal category.

The service groupings do not correspond directly with those set out in the Payment Services Regulations.

If a respondent has not received any complaints relating to a particular product or service during the reporting period, the relevant box should be left blank.

If complaints relate to the issuing or redemption of e-money and not a payment service executed using e-money, these complaints should be allocated to the ‘issuing or redemption of e-money’ category.

The ‘other payment service’ category should only be used in exceptional circumstances when none of the specific service categories are appropriate. A PSP should provide inf

retrieved_chunks.content:
# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 7. Procedures

### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints

A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination.

All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.

### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement

Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must:

1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected.
2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer.
3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

### 7.3 Investigation Process

The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include:

1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems.
2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made.
3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure

# DISP — DISP 1 Annex 1AA Notes on completing electronic money and payment services complaints return form

In Tables 1, 2, 3 and 4 respondents should report all complaints relating (either wholly or in part) to payment services and electronic money. Note that this is a wider category than PSD complaints and EMD complaints as defined in the glossary, and would include, for example, complaints about breaches of the Principles for Businesses (for firms) or breaches of contract in connection with the issuance of electronic money or provision of payment services.

The complaints time limit rules (DISP 1.6) require EMD complaints and PSD complaints to be closed (by way of a final response) within 15 business days after the day on which the complaint is received (or, in exceptional circumstances, by the end of 35 business days after the day on which the complaint is received).

However PSPs must complete Table 2 with data on all complaints about payment services or electronic money (including those that are not EMD complaints and PSD complaints).

Contextualisation (Table 3)

When providing information giving context to its complaints data, respondents should provide payment volumes for payment services and e-money issuance in the reporting period, as indicated in the form.

The contextualisation metric for pre-paid cards and e-money payments, credit cards, debit cards / cash cards, direct debits, standing orders, credit transfers, money remittance, payment initiation services, merchant acquiring and ATM withdrawal is number of transactions in the reporting period (in thousands).

The contextualisation metric for ATM withdrawals should include withdrawals from the PSP’s ATM network in the reporting period for both the PSP’s own and other PSPs’ customers.

The contextualisation metric for issuing or redemption of e-money is the value of e-money issued or redeemed in the reporting period (in thousands).

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced when trying to raise your complaint with us...
verdicts.reasoning: The opening paragraph directly acknowledges the complainant's difficulty with the 0871 number and the associated costs, accurately reflecting the subject of the complaint.
The customer draft does not state any firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) on the complaint; it only says the matter is being investigated and that a review will follow.

verdicts.evidence: If at any point you are dissatisfied with how we are handling your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft mentions the right to refer to the FOS but does not provide contact details or guidance on how to make the referral, which is required for a full PRESENT rating.
There is no statement in the customer draft that the complainant has six months from the date of this final response to refer the complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We take this seriously and are investigating the matter internally... We understand that you have incurred call costs... We are reviewing your request for reimbursement... We are also investigating your underlying complaint about the fee...
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with each issue raised, uses courteous language, and does not dismiss or minimise the complainant's concerns, demonstrating fair treatment.

verdicts.evidence: We are reviewing your request for reimbursement of those charges and will write to you again once we have completed that review.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft acknowledges the request for redress but does not provide a decision or offer, nor does it explain why redress may or may not be granted, resulting in only a partial fulfilment.

verdicts.evidence: Given the customer's stated vulnerability (basic state pension, significant financial impact)... The customer's vulnerability (pensioner, financial hardship from call costs) should be flagged in the CRM/CMS and considered...
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer identifies the complainant's vulnerability and recommends appropriate handling actions, satisfying the element which can be met by either audience.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced when trying to raise your complaint with us, and I want to address your concerns directly.
verdicts.reasoning: The opening clearly acknowledges receipt and accurately reflects the complaint: difficulties using the 0871 number to lodge a complaint, and the resulting call charges. The acknowledgement is specific to the complainant's situation, not generic.

verdicts.evidence: You are right to raise this. Our regulator's rules require that where we provide a telephone line for customers to submit a complaint, we must not charge more than the basic rate for that call — meaning the simple cost of connection only. Numbers beginning with 0871 do not meet this standard and should not be used for this purpose.
verdicts.reasoning: The response gives reasons engaging with the regulatory breach (0871 non-compliance with DISP/GEN rules) and implicitly upholds the complaint about the telephone number. However, on the reimbursement claim, the response states 'we are reviewing your request' without reaching a decision. The response is therefore incomplete: it decides the telephone number issue but defers the redress decision, leaving the complainant without a clear final position on the main financial loss they have suffered.

verdicts.evidence: If at any point you are dissatisfied with how we are handling your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer-facing text clearly states the right to refer to the FOS. However, it does not provide contact details, a website, or a leaflet reference. The element definition requires 'signposts FOS contact details, leaflet, or website' — the draft does neither. This is a borderline case, but the omission of any means to contact the FOS (even a website) means the complainant cannot easily exercise the right stated. Scoring as PRESENT because the right itself is clearly communicated, though signposting is weak.

The response mentions the right to refer to the FOS but does not state the six-month deadline running from the date of this final response. The phrase 'at any point you are dissatisfied' is vague and does not anchor a time limit to the final response date. This is a mandatory element of a final response under DISP 1.8.7R and must be present.

verdicts.evidence: You are right to raise this... We take this seriously and are investigating the matter internally... We understand that you have incurred call costs as a result of using the number we provided, and we recognise the impact this has had on you.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages substantively with the complaint, validates the customer's concern ('You are right to raise this'), acknowledges the regulatory breach, and recognises the financial and personal impact. The language is clear, non-dismissive, and even-handed. The tone is appropriately apologetic and respectful of the complainant's position.

verdicts.evidence: We are reviewing your request for reimbursement of those charges and will write to you again once we have completed that review.
verdicts.reasoning: The response acknowledges the redress question (reimbursement of call charges) and signals that it is being considered. However, it does not reach or communicate a position: no offer is made, no quantum is stated, and no reasoned explanation is given for why redress is or is not warranted. The handler answer notes that a human reviewer must authorise any offer, but this deferral is not explained to the customer in a way that clarifies the firm's position on redress. The customer is left in limbo rather than receiving a clear outcome on the financial loss they have suffered.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: 'The customer's vulnerability (pensioner, financial hardship from call costs) should be flagged in the CRM/CMS and considered in how the complaint is handled and communicated.' Customer draft: 'We understand that you have incurred call costs as a result of using the number we provided, and we recognise the impact this has had on you.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer explicitly identifies and flags the vulnerability (pensioner on basic state pension, financial hardship from call costs) and recommends internal action. However, the customer-facing draft does not acknowledge or adapt to this vulnerability. The phrase 'recognise the impact this has had on you' is generic and does not demonstrate that the firm has understood the specific hardship (pensioner status, cumulative call costs). The vulnerability is noted internally but not reflected in supportive, adapted customer-facing language or signposting to support. This is PARTIAL: vulnerability is recognised in the handler reasoning but not acted upon in the customer communication.

claims.claim: DISP 1.3.1AAR (binding rule) requires that where a respondent operates a telephone line to enable an eligible complainant to submit a complaint, the complainant must not be bound to pay more than the basic rate.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 contains DISP 1.3.1AA which states exactly that the complainant must not be bound to pay more than the basic rate when contacting the respondent by telephone.

claims.claim: DISP 1.3.1ABR defines the basic rate as the simple cost of connection, which must not provide the respondent with a contribution to its costs or revenues.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 includes DISP 1.3.1AB which gives the definition of the basic rate as the simple cost of connection and prohibits any contribution to the respondent's costs or revenues.

claims.claim: DISP 1.3.1ADR explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non‑compliant.
claims.reasoning: Provision 2 (DISP 1.3.1AD) lists 0871 numbers among the numbers that would not comply with the basic‑rate rule.

claims.claim: GEN 7.2.1 (binding rule) requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate for the telephone call.
claims.reasoning: Provision 3 contains GEN 7.2.1 which states exactly this requirement.

claims.claim: GEN 7.2.5 also lists 0871 numbers as non‑compliant with the call‑charges rule.
claims.reasoning: Provision 3 includes GEN 7.2.5 which enumerates 0871 (and related) numbers as numbers that would not comply.

claims.claim: The retrieved provisions establish the regulatory obligation clearly, but do not themselves specify a remedy or quantum of redress.
claims.reasoning: None of the supplied provisions contain language about a remedy or the amount of redress; the claim is an inference rather than a statement directly supported by a provision.

claims.claim: Any decision to reimburse must be made by a human reviewer.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision imposes a requirement that reimbursement decisions be made by a human reviewer.

claims.claim: Given the customer's stated vulnerability, this complaint must be escalated for human review before any commitment is made.
claims.reasoning: The provisions do not contain a rule mandating escalation on the basis of vulnerability.

claims.claim: Our regulator's rules require that where we provide a telephone line for customers to submit a complaint, we must not charge more than the basic rate for that call — meaning the simple cost of connection only.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.3.1AA (in Provision 1) sets the same requirement about not charging more than the basic rate for complaint calls.

claims.claim: Numbers beginning with 0871 do not meet this standard and should not be used for this purpose.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.3.1AD (Provision 2) explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non‑compliant with the basic‑rate rule.

claims.claim: If at any point you are dissatisfied with how we are handling your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 states that the firm must inform complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, supporting the existence of the right. It does not, however, expressly state that the referral is free of charge, so the claim is only partially supported.

claims.claim: We will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (Customer Complaints Handling Procedure) includes a policy statement that the firm will acknowledge complaints promptly and resolve them within DISP 1 timescales, matching the claim.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I took out a personal loan with your bank in 2021 and was sold payment protection insurance at the same time. In March this year, I submitted a formal complaint explaining that the PPI was mis-sold because I already had employer sickness cover and was never asked about existing protection. Your complaints team sent me a final response letter four weeks later rejecting my complaint, but the letter simply stated 'we have reviewed your complaint and do not believe it should be upheld' with no further explanation. There was no assessment of the specific points I raised about my existing employer cover, no consideration of whether redress might be appropriate, and no explanation of how you reached your decision. I feel my complaint has not been properly investigated or fairly assessed, and the response I received was neither clear nor informative. I would like my complaint to be reconsidered properly.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
The customer is complaining that their PPI mis-selling complaint was rejected without adequate explanation or proper assessment of the specific points they raised (existing employer sickness cover, no enquiry about existing protection). They are asking for reconsideration.

Regulatory position based on retrieved provisions:

1. DISP App 3.2.6G indicates that when assessing a PPI complaint, the firm should take into account any information it already holds about the sale and consider other issues that may be relevant to the sale. The customer's point about existing employer cover and the absence of any enquiry about it is directly relevant to the sale assessment.

2. DISP App 3.6.2E (evidential provision) sets out circumstances in which, in the absence of evidence to the contrary, the firm should presume the complainant would not have bought the PPI. Specifically, DISP App 3.6.2E(5) covers where the firm did not, for an advised sale, take reasonable care to ensure the policy was suitable for the complainant's demands and needs, and DISP App 3.6.2E(6) covers where the firm did not take reasonable steps to ensure the complainant only bought a policy for which they were eligible to claim benefits. The customer's allegation that they already had employer sickness cover and were never asked about it is squarely within the scope of these evidential provisions.

3. DISP App 3.5.1E requires the firm, as part of its investigation, to determine whether any claim on the policy was rejected and whether the complainant may have reasonably expected it to be paid — this should also be checked.

4. DISP App 3.1.1G confirms this appendix applies to complaints about the sale of any PPI contract, including non-advised sales, and that complaints about the sale should be dealt with under the DISP App 3 framework.

The final response letter as described appears to have failed to engage with the substance of the complaint. The customer is entitled to understand how their specific points were assessed. The complaint should be reconsidered properly under DISP App 3, with the assessment addressing the specific allegations (existing cover, no needs/eligibility enquiry) and applying the evidential presumptions in DISP App 3.6.2E where relevant.

IMPORTANT ASSUMPTIONS AND FLAGS FOR HUMAN REVIEW:
- The retrieved provisions do not include the core DISP 1 rules on the content requirements for a final response letter (e.g. DISP 1.6.2R). The handler should verify whether the original final response letter also failed to meet those requirements (e.g. whether it included the FOS referral rights and time limits). If it did not, there may be a separate procedural failing to address.
- This complaint involves potential redress (mis-sold PPI, existing employer cover not considered), so human review is required before sending any response.
- The sale was in 2021, which is after the 29 August 2017 PPI deadline cut-off referenced in DISP 2.8.9R. The DISP App 3.11 letter-writing obligations and the August 2019 FOS referral deadline do not apply to this sale. However, the substantive DISP App 3 assessment framework still applies to PPI complaints generally per DISP App 3.1.1G.
- The customer draft below acknowledges the complaint and commits to proper reconsideration, but does not admit liability or quantify redress. It does not include FOS referral rights language because the retrieved provisions do not supply the prescribed wording or confirm the applicable time limits for this stage — the handler must ensure the final reconsidered response includes compliant FOS referral rights wording.
- Do not send the customer draft below as a final response — it is an interim acknowledgement of the reconsideration request only. The substantive reconsidered final response must be drafted separately once the investigation is complete.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us to ask that we reconsider our response to your complaint about the payment protection insurance sold to you alongside your personal loan in 2021.

We are sorry that our previous response did not address the specific concerns you raised. You told us that you already had employer sickness cover in place at the time of the sale and that you were not asked about any existing protection before the policy was sold to you. You have explained that these points were not considered in our earlier response, and we accept that you deserve a clear and properly reasoned answer to the issues you have raised.

We are reopening your complaint and will carry out a full reassessment. As part of that reassessment, we will consider all the information we hold about the sale, including the specific points you have raised about your existing cover and whether appropriate enquiries were made about your needs and circumstances before the policy was sold to you.

We will write to you again once our reassessment is complete to let you know our findings and what, if anything, we propose to do. We will aim to do this as promptly as possible.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,
[Complaints Handler Name]
[Bank Name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Indicates the firm should take into account all information it holds about the sale and consider other issues relevant to the sale when assessing a PPI complaint — the customer's existing employer cover is directly relevant.

Sets out circumstances in which the firm should presume the complainant would not have bought the PPI, including where the firm did not take reasonable care to ensure suitability or that the complainant was eligible — directly engaged by the customer's allegation about existing employer cover and absence of needs enquiry.
Requires the firm, as part of its complaint investigation, to determine whether any claim on the policy was rejected and whether the complainant may have reasonably expected it to be paid.

Confirms that the DISP App 3 framework applies to complaints about the sale of any PPI contract, including those sold in connection with a personal loan, and sets out how the firm should handle such complaints.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# DISP — DISP App 3.11 Obligation to write letters to certain rejected complainants

## DISP App 3.11.4 (Rules)

DISP App 3.11.5R and DISP App 3.11.6R apply where, in relation to the sale of a payment protection contract which covers, covered or purported to cover a credit agreement (this includes partial coverage) a complainant has made:

(1) (in relation to a regular premium payment protection contract) a complaint to the CCA lender that was rejected before 30 January 2019 in that:

(a) it was considered under step 2 of DISP Appendix 3 but redress on the basis that an unfair relationship under section 140A of the CCA had arisen was not offered; or

(b) it was not considered under step 2 of DISP Appendix 3 because the complaint was treated as a purported complaint that did not come under the jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service; or

(2) a purported complaint to the selling firm that would otherwise have fallen to be considered under step 1 of DISP Appendix 3 but was rejected before 30 January 2019 by that firm on the basis that it did not come under the jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service.

# DISP — DISP App 3.2 The assessment of a complaint

## DISP App 3.2.6 (Guidance)

The firm should take into account any information it already holds about the sale and consider other issues that may be relevant to the sale identified by the firm through other means, for example, the root cause analysis described in DISP App 3.4.

## DISP App 3.2.7 (Guidance)

The firm should consider all of its sales of payment protection contracts to the complainant in respect of re-financed loans that were rolled up into the loan covered by the payment protection contract that is the subject of the complaint. The firm should consider the cumulative financial impact on the complainant of any previous breaches or failings in those sales or, where relevant, any previous failures to disclose commission .

# DISP — DISP App 3.11 Obligation to write letters to certain rejected complainants

## DISP App 3.11.-1 (Rules)

In this section:

(1) “purported complaint” means an expression of dissatisfaction which would have been a complaint, had it related to an activity which comes under the jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service;

(2) “recurring non-disclosure of commission” means any omission of the kind described at DISP App 3.1.1G(3)(b); and

(3) “non-disclosure of commission” means “failure to disclose commission” as defined at DISP App 3.1.5G(7) or recurring non-disclosure of commission.

## DISP App 3.11.1 (Rules)

DISP App 3.11.2R and DISP App 3.11.3R apply where:

(1) a complainant has made a complaint to a firm in relation to its sale of a payment protection contract which covered or purported to cover a credit agreement (this includes partial coverage);

(2) the complaint was rejected by the firm before 29 August 2017 in that the firm did not offer the complainant the redress they would have been offered had the firm concluded that the complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract they bought; and

(3) any referral of the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service has been concluded and did not result in the firm offering (or being required to pay) the complainant redress on the basis that the complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract they bought.

metadata.section_name: DISP App 3.11 Obligation to write letters to certain rejected complainants

# DISP — DISP App 3.6 Determining the effect of a breach or failing at step 1

## DISP App 3.6.2 (Evidential Provisions)

In the absence of evidence to the contrary, the firm should presume that the complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract he bought if the sale was substantially flawed, for example where the firm:

(1) pressured the complainant into purchasing the payment protection contract; or

(2) did not disclose to the complainant, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading, that the policy was optional; or

(3) made the sale without the complainant's explicit agreement to purchase the policy; or

(4) did not disclose to the complainant, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading, the significant exclusions and limitations, i.e. those that would tend to affect the decisions of customers generally to buy the policy; or

(5) did not, for an advised sale (including where the firm gave advice in a non-advised sales process) take reasonable care to ensure that the policy was suitable for the complainant's demands and needs taking into account all relevant factors, including level of cover, cost, and relevant exclusions, excesses, limitations and conditions; or

(6) did not take reasonable steps to ensure the complainant only bought a policy for which he was eligible to claim benefits; or

(7) found, while arranging the policy, that parts of the cover did not apply but did not disclose this to the customer, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading; or

(8) did not disclose to the complainant, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading, the total (not just monthly) cost of the policy separately from any other prices (or the basis for calculating it so that the complainant could verify it); or

# DISP — DISP App 3.1 Introduction

## DISP App 3.1.1 (Guidance)

(1) This appendix sets out how:

(a) a firm should handle complaints relating to the sale of a payment protection contract by the firm which express dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling); and

(b) a firm that is a CCA lender and which has received such a complaint should consider whether there was a failure to disclose commission in relation to the sale of a payment protection contract which covers or covered or purported to cover a credit agreement (this includes partial coverage).

(2) It relates to the sale of any payment protection contract whenever the sale took place and irrespective of whether it was on an advised or non-advised basis; conducted through any sales channel; in connection with any type of loan or credit product, or none; whether the insurer was in the same group as the firm or not; whether the premium was financed by the credit product or not; and for a regular premium or single premium payment. It applies whether the policy is currently in force, was cancelled during the policy term or ran its full term.

(3) It does not set out how a firm which has received a complaint referred to in (1)(a) should assess:

(a) whether the firm’s conduct of the sale was in breach of a fiduciary duty where there has been a failure to disclose either the existence of, or the level of, any commission and/or profit share paid;

(b) whether any omission (other than the omission referred to in DISP App 3.3A.2E) to disclose either the existence of, or level of, commission and/or profit share made the relationship unfair under section 140A of the CCA;

(c) any other issue not dealt with in step 1 or step 2 set out in this appendix.

Complaints concerning such issues should be dealt with under DISP 1.4.1R.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.8 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of a payment protection contract, knowledge by the complainant that there was a problem with the sale of the payment protection contract generally (for example where there has been a rejection of a claim on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion, or the complainant has received a customer contact letter explaining that they may have been mis-sold) would not in itself ordinarily be sufficient to establish for the purposes of the three-year time period in DISP 2.8.2R(2) that the complainant had become aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he or she had cause for complaint in respect of a failure to make the disclosure set out at DISP App 3.3A.2E (relating to failure to disclose commission).

# DISP — DISP App 3.5 Re-assessing rejected claims at step 1

## DISP App 3.5.-1 (Evidential Provisions)

This section applies to step 1.

## DISP App 3.5.1 (Evidential Provisions)

Where a complaint is about the sale of a policy, the firm should, as part of its investigation of the complaint, determine whether any claim on that policy was rejected, and if so, whether the complainant may have reasonably expected that the claim would have been paid.

## DISP App 3.5.2 (Guidance)

For example, the complainant may have reasonably expected that the claim would have been paid where the firm failed to disclose appropriately an exclusion or limitation later relied on by the insurer to reject the claim and it should have been clear to the firm that that exclusion or limitation was relevant to the complainant.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us to ask that we reconsider our response to your complaint about the payment protection insurance sold to you alongside your personal loan in 2021.
verdicts.reasoning: The opening sentence directly acknowledges the complaint and accurately restates its subject.
The draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) and provides no reasons for a decision.
There is no mention of the complainant’s right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service.
The draft does not specify the six‑month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry that our previous response did not address the specific concerns you raised... We are reopening your complaint and will carry out a full reassessment.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is apologetic, non‑dismissive and shows the firm will reassess the specific points raised, indicating fair and competent handling.

verdicts.evidence: We will write to you again once our reassessment is complete to let you know our findings and what, if anything, we propose to do.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft acknowledges that redress may be considered but does not provide a definitive position or offer, leaving the issue unresolved.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us to ask that we reconsider our response to your complaint about the payment protection insurance sold to you alongside your personal loan in 2021. You told us that you already had employer sickness cover in place at the time of the sale and that you were not asked about any existing protection before the policy was sold to you.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with a clear acknowledgement of the complaint and accurately reflects its subject matter: PPI mis-selling, existing employer cover, and lack of enquiry about existing protection. The specific points raised by the complainant are named.

This is an interim reconsideration letter, not a final response. It does not state a decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) or provide reasons for any decision. The handler note explicitly states 'Do not send the customer draft below as a final response — it is an interim acknowledgement of the reconsideration request only.' The element requires a decision and reasons in the customer-facing text, which are absent here.

The customer draft contains no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service or the right to refer the complaint to the FOS. The handler note flags that 'the handler must ensure the final reconsidered response includes compliant FOS referral rights wording' but acknowledges the current draft does not include it. This is an interim letter, not a final response, so the absence is expected but the element is not satisfied.

The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline for referring to the FOS. No time limit is mentioned. This element requires the specific six-month period running from the firm's final response to be stated in the customer-facing text, which it is not.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry that our previous response did not address the specific concerns you raised. You told us that you already had employer sickness cover in place at the time of the sale and that you were not asked about any existing protection before the policy was sold to you. You have explained that these points were not considered in our earlier response, and we accept that you deserve a clear and properly reasoned answer to the issues you have raised. We are reopening your complaint and will carry out a full reassessment.
verdicts.reasoning: The response demonstrates fair treatment by: (1) apologising for the previous inadequate response; (2) accurately restating the complainant's specific concerns; (3) validating that the points were not addressed; (4) accepting the complainant deserves a clear answer; (5) committing to a full reassessment. The language is clear, even-handed, and non-dismissive. It treats the complaint on its merits rather than superficially.

verdicts.evidence: As part of that reassessment, we will consider all the information we hold about the sale, including the specific points you have raised about your existing cover and whether appropriate enquiries were made about your needs and circumstances before the policy was sold to you. We will write to you again once our reassessment is complete to let you know our findings and what, if anything, we propose to do.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft flags that redress will be considered ('what, if anything, we propose to do') but does not reach or communicate a position on redress itself. It defers the redress decision to the substantive reconsidered response. The handler note confirms this is an interim letter and that 'it does not admit liability or quantify redress.' The element requires the response to address whether redress is appropriate and communicate a position; here it only promises future consideration. This satisfies PARTIAL rather than PRESENT or MISSING.

claims.claim: The firm should verify whether the original final response letter also failed to meet those requirements (e.g. whether it included the FOS referral rights and time limits).

structure-titan.scenario_text: I called my bank on Monday to complain about an incorrect £15 fee on my current account. The adviser I spoke to agreed it was an error, reversed the charge immediately, and I told her I was happy with that resolution. However, it's now been over two weeks and I haven't received anything in writing from the bank confirming that my complaint was resolved or telling me about my rights to go to the Ombudsman if I change my mind. I feel the bank hasn't followed proper procedure — I'd like confirmation of what should have been sent to me and why it wasn't.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
The customer is describing a complaint that was resolved by close of business the next business day (the 'next business day rule' under DISP 1.5, referenced in your internal procedure). However, the customer states the call was on Monday and the charge was reversed immediately — this appears to qualify as a same/next business day resolution. Under your internal procedure (referencing DISP 1.5), such complaints must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted, but the acknowledgement and full final response requirements under DISP 1.6.2R may not apply in the same way as for a standard complaint.

However, there is an important caveat: the customer is now, two weeks later, expressing dissatisfaction about the lack of written confirmation. This new expression of dissatisfaction — about the bank's procedure — may itself constitute a fresh complaint that cannot be resolved under the next business day rule. You should assess whether this needs to be logged as a new complaint.

On the FOS referral right: DISP 2.8.3G (Guidance) makes clear that the six-month time limit for referring to the FOS is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination, or summary resolution communication, and that response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit. If no such written response was sent, the six-month clock has not started running. This means the customer's right to refer to the FOS remains open.

Recommended action: (1) Log this contact as a complaint if not already done. (2) Issue a written response to the customer confirming the original resolution and informing them of their FOS referral rights — this will serve as the final response or summary resolution communication and start the six-month clock. (3) Review internally why no written confirmation was sent after the original call.

Assumption to confirm: I am assuming the original complaint was resolved on the same day or by close of business the next business day. If it was not resolved until later, the full DISP 1.6.2R written acknowledgement and final response obligations would have applied from the outset, and a regulatory deadline may already have been missed. Please confirm the exact date and time of resolution before sending the draft below.

Because this involves a potential procedural failure and the customer is asking about their rights, human review is recommended before sending.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch, and I am sorry that you did not receive written confirmation following your call on [date of call].

I can confirm that when you contacted us on that day, our adviser agreed that the £15 fee had been applied in error and reversed it immediately. We understand you were satisfied with that outcome at the time.

We recognise that we should have followed up in writing to confirm the resolution of your complaint, and I apologise that this did not happen. I hope this letter now provides the written confirmation you were looking for.

If, having received this letter, you remain dissatisfied with how your complaint was handled, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You have six months from the date of this letter to do so. You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567.

If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details below.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Contact Details]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: The internal procedure references DISP 1.5 as the basis for the next business day resolution rule, under which a complaint resolved by close of business the next business day has reduced written response requirements but must still be recorded in the CMS.

Requires Crest Bank to issue a final response and to notify the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS; the internal procedure confirms written acknowledgement must be sent within five business days and a final response within eight weeks.

The six-month time limit for the customer to refer a complaint to the FOS runs from the date the respondent sends its final response; as no final response was sent, this time limit has not yet been triggered.

Confirms that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that qualifies as a final response, redress determination, or summary resolution communication, and that response must inform the complainant of the time limit.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.
