# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 7. Procedures

### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints

A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination.

All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.

### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement

Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must:

1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected.
2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer.
3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

### 7.3 Investigation Process

The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include:

1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems.
2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made.
3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 1. Document Control

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Policy Title** | Customer Complaints Handling Procedure |
| **Version** | 2.1 |
| **Effective Date** | 1 February 2025 |
| **Next Review Date** | 31 January 2026 |
| **Document Owner** | Priya Sharma, Head of Customer Operations |
| **Approving Body** | Compliance Oversight Committee |

---

## 2. Purpose

This policy establishes Crest Bank's framework for receiving, investigating, and resolving customer complaints in a manner that is fair, consistent, and compliant with FCA requirements. It ensures that customers who are dissatisfied with Crest Bank's products or services receive a timely and transparent response, and that complaint data is used systematically to drive service improvement. The policy applies to all regulated and unregulated complaints received across Crest Bank's digital channels.

---

## 3. Scope

This policy applies to:

- **Staff:** All customer-facing staff in Customer Operations, as well as staff in Product, Risk, Compliance, and Technology who may be involved in complaint investigation or root cause analysis.
- **Products:** Personal current accounts, instant-access and fixed-term savings accounts, personal loans, and SME business current accounts.
- **Customer Segments:** All retail customers (approximately 850,000) and SME customers (approximately 12,000).
- **Channels:** In-app messaging, email, web contact form, and any written correspondence received at Crest Bank's registered address.
- **Systems:** Crest Bank's complaint management system (CMS), core banking platform, and customer identity and verification (CIV) system.

---

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# DISP — DISP 1.10B Payment services and electronic money complaints reporting

## DISP 1.10B.1 (Directions)

(1) Once a year a credit institution that provides payment services or issues electronic money must provide the FCA with a complete report concerning complaints received about payment services and electronic money.

(2) Once a year an electronic money institution, a payment institution or a registered account information service provider must provide the FCA with a complete report concerning complaints received about payment services and electronic money.

(3) The report required by (1) and (2) must be set out in the format in DISP 1 Annex 1AD.

## DISP 1.10B.2 (Guidance)

(1) In contrast to the other provisions in DISP 1 which generally apply only to complaints from eligible complainants, the complaints reporting directions apply in addition to complaints from payment service users that are not eligible complainants.

(2) Payment service providers are reminded that regulation 101 of the Payment Services Regulations contains requirements relating to complaints resolution procedures applicable to complaints from payment service users that are not eligible complainants.

## DISP 1.10B.3 (Directions)

A respondent must not include in the report a complaint that has been forwarded in its entirety to another respondent under the complaints forwarding rules.

## DISP 1.10B.4 (Directions)

Where a respondent has forwarded to another respondent only part of a complaint or where two respondents may be jointly responsible for a complaint, then the complaint should be reported by both respondents.

# DISP — DISP 1 Annex 3 Appropriate wording for inclusion in a final response or written acceptance

The complaint was received outside the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R (2) and the

respondent does not consent to waive those time limits but does consent to waive

the six-month time limit in DISP 2.8.2 R (1)

(3)

“You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.

The Ombudsman might not be able to consider your complaint if:• what you’re complaining about happened more than six years ago, and• you’re complaining more than three years after you realised (or should have realised) that there was a problem.We think that your complaint was made outside of these time limits but this is a matter for the Ombudsman to decide. If the Ombudsman agrees with us, they will not have our permission to consider your complaint and so will only be able to do so in very limited circumstances. For example, if the Ombudsman believes that the delay was as a result of exceptional circumstances.The time limit for referring complaints to the Ombudsman is usually six months but we will consent to the Ombudsman considering your complaint even if you refer the complaint later than this.”

The respondent does not consent to waive the time limits in DISP 2.8.7 R relating to mortgage endowment complaints

(4)

“You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge — but you must do so within six months of the date of this letter.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry that you did not receive written confirmation following your call on [date of call].
verdicts.reasoning: The opening sentence directly acknowledges the complainant's issue about not receiving written confirmation and references the specific call, satisfying the acknowledgement requirement.
The draft does not state a clear decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) on the complaint; it only apologises and provides confirmation, without a formal decision and explicit reasons.

verdicts.evidence: you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing text explicitly informs the complainant of the right to refer the complaint to the FOS and provides contact details.

verdicts.evidence: You have six months from the date of this letter to do so.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft clearly states the six‑month deadline for referring to the FOS, anchored to the date of the final response.

verdicts.evidence: We recognise that we should have followed up in writing to confirm the resolution of your complaint, and I apologise that this did not happen.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the complainant's concerns, apologises, and uses courteous, non‑dismissive language, demonstrating fair treatment.

verdicts.evidence: I can confirm that ... the £15 fee had been applied in error and reversed it immediately.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft addresses the redress already provided (reversal of the £15 fee) and indicates that the complainant was satisfied, thereby covering the redress consideration requirement.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch, and I am sorry that you did not receive written confirmation following your call on [date of call]. I can confirm that when you contacted us on that day, our adviser agreed that the £15 fee had been applied in error and reversed it immediately.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with a clear acknowledgement of the complaint and accurately reflects its subject matter: the lack of written confirmation after the call and the original fee error. The response demonstrates understanding of both the original issue (the fee) and the procedural complaint (no written follow-up).

verdicts.evidence: We recognise that we should have followed up in writing to confirm the resolution of your complaint, and I apologise that this did not happen.
verdicts.reasoning: The response states a clear decision: the bank acknowledges it failed to provide written confirmation and apologises. The reasons engage with the specific point raised — the customer's complaint was about the lack of written procedure — and the bank addresses this directly by acknowledging the procedural failure and providing the written confirmation now.

verdicts.evidence: If, having received this letter, you remain dissatisfied with how your complaint was handled, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the right to refer to the FOS and provides two means of contact: website and telephone number. The language makes clear the complainant can refer 'your complaint' to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: You have six months from the date of this letter to do so.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft explicitly states the six-month deadline and anchors it to 'the date of this letter' (the firm's final response). This satisfies the requirement to communicate the six-month referral limit running from the final response.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry that you did not receive written confirmation following your call on [date of call]... We recognise that we should have followed up in writing to confirm the resolution of your complaint, and I apologise that this did not happen.
verdicts.reasoning: The response treats the complaint fairly by acknowledging the procedural failure, apologising twice, and providing the written confirmation the customer requested. The tone is even-handed and non-dismissive; it does not minimise the customer's concerns about the lack of procedure. The language is clear and appropriate.

verdicts.evidence: I can confirm that when you contacted us on that day, our adviser agreed that the £15 fee had been applied in error and reversed it immediately.
verdicts.reasoning: The response confirms that the original £15 fee was reversed, which addresses the financial loss from the original error. However, the response does not explicitly address whether any additional redress is warranted for the procedural failure (the lack of written confirmation for two weeks). The customer's complaint includes both the original fee error (already remedied) and the procedural failure (acknowledged but no additional redress offered or considered). The response does not reach a clear position on whether the procedural failure itself warrants further redress beyond the apology and written confirmation now provided.

claims.claim: The complaint was resolved by close of business the next business day (the 'next business day rule') under DISP 1.5.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (section 7.1) states that a complaint resolved by close of business on the next business day is covered by the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5.

claims.claim: Such complaints must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 requires that even when the next business day rule applies, the complaint must be recorded in the CMS with the outcome.

claims.claim: The acknowledgement and full final response requirements under DISP 1.6.2R may not apply in the same way as for a standard complaint.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision states that the acknowledgement or final response obligations are reduced or waived for next‑business‑day resolutions.

claims.claim: A new expression of dissatisfaction may constitute a fresh complaint that cannot be resolved under the next business day rule.
claims.reasoning: The provisions do not address whether a subsequent expression of dissatisfaction is a separate complaint or its applicability to the next‑business‑day rule.

claims.claim: The six‑month time limit for referring to the Financial Ombudsman Service is only triggered by a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication, and that response must tell the complainant about the six‑month limit.
claims.reasoning: Provision 6 (DISP 2.8.3 Guidance) explicitly states this requirement.

claims.claim: If no such written response was sent, the six‑month clock has not started running.
claims.reasoning: Same guidance (DISP 2.8.3) makes clear the clock starts only after a qualifying response is sent.

claims.claim: The customer's right to refer to the FOS remains open.
claims.reasoning: Because the six‑month period has not started, the right to refer is still available per DISP 2.8.3.

claims.claim: If the original complaint was not resolved until later, the full DISP 1.6.2R written acknowledgement and final response obligations would have applied from the outset.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (7.2) requires a written acknowledgement within five business days and, under DISP 1.6.2R, a final response within eight weeks.

claims.claim: You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (7.5) obliges the firm to notify complainants of this right.

claims.claim: You have six months from the date of this letter to refer the complaint to the FOS.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.3 Guidance requires the response to tell the complainant about the six‑month limit.

claims.claim: All final response letters must be issued within eight weeks of the date the complaint was received.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (7.4) sets the eight‑week deadline for final response letters.

claims.claim: The final response must state clearly whether the complaint is upheld, partially upheld, or rejected, with reasons.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (7.4) lists this as a required element of the final response.

claims.claim: The final response must inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (7.5) requires inclusion of the FOS referral right in the final response.

claims.claim: All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details and must state that the customer has six months from the date of the final response to refer.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (7.5) requires contact details, and DISP 2.8.3 Guidance requires stating the six‑month limit; the combined statement is supported but no single provision contains both elements together.

claims.claim: If a final response letter is not sent within eight weeks, a written response must be sent at eight weeks informing the FOS referral right.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (7.5) specifies that a written response at eight weeks must include the FOS referral right when the final response is delayed.

claims.claim: The bank must maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt.
claims.reasoning: Provision 3 (point 6) sets a five‑year retention period for complaint records.

claims.claim: All complaints must be logged in the CMS on the same business day they are received.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (7.1) requires same‑day logging of complaints.

claims.claim: If a complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (7.1) explicitly states this requirement for next‑business‑day resolutions.

claims.claim: A written acknowledgement must be sent within five business days of receipt.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (7.2) sets the five‑business‑day deadline for written acknowledgements.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I made a formal complaint to my bank on 14 March about an incorrect fee applied to my current account. I received a brief email the same day saying 'thanks, we've got your complaint' but it didn't tell me anything about when I could expect a response or what the process would be. It's now been three weeks and I've heard absolutely nothing since that initial email — no updates, no indication of progress, nothing. I feel like my complaint has just been forgotten about. I want to know what's happening and when I'll get a proper answer.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
The customer submitted a formal complaint on 14 March. Based on the retrieved provisions, there are two issues to address:

1. **Acknowledgement adequacy**: Under Crest Bank's internal procedure (section 7.2, referencing DISP 1.6.2R), a written acknowledgement must be sent within five business days of receipt and must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale. The initial 'thanks, we've got your complaint' email does not appear to have met these requirements — it lacked the reference number, handler contact details, and resolution timescale. You should check the CMS to confirm what was actually sent.

2. **Eight-week deadline**: Under DISP 1.6.2R, a final response (or a written explanation of delay with FOS referral rights) must be issued by the end of eight weeks from receipt of the complaint. Eight weeks from 14 March falls on approximately 9 May. The complaint is currently three weeks old, so the deadline has not yet been missed — but the customer has received no substantive update.

Recommended action: Send the customer a proper acknowledgement/update letter now that (a) confirms the complaint reference number, (b) names the handler and provides contact details, (c) explains the investigation is under way, and (d) confirms the eight-week deadline by which a final response will be issued. Do not commit to a specific outcome or any redress at this stage.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the draft below:**
- Confirm the complaint was correctly logged in the CMS on 14 March and that a unique complaint reference number was assigned.
- Confirm the name and contact details of the assigned handler to insert into the letter.
- Confirm no substantive update has been sent since the initial email (check CMS correspondence log).

**Human review flag**: This draft does not involve redress, but the adequacy of the original acknowledgement is a potential compliance gap. A human should review before sending to ensure the CMS record is accurate and the handler details are correct.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch, and I am sorry that you have not received a proper update since your complaint was received on 14 March.

I want to confirm that your complaint about an incorrect fee applied to your current account has been formally logged and is being investigated. Your complaint reference number is [REFERENCE NUMBER]. If you need to contact us about your complaint in the meantime, please get in touch with [HANDLER NAME] at [HANDLER CONTACT DETAILS].

We are working to complete our investigation as quickly as possible. We are required to send you a final response within eight weeks of the date we received your complaint. If, for any reason, we are not in a position to provide a final response by that date, we will write to you to explain why and to let you know when you can expect one.

Once our investigation is complete, we will write to you with our findings and set out clearly whether we are upholding, partially upholding, or rejecting your complaint, together with our reasons.

If at any point you remain dissatisfied with how we are handling your complaint, you have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service. You can contact them at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567.

Thank you for your patience, and I apologise again for the lack of communication since your complaint was received.

Yours sincerely,

[HANDLER NAME]
[JOB TITLE]
Crest Bank

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.8 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of a payment protection contract, knowledge by the complainant that there was a problem with the sale of the payment protection contract generally (for example where there has been a rejection of a claim on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion, or the complainant has received a customer contact letter explaining that they may have been mis-sold) would not in itself ordinarily be sufficient to establish for the purposes of the three-year time period in DISP 2.8.2R(2) that the complainant had become aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he or she had cause for complaint in respect of a failure to make the disclosure set out at DISP App 3.3A.2E (relating to failure to disclose commission).

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 7. Procedures

### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints

A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination.

All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.

### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement

Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must:

1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected.
2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer.
3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

### 7.3 Investigation Process

The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include:

1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems.
2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made.
3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# DISP — DISP 1.10B Payment services and electronic money complaints reporting

## DISP 1.10B.9 (Directions)

The relevant reporting period is the year immediately following:

(1) where the respondent has an accounting reference date, its accounting reference date; and

(2) where the respondent does not have an accounting reference date, 31 December each year.

## DISP 1.10B.10 (Directions)

Reports are to be submitted to the FCA within 30 business days of the end of the relevant reporting periods through, and in the electronic format specified in, the FCA complaints reporting system or the appropriate section of the FCA website.

## DISP 1.10B.11 (Directions)

If a respondent is unable to submit a report in electronic format because of a systems failure of any kind, the respondent must notify the FCA, in writing and without delay, of that systems failure.

## DISP 1.10B.12 (Rules)

(1) If a respondent does not submit a complete report by the date on which it is due, in accordance with DISP 1.10B.10D, the respondent must pay the administrative fee specified in SUP 16.3.14R.(2) The administrative fee in (1) does not apply if the respondent has notified the FCA of a systems failure in accordance with DISP 1.10B.11R.

## DISP 1.10B.13 (Directions)

A closed complaint is a complaint where:

(1) the respondent has sent a final response; or

(2) the complainant has indicated in writing acceptance of the respondent’s earlier response under DISP 1.6.4R (where applicable).

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

claims.claim: A written acknowledgement must be sent within five business days of receipt and must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

claims.claim: Under DISP 1.6.2R, a final response (or a written explanation of delay with FOS referral rights) must be issued by the end of eight weeks from receipt of the complaint.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2 (Provision 4) states that the respondent must, by the end of eight weeks after receipt, send either a final response or a written response explaining why a final response cannot be provided, including FOS referral information.

claims.claim: We are required to send you a final response within eight weeks of the date we received your complaint.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2 (Provision 4) imposes an eight‑week deadline for the final response.

claims.claim: If, for any reason, we are not in a position to provide a final response by that date, we will write to you to explain why and to let you know when you can expect one.
claims.reasoning: Rule 2(a) of DISP 1.6.2 requires a written response explaining why a final response cannot be given and indicating when it is expected.

claims.reasoning: Provision 1, section 7.4, requires the final response letter to state clearly whether the complaint is upheld, partially upheld, or rejected, and to give reasons.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I made a complaint to my bank, Westfield Banking PLC, six weeks ago about a payment protection insurance policy that was sold to me through their branch but was actually underwritten by a separate insurer. Three weeks after I complained, the bank wrote to me saying they were forwarding my complaint to the insurer because the insurer was solely responsible. However, the bank didn't tell me the insurer's contact details in that letter — just said 'the relevant insurer' without a name, address, or phone number. I also feel that three weeks was not 'prompt' — I was left waiting with no update for all that time. I've now heard nothing from the insurer either, and I don't even know who to chase. I want to know why the bank took so long to forward my complaint and why they failed to give me the insurer's details so I could follow up myself.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues under DISP 1.7.1R, both of which appear to have merit on the facts as described.

**Issue 1 — Failure to provide the other respondent's contact details**
DISP 1.7.1R(2) requires that when forwarding a complaint, the respondent must inform the complainant in a final response of the other respondent's contact details. The bank's letter apparently named only 'the relevant insurer' without providing a name, address, or phone number. This appears to be a clear breach of DISP 1.7.1R(2). The customer has been left unable to identify or chase the insurer, which is precisely the harm this rule is designed to prevent.

**Issue 2 — Whether three weeks was 'prompt'**
DISP 1.7.1R(1) requires the forwarding to be done 'promptly'. Three weeks without any update or forwarding letter is likely to be difficult to defend as prompt, though the FCA Handbook does not define a specific number of days for this purpose in the retrieved provisions. You should check your internal records to see whether there was any legitimate reason for the delay (e.g., complexity in identifying the correct insurer). However, absent a good reason, three weeks is likely to fall short of the 'promptly' standard.

**Issue 3 — Current position and next steps**
Under DISP 1.7.2R, once the insurer received the forwarded complaint, it is treated as if the complaint had been made directly to it from the date it received the forwarded complaint. Under DISP 1.7.3G, the standard eight-week time limit (DISP 1.6.2R) runs from that date. However, the customer does not know who the insurer is, so they cannot monitor that deadline or chase for a response. The bank should urgently provide the insurer's full contact details to the customer.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending:**
1. Confirm whether the bank's forwarding letter constituted a 'final response' for the purposes of DISP 1.7.1R(2) — the rule requires the contact details to be given in a final response, so if the letter was not framed as a final response, there may be a separate question about whether the bank has also failed to issue a compliant final response within eight weeks (the complaint is now six weeks old, so the eight-week deadline under DISP 1.6.2R is approaching for the bank too, if it has not validly discharged its obligations by forwarding).
2. Confirm the identity of the insurer so the customer can be given their contact details.
3. This complaint involves a potential regulatory breach by the bank and the customer has been left without recourse — human review is required before sending.

**Internal policy note:** Under Crest Bank's internal procedure (section 7.4), any final response must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. Given the regulatory breach involved, this should be treated as having potential regulatory significance regardless of the financial amount.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us about your complaint regarding the payment protection insurance policy sold to you through our branch. I am sorry for the frustration and uncertainty you have experienced, and I want to address the concerns you have raised directly.

**Forwarding your complaint to the insurer**

When we wrote to you three weeks after receiving your complaint, we informed you that we were forwarding your complaint to the insurer on the basis that they may be solely responsible for the matter you raised. Our rules require us to do this promptly and to provide you with the other firm's contact details at the same time. I recognise that our letter did not include the insurer's name or contact details, and I apologise for that. This meant you were left without the information you needed to follow up your complaint directly, which should not have happened.

We are urgently looking into this and will write to you again as soon as possible with the insurer's full name and contact details so that you can chase your complaint with them directly.

**The delay in forwarding**

I also note your concern that three weeks passed before we wrote to you. We are reviewing the handling of your complaint to understand why it took that long, and I will update you on our findings.

**What happens next**

Once the insurer receives your complaint, they are required to handle it as though it had been made directly to them, and the standard regulatory timescales apply from the date they received it. We will confirm those details to you once we have provided you with the insurer's contact information.

If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint about our handling to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. Their website is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk and their telephone number is 0800 023 4567. You have six months from the date of our final response to do so. I have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet for your reference.

Thank you again for raising this with us. I am sorry that our handling fell short of the standard you should have received.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a respondent forwarding a complaint to do so promptly and to inform the complainant in a final response of the other respondent's contact details; both requirements appear to have been breached here.

Provides that once the insurer received the forwarded complaint, it is treated as if the complaint had been made directly to it from the date of receipt, meaning the insurer's eight-week clock runs from that date.
Indicates that standard time limits apply to the receiving respondent from the date it receives the forwarded complaint.
Requires a final response to be issued within eight weeks of receipt of the complaint, and sets out the required contents including FOS referral rights and enclosure of the FOS explanatory leaflet.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.8 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of a payment protection contract, knowledge by the complainant that there was a problem with the sale of the payment protection contract generally (for example where there has been a rejection of a claim on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion, or the complainant has received a customer contact letter explaining that they may have been mis-sold) would not in itself ordinarily be sufficient to establish for the purposes of the three-year time period in DISP 2.8.2R(2) that the complainant had become aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he or she had cause for complaint in respect of a failure to make the disclosure set out at DISP App 3.3A.2E (relating to failure to disclose commission).

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# DISP — DISP 1.7 Complaints forwarding rules

## DISP 1.7.1 (Rules)

A respondent that has reasonable grounds to be satisfied that another respondent may be solely or jointly responsible for the matter alleged in a complaint may forward the complaint, or the relevant part of it, in writing to that other respondent, provided it:

(1) does so promptly;

(2) informs the complainant promptly in a final response of why the complaint has been forwarded by it to the other respondent, and of the other respondent's contact details; and

(3) where jointly responsible for the fault alleged in the complaint, it complies with its own obligations under this chapter in respect of that part of the complaint it has not forwarded.

## DISP 1.7.2 (Rules)

When a respondent receives a complaint that has been forwarded to it under DISP 1.7.1 R, the complaint is treated for the purposes of DISP as if made directly to that respondent, and as if received by it when the forwarded complaint was received.

## DISP 1.7.3 (Guidance)

On receiving a forwarded complaint, the standard time limits will apply from the date on which the respondent receives the forwarded complaint.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 7. Procedures

### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints

A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination.

All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.

### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement

Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must:

1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected.
2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer.
3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

### 7.3 Investigation Process

The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include:

1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems.
2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made.
3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I took out a payment protection insurance policy with your bank in 2002 and cancelled it in 2005. I recently heard from a friend that I might have been mis-sold PPI and decided to complain. Your bank has sent me a letter saying my complaint is 'time-barred' and that you won't look into whether I was mis-sold the policy. However, the letter simply states my complaint is rejected due to time limits — it doesn't explain my right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service or give me the six-month deadline for doing so. I feel this is unfair as I believe I should at least have been told about my options, even if you consider the complaint too old.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
The customer's complaint raises a valid procedural point. Under DISP 1.8.1R, a respondent may reject a time-barred complaint without considering the merits, but it must do so by way of a final response that complies with DISP 1.6.2R. DISP 1.6.2R requires that a final response: encloses a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; provides the FOS website address; informs the complainant they may refer the complaint to the FOS if dissatisfied; and indicates whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R, using the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R.

On the substance of the time bar: the PPI sale took place in 2002 and the policy was cancelled in 2005. Under DISP 2.8.9R, the FOS cannot consider a PPI mis-sale complaint unless the complainant referred it to the respondent or FOS on or before 29 August 2019 (or one of the other conditions in DISP 2.8.9R(2) is met). The customer has not indicated when they made their complaint — you must confirm this. If the complaint was made after 29 August 2019, the time bar under DISP 2.8.9R is likely to apply and the FOS would not be able to consider it unless an exception applies (e.g. exceptional circumstances, or the respondent consents). The general six-year/three-year limits in DISP 2.8.2R are also relevant as a backstop.

However, regardless of the substantive time bar position, the bank's letter appears to have been deficient: it did not inform the customer of their right to refer to the FOS, did not enclose the FOS leaflet or provide the FOS website, and did not include the prescribed wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R indicating the respondent's position on waiving time limits. This is a breach of DISP 1.6.2R (as required by DISP 1.8.1R). A remedying final response must be issued.

Key assumptions to confirm before sending:
1. When exactly did the customer submit their complaint to the bank? This determines which time-bar provisions apply and whether the DISP 2.8.9R PPI deadline has passed.
2. Does the bank consent to waive any of the applicable time limits (DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.9R)? This determines which Annex 3R wording to use.
3. Has the FOS explanatory leaflet been prepared for enclosure?

Human review is required before sending — the complaint involves a potential procedural failing by the bank, the substantive time-bar position depends on facts not yet confirmed, and the correct Annex 3R wording depends on the bank's consent decision. The draft below assumes the bank does NOT consent to waive the DISP 2.8.2R time limits and that the complaint was made after 29 August 2019 (making DISP 2.8.9R applicable). Adjust the Annex 3R wording if the bank's position differs.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for your complaint and for bringing this matter to our attention. We are sorry that our earlier letter did not set out your options clearly. We want to put that right.

We have reviewed your complaint about the sale of your payment protection insurance policy, which was taken out in 2002 and cancelled in 2005.

**Our position on your complaint**

Having considered your complaint, we are not able to investigate the merits of whether the policy was mis-sold to you. This is because complaints about the sale of payment protection insurance were subject to a deadline: to be considered, a complaint needed to have been referred to us or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019. We do not have a record of a complaint from you before that date. We are therefore rejecting your complaint on the grounds that it falls outside the applicable time limits.

We recognise this may be disappointing, and we are sorry that our previous letter did not explain this properly or tell you about your right to take the matter further.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge — but you must do so within six months of the date of this letter.

The Ombudsman might not be able to consider your complaint if it was made after 29 August 2019 and does not fall within one of the limited exceptions to that deadline. Whether your complaint falls within those exceptions is a matter for the Ombudsman to decide. If the Ombudsman agrees with our view on timing, they will not have our permission to consider your complaint and will only be able to do so in very limited circumstances — for example, if the Ombudsman considers that any delay was due to exceptional circumstances.

We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, which explains how to refer a complaint and the time limits that apply. You can also find further information at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.

If you have any questions about this letter, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Name]
[Job title]
[Bank name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Permits the respondent to reject a complaint outside the FOS time limits without considering the merits, but requires it to do so in a final response complying with DISP 1.6.2R.

Sets out the mandatory content of a final response, including enclosing the FOS leaflet, providing the FOS website, informing the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and including prescribed wording on time-limit waiver — all of which were absent from the bank's original letter.
Provides that the FOS cannot consider a PPI mis-sale complaint unless it was referred to the respondent or FOS on or before 29 August 2019, subject to specified exceptions.
Sets out the general six-year and three-year time limits for referring complaints to the FOS, and the six-month limit from the date of the final response.

Indicates that the six-month time limit for FOS referral is only triggered by a response that qualifies as a final response and must tell the complainant about that limit — reinforcing why the original letter was deficient.

Prescribes the wording to be used in a final response where the complaint is outside the DISP 2.8.2R(2) time limits and the respondent does not consent to waive those limits but does consent to waive the six-month limit.

# DISP — DISP 1.8 Complaints time barring rule

## DISP 1.8.1 (Rules)

If a respondent receives a complaint which is outside the time limits for referral to the Financial Ombudsman Service (see DISP 2.8 ) it may reject the complaint without considering the merits, but must explain this to the complainant in a final response in accordance with DISP 1.6.2 R or DISP 1.6.2AR .

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.8 Complaints time barring rule

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.5 (Rules)

The six-year and the three-year time limits do not apply where:

(1) [deleted]

(2) the complaint concerns a contract or policy which is the subject of a review directly or indirectly under:

(a) the terms of the Statement of Policy on 'Pension transfers and Opt-outs' issued by the FSA on 25 October 1994; or

(b) the terms of the policy statement for the review of specific categories of FSAVC business issued by the FSA on 28 February 2000.

## DISP 2.8.6 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage, the receipt by the complainant of a letter which states that there is a risk (rather than a high risk) that the policy would not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount is not, itself, sufficient to cause the three year time period in DISP 2.8.2R (2) to start to run.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.8 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of a payment protection contract, knowledge by the complainant that there was a problem with the sale of the payment protection contract generally (for example where there has been a rejection of a claim on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion, or the complainant has received a customer contact letter explaining that they may have been mis-sold) would not in itself ordinarily be sufficient to establish for the purposes of the three-year time period in DISP 2.8.2R(2) that the complainant had become aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he or she had cause for complaint in respect of a failure to make the disclosure set out at DISP App 3.3A.2E (relating to failure to disclose commission).

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.
