# MCOB — MCOB 2A.4 Early repayment

## MCOB 2A.4.1 (Rules)

(1) An MCD mortgage lender must give a consumer who enters into an MCD regulated mortgage contract the right to discharge fully or partially his obligations under that MCD regulated mortgage contract prior to its expiry.

(2) If the consumer exercises the right in (1), the MCD mortgage lender must reduce the total cost of the credit to the consumer by an amount equal to the interest and costs for the remaining duration of the MCD regulated mortgage contract.

[Note: article 25(1) of the MCD]

## MCOB 2A.4.2 (Rules)

MCOB 2A.4.1R (1) does not prevent an MCD mortgage lender from imposing an early repayment charge in accordance with MCOB 12.3.1 R.

## MCOB 2A.4.3 (Guidance)

MCOB 7A.3 sets out the MCOB disclosure rules in relation to early repayment.

# DISP — DISP App 1.4 Policy reconstruction

## DISP App 1.4.15 (Guidance)

Example 9

Example 9

Term extends beyond retirement age: example of failure to explain investment risks

Background

45 year old male non-smoker, having taken out a £50,000 loan in 1998 for a term of 25 years. Unsuitable sale identified on the grounds of affordability and complaint raised on 12th anniversary.

It has always been the intention of the complainant to retire at state retirement age 65.

Term from date of sale to retirement is 20 years and the maturity date of the mortgage is five years after retirement.

In addition, an endowment does not meet the complainant's attitude to investment risk and a repayment mortgage would have been taken out if properly advised.

Established facts

Surrender value (on the 25 year policy) at time complaint assessed:

£12,500

Capital repaid under repayment mortgage of term to retirement date (20 years):

£21,000

Surrender value less capital repaid:

(£8.500)

Difference in outgoings (repayment - endowment):

£5,400

Cost of converting from endowment mortgage to repayment mortgage:

£200

Basis of compensation:

The surrender value of the (25 year term) endowment policy is compared to the capital that would have been repaid to date under a repayment mortgage arranged to repay the loan at retirement age, in this example, a repayment mortgage for a term of 20 years. The complainant has gained from lower outgoings of the endowment mortgage to date. In calculating the redress, the gain may be offset against the loss unless the complainant's particular circumstances are such that it would be unreasonable to take account of the gain. The conversion costs are also taken into account in calculating the redress.

Redress generally

Loss from surrender value less capital repaid:

(£8,500)

Gain from total lower outgoings under endowment mortgage:

£5,400

Cost of converting to a repayment mortgage:

(£200)

Net loss:

(£3,300)

Therefore total redress is:

£

# MCOB — MCOB 5.6 Content of illustrations

## MCOB 5.6.88 (Rules)

(1) In calculating example cash amounts in accordance with MCOB 5.6.84 R(1)(f), it must be assumed that:

(a) the regulated mortgage contract is repaid in full;

(b) unless the original amount borrowed is used, that all payments due on the regulated mortgage contract are actually paid;

(c) additional fees and charges such as insurance premiums have been paid; and

(d) no underpayments or overpayments have been made.

(2) If:

(a) cashbacks or other incentives need to be repaid; or

(b) fees need to be paid;

the amounts that would need to be repaid or paid must be included in the example cash amounts.

(3) Where the calculation of the early repayment charge is based on the interest rate charged on the regulated mortgage contract or on interest rates generally, the interest rates used for the calculation of the example cash amounts must be those in force at the date that the illustration is issued to the customer.

(4) The example cash amounts must reflect the maximum charge in a particular year. Where it is possible to state exact early repayment charges (that is, where all such charges are based on the original amount borrowed), the illustration must do so.

## MCOB 5.6.89 (Rules)

Where the cash examples from MCOB 5.6.88 R included in the illustration would vary either if the interest rate charged on the regulated mortgage contract changed or with changes in interest rates generally, an appropriate warning that the early repayment charges may vary from the cash examples must be included in the illustration.

# MCOB — MCOB 9.4 Content of illustrations

## MCOB 9.4.88 (Rules)

(1) In calculating example cash amounts in accordance with MCOB 9.4.83 R(1)(e), it must be assumed that:

(a) the lifetime mortgage is repaid in full;

(b) all payments due (if applicable) on the lifetime mortgage are actually paid;

(c) additional fees and charges such as insurance premiums have been paid; and

(d) no underpayments or overpayments (if applicable) have been made.

(2) If:

(a) cashbacks or other incentives need to be repaid; or

(b) fees need to be paid;

the amounts that would need to be repaid or paid must be included in the example cash amounts.

(3) Where the calculation of the early repayment charge is based on the interest rate charged on thelifetime mortgage, or on interest rates generally, the interest rate(s) used for the calculation of the example cash amounts must be those in force at the date that the illustration is issued to the customer.

(4) The example cash amounts must reflect the maximum charge in a particular year. Where it is possible to state exact early repayment charges (that is, where all such charges are based on the original amount borrowed), the illustration must do so.

## MCOB 9.4.89 (Rules)

Where the cash examples from MCOB 9.4.88 R included in the illustration would vary either if the interest rate charged on the lifetime mortgage changed or with changes in interest rates generally, an appropriate warning that the early repayment charges may vary from the cash examples must be included in the illustration.

## MCOB 9.4.90 (Rules)

Where the early repayment charge could be higher than those stated in the illustration if the lifetime mortgage continued after the end of the term estimated in accordance with MCOB 9.4.10 R or MCOB 9.4.12 R, Section 13 of the illustration must include a clear statement to that effect.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us about the early repayment charge of £8,400 applied when you remortgaged last month.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing draft opens by acknowledging the specific complaint and accurately reflects its subject matter.
The draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor provide reasons for a decision; it only says the complaint is being investigated.
There is no mention of the complainant’s right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service in the customer draft.
The draft does not specify the six‑month period from the final response within which the complainant may refer the matter to the FOS.
The final‑response draft contains no reference to the firm’s eight‑week deadline for a final response or the option to refer after eight weeks.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry to hear that this has caused you significant financial difficulty, and I want to assure you that we are taking your concerns seriously… We understand that this situation has had a real impact on your finances and your peace of mind, and we will treat your complaint as a priority.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the complainant’s concerns, uses empathetic language, and does not dismiss or minimise the issue, demonstrating fair treatment.
The draft does not address whether any redress will be offered or explain why none may be due; it only states the investigation is ongoing.

verdicts.evidence: The customer is 74 years old, on a fixed pension income, and has suffered significant financial hardship — having had to use savings set aside for care costs to pay the charge. This is a clear vulnerability indicator.
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer recognises the vulnerability indicators and recommends appropriate handling, satisfying the ‘either’ audience requirement.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us about the early repayment charge of £8,400 applied when you remortgaged last month. I am sorry to hear that this has caused you significant financial difficulty, and I want to assure you that we are taking your concerns seriously. You have raised a number of important points: that you were not given a clear explanation of how the charge was calculated or what costs it was intended to cover; that you do not recall the charge being disclosed in your original mortgage illustration or offer document; and that you have not received a substantive response since you first contacted us over three weeks ago.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with a clear acknowledgement of the complaint, accurately reflects its subject matter (the £8,400 ERC, lack of explanation, non-disclosure, and handling delay), and demonstrates understanding of the complainant's concerns and distress.

The response is a holding letter, not a final response. It states that the firm 'is now investigating' and will 'write to you again with our findings and our response' but does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) or provide reasons engaging with the substantive points. The handler answer confirms this is not yet a final response.

The customer draft contains no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service or the right to refer the complaint to the FOS. The handler answer notes that 'it is not yet a final response' and that 'if this letter is intended to serve as a final response, it must be redrafted accordingly and the FOS referral rights included.' Since this is marked as a final response stage but the draft does not include FOS referral rights, this element is missing.

The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS from the date of the final response. This is a final response stage, yet no such deadline is communicated to the customer.

Although this is marked as a final response stage (not a holding response), the customer draft does not set out an eight-week timescale or explain the complainant's option to refer to the FOS once eight weeks have passed. The letter states 'We will write to you again with our findings' but does not specify when, and does not reference the eight-week clock or the FOS option at that point.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry to hear that this has caused you significant financial difficulty, and I want to assure you that we are taking your concerns seriously... I apologise for that delay... We understand that this situation has had a real impact on your finances and your peace of mind, and we will treat your complaint as a priority.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages substantively with the complaint, acknowledges the complainant's distress and the three-week delay, sets out the specific areas being investigated, and uses clear, empathetic, non-dismissive language. It does not minimise the concerns and treats the complaint seriously.

The customer draft does not address redress at all. The complaint plainly raises a financial loss (£8,400 charge, use of care savings, financial hardship), yet the response neither offers redress nor explains why none is warranted. The handler answer notes that 'the question of redress (potentially refund of the £8,400 or part thereof) will need to be assessed' but this position is not communicated to the customer.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: 'The customer is 74 years old, on a fixed pension income, and has suffered significant financial hardship — having had to use savings set aside for care costs to pay the charge. This is a clear vulnerability indicator. Any response must be handled with particular care, and the complaint should be escalated appropriately.' Customer draft: 'We understand that this situation has had a real impact on your finances and your peace of mind, and we will treat your complaint as a priority.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer clearly identifies and recognises the vulnerability indicators (age, fixed pension income, financial hardship, use of care savings) and recommends appropriate escalation and careful handling. However, the customer draft does not explicitly acknowledge these specific vulnerability factors; it uses only generic supportive language ('real impact on your finances and your peace of mind'). The vulnerability is recognised in the handler reasoning but not adequately reflected in the customer-facing communication or adapted handling.

claims.claim: An early repayment charge must be expressible as a cash value and be a reasonable pre‑estimate of the lender’s costs.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.3.1R states the two limbs: (1) cash value and (2) reasonable pre‑estimate of costs.

claims.claim: A firm should not use the ‘Rule of 78’ method to calculate early repayment charges.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.3.2G guidance explicitly says a firm should not use the Rule of 78.

claims.claim: Before entering into a regulated mortgage contract, the firm must disclose the maximum early repayment charge in the illustration and in the offer document.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.3.4R requires disclosure of the maximum ERC in the illustration (per MCOB 5) and in the offer document (per MCOB 6.4.1R).

claims.claim: The illustration must include, under the heading ‘What happens if you do not want this mortgage any more?’, an explanation of whether early repayment charges are payable, when they are payable, the basis of calculation, example cash amounts, and the maximum charge as a cash amount.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 5.6.84R (and similarly MCOB 9.4.83R) list these exact items as required content of the illustration.

claims.claim: The customer contacted the firm over three weeks ago and has not received a substantive response; the firm should check its internal complaints handling timelines.
claims.reasoning: No DISP or other provision concerning specific complaint‑handling time limits was retrieved.

claims.claim: The customer is 74 years old, on a fixed pension income, and has used savings set aside for care costs to pay the charge, indicating vulnerability.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provisions do not contain any rule defining vulnerability criteria.

claims.claim: Any response must be handled with particular care and the complaint should be escalated appropriately due to vulnerability.
claims.reasoning: No specific FCA rule on escalation for vulnerable customers is present in the supplied provisions.

claims.claim: If breaches are confirmed, redress (potentially a refund of the £8,400) must be assessed and decided by a human.
claims.reasoning: The provisions supplied do not set a rule that redress decisions must be made by a human or specify the amount.

claims.claim: The draft does not admit liability, quantify redress, or include a final response FOS referral notice; if intended as a final response, it must be redrafted to include FOS referral rights.
claims.reasoning: No provision about the content of a final response or mandatory FOS referral notice is included in the retrieved set.

claims.claim: The firm will investigate whether the early repayment charge calculation meets the requirements that apply to such charges.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.3.1R sets the requirements for ERCs (cash value and reasonable pre‑estimate).

claims.claim: The firm will investigate whether the charge and its basis were properly disclosed to the customer before the mortgage was entered into.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.3.4R (and MCOB 5.6.84R) require disclosure of the maximum ERC and related information before entering the contract.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to complain about my home insurance policy. I am a retired 78-year-old and I took out a home contents and buildings policy through your branch last year. When I purchased the policy, I specifically told your adviser that I also rent out a small annexe attached to my property on a buy-to-let basis, and asked whether this would be covered. I was assured it would be fine under my standard home policy. However, when I recently made a claim for storm damage to the annexe roof, you rejected it saying the annexe is a commercial let property and isn't covered under my consumer home insurance policy. I feel I was mis-sold this policy — I was never told I needed a separate commercial policy for the annexe, and your adviser should have categorised my needs properly. I'm now facing a £4,200 repair bill I cannot afford on my pension. To make matters worse, I first raised this complaint by telephone over five weeks ago and have received no acknowledgement whatsoever — no letter, no email, nothing. I had to call again today and was told there was no record of my earlier call. This is completely unacceptable treatment of a loyal customer.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious concerns that require careful handling and immediate escalation.

**1. Vulnerability flag — URGENT**
The customer is 78 years old and retired, living on a pension, and is facing a £4,200 bill they state they cannot afford. This strongly indicates potential vulnerability. Under Crest Bank's Complaints Handling Procedure (section 7.7), you must immediately flag this complaint as involving a potentially vulnerable customer in the CMS, prioritise the investigation, and aim to issue a final response within four weeks rather than the standard eight. You should also consider whether additional support or referral to specialist services is appropriate.

**2. Acknowledgement failure — URGENT**
The customer states they raised this complaint by telephone over five weeks ago and have received no acknowledgement. Under Crest Bank's procedure (section 7.2, referencing DISP 1.6.2R), a written acknowledgement must be sent within five business days of receipt. If the original call was indeed a complaint and was not logged, this is a procedural breach. You must:
- Investigate whether the original call was received and why it was not logged in the CMS.
- Send an acknowledgement immediately (the draft below serves this purpose).
- Consider whether a regulatory deadline breach has occurred — if the complaint is now over five weeks old with no acknowledgement, the eight-week final response clock may already be running from the original call date. This must be assessed urgently.

**3. Potential escalation required**
The complaint involves potential financial redress of £4,200. Under section 8.3 of the Complaints Handling Procedure, any complaint involving financial redress exceeding £10,000 triggers mandatory escalation — this complaint is below that threshold, but given the vulnerability indicators, the acknowledgement failure, and the potential mis-sale allegation, you should consider escalating to the Head of Customer Operations and CCO in any event.

**4. Substance of the complaint — mis-sale allegation**
The customer alleges they were mis-sold the policy because the adviser failed to identify that the annexe let constituted a risk requiring separate cover, despite being told about it at point of sale. This is a substantive mis-sale allegation. The retrieved provisions include ICOBS 8.1.2A (Guidance) and ICOBS 8.1.2B (Rule) relating to claim rejection, which are relevant to the claim rejection element. However, the retrieved provisions do not fully cover the mis-sale/suitability assessment aspects of this complaint (e.g., ICOBS rules on advised sales and suitability). You should flag `insufficient_context` accordingly and ensure a qualified reviewer assesses the full regulatory position on the mis-sale before any substantive response is issued.

**5. Claim rejection**
ICOBS 8.1.2A(G) provides FCA guidance that rejection of a consumer's claim may be unreasonable in certain circumstances. ICOBS 8.1.2B(R) is relevant where a claim is rejected for breach of a condition or warranty — you will need to assess whether the rejection falls within the scope of that rule. This requires legal/compliance input before any position is communicated to the customer.

**6. FOS referral rights**
Given the complaint is already over five weeks old (and may be approaching or past the eight-week deadline from the original call), you must be prepared to issue a final response or a holding letter promptly. The customer must be informed of their right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service at the appropriate time.

**Recommended immediate actions:**
1. Flag as vulnerable customer in CMS now.
2. Investigate the original call record urgently — establish the true receipt date.
3. Send the acknowledgement letter below today.
4. Escalate to Head of Customer Operations given vulnerability, acknowledgement failure, and mis-sale allegation.
5. Refer the claim rejection and mis-sale substance to Compliance for assessment against ICOBS before issuing any substantive response.
6. Assess whether the eight-week deadline is at risk given the original call date.

**ASSUMPTION:** The draft below treats today as the acknowledgement date and does not commit to a specific final response date, because the true complaint receipt date (the original call) is uncertain. If the original call date can be confirmed, the final response deadline runs from that date and must be recalculated. Do not send the draft without confirming this.

Human review is required before sending — see flags above.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for contacting us, and I am sorry that you have had to call us again today. I want to acknowledge your complaint and apologise unreservedly for the fact that you did not receive any response following your initial call over five weeks ago. This is not the standard of service we expect to provide, and I understand how frustrating and worrying this must be, particularly given the circumstances you have described.

I am writing to confirm that we have now registered your complaint. Your complaint reference number is [REFERENCE NUMBER], and your complaint is being handled by [HANDLER NAME], who can be contacted at [CONTACT DETAILS].

Your complaint concerns two matters:
1. The rejection of your claim for storm damage to the annexe roof of your property; and
2. Your concern that you were not properly advised at the point of sale about the cover you needed, given that you told the adviser about the annexe let at the time.

We take both of these concerns very seriously. We will investigate them fully and impartially, reviewing all relevant records including the details of your original policy sale.

We aim to send you our final response as quickly as possible. We will keep you informed of our progress. If for any reason we are unable to resolve your complaint within eight weeks of the date it was first raised with us, we will write to you to explain why and to set out your options at that stage.

If at any point you are unhappy with the progress of your complaint, or if you do not feel we have resolved it to your satisfaction once we have issued our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with full details of how to do this at the appropriate time.

If there is anything you would like to discuss in the meantime, or if you would find it helpful to communicate with us in a different format or through a different channel, please do not hesitate to contact [HANDLER NAME] directly.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank Customer Operations
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a written acknowledgement to be sent to the complainant within five business days of receipt — the customer has received no acknowledgement despite the complaint being raised over five weeks ago, indicating a breach of this requirement.
FCA guidance indicating that rejection of a consumer's claim may be unreasonable in certain circumstances, relevant to assessing whether the rejection of the storm damage claim was appropriate.

For contracts entered into on or after 1 August 2017, rejection of a consumer policyholder's claim for breach of a condition or warranty is unreasonable unless the circumstances of the claim are connected to the breach — relevant to the basis on which the claim was rejected.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 1. Document Control

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Policy Title** | Customer Complaints Handling Procedure |
| **Version** | 2.1 |
| **Effective Date** | 1 February 2025 |
| **Next Review Date** | 31 January 2026 |
| **Document Owner** | Priya Sharma, Head of Customer Operations |
| **Approving Body** | Compliance Oversight Committee |

---

## 2. Purpose

This policy establishes Crest Bank's framework for receiving, investigating, and resolving customer complaints in a manner that is fair, consistent, and compliant with FCA requirements. It ensures that customers who are dissatisfied with Crest Bank's products or services receive a timely and transparent response, and that complaint data is used systematically to drive service improvement. The policy applies to all regulated and unregulated complaints received across Crest Bank's digital channels.

---

## 3. Scope

This policy applies to:

- **Staff:** All customer-facing staff in Customer Operations, as well as staff in Product, Risk, Compliance, and Technology who may be involved in complaint investigation or root cause analysis.
- **Products:** Personal current accounts, instant-access and fixed-term savings accounts, personal loans, and SME business current accounts.
- **Customer Segments:** All retail customers (approximately 850,000) and SME customers (approximately 12,000).
- **Channels:** In-app messaging, email, web contact form, and any written correspondence received at Crest Bank's registered address.
- **Systems:** Crest Bank's complaint management system (CMS), core banking platform, and customer identity and verification (CIV) system.

---

# ICOBS — ICOBS 8.1 Insurers: general

## ICOBS 8.1.2A (Guidance)

(1) Cases in which rejection of a consumer’s claim would be unreasonable (in the FCA’s view) include, but are not limited to rejection:

(a) for misrepresentation, unless it is a qualifying misrepresentation (see ICOBS 8.1.3R);

(b) where the claim is subject to the Insurance Act 2015, for breach of warranty or term, or for fraud, unless the insurer is able to rely on the relevant provisions of the Insurance Act 2015; and

(c) where the policy is drafted or operated in a way that does not allow the insurer to reject.

(2) The Insurance Act 2015 sets out a number of situations in which an insurer may have no liability or obligation to pay. For example:

(a) section 10 provides situations in which an insurer has no liability under a policy due to a breach of warranty;

(b) section 11 places restrictions on an insurer’s ability to reject a claim for breach of a term where compliance is aimed at reducing certain types of risk; and

(c) sections 12 and 13 provide for the extent to which a firm is entitled to reject fraudulent claims.

## ICOBS 8.1.2B (Rules)

For contracts entered into or variations agreed on or after 1 August 2017, a rejection of a consumer policyholder's claim for breach of a condition or warranty (that is not subject to and within section 10 or 11 of the Insurance Act 2015) is unreasonable unless the circumstances of the claim are connected to the breach.

Example 8

Term extends beyond retirement age and policy reconstruction

Background

45 year old male non-smoker, having taken out a £50,000 loan in 1998 for a term of 25 years. Unsuitable sale identified on the grounds of affordability and complaint raised on 12th policy anniversary.

It has always been the intention of the complainant to retire at State retirement age 65.

Term from date of sale to retirement is 20 years and the maturity date of the mortgage is 5 years after retirement.

£111.20

£12,500

£21,300

Difference in policy values at time complaint assessed:

£8,800

Difference in outgoings (20 year policy - 25 year policy):

£4,320

Basis of compensation

The policy is reconstructed as if it had been set up originally on a term to mature at retirement age, in this example, a term of 20 years. The difference in the current value of the policy actually sold to the complainant and the current value of the reconstructed policy, as if the premium on the reconstructed policy had been paid from outset, is calculated. The complainant has gained from lower outgoings (lower premiums) of the actual endowment policy to date. In calculating the redress, the gain may be offset against the loss unless the complainant's particular circumstances are such that it would be unreasonable to take account of the gain.

Redress generally if it is not unreasonable to take account of the whole of the gain from lower outgoings

(£8,800)

retrieved_chunks.content:
# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 7. Procedures

### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints

A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination.

All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.

### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement

Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must:

1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected.
2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer.
3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

### 7.3 Investigation Process

The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include:

1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems.
2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made.
3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis

In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows:

1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure.
2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes.
3. **Formal RCA:** Where a theme generates five or more upheld complaints within a rolling 30-day period, or where a single complaint indicates a systemic product or process failure, the Head of Customer Operations will commission a formal RCA report within **ten business days**.
4. The formal RCA report will be shared with the relevant business area owner and the CCO, who will agree a remediation plan with defined owners and completion dates.
5. Remediation plans will be tracked in the CMS and reported to the Executive Risk Committee monthly until closure.

### 7.7 Vulnerable Customers

Where a complaint is received from a customer who has been identified as potentially vulnerable, as defined in Crest Bank's Vulnerable Customer Policy, the complaints handler must:

1. Flag the complaint as involving a vulnerable customer in the CMS.
2. Prioritise the investigation and aim to issue a final response within **four weeks** rather than the standard eight weeks.
3. Offer alternative communication formats or channels if requested by the customer.
4. Consider whether the circumstances giving rise to the complaint indicate that additional support or referral to specialist services is appropriate.

---

# Treating Customers Fairly Policy

---

## 1. Document Control

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Policy Title** | Treating Customers Fairly Policy |
| **Version** | 2.1 |
| **Effective Date** | 1 February 2025 |
| **Next Review Date** | 31 January 2026 |
| **Document Owner** | Rachel Whitfield, Chief Compliance Officer |
| **Approving Body** | Compliance Oversight Committee |

---

## 2. Purpose

This policy sets out Crest Bank's commitment to ensuring that fair treatment of customers is embedded throughout the organisation, from product design through to post-sale service and complaint resolution. It gives effect to the FCA's Consumer Duty requirements and the longstanding Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) framework, translating regulatory obligations into actionable standards for all staff. The policy provides a consistent basis against which Crest Bank's conduct can be assessed, monitored, and improved.

---

## 3. Scope

This policy applies to:

- **Staff:** All Crest Bank employees, contractors, and third-party agents who interact with customers, design products, develop communications, or make decisions affecting customer outcomes.
- **Products and services:** Personal current accounts, instant-access and fixed-term savings accounts, personal loans, and SME business current accounts.
- **Customer segments:** All retail customers (approximately 850,000) and SME customers (approximately 12,000), including prospective customers at the pre-sale stage.
- **Systems and channels:** All digital channels through which Crest Bank delivers services, including the mobile application, web platform, in-app messaging, and automated decisioning systems.

This policy does not apply to Crest Bank's relationships with wholesale counterparties or institutional clients.

---

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 8. Monitoring, Reporting and Escalation

### 8.1 Complaint MI

The Head of Customer Operations will produce a monthly Complaint MI Report covering:

- Total complaints received, broken down by product, channel, and complaint category.
- Complaints resolved within timescale vs. those breaching the eight-week deadline.
- Upheld rate, partial upheld rate, and rejection rate.
- Total financial redress paid.
- Complaints referred to the FOS and outcomes of FOS decisions.
- Open RCA actions and their status.

### 8.2 Reporting to Governance Bodies

| Report | Recipient | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Complaint MI Report | Executive Risk Committee | Monthly |
| Complaint MI Summary | Compliance Oversight Committee | Monthly |
| Complaint Trends Summary | Board Risk Committee | Quarterly |
| FCA Complaints Return (DISP 1 Annex 1) | FCA (via RegData) | Half-yearly |

### 8.3 Escalation

- Any complaint involving potential regulatory breach, media risk, or financial redress exceeding £10,000 must be escalated to the CCO and Head of Customer Operations within **one business day** of identification.
- Any complaint indicating potential financial crime must be referred to the MLRO on the same business day.
- Any complaint indicating a material data breach must be referred to the DPO on the same business day, who will assess notification obligations under UK GDPR.
- Systemic issues identified through RCA that cannot be remediated within 30 days must be escalated to the Executive Risk Committee for direction.

---

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 6. Roles and Responsibilities

| Role | Responsibilities Under This Policy |
|---|---|
| **Priya Sharma, Head of Customer Operations** | Policy owner; oversees day-to-day complaint handling operations; approves final response letters for complex or high-value complaints; ensures adequate resourcing of the complaints team. |
| **Rachel Whitfield, Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)** | Ensures the policy remains compliant with DISP and other applicable rules; reviews complaint MI; chairs the Compliance Oversight Committee agenda item on complaints. |
| **James Patel, MLRO** | Reviews complaints that indicate potential financial crime, fraud, or suspicious activity and determines whether a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) is required. |
| **Fatima Al-Rashid, Data Protection Officer (DPO)** | Reviews complaints that raise data protection or privacy concerns; liaises with the ICO where required. |
| **Tom Hargreaves, Chief Technology Officer (CTO)** | Responsible for complaints arising from platform or technology failures; supports root cause analysis for technology-related complaints. |
| **Complaints Handlers (Customer Operations)** | Receive, log, acknowledge, investigate, and resolve complaints within prescribed timescales; draft final response letters; escalate where required. |
| **Executive Risk Committee** | Receives monthly complaint MI; directs remedial action where systemic issues are identified. |
| **Board Risk Committee** | Receives quarterly complaint MI summary; provides board-level oversight of complaint trends and outcomes. |

---

verdicts.evidence: I want to acknowledge your complaint and apologise unreservedly for the fact that you did not receive any response following your initial call over five weeks ago... Your complaint concerns two matters: 1. The rejection of your claim for storm damage to the annexe roof of your property; and 2. Your concern that you were not properly advised at the point of sale...
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing draft opens by explicitly acknowledging the complaint, apologising for the lack of response, and restates the two specific issues raised, matching the definition of acknowledgement.
The draft does not state any firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor provide reasons for a decision; it only says a final response will be sent later.

verdicts.evidence: you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with full details of how to do this at the appropriate time.
verdicts.reasoning: The text informs the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS and promises to give the details, satisfying the requirement.
The draft mentions the right to refer to the FOS but does not specify the six‑month period from the date of the final response.

verdicts.evidence: If for any reason we are unable to resolve your complaint within eight weeks of the date it was first raised with us, we will write to you to explain why and to set out your options at that stage.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing text clearly sets out the eight‑week deadline for the firm’s final response and links it to the complainant’s subsequent options.

verdicts.evidence: We take both of these concerns very seriously. We will investigate them fully and impartially... If there is anything you would like to discuss in the meantime, or if you would find it helpful to communicate with us in a different format or through a different channel, please do not hesitate to contact...
verdicts.reasoning: The language is apologetic, non‑dismissive, and promises an impartial investigation, demonstrating fair treatment.
The draft does not address whether any monetary redress will be offered or explain why none would be offered, despite the complaint involving a £4,200 loss.

verdicts.evidence: The customer is 78 years old and retired, living on a pension, and is facing a £4,200 bill they state they cannot afford... you must immediately flag this complaint as involving a potentially vulnerable customer...
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer identifies vulnerability indicators and instructs the firm to flag the complaint and consider additional support, satisfying the element (audience either).

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for contacting us, and I am sorry that you have had to call us again today. I want to acknowledge your complaint and apologise unreservedly for the fact that you did not receive any response following your initial call over five weeks ago... Your complaint concerns two matters: 1. The rejection of your claim for storm damage to the annexe roof of your property; and 2. Your concern that you were not properly advised at the point of sale about the cover you needed, given that you told the adviser about the annexe let at the time.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with a clear acknowledgement of the complaint and accurately reflects both substantive elements: the claim rejection and the mis-sale allegation. It also acknowledges the procedural failure (lack of response to the initial call). This satisfies the PRESENT band.

This is a holding response, not a final response. The customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) on either the claim rejection or the mis-sale allegation. It commits only to investigation and promises a future final response. While appropriate for a holding letter, this means the decision_and_reasons element is not present. The handler answer correctly flags that compliance input is needed before any substantive position is communicated.

verdicts.evidence: If at any point you are unhappy with the progress of your complaint, or if you do not feel we have resolved it to your satisfaction once we have issued our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with full details of how to do this at the appropriate time.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft mentions the right to refer to the FOS and identifies it by name, but does not provide the means to do so (contact details, leaflet reference, or website). It defers provision of 'full details' to 'the appropriate time' (i.e., the final response). For a holding letter this may be acceptable practice, but the element as defined requires the means to be given. This falls into PARTIAL: the right is stated but the mechanism is omitted.

The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline running from the date of the final response. It mentions the right to refer to the FOS but does not specify the six-month window. This is a holding letter, not a final response, so the six-month clock has not yet started; however, the element requires that the final response inform the complainant of the six-month limit. Since this is a holding letter, the element is not yet applicable, but it is not present in the draft. MISSING.

verdicts.evidence: We aim to send you our final response as quickly as possible. We will keep you informed of our progress. If for any reason we are unable to resolve your complaint within eight weeks of the date it was first raised with us, we will write to you to explain why and to set out your options at that stage.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft sets out the eight-week timescale and references the complainant's options 'at that stage' (implying the FOS option once eight weeks have passed). The language 'if for any reason we are unable to resolve your complaint within eight weeks' and 'set out your options at that stage' communicates that the FOS becomes available after eight weeks. This satisfies the PRESENT band for a holding response.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry that you have had to call us again today... This is not the standard of service we expect to provide, and I understand how frustrating and worrying this must be, particularly given the circumstances you have described. We take both of these concerns very seriously. We will investigate them fully and impartially, reviewing all relevant records including the details of your original policy sale.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft is clear, even-handed, and non-dismissive. It acknowledges the procedural failure, validates the complainant's concerns, commits to impartial investigation, and uses language appropriate to the complainant's circumstances (acknowledging the worry and frustration). It does not minimise or dismiss the complaint. This satisfies the PRESENT band.

This is a holding letter and does not reach a position on redress. The customer draft does not address whether redress or remedial action is appropriate — it commits only to investigation. While this is appropriate for a holding response, the element as defined requires that the response 'addresses whether redress or remedial action is appropriate' and either offers redress or explains why none is due. A holding letter that defers this question entirely does not satisfy the element. MISSING.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: 'The customer is 78 years old and retired, living on a pension, and is facing a £4,200 bill they state they cannot afford. This strongly indicates potential vulnerability... you must immediately flag this complaint as involving a potentially vulnerable customer in the CMS, prioritise the investigation, and aim to issue a final response within four weeks rather than the standard eight. You should also consider whether additional support or referral to specialist services is appropriate.' Customer draft: 'If there is anything you would like to discuss in the meantime, or if you would find it helpful to communicate with us in a different format or through a different channel, please do not hesitate to contact [HANDLER NAME] directly.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer clearly identifies vulnerability (age 78, retired, pension, financial hardship) and recommends appropriate action (flag in CMS, prioritise, four-week target, consider specialist support). However, the customer draft does not acknowledge or reflect the vulnerability. It offers generic flexibility in communication but does not signal that the firm recognises the complainant's circumstances or is taking tailored action. The vulnerability is recognised and acted upon in the handler answer but not communicated to the customer. This is PARTIAL: the response acts on vulnerability in the recommended handling but does not adapt the customer-facing communication to reflect it.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am a 72-year-old retired customer and I invested £45,000 in a structured product through my bank two years ago. I was told it was low-risk and suitable for my retirement income needs. In reality, it was a high-risk product and I have lost approximately £12,000. When I complained to my bank three weeks ago, they wrote back saying they were not responsible because the product was manufactured by another investment firm, and they forwarded my complaint to that other firm. However, my bank was the one that advised me to buy it and assessed my risk profile. They have not addressed their own role in the advice at all, and I have not received any substantive response from either firm. I am on a fixed pension income and this loss has caused me significant financial hardship — I am struggling to pay my utility bills. I want my bank to properly investigate their part in this and provide appropriate redress for the unsuitable advice they gave me.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious regulatory concerns that require careful handling and mandatory human review before any response is sent.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Suitability of advice:** The customer alleges the bank recommended a high-risk structured product as low-risk and suitable for their retirement income needs. Under COBS 9A.2.20AR, a firm must not make a recommendation where none of the insurance-based investment products are suitable for the client. COBS 9A.2.21G further indicates that a transaction may be unsuitable due to the risks of the associated financial instruments or the type of transaction. If the bank provided a personal recommendation, it cannot deflect responsibility to the product manufacturer — the advice obligation sits with the advising firm.

2. **Deflection of complaint to manufacturer:** The bank's response forwarding the complaint to the product manufacturer and disclaiming responsibility for its own advisory role is a significant concern. Crest Bank's own complaints handling procedure (section 5.3) requires impartial investigation of each complaint without allowing commercial considerations to influence the outcome. The bank must investigate its own role in the advice, not simply redirect the complaint.

3. **Timescales:** The complaint was received three weeks ago. Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in section 7.4 of the complaints handling procedure), a final response must be issued within eight weeks of receipt. The bank has approximately five weeks remaining. If no substantive response has been provided, the handler should ensure the investigation is progressed urgently. If eight weeks cannot be met, a written response explaining the delay and confirming FOS referral rights must be issued at that point.

4. **FOS referral rights:** The customer must be informed of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service in the final response letter, including FOS contact details and the six-month time limit from the date of the final response to refer.

5. **Customer vulnerability:** The customer is 72 years old, retired, on a fixed pension income, and has disclosed significant financial hardship (struggling to pay utility bills). This is a strong vulnerability indicator. Under Crest Bank's Vulnerable Customers Policy (sections 7.3 and 7.3.2, referencing PRIN 2A and BCOBS 5), communications must be reviewed to ensure they are clear, appropriately toned, and do not create additional distress. Reasonable adjustments should be considered. This must be flagged on the customer's account.

6. **Redress:** The complaint involves a claimed loss of approximately £12,000 and potential financial redress. Under section 7.4 of the complaints handling procedure, any final response involving financial redress of £500 or more must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. Do not commit to any specific redress figure in the customer letter at this stage — the investigation must be completed first.

**Recommended actions:**
- Flag the customer as potentially vulnerable on the system immediately and consider reasonable adjustments to communications.
- Escalate to the Head of Customer Operations given the complexity, potential redress, and vulnerability indicators.
- Investigate the bank's own advisory role thoroughly — do not rely on the manufacturer's investigation as a substitute.
- Do not send the draft customer letter below without Head of Customer Operations countersignature, given the financial redress element.
- Confirm whether the bank provided a personal recommendation (as opposed to execution-only or basic advice), as this determines which suitability obligations apply.
- Confirm the exact date the complaint was received to track the eight-week deadline.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending:**
- That the bank did provide a personal recommendation (the draft below proceeds on that assumption).
- That the complaint was received three weeks ago and the eight-week deadline has not yet passed.
- That no substantive acknowledgement or interim response has already been sent to the customer.
- The retrieved provisions do not include the full text of DISP 1.6.2R directly from the FCA Handbook — the obligation is reflected in Crest Bank's internal procedure. The handler should verify the current DISP 1.6.2R wording independently if needed.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced, and I want to assure you that we take your concerns very seriously.

**What your complaint is about**

You have told us that two years ago you invested £45,000 in a structured product on the basis of advice from us, that you were told the product was low-risk and suitable for your retirement income needs, and that you have since suffered a loss of approximately £12,000. You have also told us that our previous response did not address our own role in the advice you received, and that this loss is causing you significant financial hardship.

We understand how distressing this situation must be, and we recognise the impact it is having on you.

**What we are doing**

We are now investigating your complaint fully, including our own role in the advice and suitability assessment we carried out at the time of your investment. We are not able to rely on the product manufacturer's investigation as a substitute for our own, and we will consider all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion.

We aim to send you our final response within eight weeks of the date we received your complaint. If for any reason we are unable to do so, we will write to you before that deadline to explain why and to confirm your options.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you remain dissatisfied with our final response, or if you have not received a final response within eight weeks of making your complaint, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at:

- **Website:** www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- **Telephone:** 0800 023 4567

You will have six months from the date of our final response letter to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

**A note about your circumstances**

You have mentioned that you are on a fixed pension income and are experiencing financial difficulty as a result of this situation. We want to make sure we communicate with you in a way that is helpful and appropriate. If there is anything we can do to make this process easier for you — for example, if you would prefer to speak with someone directly or would like information in a different format — please do not hesitate to let us know.

We will be in touch as soon as our investigation is complete.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires that a firm must not make a personal recommendation on an insurance-based investment product where none of the products are suitable for the client, directly relevant to the allegation of unsuitable advice.

Indicates that a transaction may be unsuitable due to the risks of the associated financial instruments or the type of transaction, supporting the assessment of whether the structured product recommendation was appropriate.

Requires the firm to issue a final response within eight weeks of receipt of the complaint and to inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, as reflected in the bank's complaints handling procedure.

Requires Crest Bank to investigate each complaint impartially without allowing commercial considerations to influence the outcome, meaning the bank cannot deflect responsibility to the product manufacturer.
Requires final response letters involving financial redress of £500 or more to be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.
Requires the bank to notify the complainant of FOS referral rights in the final response and in any eight-week written response, including FOS contact details and the six-month referral time limit.

Requires that communications with customers who have an active vulnerability flag are reviewed to ensure they are clear, appropriately toned, and do not create additional distress, applicable given the customer's age, fixed income, and disclosed financial hardship.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# DISP — DISP App 1.4 Policy reconstruction

## DISP App 1.4.15 (Guidance)

Example 9

Example 9

Term extends beyond retirement age: example of failure to explain investment risks

Background

45 year old male non-smoker, having taken out a £50,000 loan in 1998 for a term of 25 years. Unsuitable sale identified on the grounds of affordability and complaint raised on 12th anniversary.

It has always been the intention of the complainant to retire at state retirement age 65.

Term from date of sale to retirement is 20 years and the maturity date of the mortgage is five years after retirement.

In addition, an endowment does not meet the complainant's attitude to investment risk and a repayment mortgage would have been taken out if properly advised.

Established facts

Surrender value (on the 25 year policy) at time complaint assessed:

£12,500

Capital repaid under repayment mortgage of term to retirement date (20 years):

£21,000

Surrender value less capital repaid:

(£8.500)

Difference in outgoings (repayment - endowment):

£5,400

Cost of converting from endowment mortgage to repayment mortgage:

£200

Basis of compensation:

The surrender value of the (25 year term) endowment policy is compared to the capital that would have been repaid to date under a repayment mortgage arranged to repay the loan at retirement age, in this example, a repayment mortgage for a term of 20 years. The complainant has gained from lower outgoings of the endowment mortgage to date. In calculating the redress, the gain may be offset against the loss unless the complainant's particular circumstances are such that it would be unreasonable to take account of the gain. The conversion costs are also taken into account in calculating the redress.

Redress generally

Loss from surrender value less capital repaid:

(£8,500)

Gain from total lower outgoings under endowment mortgage:

£5,400

Cost of converting to a repayment mortgage:

(£200)

Net loss:

(£3,300)

Therefore total redress is:

£

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 1. Document Control

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Policy Title** | Customer Complaints Handling Procedure |
| **Version** | 2.1 |
| **Effective Date** | 1 February 2025 |
| **Next Review Date** | 31 January 2026 |
| **Document Owner** | Priya Sharma, Head of Customer Operations |
| **Approving Body** | Compliance Oversight Committee |

---

## 2. Purpose

This policy establishes Crest Bank's framework for receiving, investigating, and resolving customer complaints in a manner that is fair, consistent, and compliant with FCA requirements. It ensures that customers who are dissatisfied with Crest Bank's products or services receive a timely and transparent response, and that complaint data is used systematically to drive service improvement. The policy applies to all regulated and unregulated complaints received across Crest Bank's digital channels.

---

## 3. Scope

This policy applies to:

- **Staff:** All customer-facing staff in Customer Operations, as well as staff in Product, Risk, Compliance, and Technology who may be involved in complaint investigation or root cause analysis.
- **Products:** Personal current accounts, instant-access and fixed-term savings accounts, personal loans, and SME business current accounts.
- **Customer Segments:** All retail customers (approximately 850,000) and SME customers (approximately 12,000).
- **Channels:** In-app messaging, email, web contact form, and any written correspondence received at Crest Bank's registered address.
- **Systems:** Crest Bank's complaint management system (CMS), core banking platform, and customer identity and verification (CIV) system.

---

# COBS — COBS 9A.2 Assessing suitability: the obligations

## COBS 9A.2.20A (Rules)

When providing a personal recommendation on an insurance-based investment product in accordance with COBS 9A.2.1R and COBS 9A.2.16R, a firm must not make a recommendation where none of the insurance-based investment products are suitable for the client.

[Note: article 9(6) of the IDD Regulation]

## COBS 9A.2.21 (Guidance)

(1) A transaction may be unsuitable for a client due to the risks of the associated financial instruments, the type of transaction, the characteristics of the order or the frequency of the trading.(1A) An insurance-based investment product may be unsuitable for a client due to the risks of the underlying investment assets, the type or characteristics of the product or the frequency of switching of underlying investment assets.(2) A series of transactions, each of which are suitable when viewed in isolation may be unsuitable if the recommendation or the decisions to trade are made with a frequency that is not in the best interests of the client.(3) In the case of portfolio management, a transaction might be unsuitable if it would result in an unsuitable portfolio.[Note: recital 9 to the IDD Regulation]

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# COBS — COBS 9.6 Special rules for giving basic advice on a stakeholder product

## COBS 9.6.10 (Rules)

When a firm gives basic advice it must not:

(1) describe or recommend a stakeholder product outside the firm's range; or

(2) describe or recommend a smoothed linked long term stakeholder product; or

(3) describe fund choice, or recommend a particular fund, if a stakeholder product offers a choice of funds; or

(4) recommend the level of contributions required to be made to a stakeholder pension scheme to achieve a specific income in retirement; or

(5) recommend or agree that a client makes a contribution to an ISA which exceeds the HM Revenue & Customs ISA limits.

## COBS 9.6.11 (Rules)

(1) If a firm starts the sales process for a stakeholder product that is not a deposit-based stakeholder product, it must not depart from that process unless it has advised the retail client that it will not provide basic advice on stakeholder products during the period of departure. A firm that does that must not provide basic advice during the departure period.

(2) Before a firm returns to the sales process for stakeholder products, it must tell the retail client that that process is about to recommence.

## COBS 9.6.12 (Rules)

A firm must only recommend a stakeholder product to a retail client if:

(1) it has taken reasonable steps to assess the client's answers to the scripted questions and any other facts, circumstances or information disclosed by the client during the sales process;

(2) (unless the relevant product is a deposit-based stakeholder product) having done so, it has reasonable grounds for believing that the stakeholder product is suitable for the client; and

(3) the firm reasonably believes that the client understands the firm's advice and the basis on which it was provided.

## COBS 9.6.13 (Guidance)

COBS 9 Annex 2 gives guidance on the steps a firm could take to help it meet these suitability obligations.

# Vulnerable Customers Policy

7.2.5 Customers will be informed that a note has been made on their account to help Crest Bank provide appropriate support, and will be advised of their right to request its removal.

### 7.3 Making Reasonable Adjustments

7.3.1 Upon identifying a vulnerability indicator, staff will consider and offer adjustments appropriate to the customer's circumstances. Adjustments may include, but are not limited to:

- Providing communications in alternative formats (e.g. larger text, simplified language, or audio).
- Allowing additional time during telephone interactions.
- Directing the customer to Crest Bank's dedicated vulnerability support line.
- Facilitating third-party account access under Crest Bank's Third Party Mandate procedure.
- Temporarily pausing collection activity or fee application where a customer discloses acute financial difficulty.
- Signposting to external support services (e.g. StepChange, Mind, Citizens Advice).

7.3.2 In accordance with PRIN 2A and BCOBS 5, all communications with customers who have an active vulnerability flag will be reviewed to ensure they are clear, appropriately toned, and do not create additional distress or confusion.

7.3.3 Staff will not pressure a vulnerable customer to complete a transaction or application where the customer has expressed uncertainty or distress.

### 7.4 Product Suitability and Design

7.4.1 In accordance with PRIN 12 and PRIN 2A, Product Managers will conduct a vulnerability impact assessment as part of the standard product approval and review process. This assessment will consider whether the product's features, terms, eligibility criteria, and associated communications are appropriate for customers who may be in vulnerable circumstances.

7.4.2 Where a product assessment identifies that a product or feature carries elevated risk of harm for vulnerable customers, the Product Governance Committee will determine whether design changes, additional safeguards, or targeted elig

# COBS — COBS 19.10 Drawdown, investment pathways and cash warnings

## COBS 19.10.10 (Rules)

The requirements in this section do not apply to a firm:(1) in relation to sums or assets in a retail client’s pension scheme that the retail client requests to use to purchase a fixed-term product that:(a) provides a guaranteed income, a guaranteed capital return or both, to the retail client or the retail client’s beneficiary; and(b) does not involve any investment risk to the retail client if the retail client remains in the product for the fixed term;(2) when the firm carries out the retail client’s previous instructions to designate their funds on a regular basis into a drawdown fund;(3) when the retail client has been taken through the investment pathways by another firm (F1) and has been referred to the firm’s (F2’s) drawdown fund to invest in one of the pathway investments that the firm (F2) offers (see COBS 19.10.1R(7));(4) when the firm has determined, on reasonable grounds, that the retail client has received a personal recommendation in relation to the action referred to in COBS 19.10.8R(1) or (2); or(5) when the firm has provided a targeted support service to the retail client, or the firm arranged for another firm to provide that targeted support service, where:(a) the targeted support service includes a recommendation to the client on how they should invest their drawdown funds;(b) the retail client has accepted and is taking steps to action the recommendation referred to in (a); and(c) as part of the design and delivery of that targeted support service, the firm providing the targeted support service considered the factors that a firm would need to take into account in following the requirements in this section.
