# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# FEES — FEES 5.5E Representative case fees (for financial years beginning on or after 1 April 2026)

## FEES 5.5E.1 (Rules)

FEES 5.5E applies to a complainant representative in relation to a complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service which is closed on or after 1 April 2026.

## FEES 5.5E.2 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E does not apply to the Voluntary Jurisdiction.

## FEES 5.5E.3 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E sets out when a complainant representative that is representing a complainant must pay fees in respect of complaints referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## FEES 5.5E.4 (Rules)

A complainant representative will not be liable for a representative case fee under FEES 5.5E if it is acting entirely pro bono in relation to the complaint.

## FEES 5.5E.5 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E.4R applies where a complainant representative is representing the complainant without any fees, charges or other form of remuneration becoming payable by the complainant in any circumstance.

## FEES 5.5E.6 (Rules)

Where a complaint is closed by the Financial Ombudsman Service during a financial year in circumstances where:(1) the complaint was referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or after 1 April 2025; and(2) a category B complainant representative was (at any time) representing the complainant in relation to that complaint, the category B complainant representative which was first representing the complainant (even if it is no longer doing so at the time the complaint is closed) must pay to the FOS Ltd:(3) the reduced representative case fee specified in FEES 5 Annex 5R Part 2, if the complaint was closed as a change in outcome in favour of the complainant; or(4) the standard representative case fee specified in FEES 5 Annex 5R Part 2, if the complaint was closed other than as a change in outcome in favour of the complainant.

metadata.section_name: FEES 5.5E Representative case fees (for financial years beginning on or after 1 April 2026)

# DISP — DISP 1.10B Payment services and electronic money complaints reporting

## DISP 1.10B.1 (Directions)

(1) Once a year a credit institution that provides payment services or issues electronic money must provide the FCA with a complete report concerning complaints received about payment services and electronic money.

(2) Once a year an electronic money institution, a payment institution or a registered account information service provider must provide the FCA with a complete report concerning complaints received about payment services and electronic money.

(3) The report required by (1) and (2) must be set out in the format in DISP 1 Annex 1AD.

## DISP 1.10B.2 (Guidance)

(1) In contrast to the other provisions in DISP 1 which generally apply only to complaints from eligible complainants, the complaints reporting directions apply in addition to complaints from payment service users that are not eligible complainants.

(2) Payment service providers are reminded that regulation 101 of the Payment Services Regulations contains requirements relating to complaints resolution procedures applicable to complaints from payment service users that are not eligible complainants.

## DISP 1.10B.3 (Directions)

A respondent must not include in the report a complaint that has been forwarded in its entirety to another respondent under the complaints forwarding rules.

## DISP 1.10B.4 (Directions)

Where a respondent has forwarded to another respondent only part of a complaint or where two respondents may be jointly responsible for a complaint, then the complaint should be reported by both respondents.

# DISP — DISP 1.10B Payment services and electronic money complaints reporting

## DISP 1.10B.9 (Directions)

The relevant reporting period is the year immediately following:

(1) where the respondent has an accounting reference date, its accounting reference date; and

(2) where the respondent does not have an accounting reference date, 31 December each year.

## DISP 1.10B.10 (Directions)

Reports are to be submitted to the FCA within 30 business days of the end of the relevant reporting periods through, and in the electronic format specified in, the FCA complaints reporting system or the appropriate section of the FCA website.

## DISP 1.10B.11 (Directions)

If a respondent is unable to submit a report in electronic format because of a systems failure of any kind, the respondent must notify the FCA, in writing and without delay, of that systems failure.

## DISP 1.10B.12 (Rules)

(1) If a respondent does not submit a complete report by the date on which it is due, in accordance with DISP 1.10B.10D, the respondent must pay the administrative fee specified in SUP 16.3.14R.(2) The administrative fee in (1) does not apply if the respondent has notified the FCA of a systems failure in accordance with DISP 1.10B.11R.

## DISP 1.10B.13 (Directions)

A closed complaint is a complaint where:

(1) the respondent has sent a final response; or

(2) the complainant has indicated in writing acceptance of the respondent’s earlier response under DISP 1.6.4R (where applicable).

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 1. Document Control

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Policy Title** | Customer Complaints Handling Procedure |
| **Version** | 2.1 |
| **Effective Date** | 1 February 2025 |
| **Next Review Date** | 31 January 2026 |
| **Document Owner** | Priya Sharma, Head of Customer Operations |
| **Approving Body** | Compliance Oversight Committee |

---

## 2. Purpose

This policy establishes Crest Bank's framework for receiving, investigating, and resolving customer complaints in a manner that is fair, consistent, and compliant with FCA requirements. It ensures that customers who are dissatisfied with Crest Bank's products or services receive a timely and transparent response, and that complaint data is used systematically to drive service improvement. The policy applies to all regulated and unregulated complaints received across Crest Bank's digital channels.

---

## 3. Scope

This policy applies to:

- **Staff:** All customer-facing staff in Customer Operations, as well as staff in Product, Risk, Compliance, and Technology who may be involved in complaint investigation or root cause analysis.
- **Products:** Personal current accounts, instant-access and fixed-term savings accounts, personal loans, and SME business current accounts.
- **Customer Segments:** All retail customers (approximately 850,000) and SME customers (approximately 12,000).
- **Channels:** In-app messaging, email, web contact form, and any written correspondence received at Crest Bank's registered address.
- **Systems:** Crest Bank's complaint management system (CMS), core banking platform, and customer identity and verification (CIV) system.

---

verdicts.evidence: I am writing to you about the complaint you raised with us on 15 March 2025 regarding the £250 account maintenance fee applied to your current account.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately restates the issue (undisclosed £250 fee).
The draft provides no decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) and gives no reasons; it only states the investigation is ongoing.

verdicts.evidence: In the meantime, I want to make sure you are aware of your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service... You can reach them at: Financial Ombudsman Service Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk Telephone: 0800 023 4567
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS and provides contact details.

verdicts.evidence: Once we do send you our final response, you will also have six months from the date of that letter to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service if you remain dissatisfied.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft states the six‑month deadline measured from the date of the final response.

verdicts.evidence: We should have provided you with a full response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint, and we have not done so... Because we have not issued our final response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint, you are entitled to contact the Financial Ombudsman Service now...
verdicts.reasoning: The draft sets out the eight‑week deadline, acknowledges it was missed, and links the missed deadline to the complainant’s right to refer to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry that we have not been in touch sooner... We take this seriously and are reviewing your account records and the relevant terms and conditions carefully.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is apologetic, non‑dismissive, and shows the firm is investigating the issue impartially.
The draft does not address whether a refund or other redress will be offered, nor does it explain why none might be given.

verdicts.evidence: Vulnerability indicator: The customer has identified themselves as a pensioner on a fixed income for whom £250 is a significant sum. This is a potential vulnerability indicator and must be factored into how the complaint is handled and communicated.
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer recognises the vulnerability indicator and instructs appropriate handling; audience is either, so this satisfies the element.

verdicts.evidence: I am writing to you about the complaint you raised with us on 15 March 2025 regarding the £250 account maintenance fee applied to your current account.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately reflects its subject matter: the undisclosed £250 account maintenance fee. The acknowledgement is specific and directly addresses what the customer complained about.

The customer draft is explicitly framed as a holding/update letter, not a final response. It states 'We are continuing to investigate your complaint as a matter of urgency' and 'We will write to you again with our full findings and decision as soon as our investigation is complete.' No decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) is stated, and no substantive reasons engaging with the specific points are provided. The handler answer confirms this is not a final response and should not be sent if the investigation is complete.

verdicts.evidence: Because we have not issued our final response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint, you are entitled to contact the Financial Ombudsman Service now, free of charge, if you do not wish to wait for our response. You can reach them at: Financial Ombudsman Service Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk Telephone: 0800 023 4567
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the right to refer to the FOS and provides specific contact details (website and telephone number). It makes clear the complainant can refer this complaint to the FOS and explains how to do so.

verdicts.evidence: Once we do send you our final response, you will also have six months from the date of that letter to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service if you remain dissatisfied.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft explicitly states the six-month deadline running from the date of the firm's final response. This is correctly anchored to the final response date, not to the current holding letter.

verdicts.evidence: We should have provided you with a full response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint, and we have not done so... Because we have not issued our final response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint, you are entitled to contact the Financial Ombudsman Service now, free of charge, if you do not wish to wait for our response.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft communicates the eight-week timescale and explains that because it has been missed, the complainant may now refer to the FOS without waiting for the firm's final response. This correctly sets out both the firm's eight-week deadline and the complainant's option to go to the FOS once eight weeks have passed.

verdicts.evidence: I want to apologise unreservedly. We should have provided you with a full response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint, and we have not done so... I recognise that this has caused you unnecessary frustration and uncertainty, and I am sorry for that. We take this seriously and are reviewing your account records and the relevant terms and conditions carefully.
verdicts.reasoning: The response demonstrates fair treatment by: (1) offering an unreserved apology for the delay; (2) acknowledging the specific failure (missed eight-week deadline and lack of progress updates); (3) recognising the customer's frustration; (4) taking the substance of the complaint seriously; and (5) using clear, non-dismissive language that engages with the complainant's concerns. The tone is respectful and appropriate.

verdicts.evidence: Your complaint concerns a charge that you tell us was not disclosed to you when you opened your account and was applied without prior notice. We take this seriously and are reviewing your account records and the relevant terms and conditions carefully.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft acknowledges the substance of the complaint (undisclosed charge) and signals that it is being investigated, but reaches no clear position on redress. The handler answer notes that redress (refund and compensation) should be considered but is not committed to in the draft, and that a decision requires human sign-off once the investigation is complete. The customer-facing text does not address whether redress is appropriate, only that the matter is under investigation. This is appropriate for a holding letter but means redress is not clearly addressed in the customer communication.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: 'The customer has identified themselves as a pensioner on a fixed income for whom £250 is a significant sum. This is a potential vulnerability indicator and must be factored into how the complaint is handled and communicated... Record the vulnerability indicator in the CMS and ensure communications are appropriately tailored.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer explicitly identifies the vulnerability indicator (pensioner on fixed income, financial hardship) and recommends action: recording it in the CMS and tailoring communications appropriately. The customer draft itself uses clear, supportive language ('I am sorry for that', 'I want to make sure you are aware', 'If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact me directly') that is appropriate to a vulnerable complainant. The vulnerability is recognised and acted upon in the recommended handling.

claims.claim: The acknowledgement sent on 16 March failed to meet the requirements that the acknowledgement must confirm the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.
claims.reasoning: Provision 2 (section 7.2) explicitly states that a written acknowledgement must confirm the reference number, handler name/contact details and the expected resolution timescale, matching the claim.

claims.claim: The acknowledgement also failed to inform the customer of their right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service; DISP 1.6.2R requires this right to be communicated in the final response and in a written response issued at eight weeks if no final response has yet been sent.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (section 7.5) mandates that the FOS referral right must be notified in the final response and in a written response at the eight‑week point when a final response is not yet issued.

claims.claim: The eight‑week deadline for a final response has been missed, constituting a breach of the eight‑week requirement under DISP 1.6.2R.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (section 7.4) requires a final response to be issued within eight weeks of receipt; missing this deadline is a breach.

claims.claim: Any final response involving financial redress of £500 or more requires countersignature by the Head of Customer Operations.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 states that final response letters for complaints with redress of £500+ (or with regulatory significance) must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations.

claims.claim: The vulnerability indicator (customer being a pensioner on a fixed income) must be factored into how the complaint is handled and communicated.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision mentions a requirement to treat vulnerability indicators in this specific way.

claims.claim: The investigation of the £250 fee must be completed (or its status confirmed) before issuing a final response.
claims.reasoning: Provision 2 (section 7.3) requires a fair and impartial investigation before a conclusion is reached, but it does not explicitly state that a final response cannot be sent until the investigation is complete; the claim extends beyond the wording.

claims.claim: We should have provided you with a full response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint, and we have not done so.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (section 7.4) sets the eight‑week deadline for a final response.

claims.claim: We should also have kept you informed of progress along the way.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (point 2) states the bank must communicate clearly with the customer at every stage, supporting the general duty to keep the complainant informed, though no specific rule on progress updates is quoted.

claims.claim: Because we have not issued our final response within eight weeks of receiving your complaint, you are entitled to contact the Financial Ombudsman Service now, free of charge, if you do not wish to wait for our response.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (section 7.5) requires the firm to notify the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS at the eight‑week point when a final response is not yet issued; the entitlement described aligns with this requirement.

claims.claim: Once we do send you our final response, you will also have six months from the date of that letter to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service if you remain dissatisfied.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (section 7.5) specifies that the communication of FOS referral rights must state the six‑month period from the date of the final response.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I opened a new savings account with your bank last month after seeing your promotional email comparing your interest rates to those of three competitor banks. The email claimed your rates were 'market-leading' and showed a comparison table, but it didn't mention that your rate was only a temporary introductory rate for 3 months, after which it drops to 0.5%. The competitors' rates shown were their standard ongoing rates. I feel this comparison was unfair and misleading — I wouldn't have switched if I'd known the rate would drop so dramatically. I transferred £40,000 from my previous provider and have now missed their loyalty bonus as a result. When I raised this issue at my branch three weeks ago, the branch manager told me it wasn't their responsibility because the email was sent by your 'digital marketing partner' and forwarded my complaint to them. I've heard nothing since from either party. I want to know who is actually dealing with my complaint and I want compensation for the interest I've lost by switching.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several distinct regulatory issues, all of which require careful handling and human review before any response is sent.

**1. Potentially misleading promotional communication (BCOBS 2.2 / BCOBS 2.3.7B)**
The customer's allegation is that the promotional email compared your savings rate with competitors' rates without disclosing that your rate was a temporary introductory rate, while the competitors' rates shown were their standard ongoing rates. BCOBS 2.3.7BR requires that any comparison of the cost of a currency transfer service be meaningful and presented in a fair and balanced way — however, the more directly applicable provision for a savings account interest rate comparison is the general fair, clear and not misleading rule in BCOBS 2.2. The retrieved provisions include BCOBS 2.2.5A and 2.2.5B (Guidance) on misleading exchange rate presentations, and BCOBS 2.3.7BR (Rule) on fair and balanced comparisons. While these provisions are not perfectly tailored to a savings rate comparison (they focus on currency transfer services), they reflect the general principle that comparisons must be fair and balanced and not give a misleading impression. The allegation — that an introductory rate was compared against competitors' permanent rates without disclosure — is a strong indicator of a potentially misleading communication. You should also note BCOBS 2.2A.1R(7), which requires that where more than one rate applies, each must be shown with equal prominence, and BCOBS 4.1(3) and (3A), which require prominent notice of material interest rate changes. Flag this for your compliance/legal team.

**2. Summary box obligations (BCOBS 2.2A.1R)**
If the promotional email was a 'direct offer financial promotion' in relation to a savings account, BCOBS 2.2A.1R requires it to include a summary box in the prescribed form. BCOBS 2.2A.2G(3) indicates that where a rate may change depending on conditions or time elapsed, the firm should incorporate the table showing rates with and without the bonus/introductory element. If the email did not include this, there may be a breach of BCOBS 2.2A.1R. You should check whether the email constituted a direct offer financial promotion and whether a compliant summary box was included.

**3. Responsibility for the communication — digital marketing partner**
The branch manager's suggestion that the bank is not responsible because the email was sent by a 'digital marketing partner' is unlikely to be correct as a regulatory matter. Under FCA rules, a firm remains responsible for financial promotions it communicates or approves, regardless of whether a third party produced or distributed them. You should confirm with your compliance team who approved and/or communicated the promotion, as the firm's regulatory obligations attach to the communication itself.

**4. Complaint handling — no response in three weeks**
The customer raised this complaint at the branch three weeks ago and has heard nothing from either the bank or the marketing partner. You should check your DISP obligations on complaint acknowledgement and timelines — note that the retrieved provisions do not include DISP provisions, so I cannot cite specific time limits from the retrieved context. However, the absence of any response is a significant concern and should be escalated urgently. Flag this to your complaints team immediately.

**5. Compensation claim**
The customer is seeking compensation for lost interest (estimated at the loyalty bonus foregone on £40,000). This is a financial redress question that I cannot assess from the retrieved provisions alone. It requires human review by your complaints/legal team, taking into account the merits of the misleading communication allegation and any applicable redress framework.

**Recommended actions:**
- Acknowledge the complaint to the customer promptly (see customer draft below).
- Escalate internally to compliance/legal to assess: (a) whether the promotional email breached BCOBS 2.2A.1R and/or the fair and balanced comparison requirement; (b) who is responsible for the communication; (c) the complaint handling timeline position under DISP.
- Do not make any admission of liability or promise of compensation in the customer response at this stage.
- Human review is required before sending.

**Assumptions to confirm:** (1) Whether the email was a 'direct offer financial promotion' within the meaning of BCOBS. (2) Whether the bank approved or communicated the email (even if produced by a third party). (3) The exact date the complaint was first received, to assess DISP deadline position. (4) Whether any acknowledgement has already been sent to the customer.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch about your concerns regarding the promotional email you received about our savings account, and I am sorry that you have not received a proper response since raising this at your branch three weeks ago.

I want to reassure you that your complaint is being taken seriously. We are responsible for the communications we send to our customers, and we are looking into the concerns you have raised about the way our interest rate was presented in that email — in particular, whether the comparison with other providers' rates was presented in a fair and balanced way, and whether all relevant information about our rate was made clear.

We are also investigating the delay in responding to you and will ensure that your complaint is handled by the right team within our organisation. You should not have been left without a response, and I apologise for that.

We will write to you again with a full response to your complaint as soon as our investigation is complete. In the meantime, if you have any questions or wish to provide any further information, please do not hesitate to contact us at [contact details].

Yours sincerely,
[Name]
[Job title]
[Bank name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires that any comparison of the cost of a service with that of another provider must be meaningful and presented in a fair and balanced way, which is relevant to the allegation that the promotional email compared an introductory rate against competitors' standard ongoing rates without disclosure.

Indicates that a communication is likely to be misleading if it presents a rate in a way likely to give the impression it is available when it is unlikely to be obtained in a typical transaction, relevant to the allegation that the introductory nature of the rate was not disclosed.

Indicates that including qualifying wording will not necessarily prevent a presentation from being misleading, relevant to assessing whether any small-print disclosure in the email would be sufficient.

Requires that a direct offer financial promotion for a savings account includes a prescribed summary box showing all applicable rates with equal prominence, which may have been breached if the introductory rate was not clearly distinguished.

Requires that where more than one rate of interest may apply, each must be shown with equal prominence and the lowest rate must not be given less prominence, directly relevant to the allegation that the post-introductory rate of 0.5% was not disclosed.

Indicates that where a rate may change depending on time elapsed or conditions, the firm should incorporate the prescribed table showing rates with and without the introductory element, relevant to whether the summary box (if included) was compliant.

Indicates that a firm should provide reasonable notice before a material change to a rate of interest that is to the disadvantage of a banking customer, relevant to whether the customer was adequately informed of the rate drop after the introductory period.

Indicates that the heading of any notice of a decrease in interest rate should clearly indicate that the rate is decreasing, relevant to the adequacy of any communications about the post-introductory rate.

# BCOBS — BCOBS 2.3 Other general requirements for communications and financial promotions

## BCOBS 2.3.7A (Guidance)

If a communication or a payment service or electronic money promotion compares a payment service or service in relation to electronic money with one or more other retail banking service, payment service or service in relation to electronic money (whether or not provided by the provider), the provider must ensure that the comparison is meaningful and presented in a fair and balanced way.

## BCOBS 2.3.7B (Rules)

If a communication or payment service or electronic money promotion compares the cost of a currency transfer service with the cost of a service provided by any other provider or providers (whether identified or not):

(1) the comparison must be meaningful and presented in a fair and balanced way; and

(2) the firm or other provider must be able to substantiate the claims made.

## BCOBS 2.3.7C (Guidance)

For the purpose of BCOBS 2.3.7BR the cost of a currency transfer service includes:

(1) any charges payable in relation to the currency conversion;

(2) any charges payable in relation to a connected payment service or electronic money issuance; and

(3) the margin between the exchange rate that would be offered to a majority of persons of the class at whom the promotion is directed and a currently applicable interbank exchange rate, calculated using an independently published interbank spot rate.

metadata.section_name: BCOBS 2.3 Other general requirements for communications and financial promotions

# CONC — CONC 2.5A Conduct of business: high-cost short-term credit (HCSTC) products on price comparison websites

## CONC 2.5A.7 (Guidance)

(1) The information displayed on the price comparison website (for example, information concerning a loan, the results of a search or claims about the market coverage of the website) will need to comply with the financial promotion rules in CONC 3. In particular, it will need to comply with the requirement for a communication or a financial promotion to be clear, fair and not misleading. The results of a search also need to comply with the detailed rules in CONC 3.5. In particular, the results will require a representative example. The relevant items of the representative example must be representative of what the firm reasonably expects, at the date on which the financial promotion is made, to be representative of credit agreements to which the representative APR applies and which are expected to be entered into as a result of the promotion.

(2) The fact that a lender or credit broker pays a commission to the firm or pays for advertising or other marketing on the price comparison website (and the amount of any such commission or payment) should not affect the ranking or prominence of display of the results of a search. Such payment should also not affect whether information about a loan from a lender or credit broker whose loans the firm arranges to compare or claims to compare appears in the results of a search.

(3) CONC 2.5A.6R does not require the firm to compare loans from a lender or credit broker where it has not arranged to do so with that lender or credit broker nor where it does not claim to compare loans from that lender or credit broker.

(4) The firm should ensure that any information concerning a loan or any result of a search which relates to another firm’s credit broking service states prominently that:

(a) the firm referred to is a credit broker and is not a lender; or

(b) if the firm referred to is both a

# BCOBS — BCOBS 2.2A Summary box for savings accounts

## BCOBS 2.2A.2 (Guidance)

(1) The summary box should be completed in accordance with the guidance set out in the notes to the summary box in BCOBS 2 Annex 1R.

(2) The requirement under BCOBS 2.2A.1R is to include the rate or rates of interest that apply to the savings account in the summary box itself. It is not, therefore, permissible to simply refer in the summary box to a separate webpage or document where the rate or rates of interest can be found.

(3) Where the rate of interest that applies to a savings account may change depending on the period that has elapsed since it was opened or on whether certain conditions are met, a firm should incorporate the table set out in BCOBS 2 Annex 2G in the row of the summary box that includes the heading “What is the interest rate?” unless it would be misleading to do so.

(4) The effect of BCOBS 2.2A.1R(1) is that the summary box must be incorporated in the direct offer financial promotion itself. It is not sufficient, for example, to include in a direct offer financial promotion that appears on a website a link to a separate page containing the summary box. BCOBS 2.2A.1R(2) provides a limited exception to this where a direct offer financial promotion is on paper, in which case the summary box may accompany the direct offer financial promotion as a separate document.

(5) The effect of BCOBS 2.2A.1R(8) is that any additional information in relation to a savings account that a firm chooses to provide must appear outside the summary box.

(6) A firm may wish to include a statement in close proximity to the summary box that the information provided in it is a summary of the key features of the savings account and is not intended to be a substitute for reading the terms and conditions that apply to the account.

# BCOBS — BCOBS 2.2 The fair, clear and not misleading rule

## BCOBS 2.2.5A (Guidance)

A communication, or payment service or electronic money promotion relating to a currency transfer service is likely to be misleading if it presents an exchange rate in a way that is likely to give the impression that the rate is available to a person or a class of persons if that rate is unlikely to be obtained by that person or class of persons with respect to a typical transaction.

## BCOBS 2.2.5B (Guidance)

The inclusion of wording in a communication or payment service or electronic money promotion to the effect that an exchange rate shown is not available to particular customers will not necessarily prevent the presentation of the exchange rate being misleading.

## BCOBS 2.2.6 (Guidance)

A communication or a financial promotion that refers to sums available by way of an authorised non-business overdraft agreement should make clear that such sums constitute borrowing or credit.

## BCOBS 2.2.7 (Guidance)

A firm is reminded of its obligations under ESG 4.3.1R in relation to a communication, or when it communicates or approves a financial promotion, that references the sustainability characteristics of a product or service.

metadata.section_name: BCOBS 2.2 The fair, clear and not misleading rule

# CONC — CONC 3.3 The clear fair and not misleading rule and general requirements

## CONC 3.3.11A (Guidance)

(1) Firms are reminded that the Consumer Protection from Unfair

Trading Regulations 2008 (SI 2008/1277), as well as Principle 7 and

CONC 3.3.1R, apply to communications and financial promotions in

relation to BNPL agreements, including communications with

borrowers under existing agreements.

(2) A communication or financial promotion in relation to a BNPL

agreement is likely to be misleading by omission if it:(a) refers to a zero percentage or low interest, introductory or

other promotional offer available under a BNPL agreement;(b) does so in a way that is likely to influence a customer’s

decision about whether to enter into a BNPL agreement or

whether and how to make use of credit available under an

existing BNPL agreement; and(c) does not also include in a fair and prominent manner material

information about relevant risks.

(3) A firm should also consider whether other communications or

financial promotions in connection with BNPL agreements could be

misleading by omission if those communications or financial

promotions do not also include in a fair and prominent manner

material information about relevant risks.

(4) Relevant risks relating to BNPL credit include the limitations that

apply to any zero percentage or low interest, introductory or other

FCA 2019/72

Page 4 of 6

promotional offer, including the circumstances in which interest or

charges could become payable and how these would be calculated if

those circumstances arose, including the date from which interest or

charges would accrue, the rate of that interest or those charges and

the amount of principal on which the interest would be charged. The

average consumer is likely to need information about these matters

to make an informed decision about whether to enter into a BNPL

agreement, or whether and how to make use of credit available under

an existing BNPL agreeme

# BCOBS — BCOBS 2.2A Summary box for savings accounts

## BCOBS 2.2A.1 (Rules)

(1) A firm must ensure that a direct offer financial promotion in relation to a savings account includes a summary box in the form set out in BCOBS 2 Annex 1R.

(2) In the case only of a direct offer financial promotion on paper, it is sufficient for the purposes of (1) if the direct offer financial promotion is accompanied by a summary box on a separate sheet.

(3) The summary box must contain, in the sequence of rows set out in the table in BCOBS 2 Annex 1R:

(a) the headings prescribed in the first column in the table in BCOBS 2 Annex 1R; and

(b) the corresponding information described in the second column in the table in BCOBS 2 Annex 1R.

(4) In the case of a direct offer financial promotion communicated by voice telephony:

(a) this rule (except for this paragraph) does not apply; and

(b) the firm must provide each of the items of information described in the second column in the table in BCOBS 2 Annex 1R (except for the information in the row that includes the heading “What would the estimated balance be after [x] months based on a £[x] deposit?”) orally in clear, easily understandable language during the telephone communication.

(5) The firm must present the summary box in clear, easily understandable language and in a prominent way.

(6) In the case of a direct offer financial promotion communicated in an electronic medium which is constrained by space, a firm may amend the form of the summary box where to do so would make the presentation of the information clearer in that particular electronic medium.

(7) Where more than one rate of interest may apply to a savings account, the summary box must show each rate of interest with equal prominence. In particular, the lowest rate of interest that may apply to the savings account must not be given any less prominence in the summary box than the other rate or rates of interest shown in the summary box.

(8) The summary box must not

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch about your concerns regarding the promotional email you received about our savings account, and I am sorry that you have not received a proper response since raising this at your branch three weeks ago.
verdicts.reasoning: The opening sentence directly acknowledges the complaint and accurately reflects the issue (the promotional email and lack of response).
The draft does not state any firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor provide reasons for a decision; it only says an investigation is ongoing.
There is no mention of the complainant’s right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service.
The draft does not set out the six‑month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: I want to reassure you that your complaint is being taken seriously... We are also investigating the delay in responding to you and will ensure that your complaint is handled by the right team... you should not have been left without a response, and I apologise for that.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is courteous, acknowledges the issues raised, promises investigation and apologises, demonstrating fair and non‑dismissive treatment.
The response does not address whether any redress or compensation will be offered, nor does it explain why none would be provided.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch about your concerns regarding the promotional email you received about our savings account, and I am sorry that you have not received a proper response since raising this at your branch three weeks ago.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with a clear acknowledgement of the complaint, accurately reflecting its subject matter: the promotional email, the interest rate comparison, and the lack of prior response. The acknowledgement is specific and demonstrates understanding of what the customer is complaining about.

This is a holding response, not a final response. The draft states 'We will write to you again with a full response to your complaint as soon as our investigation is complete' but does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) or substantive reasons engaging with the specific points raised. The response acknowledges the complaint and indicates investigation but does not resolve it. Since this is marked as 'final' stage in the scenario, the absence of a decision and reasons is a critical deficiency.

The customer draft contains no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service or the complainant's right to refer the complaint to the FOS. This is a required element in a final response under DISP 1.8.7R and DISP 1.8.8R.
The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS from the date of the final response. This is required by DISP 1.8.8R(2) in a final response.

verdicts.evidence: We are responsible for the communications we send to our customers, and we are looking into the concerns you have raised about the way our interest rate was presented in that email — in particular, whether the comparison with other providers' rates was presented in a fair and balanced way, and whether all relevant information about our rate was made clear.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages substantively with the complaint's core points (the fairness of the rate comparison, disclosure of the introductory nature) and adopts a non-dismissive tone. However, it is incomplete as a final response: it does not reach a position on the merits, does not address the responsibility issue (the 'digital marketing partner' deflection), and does not resolve the redress claim. The response reads as fair in tone but superficial in outcome, treating the complaint as still under investigation rather than resolved.

The customer draft does not address redress at all. The complaint plainly raises a financial loss (the loyalty bonus foregone and interest differential on £40,000). The response does not offer redress, explain why none is warranted, or even acknowledge that redress is being considered. The handler answer notes that redress requires human review, but this position is not communicated to the customer in any form.

claims.claim: BCOBS 2.3.7BR requires that any comparison of the cost of a currency transfer service be meaningful and presented in a fair and balanced way.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 contains BCOBS 2.3.7B (Rules) which states that a comparison must be meaningful and presented in a fair and balanced way, directly supporting the claim.

claims.claim: The general fair, clear and not misleading rule in BCOBS 2.2 applies to comparisons.
claims.reasoning: Provision 6 includes BCOBS 2.2, the fair, clear and not misleading rule, but the guidance focuses on currency‑transfer promotions; the claim extends the rule to savings‑rate comparisons, which is not explicitly covered.

claims.claim: BCOBS 2.2A.1R requires a summary box in the prescribed form for a direct offer financial promotion in relation to a savings account.
claims.reasoning: Provision 10 (BCOBS 2.2A.1 Rules) states that a firm must ensure a direct‑offer financial promotion for a savings account includes a summary box in the form set out in BCOBS 2 Annex 1R.

claims.claim: BCOBS 2.2A.2G(3) indicates that where a rate may change depending on conditions or time elapsed, the firm should incorporate the table showing rates with and without the bonus/introductory element.
claims.reasoning: Provision 5 paragraph (3) explicitly says a firm should incorporate the table set out in BCOBS 2 Annex 2G when the rate may change depending on conditions or time elapsed.

claims.claim: Under FCA rules, a firm remains responsible for financial promotions it communicates or approves, regardless of whether a third party produced or distributed them.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision addresses the firm’s responsibility for third‑party‑produced promotions.

claims.claim: You should check your DISP obligations on complaint acknowledgement and timelines.
claims.reasoning: DISP provisions are not included in the retrieved set, so the claim cannot be supported.

claims.claim: The customer raised this complaint at the branch three weeks ago and has heard nothing from either the bank or the marketing partner.
claims.reasoning: No provision provides a factual basis for the specific three‑week timeline or lack of response.

claims.claim: The customer is seeking compensation for lost interest (estimated at the loyalty bonus foregone on £40,000).
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provisions do not contain any rule on compensation or redress for lost interest.

claims.claim: We are responsible for the communications we send to our customers.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision directly states that the firm is responsible for its own communications; the claim is not supported by the given excerpts.

claims.claim: We are looking into whether the comparison with other providers' rates was presented in a fair and balanced way.
claims.reasoning: The statement is an internal investigative intention, not a rule or right, and is not backed by any provision.

claims.claim: You should not have been left without a response.
claims.reasoning: No provision sets a specific requirement that a response must be given within three weeks; the claim lacks regulatory support.

structure-titan.scenario_text: My mother, who is 78 and has been diagnosed with vascular dementia, was granted a £5,000 credit card limit increase by your bank in March 2021. I am her son and hold lasting power of attorney for her financial affairs. At the time of the limit increase, she was already showing significant signs of confusion — she couldn't remember her own address when speaking to your staff on the phone, and her GP had written to you in February 2021 confirming her diagnosis. Despite this, your bank proceeded with the credit limit increase without taking any steps to help her understand the decision or contacting me as her attorney. She has since accumulated £4,800 in debt she cannot repay on a state pension. I raised this complaint with you in November 2024. Your branch manager told me verbally that because the credit limit increase happened over three years ago, you won't look into it and said it's 'time-barred'. I believe you should have recognised her lack of mental capacity and never increased her limit. I want the debt written off and a proper explanation of why you are refusing to investigate.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This is a serious complaint involving potential mental capacity failings, vulnerability, a significant financial loss (£4,800 debt on a state pension), and a possible regulatory deadline issue. Human review is essential before anything is sent.

**Regulatory position based on retrieved provisions:**

1. **Mental capacity obligations at point of credit limit increase (CONC 2.10.8G):** The retrieved provisions indicate that a firm is likely to have reasonable grounds to suspect a mental capacity limitation where, among other things, a clinician raises a concern with the firm about the customer's capacity (CONC 2.10.8G(2)), the customer is unable to understand information provided (CONC 2.10.8G(5)), or the customer is confused about personal information such as their address (CONC 2.10.8G(9)). On the facts as stated — a GP letter in February 2021 confirming a dementia diagnosis, and the customer being unable to recall her own address on the phone — there appear to be strong grounds to conclude the bank had, or ought to have had, reasonable suspicion of a mental capacity limitation at the time of the March 2021 increase.

2. **Obligations where mental capacity limitation is suspected (CONC 2.10.12G, 2.10.16G, 2.10.17G, 2.10.18G, 2.10.19G):** The FCA's guidance indicates that where a firm understands or reasonably suspects a customer has a mental capacity limitation, it should: assist the customer to make an informed borrowing decision; apply a high level of scrutiny to the credit application; allow sufficient time to weigh up information; and not place over-reliance on information provided by the customer. The guidance also indicates firms should not inappropriately deny credit, but equally should not grant credit where the agreement may be voidable (CONC 2.10.9G(2)).

3. **Credit limit increase prohibition (CONC 6.7.7R):** This is a binding rule: a firm must not increase a customer's credit limit where the customer is at risk of financial difficulties. CONC 6.7.11G defines 'at risk of financial difficulties' in terms of arrears and repayment plans — it is not clear from the facts whether the customer met that specific definition at the time. However, the mental capacity concerns are a separate and potentially stronger basis for complaint.

4. **The 'time-bar' assertion by the branch manager:** The retrieved provisions do not include DISP time-limit rules, so I cannot confirm or deny the regulatory position on time-barring from these provisions alone. This is a significant gap. The branch manager's verbal assertion that the complaint is time-barred is not a compliant final response — DISP requires a written final response. You must not rely on a verbal statement as a valid rejection. A qualified complaints handler or compliance team must assess whether the complaint was validly rejected and whether any DISP time limits apply, including whether the customer (or her attorney) could only reasonably have become aware of the cause of complaint more recently.

5. **Debt recovery and mental capacity (CONC 7.10.1R):** This is a binding rule: the firm must suspend pursuit of debt recovery where it has been notified that the customer might not have mental capacity to manage their debt or engage in the recovery process. Given the LPA holder has now notified the bank, this rule is engaged immediately. The bank must not pursue recovery of the £4,800 debt while this complaint is live and the mental capacity issue is unresolved.

6. **LPA holder as responsible third party (CONC 7.10.3G):** The guidance notes that pursuing debt through a responsible third party acting under a registered lasting power of attorney is permissible where the customer has given prior consent — but this does not override the suspension obligation under CONC 7.10.1R in the current circumstances.

**Recommended actions before sending any response:**
- Do NOT rely on the branch manager's verbal time-bar statement as a final response. A written final response is required under DISP (not in retrieved provisions, but this is a fundamental process point — flag to compliance).
- Immediately suspend any debt recovery activity on this account under CONC 7.10.1R.
- Obtain and review: (a) the GP letter of February 2021; (b) call recordings/notes from the March 2021 credit limit increase interaction; (c) any creditworthiness assessment conducted at the time; (d) evidence of whether the LPA was registered and notified to the bank.
- Refer to compliance/legal for assessment of: (a) whether the complaint is in time under DISP; (b) whether the agreement or the limit increase may be voidable; (c) whether debt write-off is appropriate.
- This complaint involves potential redress (debt write-off of £4,800), serious misconduct allegations, a vulnerable customer, and possible regulatory breach — human review and senior sign-off are mandatory.

**Assumptions in the draft below:**
- The draft acknowledges the complaint and confirms investigation. It does NOT promise debt write-off or admit liability.
- It confirms suspension of debt recovery, which is required by CONC 7.10.1R.
- It does not include FOS referral rights or a time limit for FOS referral because the retrieved provisions do not include the relevant DISP provisions — the handler must add the correct FOS wording (including the standard explanatory leaflet reference) before sending, in line with DISP requirements not covered here.
- The draft is deliberately conservative pending full investigation.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer's name / Mr [Son's name] acting under Lasting Power of Attorney],

Thank you for contacting us about this matter. We are sorry to hear about your mother's situation and we understand how distressing this must be for your family.

We are writing to confirm that we have received your complaint and that we take it very seriously. Your complaint raises important concerns about the credit limit increase applied to your mother's account in March 2021, and in particular whether the bank properly considered her circumstances — including her health — before proceeding with that increase.

We want to be clear: we do not consider this matter closed. A verbal statement from a branch manager does not constitute our formal response to your complaint, and we are now treating this as an open complaint that requires full investigation.

As part of that investigation, we will be reviewing the information available to us at the time of the credit limit increase, including any correspondence received from your mother's GP and records of contact with your mother around that time. We will also consider whether our processes for identifying and supporting customers who may have difficulty making financial decisions were followed appropriately.

While this investigation is ongoing, we are suspending any debt recovery activity on your mother's account. We will not take steps to pursue the outstanding balance while we are looking into this complaint.

We will write to you again with the outcome of our investigation. If you have any documents you would like us to consider — such as a copy of the registered Lasting Power of Attorney or any medical correspondence — please send these to [insert address/contact details] and we will ensure they are included in our review.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us at [insert contact details].

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Identifies indicators that a firm is likely to have reasonable grounds to suspect a mental capacity limitation, including a clinician raising a concern (sub-paragraph 2), the customer being unable to understand information provided (sub-paragraph 5), and the customer being confused about personal information such as their address (sub-paragraph 9) — all of which appear to be present on the facts.

Indicates that where a firm understands or reasonably suspects a mental capacity limitation, it should assist the customer to make an informed borrowing decision and ensure its lending decision is informed and responsible — relevant to whether the bank met its obligations before granting the limit increase.

Indicates that firms' practices and procedures should be designed to assist customers with mental capacity limitations and place them on an equivalent basis to other customers, to increase the likelihood of informed borrowing decisions — relevant to whether the bank's processes were adequate.

Indicates that where a mental capacity limitation is suspected, the firm should allow sufficient time to weigh up information and make an informed decision — relevant to whether the bank gave the customer adequate opportunity to understand the credit limit increase.

Indicates that where a mental capacity limitation is suspected, the firm should apply a high level of scrutiny to the credit application to mitigate the risk of unsustainable borrowing — relevant to the adequacy of the bank's creditworthiness assessment in March 2021.

Indicates that where a mental capacity limitation is suspected, the firm should undertake an appropriate creditworthiness assessment and not place over-reliance on information provided by the customer — relevant to whether the bank properly assessed the limit increase application.

Indicates it would not be inappropriate to decline to significantly increase credit where the firm reasonably believes the agreement or decision would be voidable — relevant to whether the limit increase should have been refused on mental capacity grounds.

Binding rule prohibiting a firm from increasing a customer's credit limit where the customer is at risk of financial difficulties — relevant to whether the limit increase was permissible, subject to confirming whether the customer met the definition of 'at risk' at the time.

Provides guidance on when a customer is 'at risk of financial difficulties' for the purposes of CONC 6.7.7R — relevant to assessing whether the prohibition on credit limit increases applied at the time of the March 2021 increase.

Binding rule requiring the firm to suspend pursuit of debt recovery where it has been notified that the customer might not have mental capacity to manage their debt or engage in the recovery process — directly engaged by the son's complaint and notification of the dementia diagnosis and LPA.

Indicates that pursuing debt through a responsible third party acting under a registered lasting power of attorney is permissible — relevant to how the bank may engage with the son as LPA holder going forward.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 6.7 Post contract: business practices

## CONC 6.7.7 (Rules)

A firm must not increase, nor offer to increase, a customer's credit limit on a credit card or retail revolving credit agreement where:

(1) the firm has been advised that the customer does not wish to have any credit limit increases; or

(2) a customer is at risk of financial difficulties. [Note: paragraphs 6.6 and 6.7 of ILG]

## CONC 6.7.8 (Rules)

A firm under a retail revolving credit agreement, or a regulated credit agreement for a credit card, must:

(1) permit a customer at any time to reduce or decline offers to increase the credit limit; and

(2) permit a customer to decline to receive offers of credit limit increases.[Note: paragraphs 6.8 and 6.9 of ILG]

## CONC 6.7.9 (Rules)

(1) This rule applies to a regulated credit agreement for a credit card and to a retail revolving credit agreement.

(2) A firm must notify the customer of a proposed increase in the credit limit under the agreement:

(a) in the case of a regulated credit agreement for a credit card or a store card, at least 30 days before the increase comes into effect; and

(b) in the case of a retail revolving credit agreement (other than an agreement for a store card), at least 28 days before the increase comes into effect,

except in the circumstances described in (3).

(3) The notification in (2) is not required where:

(a) the increase is at the express request of the customer; or

(b) the increase is proposed by the firm, but the customer agrees to it at that time and wishes it to come into effect in less than 30 days or 28 days (as the case may be).

[Note: paragraph 6.17 of ILG]

metadata.section_name: CONC 6.7 Post contract: business practices

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 6.7 Post contract: business practices

## CONC 6.7.10 (Rules)

Where a customer is at risk of financial difficulties, a firm under a retail revolving credit agreement or a regulated credit agreement for a credit card must, other than where a promotional rate of interest ends, not increase the rate of interest under the agreement.

[Note: paragraph 6.10 of ILG]

## CONC 6.7.11 (Guidance)

For the purposes of CONC 6.7.7 R and CONC 6.7.10 R a customer is at risk of financial difficulties if the customer:

(1) is two or more payments in arrears; or

(2) has agreed a repayment plan with the firm in question; or

(3) is in serious discussion with a firm which carries on debt counselling with a view to entering into a debt management plan and the firm has been notified of this fact.[Note: paragraph 6.10 (box) of ILG]

## CONC 6.7.13 (Rules)

Where a firm proposes to exercise a power under a regulated credit agreement for a credit card or store card to increase the interest rate, the firm must:

(1) permit the customer sixty days, from the date of the firm's notice of the proposed increase during which period the customer may give notice to the firm requiring it to close the account;

(2) permit the customer to pay off the outstanding balance at the rate of interest before the proposed increase and over a reasonable period; and

(3) give notice to the customer of the rights in (1) and (2). [Note: paragraphs 6.11 and 6.19 of ILG]

## CONC 6.7.14 (Rules)

Where a firm has a right to increase the interest rate under a regulated credit agreement, the firm must not increase the interest rate unless there is a valid reason for doing so.

[Note: paragraph 6.20 of ILG]

metadata.section_name: CONC 6.7 Post contract: business practices

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 6.7 Post contract: business practices

## CONC 6.7.34 (Guidance)

References in CONC 6.7.27R, CONC 6.7.31R(3) and CONC 6.7.32G(1) to a customer increasing payments to the firm include circumstances where the amount a customer pays remains fixed at the same amount the customer was previously paying but, assuming there is no further spending on the account, represents an increase in the percentage of the outstanding principal that is repaid each month as the balance reduces.

## CONC 6.7.35 (Rules)

(1) Where a customer does not respond to a firm’s request under CONC 6.7.31R(3), a firm must, at the end of the period specified in the request, suspend or cancel the customer’s use of the credit card or retail revolving credit facility .

(2) Where a customer confirms that one or more of the options proposed under CONC 6.7.31 R(3) is sustainable, but states that they will not make the increased payments, a firm must suspend or cancel the customer’s use of the credit card or retail revolving credit facility .

(3) Where a firm suspends the customer’s use of the credit card or retail revolving credit facility under paragraph (1) and the customer subsequently responds to the firm’s request under CONC 6.7.31R(3), the firm may withdraw the suspension if this would be in line with the other provisions in this section.

metadata.section_name: CONC 6.7 Post contract: business practices
CONC 2.10 Mental capacity guidance

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 7.10 Treatment of customers with mental capacity limitations

## CONC 7.10.1 (Rules)

A firm must suspend the pursuit of recovery of a debt from a customer when:

(1) the firm has been notified that the customer might not have the mental capacity to make relevant financial decisions about the management of the customer's debt and/or to engage in the debt recovery process at the time; or

(2) the firm understands or ought reasonably to be aware that the customer lacks mental capacity to make relevant financial decisions about the management of the customer's debt and/or to engage in the debt recovery process at the time.

[Note: paragraphs 3.7r of DCG and 7.13 of ILG]

## CONC 7.10.2 (Guidance)

A firm should allow a customer or a person acting on behalf of the customer a reasonable period of time to provide evidence as to the likely impact of any mental capacity limitation on the customer's ability to engage with the firm.

[Note: paragraph 3.7r (box) of DCG]

## CONC 7.10.3 (Guidance)

CONC 7.10.1 R does not prevent a firm from pursuing the debt through a responsible third party acting on behalf of the customer, where the customer has given prior consent, for example, pursuant to a registered lasting power of attorney.

[Note: paragraph 3.7r (box) of DCG]

## CONC 7.10.4 (Guidance)

Firms should note CONC 7.2.1 R (and its accompanying guidance) which requires firms to establish and implement policies and procedures for the fair and appropriate treatment of vulnerable customers.

metadata.section_name: CONC 7.10 Treatment of customers with mental capacity limitations

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 2.10 Mental capacity guidance

## CONC 2.10.16 (Guidance)

Firms' practices and procedures should be designed to assist customers that firms understand have, or reasonably suspect of having, mental capacity limitations to overcome, to the extent possible, the effect of the limitations and place them, to the extent possible, on an equivalent basis to customers who do not have such limitations, to increase the likelihood of customers being able to make informed borrowing decisions.

[Note: paragraph 4.4 of MCG]

## CONC 2.10.17 (Guidance)

Where a firm understands, or reasonably suspects, a customer has or may have a mental capacity limitation it should consider allowing the customer:

(1) sufficient time in the circumstances to weigh up the information and explanations the firm has given;

(2) sufficient time in the circumstances to make an informed borrowing decision;

(3) to defer a decision to borrow to a later date.

[Note: paragraphs 4.26, 4.27 and 4.28 of MCG]

## CONC 2.10.18 (Guidance)

Where a firm understands, or reasonably suspects, a customer has or may have a mental capacity limitation it should apply a high level of scrutiny to the customer's application for credit, in order to mitigate the risk of the customer entering into unsustainable borrowing .

[Note: paragraphs 4.32 and 4.33 of MCG]

## CONC 2.10.19 (Guidance)

(1) A firm should balance the risk of a customer taking on unsustainable borrowing against inappropriately or unnecessarily denying credit to a customer.

(2) Where a firm understands or reasonably suspects a customer has or may have a mental capacity limitation, it should undertake an appropriate and effective creditworthiness assessment (see CONC 5.2A) and it would be appropriate not to place over-reliance on information provided by the customer for the assessment.

[Note: paragraph 4.34 of MCG]

metadata.section_name: CONC 2.10 Mental capacity guidance
