# MCOB — MCOB 9.4 Content of illustrations

## MCOB 9.4.58 (Rules)

Where the customer is not required to make payments to the mortgage lender on the lifetime mortgage and therefore all or part of the interest is rolled up, Section 9: 'Will the interest rate change?' must also contain (if applicable):

(1) if the interest rate is variable, the following text:'If the interest rate increases, the amount you owe will also increase. If the interest rate was [one percentage point higher than shown MCOB 9.4.51 R(4)(b)] throughout the example term of [insert number of years ' see MCOB 9.4.10 R or MCOB 9.4.12 R] years, the amount you would owe us at the end of that time would be: [insert amount].'; or

(2) if the interest rate will become variable at the end of a fixed or capped rate period, the following text:'If the interest rate increases after the [insert as applicable: fixed rate period or capped rate period] ends, the amount you owe will also increase. If the interest rate was [one percentage point higher than shown in MCOB 9.4.51 R(4)(b) above] throughout the remainder of the example term of [insert number of years - see MCOB 9.4.10 R or MCOB 9.4.12 R] years, the amount you would owe us at the end of that time would be: [insert amount].'; or

(3) if a capped rate applies throughout the life of the lifetime mortgage and the interest rate at the date of issue of the illustration is lower than the capped rate, the following text:'If the interest rate increases, the amount you owe will also increase. The interest rate on this lifetime mortgage cannot be higher than [rate at which interest is capped] If the interest rate was [rate at which interest is capped] throughout the example term of [insert number of years - see MCOB 9.4.10 R and MCOB 9.4.12 R] years, the amount you would owe us at the end of that time would be: [insert amount].'.

metadata.section_name: MCOB 9.4 Content of illustrations

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 2.10 Mental capacity guidance

## CONC 2.10.9 (Guidance)

(1) A firm should not unfairly discriminate against a customer who it understands, or reasonably suspects, has a mental capacity limitation, in particular, by inappropriately denying the customer access to credit. [Note: paragraph 4.8 of MCG]

(2) It would not be inappropriate not to grant credit nor significantly increase the amount of credit under an agreement nor set a credit limit for running account credit where the firm reasonably believes the agreement or decision would be voidable at the instance of the customer or the agreement is void.

## CONC 2.10.10 (Guidance)

(1) In accordance with Principle 6, firms should take reasonable steps to ensure they have suitable business practices and procedures in place for the fair treatment of customers who they understand, or reasonably suspect, have or may have a mental capacity limitation. [Note: paragraph 4.1 of MCG]

(2) CONC 7.2.1 R requires firms to establish and implement arrears policies and procedures, which include policies and procedures for the fair and appropriate treatment of customers the firm understands or reasonably suspects of having mental capacity limitations.

## CONC 2.10.11 (Guidance)

A firm should document practices and procedures to set out the steps that it takes when it receives applications for credit from such customers.

[Note: paragraph 4.2 of MCG]

## CONC 2.10.12 (Guidance)

Where a firm understands, or reasonably suspects, a customer has or may have a mental capacity limitation the firm should use its business practices and procedures to:

(1) assist the customer, where possible, to make an informed borrowing decision; and

(2) ensure its lending decision is informed and responsible in the circumstances and mitigates the potential risks to the customer.

[Note: paragraphs 4.3 and 4.5 of MCG]

metadata.section_name: CONC 2.10 Mental capacity guidance

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 6.7 Post contract: business practices

## CONC 6.7.31 (Rules)

Where a firm is required to assist a customer to repay more quickly under CONC 6.7.30R(3), the firm must contact the customer to:

(1) explain that increasing this level of payment would reduce the cost of borrowing and the amount of time it would take to repay the balance;

(2) provide contact details for not-for-profit debt advice bodies and encourage the customer to contact one of them;

(3) set out options for the customer to increase payments and request that the customer, within a specified reasonable period, respond to either:

(a) confirm that the customer will increase payments in accordance with one of the options; or

(b) where applicable, confirm that the options proposed are not sustainable for the customer; and

(4) inform the customer that if the firm does not receive a response to the request under paragraph (3) in the time specified, the firm will suspend or cancel the use of the credit card or retail revolving credit facility .

metadata.section_name: CONC 6.7 Post contract: business practices

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 2.10 Mental capacity guidance

## CONC 2.10.5 (Guidance)

Where a firm reasonably suspects a customer has, or may have, some form of mental capacity limitation which would constrain the customer's ability to make a decision to borrow, the firm should not regard the customer as lacking capacity to make the decision unless the firm has taken reasonable steps without success to assist the customer to make a decision.

[Note: paragraph 3.2 of MCG]

## CONC 2.10.6 (Guidance)

Amongst the most common potential causes of mental capacity limitations are the following examples, a mental health condition, dementia, a learning disability, a developmental disorder, a neurological disability or brain injury and alcohol or drug (including prescribed drugs) induced intoxication.

[Note: paragraph 2.9 of MCG]

## CONC 2.10.7 (Guidance)

Where a firm understands or reasonably suspects a customer has a condition of a type in CONC 2.10.6 G, this does not necessarily mean that the customer does not have the mental capacity to make an informed borrowing decision. See also CONC 2.10.15 G.

[Note: paragraph 2.10 of MCG]

metadata.section_name: CONC 2.10 Mental capacity guidance

verdicts.evidence: We are writing to confirm that we have received your complaint and that we take it very seriously. Your complaint raises important concerns about the credit limit increase applied to your mother's account in March 2021, and in particular whether the bank properly considered her circumstances — including her health — before proceeding with that increase.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing draft opens by confirming receipt of the complaint and accurately restates the key issue (credit limit increase and the mother's health/vulnerability).
The draft does not state a firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) and provides no conclusive reasons for a decision; it only says the matter is being investigated.
There is no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service or how the complainant may refer the complaint to it.
The draft does not state the six‑month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry to hear about your mother's situation and we understand how distressing this must be for your family… We are suspending any debt recovery activity on your mother's account. We will not take steps to pursue the outstanding balance while we are looking into this complaint.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is empathetic, acknowledges the complainant’s concerns, explains interim actions (suspending debt recovery) and promises a further outcome, showing a fair and non‑dismissive approach.
The response does not address whether any redress (e.g., debt write‑off) will be offered or explain why none is due.

verdicts.evidence: This is a serious complaint involving potential mental capacity failings, vulnerability… (handler answer) … The draft also notes the mother's health and suspends debt recovery while the issue is investigated.
verdicts.reasoning: Both the internal handler note and the customer‑facing draft recognise the customer's vulnerability (age, dementia, mental capacity) and take appropriate action (suspending debt recovery, planning a detailed review).

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for contacting us about this matter. We are sorry to hear about your mother's situation and we understand how distressing this must be for your family. We are writing to confirm that we have received your complaint and that we take it very seriously. Your complaint raises important concerns about the credit limit increase applied to your mother's account in March 2021, and in particular whether the bank properly considered her circumstances — including her health — before proceeding with that increase.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with a clear acknowledgement of receipt and accurately reflects the substance of the complaint: the credit limit increase in March 2021, the failure to consider the mother's health/circumstances, and the family's concerns about the decision-making process. The acknowledgement is specific, not generic.

The customer draft does not state a clear decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). It confirms that an investigation will take place and suspends debt recovery, but it does not communicate a final decision on the complaint itself or reasons engaging with the substantive points (e.g. whether the bank breached its mental capacity obligations, whether the credit limit increase was wrongful, or whether redress is warranted). This is a holding/investigation letter, not a final response with a decision.

The customer draft contains no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service or the complainant's right to refer the complaint to the FOS. The handler answer notes this gap explicitly: 'It does not include FOS referral rights or a time limit for FOS referral because the retrieved provisions do not include the relevant DISP provisions — the handler must add the correct FOS wording before sending.' This is a critical omission for a final response.
The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS. This element is absent entirely, as is the FOS referral rights element on which it depends.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry to hear about your mother's situation and we understand how distressing this must be for your family. We want to be clear: we do not consider this matter closed. A verbal statement from a branch manager does not constitute our formal response to your complaint, and we are now treating this as an open complaint that requires full investigation. While this investigation is ongoing, we are suspending any debt recovery activity on your mother's account.
verdicts.reasoning: The response is clear, empathetic, and non-dismissive. It directly contradicts the branch manager's verbal rejection, treats the complaint seriously, and engages with the substance (mental capacity, health circumstances, debt recovery suspension). The language is appropriate to the complainant's circumstances and does not minimise their concerns. The response reads as even-handed and competent.

verdicts.evidence: While this investigation is ongoing, we are suspending any debt recovery activity on your mother's account. We will not take steps to pursue the outstanding balance while we are looking into this complaint.
verdicts.reasoning: The response addresses one form of interim remedial action (suspension of debt recovery), which is appropriate and required by CONC 7.10.1R. However, it does not reach or communicate a position on the substantive redress sought (debt write-off of £4,800). The handler answer notes this is pending investigation and compliance review, but the customer draft does not explain whether or when redress will be considered or on what basis. A final response should address redress; this draft does not, treating it as deferred pending investigation.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: 'This is a serious complaint involving potential mental capacity failings, vulnerability, a significant financial loss (£4,800 debt on a state pension)... Human review is essential before anything is sent.' Customer draft: 'We are sorry to hear about your mother's situation and we understand how distressing this must be for your family... we will be reviewing the information available to us at the time of the credit limit increase, including any correspondence received from your mother's GP and records of contact with your mother around that time. We will also consider whether our processes for identifying and supporting customers who may have difficulty making financial decisions were followed appropriately. While this investigation is ongoing, we are suspending any debt recovery activity on your mother's account.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer explicitly identifies vulnerability (age 78, dementia diagnosis, state pension, significant financial loss) and mandates human review and senior sign-off. The customer draft reflects this by: using empathetic language; acknowledging the distressing nature of the situation; committing to review GP correspondence and mental capacity processes; and immediately suspending debt recovery. The response adapts its handling and communication to the vulnerability identified.

claims.claim: The firm had, or ought to have had, reasonable suspicion of a mental capacity limitation at the time of the March 2021 credit limit increase.
claims.reasoning: CONC 2.10.8G lists specific indications (e.g., clinician concern, inability to recall address) that give a firm reasonable grounds to suspect a mental capacity limitation. The facts described (GP letter confirming dementia and inability to recall address) match these indications.

claims.claim: Where a firm understands or reasonably suspects a customer has a mental capacity limitation, it should assist the customer to make an informed borrowing decision, apply a high level of scrutiny to the credit application, allow sufficient time to weigh up information, and not place over‑reliance on information provided by the customer.
claims.reasoning: CONC 2.10.16‑19 G provide guidance that firms should help customers with capacity limitations, give them sufficient time, apply high scrutiny and avoid over‑reliance on their information. These provisions directly support the stated obligations.

claims.claim: A firm must not increase a customer's credit limit where the customer is at risk of financial difficulties.
claims.reasoning: CONC 6.7.7 R explicitly states that a firm must not increase (or offer to increase) a credit limit where the customer is at risk of financial difficulties.

claims.claim: The branch manager's verbal assertion that the complaint is time‑barred is not a compliant final response — DISP requires a written final response.
claims.reasoning: No DISP time‑limit or final‑response rule was retrieved among the provided provisions, so the claim cannot be linked to a specific FCA rule.

claims.claim: The firm must suspend the pursuit of debt recovery when it has been notified that the customer might not have mental capacity to manage their debt.
claims.reasoning: CONC 7.10.1 R requires a firm to suspend debt‑recovery activity in the circumstances described.

claims.claim: Pursuing debt through a responsible third party acting under a registered lasting power of attorney is permissible where the customer has given prior consent, but this does not override the suspension obligation under CONC 7.10.1R.
claims.reasoning: CONC 7.10.3 G clarifies that debt can be pursued via a responsible third party with consent, yet the suspension requirement of 7.10.1 R remains in force.

claims.claim: The draft acknowledges the complaint and confirms investigation; it does NOT promise debt write‑off or admit liability.
claims.reasoning: No FCA provision governs whether a draft may or may not promise a debt write‑off; the statement is a factual description of the draft's content, not supported by the retrieved rules.

claims.claim: A verbal statement from a branch manager does not constitute our formal response to your complaint.
claims.reasoning: The requirement for a written final response is set out in DISP rules, which were not among the retrieved provisions.

claims.claim: We are suspending any debt recovery activity on your mother's account and will not pursue the outstanding balance while the complaint is being investigated.
claims.reasoning: The statement mirrors the obligation in CONC 7.10.1 R to suspend debt recovery when a mental‑capacity limitation is notified.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I opened an execution-only share dealing account with your firm last year to buy and sell shares listed on the London Stock Exchange. When I signed up, I was never told anything about your complaints procedure — no leaflet, no mention of it at all. I wasn't even told about the Financial Ombudsman Service. More recently, I purchased units in a structured product through the same account. The product turned out to be far more complex than I expected — it embeds a derivative and I don't understand the risks at all. Nobody at your firm asked me any questions about my knowledge or experience before letting me buy it, and I wasn't warned that you weren't assessing whether it was appropriate for me. I've now lost a significant amount of money. I believe your firm failed in its obligations both by not assessing appropriateness for a complex product and by never informing me of my rights to complain or access the ombudsman.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct issues: (1) failure to provide complaints procedure information and FOS details at account opening; and (2) failure to assess appropriateness before allowing the customer to purchase a complex structured product embedding a derivative.

**Issue 1 — Complaints procedure and FOS information**
The retrieved provisions address MiFID investment firm obligations under DISP 1.1A. DISP 1.1A.10R requires MiFID investment firms to publish complaints process details and provide them to clients on request or when acknowledging a MiFID complaint. DISP 1.1A.11R requires MiFID investment firms to provide information about the FOS (including its website) on their website and in general conditions of contracts with eligible complainants. DISP 1.1A.12R requires MiFID investment firms to establish, implement and maintain effective and transparent complaints management policies and procedures. If your firm is a MiFID investment firm (which a share-dealing firm typically would be — please confirm), these rules apply. The customer's allegation that they received no information about the complaints procedure or FOS at sign-up is potentially a breach of DISP 1.1A.11R (general conditions of contracts) and DISP 1.1A.12R. You should check whether the FOS information was included in the account terms and conditions and whether it appears on the firm's website.

**Issue 2 — Appropriateness assessment for the structured product**
The retrieved provisions do not include the MiFID/COBS appropriateness rules (e.g. COBS 10) that would govern the obligation to assess a retail client's knowledge and experience before allowing execution-only purchase of a complex product. The MCOB 8.6A.4R provision retrieved relates to equity release transactions, not share dealing or structured products, so it does not apply here. I therefore cannot ground a regulatory position on the appropriateness failure from the retrieved context alone.

**Recommended action:**
- Confirm whether the firm is a MiFID investment firm for the purposes of DISP 1.1A.
- Check whether FOS information was included in the account opening terms and conditions and on the firm's website.
- Escalate the appropriateness/suitability element to a senior handler or compliance team, as the retrieved provisions do not cover COBS 10 appropriateness obligations and this involves a significant financial loss — human review is essential.
- Do not admit liability or quantify redress in any customer communication at this stage.
- The customer draft below acknowledges the complaint, addresses the complaints information point under the available provisions, and flags the FOS referral right. It deliberately does not make any admission or commitment on the appropriateness issue, given the gap in retrieved context.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending:**
1. The firm is a MiFID investment firm subject to DISP 1.1A.
2. The structured product with an embedded derivative is a MiFID financial instrument (not, e.g., an insurance-based product).
3. The firm's current final response process and whether this letter is intended as a final response or an acknowledgement — the FOS referral wording in the draft assumes this is a final response; if it is only an acknowledgement, the FOS section should be removed.
4. Whether the FOS standard explanatory leaflet is being enclosed — DISP 1.1A.31R requires this with a final response for MiFID complaints.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. We are sorry to hear about your experience and want to assure you that we take your concerns seriously. We are writing to acknowledge your complaint and to set out what happens next.

**Your complaint**

You have raised two concerns: first, that when you opened your execution-only share dealing account you were not given information about our complaints procedure or about the Financial Ombudsman Service; and second, that when you purchased a structured product through your account, we did not ask you about your knowledge or experience of complex products before allowing the purchase, and did not warn you that we were not assessing whether the product was appropriate for you. You have told us that you have suffered a significant financial loss as a result.

**Complaints procedure and Financial Ombudsman Service information**

We are required to make information about our complaints handling process available to our clients, including details of how to contact our complaints team. We are also required to provide information about the Financial Ombudsman Service — including its website address — on our website and in the general conditions of our contracts with clients. We are reviewing whether this information was provided to you at the time you opened your account and will address this as part of our investigation.

**The structured product purchase**

We are also investigating your concerns about the purchase of the structured product, including whether the correct steps were followed before that transaction was completed. We will write to you again once our investigation is complete.

**What happens next**

We aim to complete our investigation and send you a full response as promptly as possible. If we are unable to resolve your complaint within the applicable timeframe, we will write to you to explain why and to let you know when you can expect a final response.

If you remain dissatisfied with our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with full details of how to do this, including the FOS's contact information and explanatory leaflet, when we send our final response.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details below.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Contact details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires MiFID investment firms to publish complaints process details and provide them to clients on request or when acknowledging a MiFID complaint, directly relevant to the customer's allegation that no complaints procedure information was given.

Requires MiFID investment firms to provide FOS information (including website address) on their website and in general conditions of contracts with eligible complainants, directly relevant to the allegation that the customer was never told about the FOS.

Requires MiFID investment firms to establish, implement and maintain effective and transparent complaints management policies and procedures, relevant to the systemic failure alleged in not informing the customer of their complaints rights.
Requires MiFID investment firms to communicate clearly and in plain language and reply to complaints without undue delay, governing the standard for this response.
Requires MiFID investment firms to communicate their position on a MiFID complaint and inform the client of their options including referral to an ADR entity such as the FOS.

Requires the explanation given under DISP 1.1A.30R to enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, and inform the complainant of the right to refer to FOS — relevant to what must be included in the final response.

Indicates that FOS information in a final response should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently — relevant to how the FOS referral section should be presented.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 1.11 The Society of Lloyd's

## DISP 1.11.7 (Guidance)

Each member of the Society is individually subject to the rules in this chapter as a result of the insurance market direction given in

DISP 2.1.7 D

under section 316 of the Act (Direction by a regulator).

## DISP 1.11.8 (Guidance)

However, the Society operates a two-tier internal complaints handling procedure, currently set out in the 'Code for Underwriting agents: UK Personal Lines Claims and Complaints Handling'. Under this procedure, complaints by policyholders against members of the Society are considered by the managing agent and then, if necessary, by the Society's in-house Complaints Department. This procedure (and any procedure that may replace it) will be subject to the requirements in this chapter.

## DISP 1.11.9 (Guidance)

Members will individually comply with this chapter if and only if all complaints by policyholders against members are dealt with under the Lloyd's complaints procedures. Accordingly, certain of the obligations under this chapter, for example the obligation to report on complaints received and the obligation to pay fees under the rules relating to the funding of the Financial Ombudsman Service (FEES 5), must be complied with by the Society on behalf of members. Managing agents will not have to make a separate report to the

FCA

on complaints reported under the complaints reporting rules sent by the Society.

## DISP 1.11.10 (Rules)

A members' adviser must establish and maintain effective arrangements for handling any complaint from a member of the Society regarding advice given to the member in connection with the acquiring or disposing of syndicate participation.

## DISP 1.11.11 (Guidance)

Complaints from members of the Society regarding the activities of members' advisers, which cannot be resolved by the members' adviser, cannot be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.11 The Society of Lloyd's

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# DISP — DISP 1.1A Complaints handling requirements for MiFID complaints

## DISP 1.1A.10 (Rules)

MiFID investment firms must publish the details of the process to be followed when handling a MiFID complaint. Such details must include information about the complaints management policy and the contact details of the complaints management function. This information must be provided to clients or potential clients, on request, or when acknowledging a MiFID complaint.

## DISP 1.1A.11 (Rules)

A MiFID investment firm must provide information to eligible complainants, in a clear, comprehensible and easily accessible way, about the Financial Ombudsman Service (including the Financial Ombudsman Service’s website address):

(1) on its website, where one exists; and

(2)

if applicable, in the general conditions of its contracts with eligible complainants.

[Note: subject to a few minor changes reflecting its amended application, this provision replicates DISP 1.2.1R(4)]

[Note: article 13(2) of the ADR Directive, article 14(1) of the ODR Regulation, and regulation 19 of the ADR Regulations]

## DISP 1.1A.12 (Rules)

MiFID investment firms must establish, implement and maintain effective and transparent complaints management policies and procedures for the prompt handling of MiFIDclients’ or potential clients’ complaints.

## DISP 1.1A.13 (Rules)

The complaints management policy must provide clear, accurate and up-to-date information about the complaints-handling process. This policy must be endorsed by the MiFID investment firm's management body.

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.1A Complaints handling requirements for MiFID complaints

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.8 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of a payment protection contract, knowledge by the complainant that there was a problem with the sale of the payment protection contract generally (for example where there has been a rejection of a claim on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion, or the complainant has received a customer contact letter explaining that they may have been mis-sold) would not in itself ordinarily be sufficient to establish for the purposes of the three-year time period in DISP 2.8.2R(2) that the complainant had become aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he or she had cause for complaint in respect of a failure to make the disclosure set out at DISP App 3.3A.2E (relating to failure to disclose commission).

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 8.6A Execution-only sales

## MCOB 8.6A.4 (Rules)

A firm must not enter into or arrange an execution-only sale for a equity release transaction unless:(1) the customer has rejected the advice given by the firm and instead requested an execution-only sale of an equity release transaction;(2) the customer has identified which particular equity release transaction they wish to purchase, and specified to the firm at least the required additional information (where applicable);(3) after providing the required information in (2), the customer has been informed, clearly and prominently and in a durable medium, and that the customer will not benefit from the protection of the rules (in MCOB 8.5A) on assessing suitability:(a) in any case where the firm has advised the customer that the equity release transaction is unsuitable for the customer, that that is the case; and(b) in any other case, that in the provision of its services for the execution-only sale the firm is not required to assess the suitability of that equity release transaction;and in either case that the customer will not benefit from the protection of the rules (in MCOB 8.5A) on assessing suitability. In any case where there is spoken dialogue between the firm and the customer at any point, the firm must also provide this information orally; and(4) after the customer has been provided with the information in (3), in any case where there is spoken or other interactive dialogue between the firm and the customer at any point, the customer has confirmed in writing to the firm that they are aware of the consequences of losing the protections of the rules on assessing suitability and are making a positive election to proceed with an execution-only sale. The written confirmation must be in the same document as the information in durable medium in (3), which must be separate from any other information and contractual documentation.

metadata.section_name: MCOB 8.6A Execution-only sales

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 1.3 Complaints handling rules

## DISP 1.3.6 (Guidance)

Where a firm identifies (from its complaints or otherwise) recurring or systemic problems in its provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or claims management service , it should (in accordance with Principle 6 (Customers' interests) and to the extent that it applies) consider whether it ought to act with regard to the position of customers who may have suffered detriment from, or been potentially disadvantaged by, such problems but who have not complained and, if so, take appropriate and proportionate measures to ensure that those customers are given appropriate redress or a proper opportunity to obtain it. In particular, the firm should:

(1) ascertain the scope and severity of the consumer detriment that might have arisen; and

(2) consider whether it is fair and reasonable for the firm to undertake proactively a redress or remediation exercise, which may include contacting customers who have not complained.

## DISP 1.3.7 (Rules)

(1) A firm must appoint an individual at the firm, or in the same group as the firm, to have responsibility for oversight of the firm's compliance with DISP 1.

(2) The individual appointed must be carrying out a

FCA governing function

at the firm or in the same group as the firm.

(3) If there are no individuals at the firm or in the same group as the firm within (2), the firm must appoint an individual of appropriate seniority.

(4) A person approved to perform the limited scope function for the firm or for a firm in the same group as the firm satisfies the condition in (3).

## DISP 1.3.8 (Guidance)

Firms are not required to notify the name of the individual to the

FCA

or the Financial Ombudsman Service but would be expected to do so promptly on request. There is no bar on a firm appointing different individuals to have the responsibility at different times where this is to accommodate part-time or flexible working.

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.3 Complaints handling rules

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 1.1 Purpose and application

## DISP 1.1.9A (Guidance)

The scope of this sourcebook does not include:

(1) a complaint about pre-commencement investment business which was regulated by a recognised professional body (those complaints will be handled under the arrangements of that professional body); or

(2) a complaint about the administration of an occupational pension scheme, because this is not a regulated activity (firms should refer complainants to the Pensions Ombudsman rather than to the Financial Ombudsman Service and should refer consumers’ general requests for information or guidance to MoneyHelper ).

## DISP 1.1.10 (Rules)

In relation to a firm's obligations under this chapter, references to a complaint also include an expression of dissatisfaction which is capable of becoming a:

(1) relevant new complaint;

(2) relevant transitional complaint;

(3) relevant new credit-related complaint;

(4) relevant new claims management complaint; or

(5) relevant transitional funeral plan complaint.

## DISP 1.1.10-A (Rules)

Where insurance distribution activities are carried on from an establishment maintained by it or its appointed representative in the United Kingdom (or in an EEA State, in the case of a TP firm with respect to services provided into the United Kingdom) , a firm must have in place and operate appropriate and effective procedures for registering and responding to complaints from a person who is not an eligible complainant.

[Note: article 14 of the IDD]

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.1 Purpose and application

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# DISP — DISP 1.1A Complaints handling requirements for MiFID complaints

## DISP 1.1A.29 (Rules)

When handling a MiFID complaint, MiFID investment firms must communicate with clients or potential clients clearly, in plain language that is easy to understand and must reply to the complaint without undue delay.

## DISP 1.1A.30 (Rules)

MiFID investment firms must communicate their position on the MiFID complaint to clients or potential clients and inform the clients or potential clients about their options, including that they may be able to refer the complaint to an alternative dispute resolution entity, as defined in regulation 4 of the ADR Regulations, or that the client may be able to take civil action.[Note: See the ADR Directive.]

## DISP 1.1A.31 (Rules)

The explanation given by MiFID investment firms to clients or potential clients in accordance with DISP 1.1A.30R must also:(1) enclose a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet;(2) provide the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service;(3) inform the complainant that if, still dissatisfied with the respondent's response, the complaint may now be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service; and(4) indicate whether or not the respondent consents to waiving the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R (Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?) by including the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3R.[Note: article 13 of the ADR Directive]

## DISP 1.1A.32 (Guidance)

The information regarding the Financial Ombudsman Service required to be provided in a final response sent under DISP 1.1A.30R and referred to in DISP 1.1A.31R should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text of those responses.[Note: article 13 of the ADR Directive]

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.1A Complaints handling requirements for MiFID complaints

# DISP — DISP App 3.11 Obligation to write letters to certain rejected complainants

## DISP App 3.11.-1 (Rules)

In this section:

(1) “purported complaint” means an expression of dissatisfaction which would have been a complaint, had it related to an activity which comes under the jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service;

(2) “recurring non-disclosure of commission” means any omission of the kind described at DISP App 3.1.1G(3)(b); and

(3) “non-disclosure of commission” means “failure to disclose commission” as defined at DISP App 3.1.5G(7) or recurring non-disclosure of commission.

## DISP App 3.11.1 (Rules)

DISP App 3.11.2R and DISP App 3.11.3R apply where:

(1) a complainant has made a complaint to a firm in relation to its sale of a payment protection contract which covered or purported to cover a credit agreement (this includes partial coverage);

(2) the complaint was rejected by the firm before 29 August 2017 in that the firm did not offer the complainant the redress they would have been offered had the firm concluded that the complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract they bought; and

(3) any referral of the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service has been concluded and did not result in the firm offering (or being required to pay) the complainant redress on the basis that the complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract they bought.

metadata.section_name: DISP App 3.11 Obligation to write letters to certain rejected complainants

verdicts.evidence:
We are writing to acknowledge your complaint and to set out what happens next.

You have raised two concerns: first, that when you opened your execution‑only share dealing account you were not given information about our complaints procedure or about the Financial Ombudsman Service; and second, that when you purchased a structured product ... you did not ask you about your knowledge or experience ...
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing draft opens by explicitly acknowledging the complaint and accurately restates the two issues raised, satisfying the acknowledgement requirement.
The draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor provide reasons for a decision; it only says the firm is investigating and will write again.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with full details of how to do this, including the FOS's contact information and explanatory leaflet, when we send our final response.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer the complaint to the FOS and indicates that contact details and a leaflet will be supplied.
The draft mentions the right to refer to the FOS but does not specify the six‑month deadline from the date of the final response.

verdicts.evidence:
We are sorry to hear about your experience and want to assure you that we take your concerns seriously.
We are also investigating your concerns about the structured product... We aim to complete our investigation and send you a full response as promptly as possible.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is courteous, acknowledges the complainant’s loss, and commits to investigate the issues raised without dismissing them, demonstrating fair and balanced treatment.
The draft does not address whether any redress or remedial action will be offered, nor does it explain why none would be provided, despite the complaint involving a financial loss.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us. We are sorry to hear about your experience and want to assure you that we take your concerns seriously. We are writing to acknowledge your complaint and to set out what happens next. **Your complaint** You have raised two concerns: first, that when you opened your execution-only share dealing account you were not given information about our complaints procedure or about the Financial Ombudsman Service; and second, that when you purchased a structured product through your account, we did not ask you about your knowledge or experience of complex products before allowing the purchase, and did not warn you that we were not assessing whether the product was appropriate for you.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with a clear acknowledgement and accurately reflects both substantive elements of the complaint: (1) failure to provide complaints procedure and FOS information at account opening, and (2) failure to assess appropriateness before the structured product purchase. The summary is specific and demonstrates understanding of what is being complained about.

The customer draft does not state a decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) on either complaint point. It states 'We are reviewing whether this information was provided to you' and 'We are also investigating your concerns about the purchase of the structured product' but does not reach or communicate a final decision. The draft explicitly defers both issues to a future communication ('We will write to you again once our investigation is complete'). This is an acknowledgement/holding response, not a final response with a decision and reasons. The rubric requires a decision and substantive reasons; neither is present.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with full details of how to do this, including the FOS's contact information and explanatory leaflet, when we send our final response.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the right to refer to the FOS and signposts how to do so (FOS contact information and explanatory leaflet will be provided with the final response). The language is clear and identifies the FOS by name. Although the full contact details are deferred to the final response, the commitment to provide them is explicit.

The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS. It says 'If you remain dissatisfied with our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service' but does not specify that this must be done within six months of the final response date. The six-month referral limit is a mandatory disclosure under DISP 1.1A.31R and is not present in the customer-facing text.

verdicts.evidence: We are reviewing whether this information was provided to you at the time you opened your account and will address this as part of our investigation. We are also investigating your concerns about the purchase of the structured product, including whether the correct steps were followed before that transaction was completed.
verdicts.reasoning: The response is courteous and non-dismissive in tone ('We are sorry to hear about your experience and want to assure you that we take your concerns seriously'). However, it does not engage substantively with the complaint on its merits. Both issues are deferred to future investigation without any substantive engagement, analysis, or explanation of the firm's position. The response reads as a holding acknowledgement rather than a fair treatment of the complaint itself. The complainant is left without any substantive response to their concerns, which undermines fair treatment despite the courteous framing.

The customer draft does not address redress at all. The complaint explicitly raises a significant financial loss ('I've now lost a significant amount of money'). The response does not offer redress, explain why none is warranted, or even acknowledge that redress is being considered. The handler answer notes 'Do not admit liability or quantify redress in any customer communication at this stage' and flags the appropriateness issue for escalation, but this position is not communicated to the customer. The customer-facing draft simply defers the matter without addressing whether remedial action or compensation is appropriate.

claims.claim: DISP 1.1A.10R requires MiFID investment firms to publish complaints process details and provide them to clients on request or when acknowledging a MiFID complaint.
claims.reasoning: The provision DISP 1.1A.10 (Rules) states exactly this requirement.

claims.claim: DISP 1.1A.11R requires MiFID investment firms to provide information about the Financial Ombudsman Service (including its website) on their website and in the general conditions of contracts with eligible complainants.
claims.reasoning: The provision DISP 1.1A.11 (Rules) contains this exact requirement.

claims.claim: DISP 1.1A.12R requires MiFID investment firms to establish, implement and maintain effective and transparent complaints management policies and procedures.
claims.reasoning: The provision DISP 1.1A.12 (Rules) sets out this obligation.

claims.claim: A share‑dealing firm would typically be a MiFID investment firm.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision states that share‑dealing firms are automatically MiFID investment firms; the claim is an assumption not supported by the provided text.

claims.claim: The customer's allegation that they received no information about the complaints procedure or FOS at sign‑up is potentially a breach of DISP 1.1A.11R and DISP 1.1A.12R.
claims.reasoning: The provisions set out the required behaviour but do not confirm that a breach has occurred; the claim about a breach is not supported by the retrieved rules.

claims.claim: DISP 1.1A.31R requires the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet to be enclosed with a final response for MiFID complaints.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.1A.31 (Rules) explicitly lists the requirement to enclose the FOS leaflet in a final response.

claims.claim: We are required to make information about our complaints handling process available to our clients, including details of how to contact our complaints team.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.1A.10R obliges firms to publish details of the complaints handling process and provide them to clients.

claims.claim: We are also required to provide information about the Financial Ombudsman Service — including its website address — on our website and in the general conditions of our contracts with clients.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.1A.11R sets out this exact requirement.

claims.claim: If we are unable to resolve your complaint within the applicable timeframe, we will write to you to explain why and to let you know when you can expect a final response.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.1A.29 requires firms to reply without undue delay, but it does not specify a particular “applicable timeframe”; the claim goes beyond the provision.

claims.claim: If you remain dissatisfied with our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.1A.30 and 1.1A.31 require firms to inform clients of the right to refer a complaint to the FOS.

claims.claim: We will provide you with full details of how to do this, including the FOS's contact information and explanatory leaflet, when we send our final response.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.1A.31 mandates that the final response must include the FOS contact details and the standard explanatory leaflet.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I have a regulated mortgage with your bank and I'm writing to complain about two issues. First, when I fell into arrears last year, you applied a £250 'arrears management fee' each month for three consecutive months, plus compound interest on those fees. I've checked with other lenders and nobody charges anything close to that — most charge £25-50 at most. I believe these charges are grossly excessive and the compound interest on a second charge mortgage is unlawful. Second, when I tried to call your complaints line to raise this issue, the only number provided was an 0871 number. I was on hold for 45 minutes and the call cost me over £18. I shouldn't have to pay a premium rate number just to make a complaint. I'm currently on long-term sick leave following a cancer diagnosis and am struggling financially as a result of reduced income. I'd like a full refund of the excessive charges, the interest applied to them, and reimbursement of my phone costs.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises three distinct regulatory issues, all of which have clear grounding in the retrieved provisions. The customer is also showing clear signs of vulnerability (cancer diagnosis, financial hardship from reduced income), which requires careful handling and mandates human review before sending.

**Issue 1: Arrears management charges (£250/month)**
MCOB 12.4.1R requires that payment shortfall charges must be objectively justifiable as equal to or lower than a reasonable calculation of the cost of the additional administration required. A charge of £250/month, compared to market rates of £25–50, is likely to be very difficult to justify under this rule. You should urgently review whether the firm can produce an objective cost justification for the £250 figure. If it cannot, the charges are likely to be in breach of MCOB 12.4.1R and should be refunded. MCOB 12.4.1AR (Evidential) also notes that imposing charges on a customer who is adhering to an agreed repayment arrangement tends to show contravention — check whether the customer was on any such arrangement during this period.

MCOB 12.5.2R and MCOB 12.5.1R also apply: charges in connection with administering a regulated mortgage must not be excessive. MCOB 12.5.3G sets out factors to consider, including comparison with market rates and the degree to which charges abuse the customer's trust — both of which appear adverse here.

**Issue 2: Compound interest on arrears charges (second charge mortgage)**
MCOB 12.5.5R is directly on point: a second charge lender may only charge interest on charges applied for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract if the interest is simple interest. If this is a second charge mortgage and compound interest has been applied to the arrears management fees, that is a breach of MCOB 12.5.5R and the excess interest must be refunded. You need to confirm: (a) is this a second charge regulated mortgage contract? (b) was compound interest applied to the arrears charges? If both are yes, refund of the compound element is required.

**Issue 3: 0871 complaints line**
GEN 7.2.1R requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate. GEN 7.2.3R defines basic rate as the simple cost of connection, providing no contribution to the firm's costs or revenues. GEN 7.2.5R explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant. The use of an 0871 number for the complaints line is a breach of GEN 7.2.1R. The customer's call costs (£18+) should be reimbursed. You should also arrange for the complaints line number to be changed immediately.

**Vulnerability**
The customer has disclosed a cancer diagnosis and financial hardship. This is a clear vulnerability indicator. The response and any remediation must be handled with appropriate care and sensitivity. This is an additional reason for human review.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending:**
1. Is this a second charge regulated mortgage contract? (Determines whether MCOB 12.5.5R on simple interest applies.)
2. Was compound interest actually applied to the arrears charges, and if so, what is the quantum?
3. Can the firm produce an objective cost justification for the £250/month arrears management fee?
4. Was the customer adhering to any agreed repayment arrangement during the period the charges were applied? (Relevant to MCOB 12.4.1AE.)
5. What is the exact amount of the call costs claimed?
6. The customer is requesting a full refund — any decision to offer or decline redress must be made by a human reviewer, not this draft.

The customer draft below acknowledges the complaint, confirms the issues are being investigated, and does not commit to specific redress amounts — that must follow human review. The FOS referral right is included as the complaint is being treated as received.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear about your health and the financial pressures you are facing, and I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint seriously.

You have raised three concerns, which I will address in turn.

**Arrears management charges**

You have complained that the arrears management fees applied to your account — £250 per month for three months — are excessive compared with what other lenders charge. We are required by our regulator to ensure that charges applied when a customer has a payment shortfall are no higher than a reasonable calculation of the cost of the additional administration involved. We are also required to ensure that charges in connection with administering your mortgage are not excessive. We take these obligations seriously and are reviewing whether the charges applied to your account meet these standards, including by reference to the factors our regulator expects us to consider, such as how our charges compare with the market.

**Interest applied to arrears charges**

You have also raised a concern about the interest charged on top of those fees. We are reviewing how interest was calculated and applied in your case, and whether it was applied in the manner our regulatory obligations require.

**Cost of calling our complaints line**

You are right to raise this. Our regulator requires that any telephone line we operate for customers to contact us about their contract must not cost more than the basic rate of a call — that is, the simple cost of connection, with no additional charge going to us. Numbers beginning with 0871 do not meet this standard. We should not have directed you to an 0871 number to make your complaint, and I apologise for this. We are investigating the call costs you incurred and will address this as part of our response.

**Next steps**

We are investigating all three aspects of your complaint and will write to you again with our findings and our final response. We aim to do this as promptly as possible, and we will keep you informed of progress.

If you remain dissatisfied with our final response, or if we have not resolved your complaint within eight weeks of receiving it, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. I will enclose their explanatory leaflet with this letter.

Please do not hesitate to contact us if you have any questions in the meantime. We are committed to treating you fairly and with the care your situation deserves.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires that payment shortfall charges must be objectively justifiable as equal to or lower than a reasonable calculation of the cost of additional administration — directly applicable to the £250/month arrears management fee.
Imposing a payment shortfall charge on a customer adhering to an agreed repayment arrangement tends to show contravention of MCOB 12.4.1R(1) — relevant if the customer was on such an arrangement.
Requires that a regulated mortgage contract does not impose, and cannot be used to impose, excessive charges upon a customer.
Requires that charges in connection with administering a regulated mortgage contract are not excessive — applies to the arrears management fees.

Sets out factors for determining whether a charge is excessive, including comparison with market rates and the degree to which charges abuse the customer's trust — both factors are adverse on the facts described.

A second charge lender may only charge simple interest on charges applied for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract — directly applicable to the compound interest allegation if this is a second charge mortgage.
Requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it about a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate — breached by use of an 0871 complaints line.
Defines basic rate as the simple cost of connection, providing no contribution to the firm's costs or revenues — the 0871 number does not meet this standard.
Explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant with the call charges rule — confirms the complaints line number is in breach.

# MCOB — MCOB 12.5 Excessive charges: regulated mortgage contracts, home reversion plans and regulated sale and rent back agreements

## MCOB 12.5.1 (Rules)

A firm must ensure that any regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement that it enters into does not impose, and cannot be used to impose, excessive charges upon a customer.

## MCOB 12.5.2 (Rules)

A firm must ensure that its charges to a customer in connection with the firm entering into, making a further advance or further release on, administering, arranging or advising on a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement , or arranging or advising on a variation to the terms of a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement are not excessive.

## MCOB 12.5.3 (Guidance)

When determining whether a charge is excessive, a firm should consider:

(1) the amount of its charges for the services or products in question compared with charges for similar products or services on the market;

(2) the degree to which the charges are an abuse of the trust that the customer has placed in the firm; and

(3) the nature and extent of the disclosure of the charges to the customer.

## MCOB 12.5.4 (Guidance)

Mortgage lenders are also subject to requirements relating to responsible lending (see MCOB 11).

## MCOB 12.5.5 (Rules)

A second charge lender may only charge interest on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract if the interest is simple interest.

metadata.section_name: MCOB 12.5 Excessive charges: regulated mortgage contracts, home reversion plans and regulated sale and rent back agreements

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 12.1 Application

## MCOB 12.1.4 (Rules)

The payment shortfall charges and excessive charges requirements in this chapter will continue to apply to a firm after a regulated mortgage contract has come to an end following the sale of a repossessed property. The excessive charges requirements will continue to apply to a firm after a home reversion plan has ended. References in this chapter to 'customer' will include references to a former customer as appropriate.

## MCOB 12.1.5 (Guidance)

The FCA will expect a firm to ensure that charges made to a customer arising from the sale of a repossessed property and charges arising in relation to a sale shortfall are not excessive and are subject to the same considerations as apply with respect to payment shortfall charges under this chapter.

## MCOB 12.1.5A (Rules)

The rules in MCOB 12.4 (Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts) and MCOB 12.5 (Excessive charges: regulated mortgage contracts, home reversion plans and regulated sale and rent back agreements) apply to:

(1) second charge regulated mortgage contracts entered into before 21 March 2016, in relation to charges imposed on a customer for events occurring on or after 21 March 2016; and

(2) regulated mortgage contracts which are legacy CCA mortgage contracts secured by a first charge legal mortgage, in relation to charges imposed on a customer for events occurring on or after the earliest of:

(a) the date on which the lender first acts in compliance or purported compliance with rules in the FCA Handbook which apply to regulated mortgage contracts in respect of the contract;

(b) the date from which the lender notifies the borrower in writing that it will act in compliance with such rules in respect of the contract; and

(c) 21 March 2017.

metadata.section_name: MCOB 12.1 Application

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 12.2 Purpose

## MCOB 12.2.1 (Guidance)

(1) Principle 6 requires a firm to pay due regard to the interests of its customers and treat them fairly. A firm is also under an obligation, as a consequence of this sourcebook's disclosure requirements, to make charges transparent to customers. This chapter reinforces these requirements by preventing a firm from imposing unfair and excessive charges.

(2) The level of charges under a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement is not typically a matter for regulation. However, in certain limited circumstances, the FCA believes that customers should be protected from unfair and excessive charging practices. This chapter considers four specific circumstances, where:

(a) the charges imposed upon a customer seeking to terminate a regulated mortgage contract before the end of the term of the contract do not reflect the cost of termination to the firm;

(b) the charges imposed on a customer in payment difficulties are not based upon the costs incurred by the firm;

(c) the charges (including rates of interest) imposed on a customer under a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement are excessive and contrary to the customer's interests; and

(d) the charges made to a customer in connection with a firm entering into, making a further advance on, administering, arranging or advising on a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement , or arranging or advising on a variation to the terms of a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement are excessive.

metadata.section_name: MCOB 12.2 Purpose

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 12.4 Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts

## MCOB 12.4.1D (Guidance)

A firm may recalculate the periodic instalment of capital or interest (or both), provided that any such recalculation is consistent with the firm’s obligations under the Handbook.

If a firm exercises a power under the terms of a mortgage contract to recalculate periodic instalments of capital or interest (or both) using a mortgage balance that includes charges (such as arrears management charges) or interest arising because one or more monthly instalments were missed, the firm does not have to keep a record of the amount of each periodic instalment of capital or interest (or both) omitting any element of such charges or interest to comply with MCOB 12.4.1BR.

## MCOB 12.4.2 (Guidance)

For each type of payment shortfall charge (for example, a monthly arrears management charge), a firm may calculate the same level of additional administration costs and payment shortfall charges for all regulated mortgage contracts where the customer is in payment shortfall , rather than performing a calculation on the basis of the individual regulated mortgage contract with the particular customer.

## MCOB 12.4.3 (Guidance)

Firms are also subject to requirements on information provision and standards relating to payment shortfalls and repossessions (see MCOB 13 (Payment difficulties and repossessions)).

metadata.section_name: MCOB 12.4 Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 4.6A Rolling-up of fees or charges into loan

## MCOB 4.6A.1 (Rules)

A mortgage lender may not offer a regulated mortgage contract to a customer on the basis that fees or charges of any kind (receivable either by the mortgage lender or another party) are automatically added to the sum advanced.

## MCOB 4.6A.2 (Rules)

A firm must not undertake any action that commits a customer to an application for a regulated mortgage contract where a fee or charge of any kind (receivable either by the firm or another party) is to be added to the sum advanced under the regulated mortgage contract, unless the customer has made a positive choice to add the fee or charge to the sum advanced.

metadata.section_name: MCOB 4.6A Rolling-up of fees or charges into loan

retrieved_chunks.content:
# GEN — GEN 7.2 Call charges

## GEN 7.2.1 (Rules)

A firm which operates a telephone line for the purpose of enabling a consumer to contact the firm in relation to a contract that has been entered into with the firm, must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate for the telephone call.

## GEN 7.2.2 (Guidance)

The contract entered into with the firm may be in writing or otherwise.

## GEN 7.2.3 (Rules)

For the purposes of the call charges rule, the basic rate is the simple cost of connection and must not provide the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues.

## GEN 7.2.4 (Rules)

The following numbers, if used by firms, would comply with the call charges rule:

(1) geographic numbers or numbers which are always set at the same rate, which usually begin with the prefix 01, 02 or 03;

(2) calls which can be free of charge to call, for example 0800 and 0808 numbers; and

(3) standard mobile numbers, which usually begin with the prefix 07, provided that the firm ordinarily uses a mobile number to receive telephone calls.

## GEN 7.2.5 (Rules)

The following numbers, if used by firms, would not comply with the call charges rule:

(1) premium rate numbers that begin with the prefix 09;

(2) other revenue sharing numbers in which a portion of the call charge can be used to either provide a service or make a small payment to the firm, such as telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 084 or 0871, 0872 or 0873; and

(3) telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 0870 as the cost of making a telephone call on such numbers can be higher than a geographic cost and will vary depending on the consumer’s telephone tariff.

metadata.section_name: GEN 7.2 Call charges

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 12.4 Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts

## MCOB 12.4.1 (Rules)

(1) A firm must ensure that any regulated mortgage contract that it enters into does not impose, and cannot be used to impose, a charge or charges for a payment shortfall on a customer unless the firm is able objectively to justify that the charge is equal to or lower than a reasonable calculation of the cost of the additional administration required as a result of the customer having a payment shortfall .

(2) [deleted]

## MCOB 12.4.1A (Evidential Provisions)

The imposition of a charge for a payment shortfall on a customer who is adhering to an arrangement under which the customer and the firm agree that the customer will make payments of a set amount per month (or other agreed period) on agreed dates may be relied upon as tending to show contravention of MCOB 12.4.1R (1)

## MCOB 12.4.1B (Rules)

When:

(1) a customer has a payment shortfall in respect of a regulated mortgage contract;

(2) a payment is made which is not sufficient to cover all of the amounts that are currently due under that contract and the firm must therefore decide how to allocate the payment; and

(3) as part of that decision, the firm is considering the respective priority to be given to:

(a) the current month’s periodic instalment of capital or interest (or both);

(b) the payment shortfall; and

(c) interest or charges resulting from the payment shortfall,

the firm must set that order of priority in a way that will minimise the amount of the payment shortfall once the payment has been allocated.

## MCOB 12.4.1C (Guidance)

MCOB 12.4.1BR does not preclude a firm applying part or all of a payment received to fees and charges not resulting from the payment shortfall (such as ground rent settled on behalf of the customer).

metadata.section_name: MCOB 12.4 Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts
MCOB 12.4 Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts

# MCOB — MCOB 12.3 Early repayment charges: regulated mortgage contracts

## MCOB 12.3.1 (Rules)

A firm must ensure that any regulated mortgage contract that it enters into does not impose, and cannot be used to impose, an early repayment charge other than one that is:

(1) able to be expressed as a cash value; and

(2) a reasonable pre-estimate of the costs as a result of the customer repaying the amount due under the regulated mortgage contract before the contract has terminated.

## MCOB 12.3.2 (Guidance)

A firm can choose the method it employs for calculating early repayment charges in accordance with MCOB 12.3.1 R. A firm should not use the 'Rule of 78' , which is not appropriate as it effectively overstates the cost to the mortgage lender.

## MCOB 12.3.3 (Guidance)

A firm may calculate the same level of early repayment charge for all regulated mortgage contracts of a similar type (for example a tranche of regulated mortgage contracts offering a particular fixed rate of interest), rather than on the basis of the individual regulated mortgage contract with the particular customer.

## MCOB 12.3.4 (Rules)

Before:

(1) entering into a regulated mortgage contract with a customer; or

(2) making a further advance on an existing regulated mortgage contract; or

(3) changing all or part of a regulated mortgage contract from one interest rate to another; a firm must disclose to the customer:

(a) in the illustration provided in accordance with MCOB 5, MCOB 7.6.7 R, MCOB 7.6.18 R, MCOB 7.6.22 R, MCOB 7.6.31 R, or MCOB 9; and

(b) in the illustration provided as part of the offer document in accordance with MCOB 6.4.1 R(1) and MCOB 9.5;

the maximum amount payable as an early repayment charge in respect of that regulated mortgage contract, if an early repayment charge applies.

metadata.section_name: MCOB 12.3 Early repayment charges: regulated mortgage contracts

# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

(c) If the agreement provides that a charge is payable by a customer in exchange for the creation or continuation of an arranged overdraft facility, whether or not the customer in fact uses the facility, this charge is also caught by the definition of an arranged overdraft charge unless the facility has a pre-agreed limit in excess of £10,000. A charge of this sort is often referred to as a “facility fee” and payable periodically, for example annually.

(d) The definitions of an arranged overdraft charge and an unarranged overdraft charge are not limited to charges that are described as financial consideration for the provision of credit. They could include, for example, a charge that is expressed as being referable to the execution of the payment transaction, if the charge is payable only where the transaction results in the account being in an overdrawn position or remaining in such a position. A charge for a payment transaction that is payable irrespective of whether or not the current account has a credit balance or a debit balance is not, however, caught by these definitions.

(e) The definitions also do not include charges for operating or maintaining a personal current account (as distinct from charges for granting or continuing to make available an arranged overdraft facility in connection with the account), provided that the incidence and amount of the charges are not affected by whether or how much the customer uses an overdraft. A monthly account charge could be an example of such a charge.

(9) CONC 5C.2 requires firms to use only a rate of interest expressed as a percentage applied on an annual basis to the relevant balance of arranged overdraft or unarranged overdraft. If interest is compounded, firms are free to choose the intervals at which they add arranged overdraft charges and unarranged overdraft charges to the principal balance, provided that the same compounding frequency is used i
