# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# DISP — DISP INTRO 1 Introduction

## DISP INTRO 1.4

Chapters 2 - 4: The Financial Ombudsman ServiceChapters 2, 3 and 4 set out how the Financial Ombudsman Service (operated by FOS Ltd) considers unresolved complaints.Chapter 2 sets out the scope of the Financial Ombudsman Service's two jurisdictions:• the Compulsory Jurisdiction; and• the Voluntary Jurisdiction.The scope of the two jurisdictions is defined by: the type of activity to which the complaint relates; the place where the activity took place; the eligibility of the complainant; and the time limits for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.Chapter 3 sets out the procedures of the Financial Ombudsman Service, including consideration and determination of complaints and how the Financial Ombudsman Service deals with information received.Chapter 4 sets out the terms under which VJ participants participate in the Voluntary Jurisdiction.

## DISP INTRO 1.5

Appendix 1: FCA’s guidance on handling mortgage-endowment complaintsThis appendix contains the FCA's guidance to firms on handling complaints relating to mortgage endowments.

## DISP INTRO 1.6

Appendix 3: FCA’s rules and guidance on handling payment protection insurance complaintsThis appendix sets out the approach which firms should use when handling complaints relating to the sale of payment protection contracts.

## DISP INTRO 1.7

Financial Ombudsman Service feesThe rules on fees charged in respect of the Financial Ombudsman Service are in Chapter 5 of the Fees manual.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# FEES — FEES 5.5E Representative case fees (for financial years beginning on or after 1 April 2026)

## FEES 5.5E.1 (Rules)

FEES 5.5E applies to a complainant representative in relation to a complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service which is closed on or after 1 April 2026.

## FEES 5.5E.2 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E does not apply to the Voluntary Jurisdiction.

## FEES 5.5E.3 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E sets out when a complainant representative that is representing a complainant must pay fees in respect of complaints referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## FEES 5.5E.4 (Rules)

A complainant representative will not be liable for a representative case fee under FEES 5.5E if it is acting entirely pro bono in relation to the complaint.

## FEES 5.5E.5 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E.4R applies where a complainant representative is representing the complainant without any fees, charges or other form of remuneration becoming payable by the complainant in any circumstance.

## FEES 5.5E.6 (Rules)

Where a complaint is closed by the Financial Ombudsman Service during a financial year in circumstances where:(1) the complaint was referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or after 1 April 2025; and(2) a category B complainant representative was (at any time) representing the complainant in relation to that complaint, the category B complainant representative which was first representing the complainant (even if it is no longer doing so at the time the complaint is closed) must pay to the FOS Ltd:(3) the reduced representative case fee specified in FEES 5 Annex 5R Part 2, if the complaint was closed as a change in outcome in favour of the complainant; or(4) the standard representative case fee specified in FEES 5 Annex 5R Part 2, if the complaint was closed other than as a change in outcome in favour of the complainant.

metadata.section_name: FEES 5.5E Representative case fees (for financial years beginning on or after 1 April 2026)

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

## CONC 5C.2.1 (Rules)

(1) A firm must not:

(a) enter into an agreement with a customer that provides for an arranged overdraft charge or an unarranged overdraft charge; or

(b) impose on a customer an arranged overdraft charge or an unarranged overdraft charge,

unless the conditions in (2) to (7) are satisfied.

(2) The charge must be a rate of interest expressed as a percentage applied on an annual basis to the relevant balance of arranged overdraft or unarranged overdraft (as the case may be).

(3) The rate of interest that applies to any given balance of arranged overdraft relating to a personal current account must either be zero or the same as the rate of interest that applies to any other balance of arranged overdraft in respect of that personal current account.

(4) The rate of interest that applies to any given balance of unarranged overdraft relating to a personal current account must either be zero or the same as the rate of interest that applies to any other balance of unarranged overdraft in respect of that personal current account.

(5) A firm must not require a customer to pay more than one arranged overdraft charge or more than one unarranged overdraft charge arising out of the same event.

(6) Where a customer has an arranged overdraft, in relation to a personal current account, to which a rate of interest above zero applies, any unarranged overdraft charge imposed on the customer in relation to that personal current account must also consist of a rate of interest computed, structured and presented in an identical manner (although the level of the rate of interest that applies to the unarranged overdraft may be lower).

metadata.section_name: CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

(5) A firm is not prevented from providing in the terms and conditions of the overdraft that no interest is payable in respect of arranged overdraft balances or unarranged overdraft balances of up to specified amounts (sometimes described as “fee-free amounts” or “buffer zones”) where permitted by CONC 5C.2.1R. The purpose of CONC 5C.2.1R(7) is to prevent firms from offering fee-free amounts or buffer zones that are free only in certain circumstances. An example of a buffer zone that is not permitted is where no interest is payable if an unarranged overdraft balance does not exceed the upper threshold of the buffer zone, but where interest becomes payable in respect of the entire balance (including the part of the balance in the buffer zone) if the customer exceeds the threshold.

(6) A firm is not prevented from waiving or reducing overdraft charges (in whole or in part) in appropriate circumstances (for example, where the firm is treating a customer with forbearance in line with other rules in this sourcebook).

(7) CONC 5C.2.1R does not prohibit the level of the single, uniform contractual rate of interest from differing from customer to customer, or between personal current accounts for the same customer.

(8)

(a) The definitions of an arranged overdraft charge and an unarranged overdraft charge are broad.

(b) These definitions capture any charges that arise because a customer has used an overdraft, or that are triggered by - or the size of which are affected by - the fact that the personal current account has entered, remains in, or extended, a debit position.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

However, on the substantive complaint (system error, incorrect fee application, compensation for stress and time), the response defers investigation without engaging on the merits. The tone is appropriate and non-dismissive, but the treatment is incomplete because a final response should address the substance, not defer it. The response reads as even-handed but superficial on the core complaint.

It defers the redress decision to a future response. The handler answer notes that compensation must be reviewed by the Head of Customer Operations and that no liability should be admitted at this stage, but this is internal reasoning not reflected in the customer-facing outcome. For a final response, a position on redress (offer, rejection with reasons, or further investigation with timeline) should be communicated to the customer, not deferred.

claims.claim: The written summary the customer received said only 'your complaint is now closed' and contained no reference to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), no FOS contact details, and no statement of the six-month referral window.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision describes the specific content of the summary letter; the claim is a factual observation not supported by the provisions.

claims.claim: Under DISP 1.6.2R, any written response closing a complaint must notify the customer of their right to refer to the FOS, include FOS contact details, and state that they have six months from the date of the final response to do so.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (section 7.5) explicitly requires notification of the FOS right, inclusion of contact details and a statement that the customer has six months from the final response to refer.

claims.claim: The summary letter as described failed to meet these requirements.
claims.reasoning: The provisions set the requirements but do not provide a factual determination that this particular letter failed to comply.

claims.claim: The six‑month period for referring a complaint to the FOS is only triggered by a response that is a valid final response (or summary resolution communication) that itself tells the complainant about the six‑month limit.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2R and the accompanying DISP 2.8.3 guidance state that the six‑month clock starts only when the final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication informs the complainant of the limit.

claims.claim: Because the written summary did not include the required FOS referral information, the six‑month clock has not yet validly started running.
claims.reasoning: Since DISP 2.8.3 guidance requires the response to tell the complainant about the six‑month limit for the clock to start, the absence of that information means the condition is not satisfied.

claims.claim: Given the customer is seeking financial compensation beyond the refund already agreed, this must be reviewed by the Head of Customer Operations before any response is finalised, per section 7.4 which requires countersignature for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more or regulatory significance.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 mandates Head of Customer Operations countersignature for complaints involving redress of £500+ **or** those with potential regulatory significance. The claim correctly cites the regulatory‑significance part, but the statement that any compensation beyond the £35 refund automatically triggers the £500 threshold is not supported.

claims.claim: We aim to issue a full response within eight weeks of the date your complaint was first received, in line with our regulatory obligations.
claims.reasoning: Section 7.4 of the internal procedure (cited in Provision 1) requires a final response letter to be issued within eight weeks of receipt.

claims.claim: When we close a complaint, we are required to tell you about your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) if you remain dissatisfied, and to provide their contact details.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (section 7.5) sets out the requirement to notify the complainant of the FOS referral right and to include contact details.

claims.claim: If at any point you are not satisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: The same provision (section 7.5) confirms the complainant’s right to refer to the FOS, and the service is provided free of charge.

claims.claim: You have six months from the date of our final response letter to refer the complaint to the FOS.
claims.reasoning: Section 7.5 of the internal procedure, reflecting DISP 1.6.2R, requires the six‑month period to be stated in the final response.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with how my complaint was handled. Three weeks ago, I contacted your bank about an incorrect fee of £45 charged to my current account. Your staff member called me back the same day and offered to refund the fee, which I accepted over the phone. I then received a short letter saying the complaint was resolved, but the letter did not mention the Financial Ombudsman Service at all — no website address, no information about my right to refer the matter if I changed my mind, nothing. I have since looked into this further and I believe the fee was actually part of a wider pattern of overcharging on my account going back several months. I am no longer satisfied with the resolution and wish to escalate this matter. I want the bank to investigate the full pattern of charges and I want to know why I was not properly informed of my rights when the complaint was first closed.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct issues that must be handled separately.

**Issue 1: Defective summary resolution communication (original £45 fee complaint)**
The customer accepted a same-day verbal resolution and received a short letter closing the complaint. Under Crest Bank's procedure (reflecting DISP 1.6.2R), any written response informing a customer of complaint resolution must include FOS referral rights, including the FOS website and telephone number, and must state that the customer has six months from the date of the final response to refer to the FOS. The letter sent to the customer omitted all of this. This is a compliance failure. The six-month referral window is only triggered by a valid final response or summary resolution communication that contains the required FOS information (per DISP 2.8.3G). Because the original letter was defective in this respect, it is arguable that the six-month clock has not yet started running — you should confirm this with your compliance team before sending any customer response, as it has implications for the customer's referral rights.

**Issue 2: New/escalated complaint about a wider pattern of overcharging**
The customer is now raising a new substantive complaint about a pattern of overcharging going back several months. This is a separate complaint that must be logged in the CMS as a new complaint (or treated as a re-opened/escalated complaint), investigated impartially, and resolved within eight weeks of receipt, with a compliant final response issued in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. Given that it involves potential financial redress (multiple charges over several months), this must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch of any final response, per Crest Bank's internal procedure.

**Recommended actions before sending the customer draft:**
1. Confirm with compliance whether the defective original letter means the six-month FOS referral clock has not started — do not state a definitive position on this in the customer letter until confirmed.
2. Log the escalated complaint about the wider pattern of charges in the CMS with a new complaint reference.
3. Do not quantify or commit to any redress in the customer letter at this stage — investigation is required first.
4. The customer draft below acknowledges both issues, apologises for the omission of FOS information, confirms the new investigation, and provides the correct FOS details. It does not commit to a specific outcome on the wider charges.
5. Human review is required before sending given the potential financial redress and the compliance failure in the original response.

**Assumption flagged:** The customer draft states that the customer retains the right to refer the original complaint to the FOS, but does not specify a deadline, because the validity of the original closing letter is uncertain. If compliance confirms the original letter was a valid summary resolution communication despite the omission, the six-month clock may already be running and the draft will need to be updated to reflect the correct deadline.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry for the concerns you have raised, and I want to address them fully.

**Your original complaint about the £45 fee**

When you contacted us three weeks ago about an incorrect fee of £45, we refunded that fee and sent you a letter to confirm the matter had been resolved. I am sorry to tell you that the letter we sent you at that time was incomplete. It should have told you about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if you remained dissatisfied, and it should have included their contact details. It did not, and I apologise for that oversight.

You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge, if you are not satisfied with how we have handled it. Their contact details are:

- **Website:** www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- **Telephone:** 0800 023 4567

We will write to you separately to confirm the position on the time limit for doing so, once we have reviewed the matter internally.

**Your escalated complaint about a wider pattern of charges**

We have noted that you are now raising a separate concern about a pattern of overcharging on your account going back several months. We take this seriously. We are treating this as a new complaint and have logged it under complaint reference [REFERENCE NUMBER]. Your complaint handler is [HANDLER NAME], who can be contacted at [CONTACT DETAILS].

We will investigate your account records thoroughly and impartially. We aim to provide you with a full response within eight weeks of the date we received this complaint. If for any reason we are unable to do so within that timeframe, we will write to you to explain why and to remind you of your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service at that stage.

Thank you for bringing these matters to our attention. We are committed to resolving them fairly and will be in touch as our investigation progresses.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

Sets the six-month time limit for a customer to refer a complaint to the FOS after receiving a final response, which is relevant to whether the defective original letter triggered that clock.

Indicates that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a valid final response or summary resolution communication that tells the complainant about the six-month limit — relevant to whether the defective letter started the clock.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 7. Procedures

### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints

A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination.

All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.

### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement

Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must:

1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected.
2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer.
3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

### 7.3 Investigation Process

The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include:

1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems.
2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made.
3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure

# FEES — FEES 5.5E Representative case fees (for financial years beginning on or after 1 April 2026)

## FEES 5.5E.1 (Rules)

FEES 5.5E applies to a complainant representative in relation to a complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service which is closed on or after 1 April 2026.

## FEES 5.5E.2 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E does not apply to the Voluntary Jurisdiction.

## FEES 5.5E.3 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E sets out when a complainant representative that is representing a complainant must pay fees in respect of complaints referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## FEES 5.5E.4 (Rules)

A complainant representative will not be liable for a representative case fee under FEES 5.5E if it is acting entirely pro bono in relation to the complaint.

## FEES 5.5E.5 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E.4R applies where a complainant representative is representing the complainant without any fees, charges or other form of remuneration becoming payable by the complainant in any circumstance.

## FEES 5.5E.6 (Rules)

Where a complaint is closed by the Financial Ombudsman Service during a financial year in circumstances where:(1) the complaint was referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or after 1 April 2025; and(2) a category B complainant representative was (at any time) representing the complainant in relation to that complaint, the category B complainant representative which was first representing the complainant (even if it is no longer doing so at the time the complaint is closed) must pay to the FOS Ltd:(3) the reduced representative case fee specified in FEES 5 Annex 5R Part 2, if the complaint was closed as a change in outcome in favour of the complainant; or(4) the standard representative case fee specified in FEES 5 Annex 5R Part 2, if the complaint was closed other than as a change in outcome in favour of the complainant.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

# DISP — DISP INTRO 1 Introduction

## DISP INTRO 1.4

Chapters 2 - 4: The Financial Ombudsman ServiceChapters 2, 3 and 4 set out how the Financial Ombudsman Service (operated by FOS Ltd) considers unresolved complaints.Chapter 2 sets out the scope of the Financial Ombudsman Service's two jurisdictions:• the Compulsory Jurisdiction; and• the Voluntary Jurisdiction.The scope of the two jurisdictions is defined by: the type of activity to which the complaint relates; the place where the activity took place; the eligibility of the complainant; and the time limits for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.Chapter 3 sets out the procedures of the Financial Ombudsman Service, including consideration and determination of complaints and how the Financial Ombudsman Service deals with information received.Chapter 4 sets out the terms under which VJ participants participate in the Voluntary Jurisdiction.

## DISP INTRO 1.5

Appendix 1: FCA’s guidance on handling mortgage-endowment complaintsThis appendix contains the FCA's guidance to firms on handling complaints relating to mortgage endowments.

## DISP INTRO 1.6

Appendix 3: FCA’s rules and guidance on handling payment protection insurance complaintsThis appendix sets out the approach which firms should use when handling complaints relating to the sale of payment protection contracts.

## DISP INTRO 1.7

Financial Ombudsman Service feesThe rules on fees charged in respect of the Financial Ombudsman Service are in Chapter 5 of the Fees manual.

# FEES — FEES 5.5C Representative case fees (for financial years ending before 1 April 2026)

## FEES 5.5C.9 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5C.8R applies, for example, where the complaint is closed by the Financial Ombudsman Service with an outcome that is more favourable for the complainant compared to when the complaint was referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## FEES 5.5C.10 (Rules)

A complainant representative must pay to the FOS Ltd any representative case fee which it is liable to pay under FEES 5.5C and which is invoiced by the FOS Ltd within 30 calendar days of the date when the invoice is issued by the FOS Ltd.

## FEES 5.5C.11 (Rules)

If, at the end of the financial year, the amount standing in credit to the complainant representative under FEES 5.5C.8R exceeds the amounts invoiced under FEES 5.5C.10R which remain unpaid (including any interest or administrative fee due under FEES 5.5C.12R), the FOS Ltd will repay the difference between the 2 amounts to the complainant representative by credit transfer within 30 calendar days of the complainant representative notifying the FOS Ltd of its account details.

## FEES 5.5C.11A (Rules)

In respect of any complaint which was not closed before 1 April 2026, the FOS Ltd will credit the amount of £250 to the complainant representative and FEES 5.5E will instead apply to that complainant representative in respect of that complaint.

# FEES — FEES 5.5C Representative case fees (for financial years ending before 1 April 2026)

## FEES 5.5C.5 (Rules)

(1) Subject to FEES 5.5C.6R, a complainant representative must pay to the FOS Ltd a representative case fee of £250 in respect of a complaint which is referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or after 1 April 2025. (2) A representative case fee payable pursuant to paragraph (1) must be paid: (a) at the time a complaint is referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service if the complainant representative is representing the complainant at the time the complaint is referred; or (b) subject to paragraph (3) below, at the time a complainant representative begins to represent the complainant in respect of a complaint that has already been referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service. (3) A complainant representative will not be liable for the representative case fee under paragraph (1) above if: (a) the representative case fee in relation to the complaint has been paid by a complainant representative who was previously representing the complainant in respect of the same complaint; or (b) the complainant representative is acting entirely pro bono in relation to the complaint.

## FEES 5.5C.6 (Rules)

A complainant representative will, in any financial year, only be liable for, and the FOS Ltd will only invoice for, the representative case fee under FEES 5.5C.5R in respect of the 11th and subsequent complaints that are referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## FEES 5.5C.7 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5C.5R(3)(b) applies where a complainant representative is representing the complainant without any fees, charges or other form of remuneration becoming payable by the complainant in any circumstance.

## FEES 5.5C.8 (Rules)

In relation to any complaint which is closed by the Financial Ombudsman Service as a change in outcome in favour of the complainant, the FOS Ltd will credit the amount of £175 to the complainant representative.

On Issue 2 (the wider pattern of charges), the response acknowledges the complaint involves potential financial loss but defers any redress decision pending investigation. The handler answer correctly notes that redress should not be committed to at this stage. However, the rubric requires the response to 'address whether redress or remedial action is appropriate—offering redress where warranted, or explaining why none is due.' The draft does not reach a position on redress for the new complaint; it only promises investigation. This is PARTIAL because redress is flagged as a matter for investigation but no position is communicated to the customer on whether redress will be offered or why it might not be.

claims.claim: Any written response informing a customer of complaint resolution must include FOS referral rights, the FOS website and telephone number, and must state that the customer has six months from the date of the final response to refer to the FOS.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (section 7.5) requires the final response letter to contain the FOS contact details and to inform the complainant that they have six months from the date of the final response to refer the complaint.

claims.claim: The six‑month referral window is only triggered by a valid final response or summary resolution communication that contains the required FOS information.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance) states that the six‑month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication that tells the complainant about the six‑month limit.

claims.claim: Because the original letter omitted the required FOS information, the six‑month clock has not yet started.
claims.reasoning: Guidance in DISP 2.8.3 makes clear that the clock only starts when the required information is provided; omission means the trigger has not occurred.

claims.claim: The new substantive complaint must be logged in the CMS as a new complaint (or treated as a re‑opened/escalated complaint), investigated impartially, and resolved within eight weeks of receipt, with a final response issued in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 sets out the requirement to log complaints in the CMS, conduct a fair impartial investigation, and (via provision 1) to issue a final response within eight weeks.

claims.claim: Complaints involving potential financial redress must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch of any final response.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 requires Head of Customer Operations countersignature for final response letters involving financial redress of £500 or more; the claim does not specify the amount, so it is only partially supported.

claims.claim: You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge, if you are not satisfied with how we have handled it.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provisions require the firm to inform the complainant of the right to refer, but they do not state that the service is free of charge.

claims.claim: The letter must include the Financial Ombudsman Service website and telephone number.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (section 7.5) mandates that all communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must contain the website and telephone number.

claims.claim: We are treating this as a new complaint and have logged it under complaint reference [REFERENCE NUMBER].
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (section 7.2) requires a new complaint to be logged in the CMS and a reference number communicated to the customer.

claims.claim: We aim to provide you with a full response within eight weeks of the date we received this complaint.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (section 7.4) states that the final response must be issued within eight weeks of receipt.

claims.claim: If we are unable to meet the eight‑week timeframe we will write to you to explain why and remind you of your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service at that stage.
claims.reasoning: The requirement to remind the complainant of the FOS referral right is covered by provision 1; the specific promise to write if the deadline is missed is not stipulated in the retrieved rules.

claims.claim: The original letter omitted the required FOS information.
claims.reasoning: No provision confirms the factual content of the specific letter; the claim is a factual statement about the letter's omission, not supported by the retrieved rules.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the final response letter I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about unauthorised overdraft charges totalling £340 applied to my current account between June and September this year. Your letter rejected my complaint, stating the charges were applied correctly under the account terms and conditions. However, the letter did not include any information about the Financial Ombudsman Service, no leaflet was enclosed, no website address was provided, and there was no mention of any time limits for me to refer my complaint elsewhere. I feel I have been left completely in the dark about my options. I believe the charges were unfair as I was never notified that my account had gone overdrawn before the charges were applied, and I want a full refund. I also want to know what my rights are now, since your so-called 'final response' told me nothing about where I can escalate this matter.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct issues that must be addressed separately.

**Issue 1: Defective final response letter (regulatory breach)**
The final response letter dated 15 November is non-compliant with DISP 1.6.2R. That rule requires a final response to: (a) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (b) provide the FOS website address; (c) inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (d) include the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R regarding the six-month time limit for FOS referral. None of these were included. The bank's own internal procedure (section 7.5 of the Customer Complaints Handling Procedure) also requires FOS contact details and the six-month time limit to be stated. This is a clear regulatory breach and must be remedied.

Importantly, under DISP 2.8.3G, the six-month time limit for the customer to refer to the FOS is only triggered by a response that qualifies as a final response and that tells the complainant about the six-month time limit. Because the 15 November letter did not include that information, the six-month clock has not yet started running for this customer. A new compliant letter will start that clock from its date of issue — please confirm this assumption before sending.

**Issue 2: Substance of the complaint (unarranged overdraft charges)**
The customer is complaining about unarranged overdraft charges of £340 applied between June and September, alleging they were not notified before charges were applied. The retrieved provisions under CONC 5C.2.1R and CONC 5C.3.2R are relevant: CONC 5C.2.1R requires that any unarranged overdraft charge must be structured as a rate of interest (not a flat fee or other charge type), and CONC 5C.3.2R provides that if an unarranged overdraft charge is imposed in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b) (unarranged rate exceeding arranged rate), the obligation to pay is unenforceable and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid. You will need to verify: (a) whether the charges were structured as a rate of interest as required; and (b) whether the unarranged overdraft rate exceeded the arranged overdraft rate. If either condition is breached, the charges may be unenforceable and refundable under CONC 5C.3.2R. The retrieved provisions do not cover the notification/pre-charge warning point the customer raises, so I cannot ground a regulatory position on that aspect from the available context.

**Recommended action:**
1. Issue a new compliant response letter that remedies the DISP 1.6.2R deficiencies (FOS leaflet, website, six-month time limit wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R).
2. Before issuing, have the complaints team review the substance of the overdraft charges against CONC 5C.2.1R and CONC 5C.3.2R to determine whether the charges were lawfully structured.
3. Because this involves potential financial redress (£340), the letter must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch per the internal procedure.
4. Human review is required before sending — see flags below.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending:**
- That the new letter will serve as the remedying final response and will start the six-month FOS referral clock from its date.
- Whether the bank consents or does not consent to waive the DISP 2.8.2R time limits (this determines which Annex 3R wording to use — the draft below uses the non-consent wording as the default, but you must confirm this is the bank's position).
- The outcome of the CONC 5C charge structure review — the draft below does not uphold or reject the substance, as that requires factual investigation you must complete first.
- Whether the complaint was received within the DISP 2.8.2R(2) long-stop time limits (six years / three years from awareness) — on the facts this appears straightforward (charges from June–September this year) but confirm.

The customer draft below acknowledges the procedural failing, provides the required FOS information, and invites the customer to await the substantive outcome of the charge review. It does not uphold or reject the substance pending your investigation.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for your letter and for bringing this matter to our attention. I am writing to you about your complaint regarding unarranged overdraft charges of £340 applied to your current account between June and September this year.

**An important correction to our previous letter**

I want to begin by apologising unreservedly. Our letter to you dated 15 November did not include information that we were required to provide. Specifically, it did not tell you about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, did not enclose the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, and did not provide the time limit within which you can make such a referral. This was an error on our part, and I am sorry for the confusion and concern this has caused you.

This letter corrects that omission and sets out your rights in full.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge — but you must do so within six months of the date of this letter.

If you do not refer your complaint in time, the Ombudsman will not have our permission to consider your complaint and so will only be able to do so in very limited circumstances. For example, if the Ombudsman believes that the delay was as a result of exceptional circumstances.

The Financial Ombudsman Service can be contacted as follows:
- Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- Telephone: 0800 023 4567

I have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, which sets out how the service works and what it can consider.

**The substance of your complaint**

You have told us that you believe the unarranged overdraft charges applied to your account were unfair, and that you were not notified that your account had gone overdrawn before the charges were applied. You are asking for a full refund of £340.

We take this seriously. We are reviewing the charges applied to your account, including how they were structured and applied, to ensure they were in accordance with the applicable rules. We will write to you again with our findings and our decision on the substance of your complaint as soon as this review is complete.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us at [contact details].

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the bank to issue a final response that encloses the FOS explanatory leaflet, provides the FOS website address, informs the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and includes the appropriate Annex 3R wording on the six-month time limit — none of which were included in the 15 November letter.

Sets out the prescribed wording that must be included in a final response where the respondent does not consent to waive the six-month time limit in DISP 2.8.2R(1); the customer draft uses this wording to remedy the omission in the original letter.

Establishes the six-month time limit within which a complainant must refer a complaint to the FOS after receiving a final response, and the longer-stop time limits; relevant to confirming when the customer's referral window opens.

Indicates that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that qualifies as a final response and that tells the complainant about the six-month limit — supporting the position that the defective 15 November letter did not start the clock.

Indicates that FOS information required in responses under the complaints time limit rules should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text of the response.

Requires that any unarranged overdraft charge must be structured as a rate of interest expressed as a percentage applied on an annual basis; relevant to assessing whether the £340 charges were lawfully imposed.

Provides that if an unarranged overdraft charge is imposed in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b), the obligation to pay is unenforceable and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid — directly relevant to the customer's refund claim if a breach is established.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

## DISP 1.6.4 (Rules)

DISP 1.6.2 R does not apply if the complainant has already indicated in writing acceptance of a response by the respondent, provided that the response:

(1) informed the complainant how to pursue his complaint with the respondent if he remains dissatisfied;

(2) referred to the ultimate availability of the Financial Ombudsman Service if he remains dissatisfied with the respondent's response;

(3) enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service standard explanatory leaflet;

(4) provided the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service; and

(5) indicated whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R (Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?) by including the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3 R.

## DISP 1.6.4A (Guidance)

DISP 1.6.4R does not affect the requirements imposed by DISP 1.6.2AR. Where a complaint is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint and DISP 1.6.2AR applies a final response must always be sent unless DISP 1.5.1R applies.

## DISP 1.6.6A (Guidance)

The information regarding the Financial Ombudsman Service, required to be provided in responses sent under the complaints time limit rules (DISP 1.6.2 R, DISP 1.6.2AR and DISP 1.6.4 R), should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text of those responses.

[Note: article 13 of the ADR Directive]

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

# CONC — CONC 5C.3 Interest rates for unarranged overdrafts to be no more than the interest rates for arranged overdrafts

## CONC 5C.3.2 (Rules)

If a firm imposes an unarranged overdraft charge in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b), the obligation to pay the charge is unenforceable against the customer and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid by, or on behalf of, the customer under the obligation imposed.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

## CONC 5C.2.1 (Rules)

(1) A firm must not:

(a) enter into an agreement with a customer that provides for an arranged overdraft charge or an unarranged overdraft charge; or

(b) impose on a customer an arranged overdraft charge or an unarranged overdraft charge,

unless the conditions in (2) to (7) are satisfied.

(2) The charge must be a rate of interest expressed as a percentage applied on an annual basis to the relevant balance of arranged overdraft or unarranged overdraft (as the case may be).

(3) The rate of interest that applies to any given balance of arranged overdraft relating to a personal current account must either be zero or the same as the rate of interest that applies to any other balance of arranged overdraft in respect of that personal current account.

(4) The rate of interest that applies to any given balance of unarranged overdraft relating to a personal current account must either be zero or the same as the rate of interest that applies to any other balance of unarranged overdraft in respect of that personal current account.

(5) A firm must not require a customer to pay more than one arranged overdraft charge or more than one unarranged overdraft charge arising out of the same event.

(6) Where a customer has an arranged overdraft, in relation to a personal current account, to which a rate of interest above zero applies, any unarranged overdraft charge imposed on the customer in relation to that personal current account must also consist of a rate of interest computed, structured and presented in an identical manner (although the level of the rate of interest that applies to the unarranged overdraft may be lower).

metadata.section_name: CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

(5) A firm is not prevented from providing in the terms and conditions of the overdraft that no interest is payable in respect of arranged overdraft balances or unarranged overdraft balances of up to specified amounts (sometimes described as “fee-free amounts” or “buffer zones”) where permitted by CONC 5C.2.1R. The purpose of CONC 5C.2.1R(7) is to prevent firms from offering fee-free amounts or buffer zones that are free only in certain circumstances. An example of a buffer zone that is not permitted is where no interest is payable if an unarranged overdraft balance does not exceed the upper threshold of the buffer zone, but where interest becomes payable in respect of the entire balance (including the part of the balance in the buffer zone) if the customer exceeds the threshold.

(6) A firm is not prevented from waiving or reducing overdraft charges (in whole or in part) in appropriate circumstances (for example, where the firm is treating a customer with forbearance in line with other rules in this sourcebook).

(7) CONC 5C.2.1R does not prohibit the level of the single, uniform contractual rate of interest from differing from customer to customer, or between personal current accounts for the same customer.

(8)

(a) The definitions of an arranged overdraft charge and an unarranged overdraft charge are broad.

(b) These definitions capture any charges that arise because a customer has used an overdraft, or that are triggered by - or the size of which are affected by - the fact that the personal current account has entered, remains in, or extended, a debit position.
