claims.claim: The final response letter dated 15 November is non‑compliant with DISP 1.6.2R.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R sets the mandatory contents of a final response (leaflet, website, right to refer, consent wording). The letter omitted these, so it is non‑compliant.

claims.claim: DISP 1.6.2R requires a final response to: (a) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (b) provide the website address of the FOS; (c) inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (d) include the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R regarding the six‑month time limit for FOS referral.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R explicitly requires (d) leaflet, (da) website, (e) inform right, and (f) indicate consent wording, but it does not expressly mandate the exact Annex 3R wording about the six‑month limit; that is guidance rather than a rule.

claims.claim: The bank's internal procedure section 7.5 also requires FOS contact details and the six‑month time limit to be stated.
claims.reasoning: Section 7.5 of the Customer Complaints Handling Procedure states the firm must notify the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS, include contact details, and state the six‑month referral period.

claims.claim: Under DISP 2.8.3G, the six‑month time limit for the customer to refer to the FOS is only triggered by a response that qualifies as a final response and that tells the complainant about the six‑month time limit.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance) states exactly that the six‑month limit is triggered only by a final response (or redress determination) that informs the complainant of the limit.

claims.claim: Because the 15 November letter did not include that information, the six‑month clock has not yet started running for this customer.
claims.reasoning: Given the guidance in DISP 2.8.3, if the required information is missing, the trigger for the six‑month period does not occur.

claims.claim: A new compliant letter will start that clock from its date of issue.
claims.reasoning: A final response that includes the required six‑month wording satisfies the condition in DISP 2.8.3, thereby starting the referral period from the date of that response.

claims.claim: CONC 5C.2.1R requires that any unarranged overdraft charge must be structured as a rate of interest (not a flat fee or other charge type).
claims.reasoning: CONC 5C.2.1(2) states the charge must be a rate of interest expressed as a percentage on an annual basis.

claims.claim: CONC 5C.3.2R provides that if an unarranged overdraft charge is imposed in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b), the obligation to pay is unenforceable and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid.
claims.reasoning: The rule explicitly sets out the unenforceability and right to recovery when the charge breaches CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b).

claims.claim: The retrieved provisions do not cover the notification/pre‑charge warning point the customer raises, so I cannot ground a regulatory position on that aspect from the available context.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision states that the notification requirement is absent; the claim is a meta‑statement about the set of provisions, which is not supported by any specific rule.

claims.claim: Our letter to you dated 15 November did not include information we were required to provide: it did not tell you about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, did not enclose the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, and did not provide the time limit within which you can make such a referral.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R mandates inclusion of the leaflet, website, right to refer, and (by guidance) the six‑month time limit; the letter omitted these, making the statement correct.

claims.claim: If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge — but you must do so within six months of the date of this letter.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.3 guidance confirms that the six‑month referral period is triggered by a final response that informs the complainant of that limit.

claims.claim: If you do not refer your complaint in time, the Ombudsman will not have our permission to consider your complaint and so will only be able to do so in very limited circumstances. For example, if the Ombudsman believes that the delay was as a result of exceptional circumstances.
claims.reasoning: The Annex 3 wording (DISP 1 Annex 3) contains exactly this language, confirming the claim.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with the final response I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about unauthorised transactions on my current account. Your letter rejected my complaint, stating that I had failed to keep my PIN secure. However, I believe this conclusion is unfair as I have never shared my PIN with anyone and I reported the transactions within hours of them occurring. Your final response letter did not include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, nor did it provide their website address. It also failed to tell me whether you consent to waive any time limits if I refer my complaint to the Ombudsman. I feel I have been treated unfairly and I want to know what my options are now. I am a pensioner on a fixed income and the loss of £1,200 from these transactions has left me unable to pay my heating bills this winter.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This is a complaint about a deficient final response letter. The customer is raising two distinct issues: (1) the substantive rejection of their unauthorised transaction complaint, and (2) procedural deficiencies in the final response letter itself.

**Procedural deficiencies in the final response (DISP 1.6.2R):**
The customer is correct that the 15 November final response letter was non-compliant. Under DISP 1.6.2R, a final response must: (d) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (da) provide the FOS website address; (e) inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS; and (f) indicate whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R by including the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R. The customer confirms the letter omitted (d), (da), and (f) — and possibly (e). These are binding rule requirements, not guidance.

**Recommended action:**
You should issue a new letter to the customer that remedies the procedural deficiencies — enclosing the FOS explanatory leaflet, providing the FOS website address and contact details, and including the DISP 1 Annex 3R wording on time limit waiver. The new letter should also restate the substantive outcome of the complaint (rejection) with reasons, so the customer has a complete final response.

**Important assumptions you must confirm before sending:**
1. **Time limit waiver position:** The draft below includes a placeholder for the DISP 1 Annex 3R wording on whether Crest Bank consents to waive the FOS time limits. You must insert the correct prescribed wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R — I do not have that annex in the retrieved context and cannot draft it for you. This is mandatory under DISP 1.6.2R(f).
2. **Substantive rejection reasons:** The draft restates the rejection on the basis described by the customer (PIN security). You must confirm this accurately reflects the reasons given in the original final response and that the investigation remains sound.
3. **Financial redress:** The customer mentions a loss of £1,200. The draft does not offer or commit to any redress, as the complaint is being maintained as rejected. If there is any possibility the substantive position should be reconsidered (e.g. in light of the customer's vulnerability or the speed of reporting), that is a separate decision for the handler and is not addressed here.
4. **Vulnerability:** The customer has indicated they are a pensioner on a fixed income and that the loss has left them unable to pay heating bills. This is a clear indicator of potential financial vulnerability. This must be flagged and handled in accordance with your internal vulnerability policy. Human review is required before despatch.
5. **Six-month FOS referral clock:** The customer's right to refer to the FOS runs from the date of the final response. The original letter of 15 November was deficient. The new letter will effectively restart or clarify the referral window — confirm with your compliance team whether the six-month period runs from 15 November or from the date of this remedying letter. The draft refers to the right to refer 'following this letter' to avoid over-committing on the start date, but this needs legal/compliance sign-off.
6. **Countersignature:** Under Crest Bank's internal procedure (section 7.4 of the Complaints Handling Procedure), any complaint involving financial redress of £500 or more must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. At £1,200, this applies here even though the complaint is rejected.

**Human review is required** before this letter is sent — see reasons flagged above.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied following our letter of 15 November, and I want to address the concerns you have raised.

**Our original response — what went wrong**

You are right to point out that our letter of 15 November did not include all of the information it should have. Specifically, it did not enclose a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, did not provide their website address, and did not tell you whether we consent to waive the time limits that apply if you wish to refer your complaint to the Ombudsman. We apologise for these omissions. This letter is intended to put that right.

**Our position on your complaint**

We have reviewed your complaint about unauthorised transactions on your current account. Having considered the information available to us, we are not upholding your complaint. Our investigation concluded that [INSERT REASONS FROM ORIGINAL INVESTIGATION — e.g. the evidence available to us indicated that the transactions were carried out using your correct PIN, and we were unable to find evidence of a system failure or third-party compromise on our side]. We recognise that you dispute this conclusion and that you reported the transactions promptly, and we have taken that into account. However, on the basis of the evidence available, we are unable to offer a different outcome at this stage.

We understand that a loss of this nature is serious, and we are sorry for the distress this situation has caused you.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You may do so following receipt of this letter.

The Financial Ombudsman Service can be contacted as follows:
- Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- Telephone: 0800 023 4567

I have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, which sets out how the service works and the time limits that apply to referrals.

[INSERT DISP 1 ANNEX 3R PRESCRIBED WORDING ON TIME LIMIT WAIVER — this is a mandatory requirement and must be included before this letter is sent]

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the final response to enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and indicate the respondent's position on waiving FOS time limits — all of which were omitted from the 15 November letter.
Indicates that information about the FOS required in final responses should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text of the response.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

## DISP 1.6.4 (Rules)

DISP 1.6.2 R does not apply if the complainant has already indicated in writing acceptance of a response by the respondent, provided that the response:

(1) informed the complainant how to pursue his complaint with the respondent if he remains dissatisfied;

(2) referred to the ultimate availability of the Financial Ombudsman Service if he remains dissatisfied with the respondent's response;

(3) enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service standard explanatory leaflet;

(4) provided the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service; and

(5) indicated whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R (Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?) by including the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3 R.

## DISP 1.6.4A (Guidance)

DISP 1.6.4R does not affect the requirements imposed by DISP 1.6.2AR. Where a complaint is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint and DISP 1.6.2AR applies a final response must always be sent unless DISP 1.5.1R applies.

## DISP 1.6.6A (Guidance)

The information regarding the Financial Ombudsman Service, required to be provided in responses sent under the complaints time limit rules (DISP 1.6.2 R, DISP 1.6.2AR and DISP 1.6.4 R), should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text of those responses.

[Note: article 13 of the ADR Directive]

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

## DISP 1.6.6B (Guidance)

A respondent may, where relevant, in a response sent under the complaints time limits rules (DISP 1.6.2R and DISP 1.6.4R) refer to the availability of the Pensions Ombudsman, in addition to the Financial Ombudsman Service, by including the wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 4G.

## DISP 1.6.7 (Guidance)

It is expected that within eight weeks of their receipt, almost all complaints to a respondent will have been substantively addressed by it through a final response or response as described in DISP 1.6.4 R.

## DISP 1.6.8 (Guidance)

When assessing a respondent's response to a complaint, the

FCA

may have regard to a number of factors, including, the quality of response, as against the complaints resolution rules, as well as the speed with which it was made.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 4. Regulatory Context

| Regulatory Source | Relevance to This Policy |
|---|---|
| **DISP 1** (Dispute Resolution: Complaints) | Sets out the FCA's detailed requirements for complaint handling, including acknowledgement and resolution timescales, final response content, and record-keeping obligations. |
| **DISP 2** | Defines the scope of the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) jurisdiction and the conditions under which customers may refer complaints to the FOS. |
| **PRIN 6** (Customers' interests) | Requires Crest Bank to pay due regard to the interests of its customers and treat them fairly throughout the complaints process. |
| **PRIN 12** (Consumer Duty) | Requires Crest Bank to act to deliver good outcomes for retail customers; complaint handling is a key mechanism for identifying and remedying poor outcomes. |
| **SYSC 9.1** | Requires Crest Bank to maintain orderly records, including records of complaints and the steps taken to investigate and resolve them. |
| **BCOBS 5** (Banking: Conduct of Business) | Sets specific conduct standards applicable to payment accounts and savings products offered by Crest Bank. |

---

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# DISP — DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

(c) encloses a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service standard explanatory leaflet; and

(d) provides the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service.

[Note: article 13 of the ADR Directive]

# DISP — DISP 1 Annex 1AA Notes on completing electronic money and payment services complaints return form

The contextual information for account information services should be the number of customers that have used the firm’s account information services (AIS) in the reporting period. For authorised PIs this figure should be the same as that provided by payment and e-money institutions in Q80 of the Authorised Payment Institution Capital Adequacy Return and for authorised EMIs, in Q76 of the Authorised Electronic Money Institution Questionnaire.

Complaints relating to alleged authorised push payment fraud (Table 4)

Information on complaints relating to alleged authorised push payment fraud should be provided in Table 4. Data in this table should not be included in any total complaint figures as these complaints should already be reported in the preceding tables under the appropriate product/service groupings (for example, under ‘Credit transfer’).

…

DISP 1 Annex 1R

This return (Payment Services Complaints Return) only relates to complaints made in relation to payment services or electronic money. All complaints should be reported in DISP 1 Annex 1R.

Transparency

To improve consumer awareness and to help payment service providers compare their performance against their peers, the FCA may publish aggregated and anonymised complaints data.

The FCA may also publish respondent level complaints data where it has the respondent’s consent. If the respondent ticks the ‘Yes’ box in this report it is consenting to the FCA publishing the complaints data.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with your bank's handling of my complaint about unauthorised overdraft charges totalling £487 applied to my current account in March 2024. I originally submitted my complaint on 15 April 2024. I received your final response letter dated 28 May 2024 which rejected my complaint, stating the charges were correctly applied under the account terms and conditions. However, I believe your response was inadequate and unfair. You failed to properly consider that I was in hospital at the time the charges accrued and had no ability to manage my account. Furthermore, I note that your final response letter did not include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, nor did it provide their website address. It simply said I could 'contact the ombudsman if unhappy' without any further detail about the time limits for doing so. I want to know what my rights are and whether I can still escalate this matter, as it has now been approximately five months since I received your response.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have been submitted by the customer directly, not by a handler. Please confirm whether you want to treat this as the customer's complaint and draft a response to them, or whether you are the handler summarising the customer's position.

Assuming you are the handler and want a draft response to the customer, here is the regulatory position:

**Defective final response:** DISP 1.6.2R requires the final response to enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant of their right to refer to FOS, and include the appropriate wording on time-limit waiver (DISP 1 Annex 3R). The customer's account of the 28 May 2024 letter — which merely said they could 'contact the ombudsman if unhappy' without leaflet, website address, or time-limit information — suggests the final response was non-compliant with DISP 1.6.2R. This is significant: under DISP 2.8.3G, the six-month time limit for FOS referral is only triggered by a response that tells the complainant about that time limit. If the 28 May letter did not do so, the six-month clock may not have started running, meaning the customer may still be in time to refer to FOS. You must check the actual letter sent.

**Timing:** The complaint was received 15 April 2024. The final response was dated 28 May 2024 — that is 43 days, within the eight-week limit under DISP 1.6.2R. No breach of the deadline appears to have occurred.

**Vulnerability:** The customer states they were hospitalised when the charges accrued. DISP 2.8.4G notes that incapacitation is an example of exceptional circumstances that could allow the FOS to consider a complaint outside time limits. This is relevant both to the substance of the complaint and to any time-limit question. This must be flagged for human review.

**Overdraft charges substance:** The charges of £487 relate to March 2024. CONC 5C.2.1R requires overdraft charges to be expressed as a rate of interest applied on an annual basis, and CONC 5C.3 restricts unarranged overdraft rates. Whether the charges were correctly applied under those rules is a substantive question requiring investigation — this cannot be resolved from the provisions alone.

**Recommended action:**
1. Retrieve and review the actual 28 May 2024 final response letter to confirm whether it included the FOS leaflet, website address, and time-limit wording as required by DISP 1.6.2R.
2. If the letter was deficient, issue a remedying letter that properly complies with DISP 1.6.2R — this would restart (or properly start) the six-month FOS referral window.
3. Consider whether the customer's hospitalisation warrants reopening the substantive complaint under your vulnerability and forbearance obligations.
4. Given the potential redress amount (£487, below the £500 countersignature threshold in your internal procedure but close to it), the vulnerability indicator, and the possible regulatory breach in the original response, this must go to human review before any letter is sent.

**Assumption flagged:** The customer draft below assumes the 28 May letter was indeed deficient as described. If on review it did include all required FOS information, the draft will need to be revised — in that case the six-month clock would have run from 28 May 2024 and may have expired (approximately 28 November 2024). Do not send the draft until the original letter has been checked.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied with our response to your complaint about overdraft charges applied to your account in March 2024, and we are sorry to learn of the difficult personal circumstances you experienced at that time.

Having reviewed your correspondence, we recognise that our final response letter dated 28 May 2024 did not include all of the information we are required to provide. In particular, it did not enclose a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, did not provide their website address, and did not set out the time limit you have to refer your complaint to them. We apologise for this.

We are writing now to make sure you have that information.

If you remain dissatisfied with our response to your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You have six months from the date of this letter to do so. Their contact details are:

Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
Telephone: 0800 023 4567

A copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet is enclosed with this letter.

[HANDLER NOTE: Insert DISP 1 Annex 3R time-limit waiver wording here as required.]

We also note your account of the circumstances in which the charges were incurred, including that you were in hospital at the time. We take this seriously and would like to review your complaint further in light of this information. We will be in touch separately regarding next steps on the substance of your complaint.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the final response to enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant of their right to refer to FOS, and include time-limit waiver wording — requirements the customer states were absent from the 28 May 2024 letter.
Sets the six-month time limit from the final response for a complainant to refer a complaint to the FOS, which is relevant to whether the customer can still escalate.
Indicates that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that tells the complainant about that time limit, meaning a deficient final response may not have started the clock running.
Indicates that incapacitation is an example of exceptional circumstances that could allow the FOS to consider a complaint outside time limits, relevant given the customer's hospitalisation.

Indicates that FOS information required in responses should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently — relevant to assessing whether the original letter met the standard.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

## DISP 1.6.4 (Rules)

DISP 1.6.2 R does not apply if the complainant has already indicated in writing acceptance of a response by the respondent, provided that the response:

(1) informed the complainant how to pursue his complaint with the respondent if he remains dissatisfied;

(2) referred to the ultimate availability of the Financial Ombudsman Service if he remains dissatisfied with the respondent's response;

(3) enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service standard explanatory leaflet;

(4) provided the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service; and

(5) indicated whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R (Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?) by including the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3 R.

## DISP 1.6.4A (Guidance)

DISP 1.6.4R does not affect the requirements imposed by DISP 1.6.2AR. Where a complaint is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint and DISP 1.6.2AR applies a final response must always be sent unless DISP 1.5.1R applies.

## DISP 1.6.6A (Guidance)

The information regarding the Financial Ombudsman Service, required to be provided in responses sent under the complaints time limit rules (DISP 1.6.2 R, DISP 1.6.2AR and DISP 1.6.4 R), should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text of those responses.

[Note: article 13 of the ADR Directive]

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 5C.3 Interest rates for unarranged overdrafts to be no more than the interest rates for arranged overdrafts

## CONC 5C.3.3 (Guidance)

(1) The purpose of CONC 5C.3.1R is to forbid firms from charging a customer who borrows a particular amount using an unarranged overdraft facility more than they would have had to pay (disregarding any fee-free amount) if they had borrowed an equivalent amount using their arranged overdraft facility (or, if they do not have an arranged overdraft facility, the highest amount that would have been payable (disregarding any fee-free amount) by a not insignificant number of other customers if they had borrowed an equivalent amount under an arranged overdraft facility connected with a comparable personal current account).

(2) In CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b), CONC 5C.3.1R(3)(d)(ii) and CONC 5C.3.2R, “impose” an unarranged overdraft charge includes creating the contractual right to receive it, and relying on, or enforcing, the contractual right or purporting to do so (“imposes” and “imposed” should be read accordingly).

(3) CONC 5C.3.1R does not affect an unarranged overdraft charge, liability for which accrued before the date on which CONC 5C.3.1R came into force. CONC 5C.3.1R does affect, however, an unarranged overdraft charge liability for which accrued on or after the date on which CONC 5C.3.1R came into force, irrespective of whether the agreement was made before or after the date on which CONC 5C.3.1R came into force.

(4) A firm is not prevented by CONC 5C.3.1R from charging a customer who borrows using an unarranged overdraft less than it charges the customer for using an arranged overdraft facility or from not charging for such borrowing.

metadata.section_name: CONC 5C.3 Interest rates for unarranged overdrafts to be no more than the interest rates for arranged overdrafts

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

## CONC 5C.2.1 (Rules)

(1) A firm must not:

(a) enter into an agreement with a customer that provides for an arranged overdraft charge or an unarranged overdraft charge; or

(b) impose on a customer an arranged overdraft charge or an unarranged overdraft charge,

unless the conditions in (2) to (7) are satisfied.

(2) The charge must be a rate of interest expressed as a percentage applied on an annual basis to the relevant balance of arranged overdraft or unarranged overdraft (as the case may be).

(3) The rate of interest that applies to any given balance of arranged overdraft relating to a personal current account must either be zero or the same as the rate of interest that applies to any other balance of arranged overdraft in respect of that personal current account.

(4) The rate of interest that applies to any given balance of unarranged overdraft relating to a personal current account must either be zero or the same as the rate of interest that applies to any other balance of unarranged overdraft in respect of that personal current account.

(5) A firm must not require a customer to pay more than one arranged overdraft charge or more than one unarranged overdraft charge arising out of the same event.

(6) Where a customer has an arranged overdraft, in relation to a personal current account, to which a rate of interest above zero applies, any unarranged overdraft charge imposed on the customer in relation to that personal current account must also consist of a rate of interest computed, structured and presented in an identical manner (although the level of the rate of interest that applies to the unarranged overdraft may be lower).

metadata.section_name: CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

# BCOBS — BCOBS 8.4 Alerts

## BCOBS 8.4.15 (Rules)

(1) A firm must send an alert to a banking customer if the firm:

(a) knows based on information available to it that the banking customer has incurred a charge for attempting to enter unarranged overdraft but has not entered unarranged overdraft because the firm declined to process the transaction; or

(b) is reasonably able to determine that, taking into account information it has access to on transactions due to be settled, a situation described in (a) will occur that day in the absence of:

(i) action by the banking customer; or

(ii) a transaction other than those the firm is aware of.

(2) The alert must communicate to the banking customer in plain simple language:

(a) the reason why the alert has been sent; and

(b) that the banking customer has incurred or may incur charges, if this is the case.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

(5) A firm is not prevented from providing in the terms and conditions of the overdraft that no interest is payable in respect of arranged overdraft balances or unarranged overdraft balances of up to specified amounts (sometimes described as “fee-free amounts” or “buffer zones”) where permitted by CONC 5C.2.1R. The purpose of CONC 5C.2.1R(7) is to prevent firms from offering fee-free amounts or buffer zones that are free only in certain circumstances. An example of a buffer zone that is not permitted is where no interest is payable if an unarranged overdraft balance does not exceed the upper threshold of the buffer zone, but where interest becomes payable in respect of the entire balance (including the part of the balance in the buffer zone) if the customer exceeds the threshold.

(6) A firm is not prevented from waiving or reducing overdraft charges (in whole or in part) in appropriate circumstances (for example, where the firm is treating a customer with forbearance in line with other rules in this sourcebook).

(7) CONC 5C.2.1R does not prohibit the level of the single, uniform contractual rate of interest from differing from customer to customer, or between personal current accounts for the same customer.

(8)

(a) The definitions of an arranged overdraft charge and an unarranged overdraft charge are broad.

(b) These definitions capture any charges that arise because a customer has used an overdraft, or that are triggered by - or the size of which are affected by - the fact that the personal current account has entered, remains in, or extended, a debit position.

# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

(c) If the agreement provides that a charge is payable by a customer in exchange for the creation or continuation of an arranged overdraft facility, whether or not the customer in fact uses the facility, this charge is also caught by the definition of an arranged overdraft charge unless the facility has a pre-agreed limit in excess of £10,000. A charge of this sort is often referred to as a “facility fee” and payable periodically, for example annually.

(d) The definitions of an arranged overdraft charge and an unarranged overdraft charge are not limited to charges that are described as financial consideration for the provision of credit. They could include, for example, a charge that is expressed as being referable to the execution of the payment transaction, if the charge is payable only where the transaction results in the account being in an overdrawn position or remaining in such a position. A charge for a payment transaction that is payable irrespective of whether or not the current account has a credit balance or a debit balance is not, however, caught by these definitions.

(e) The definitions also do not include charges for operating or maintaining a personal current account (as distinct from charges for granting or continuing to make available an arranged overdraft facility in connection with the account), provided that the incidence and amount of the charges are not affected by whether or how much the customer uses an overdraft. A monthly account charge could be an example of such a charge.

(9) CONC 5C.2 requires firms to use only a rate of interest expressed as a percentage applied on an annual basis to the relevant balance of arranged overdraft or unarranged overdraft. verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us... remain dissatisfied with our response to your complaint about overdraft charges applied to your account in March 2024
verdicts.reasoning: The opening paragraph explicitly acknowledges the complaint and correctly restates its subject.
The draft does not state a firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) on the overdraft charge complaint; it only notes a procedural deficiency.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our response to your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing text clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS and provides contact details.

verdicts.evidence: You have six months from the date of this letter to do so.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft specifies a six‑month deadline measured from the date of the final response letter.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry to hear... we recognise that our final response... did not include all of the information... We will be in touch separately regarding next steps on the substance of your complaint.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is apologetic, non‑dismissive, and indicates a further review, demonstrating fair and balanced treatment.
The draft does not address whether any monetary redress will be offered or explain why none is due.

verdicts.evidence: We also note your account of the circumstances... you were in hospital at the time. We take this seriously and would like to review your complaint further in light of this information.
verdicts.reasoning: Both the handler note and the customer draft recognise the hospitalisation as a vulnerability indicator and commit to reviewing the complaint with that context.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied with our response to your complaint about overdraft charges applied to your account in March 2024, and we are sorry to learn of the difficult personal circumstances you experienced at that time.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens with a clear acknowledgement of the complaint (overdraft charges in March 2024) and reflects accurate understanding of the subject matter. It also acknowledges the customer's difficult circumstances.

The customer draft does not state a clear decision on the substantive complaint (uphold, partially uphold, or reject the overdraft charges complaint). It acknowledges the deficiency in the original response and commits to further review ('We will be in touch separately regarding next steps on the substance of your complaint') but does not provide a decision or substantive reasons engaging with whether the charges were correctly applied. This is a remedying letter addressing the procedural defect, not a substantive final response, but it does not make that distinction clear to the customer.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our response to your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge... Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk Telephone: 0800 023 4567 A copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet is enclosed with this letter.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the right to refer to the FOS and provides multiple means to do so: website, telephone number, and an enclosed leaflet. This satisfies the requirement to inform the complainant of their right to refer and how to do so.

verdicts.evidence: You have six months from the date of this letter to do so.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft explicitly states the six-month deadline and anchors it to 'the date of this letter' (the firm's final response). This satisfies the requirement to inform the complainant of the six-month referral window running from the firm's response.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied... We are sorry to learn of the difficult personal circumstances you experienced at that time. Having reviewed your correspondence, we recognise that our final response letter dated 28 May 2024 did not include all of the information we are required to provide... We apologise for this... We take this seriously and would like to review your complaint further in light of this information.
verdicts.reasoning: The response is clear, even-handed, and non-dismissive. It acknowledges the customer's circumstances sympathetically, recognises the procedural failing in the original response, apologises, and commits to further substantive review. The language is appropriate and does not minimise the customer's concerns.

verdicts.evidence: We also note your account of the circumstances in which the charges were incurred, including that you were in hospital at the time. We take this seriously and would like to review your complaint further in light of this information. We will be in touch separately regarding next steps on the substance of your complaint.
verdicts.reasoning: The response flags that redress will be considered ('review your complaint further', 'next steps on the substance') but does not reach or communicate a position on redress itself. The handler answer notes the £487 amount and vulnerability factors warrant human review, but the customer draft defers the redress decision to a future communication rather than addressing it in this response. This is PARTIAL because the question is acknowledged but no clear position is communicated to the customer.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: 'Vulnerability: The customer states they were hospitalised when the charges accrued... This must be flagged for human review.' Customer draft: 'We are sorry to learn of the difficult personal circumstances you experienced at that time... We also note your account of the circumstances in which the charges were incurred, including that you were in hospital at the time. We take this seriously and would like to review your complaint further in light of this information.'
verdicts.reasoning: The response identifies the vulnerability indicator (hospitalisation) in both the handler answer (flagged for human review) and the customer draft (acknowledged sympathetically and committed to further review in light of it). The response adapts its handling by committing to substantive reconsideration and uses supportive language. This satisfies the requirement to recognise and act on vulnerability.

claims.claim: The final response was non‑compliant with DISP 1.6.2R because it only said the complainant could ‘contact the ombudsman if unhappy’ and omitted the leaflet, website address and time‑limit information.
claims.reasoning: No provision confirms the specific content of the 28 May letter; the rule sets the requirement but does not verify the letter's omission.

claims.claim: Under DISP 2.8.3G, the six‑month time limit for Financial Ombudsman Service referral is only triggered by a response that tells the complainant about that time limit.
claims.reasoning: Provision 3 includes DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance) stating exactly that the six‑month limit is only triggered by a final response that informs the complainant of the limit.

claims.claim: If the 28 May letter did not tell the complainant about the six‑month limit, the six‑month clock may not have started running, meaning the customer may still be in time to refer to the FOS.
claims.reasoning: The guidance in DISP 2.8.3 directly implies that without the required wording the time‑limit does not commence.

claims.claim: The final response was sent 43 days after the complaint was received, which is within the eight‑week limit under DISP 1.6.2R.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R obliges the respondent to send the final response within eight weeks of receipt.

claims.claim: No breach of the eight‑week deadline appears to have occurred.
claims.reasoning: Same rule as above confirms the deadline was met.

claims.claim: DISP 2.8.4G notes that incapacitation is an example of exceptional circumstances that could allow the FOS to consider a complaint outside the time limits.
claims.reasoning: Provision 3 (DISP 2.8.4 Guidance) gives incapacitation as an example of exceptional circumstances.

claims.claim: CONC 5C.2.1R requires overdraft charges to be expressed as a rate of interest applied on an annual basis.
claims.reasoning: Provision 6 (CONC 5C.2.1 Rules) paragraph (2) states the charge must be a rate of interest expressed as a percentage on an annual basis.

claims.claim: CONC 5C.3 restricts unarranged overdraft rates.
claims.reasoning: Provision 5 (CONC 5C.3 Guidance) explains that unarranged overdraft rates must not be higher than arranged overdraft rates, which is a restriction.

claims.claim: Whether the charges were correctly applied under those rules is a substantive question requiring investigation and cannot be resolved from the provisions alone.
claims.reasoning: No provision states that the correctness of a particular charge must be investigated; this is a factual observation, not a regulatory rule.

claims.claim: Our final response letter dated 28 May 2024 did not include all of the information we are required to provide (leaflet, website address, time‑limit).
claims.reasoning: The provisions set the requirement but do not confirm that the specific letter omitted those items.

claims.claim: If you remain dissatisfied with our response to your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: Provision 1 (section 7.5) states that the firm must notify the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS.

claims.claim: You have six months from the date of this letter to do so.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2 (Rules) sets a six‑month limit after the final response; DISP 2.8.3 guidance confirms the limit is triggered by the final response.

claims.claim: A copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet is enclosed with this letter.
claims.reasoning: The provision does not verify that the leaflet is actually enclosed in this particular letter.
